concepts/city-structure.title: Ottoman City Structure (İmâret, Mahalle, and the Vakıf System)
concepts/city-structure.content:
Ottoman City Structure (İmâret, Mahalle, and the Vakıf System)
Overview
The Ottoman city, as described by Halil Inalcik, was shaped by three interlocking forces: the imâret system of pious foundations, the vakıf (pl. evkaf) endowment mechanism that financed them, and the mahalle (neighbourhood) as the basic unit of social organization. Following the near-eastern imperial tradition, Ottoman sultans sought to transform their capitals — Bursa (بروسه), Edirne (ادرنه), and Istanbul (استانبول) — into great cities by increasing their population, building monumental religious and commercial complexes, and encouraging their development as trading centres. Istanbul, rebuilt after the 1453 conquest, became the model Ottoman city and, by the first half of the sixteenth century, the largest city in Europe with a population reaching four hundred thousand souls.

The Imâret (عمارت) — The Urban Nucleus
The imâret was the foundational institution of Ottoman urbanism. Inalcik describes it as a “complex of institutions — mosque, medrese, hospital, traveller’s hostel, water installations, roads and bridges — founded with pious or charitable motives, and the institutions which provided revenue for their upkeep, such as an inn, market, caravanserai, bath house, mill, dyehouse, slaughterhouse or soup kitchen.” The religious and charitable buildings (cami, medrese, imaret proper, hospital) were grouped around a mosque, while the commercial establishments (bedesten, han, shops, hamam) stood nearby or in some suitably active location.
These imârets gave Ottoman towns their peculiar character. Until recently, they dominated the skyline of cities in Anatolia and the Balkans. A new city quarter grew up around each imâret: the founder built a mosque, endowed shops and markets to generate revenue, and within a short time people settled nearby and founded new mahalles. As Inalcik writes of Istanbul, “buildings devoted to the public good were erected around the mosque bearing the donor’s name, and within a short time people settled near these imârets and founded new quarters. Istanbul thus took on its characteristic Turkish appearance.”

Physical Components of the Ottoman City
Sur (سور) — The City Walls
The Ottoman city inherited its defensive perimeter from earlier Byzantine, Roman, or pre-Ottoman fortifications. Inalcik notes that the storming of city walls was the decisive military event in conquest — most famously the walls of Constantinople in 1453. After conquest, the Ottomans maintained and repaired existing walls but did not always enclose new neighbourhoods that grew organically outside the old walls. The Eyüp district of Istanbul, for example, grew up outside the land walls around the mosque and tomb of Ebû Eyyûb el-Ensârî, becoming “a large, separate town.”
Mahalle (محله) — The Neighbourhood
The mahalle was the fundamental unit of urban social organization. Inalcik writes: “Away from work … the members of different faiths lived apart in separate quarters of the city, under the headship of their own religious leaders. Ottoman cities always had separate Muslim, Christian and Jewish quarters, the gypsies too having their own district, regardless of their religion.”
Each mahalle was organized around its place of worship. In a Muslim ward, the imam (امام) served as the religious head of the community, while a kethüdâ (كتخدا) acted as its secular representative and spokesman before the government. Priests and rabbis performed the same functions in non-Muslim quarters. The imam’s role was crucial: he recorded births, marriages, and deaths; he represented his mahalle to the kâdî (قاضی); and he witnessed the daily life of the quarter. The mosque of the mahalle — usually a small neighbourhood mosque (mescid, مسجد) rather than a great imperial cami — was the focal point of community life.
This separation of quarters by faith, Inalcik notes, “did not hinder the good relations between Muslims and non-Muslims. Muslim men often married non-Muslim women without the woman having to change her religion. The children, however, had to be Muslim.”
Çarşı (چارشو) — The Market Street
The commercial spine of the Ottoman city was the çarşı — streets lined with shops, each street or section occupied by members of a single craft. Inalcik describes how after the conquest, Mehmed II built 984 shops in the markets surrounding the bedestan. Each group of shops, “branching out and lining both sides of a road, formed a single market, occupied by members of a single craft or by merchants selling the same type of goods. These markets were usually roofed with stone vaults, as in the market at Istanbul, or else they stayed as lanes of open shops, shaded by trees.”
Bedesten (بذستان) — The Covered Market
The bedesten was the commercial heart of every significant Ottoman city. Inalcik describes it as a “monumental building, with its stone domes and iron doors resisting fire and pillage, serving to protect not only valuable commercial goods but also the money and jewellery of the city’s rich.” Its doorkeepers, night-watchmen, and brokers were under government control.
The construction of a bedesten typically played a decisive role in the growth of an Ottoman city. Orhan Gazi had built one in Bursa as early as 1340, which “to this day has remained as the commercial centre of the town.” Trade centres grew up around bedestens in all large Ottoman towns of the Balkans: Tatar Pazarjik, Plovdiv, Sarajevo, Sofia, Skopje, Monastir, Serres, and Salonica. Evliya Çelebi, the great seventeenth-century traveller, divided Ottoman cities into two categories — those with bedestens and those without.
Mehmed II’s bedesten in Istanbul had 118 shops with storerooms inside; the surrounding markets added 984 shops. This became the main business centre of Istanbul, known today as the Kapalıçarşı (Covered Market).
Han (خان) / Kervansaray (كاروانسرای) — The Caravanserai
The han or caravanserai was the lodging and warehouse for travelling merchants. Inalcik describes the great caravanserais as “strongly fortified against brigands.” The grand vizier İbrahim Paşa built one at Tatar-Pazarjik which “according to Evliyâ Çelebi dominated the city like a fortress. It contained two hundred rooms and eighty suites where important men could stay with their families. The rooms surrounded a courtyard shaded by a great tree, and an outer courtyard, with a pond in the centre, could accommodate five or six thousand horses.”
The han provided free lodging and food for three days. Busbecq, the Habsburg ambassador, wrote of one in Niş: “They have several distinct apartments for lodging in them. There is no man to forbid the use of them, either Christian or Jew, rich or poor, they are open equally to all.”
Cami (جامع) and Külliye (كلية) — The Mosque and Mosque Complex
The great imperial mosque (cami) was the symbolic and physical centre of the Ottoman city. Around it was arranged the külliye — the full complex of charitable and educational institutions: medreses (مدارس, theological schools), a sıbyan mektebi (مكتب, primary school), a kütüphane (كتابخانه, library), a darüşşifa (دارالشفا, hospital), tabhane (طبخانه, hostel for travellers), and a imaret (عمارت, public kitchen). The külliye of Mehmed II in Istanbul (1463-1470) included eight medreses, a school for children, a library, a hospital, two hostels for travellers, and a refectory, supported by a market of 318 shops nearby. Six hundred students studied in the medreses and each day the hostels accommodated 160 travellers.
Hamam (حمام) — The Bathhouse
The hamam was a standard component of the imâret complex and an essential feature of every Ottoman neighbourhood. Inalcik notes that Mehmed II built thirteen bathhouses as part of his vakıf endowments in Istanbul. The hamam served both hygienic and social functions — it was a place of cleansing before prayer, a meeting place, and an income-generating property whose revenues supported the charitable institutions of the vakıf. Income from bathhouses frequently funded the maintenance of bridges, hostels, and mosques, as when Murad II used the revenues from “a boza shop, bath-house and shops” to maintain the great Ergene bridge at Uzunköprü.

The Vakıf (وقف) System
Inalcik devotes considerable attention to the vakıf as the financial engine of Ottoman urbanism. In Islamic society, charitable institutions were nearly always established as vakıfs. This ensured their continued existence, since a vakıf devoted in perpetuity the profits from any source to some charitable purpose, “without impairing the capital.” It was a legal fiction that from the time of its endowment only God had proprietary rights over a vakıf, so that “even if governments or states should change the continuance of the public service was guaranteed.”
How a Vakıf Worked
The founder (vâkıf, واقف) drew up a vakfiye (وقفیه) — the deed of endowment — before a kâdî, entered it in his register, and confirmed it with the sultan. The vakfiye determined the endowment’s purpose, conditions, and forms of management, and appointed a mütevellî (متولی) — chief trustee. In large vakıfs a nâzır (ناظر) — superintendent — was also appointed to inspect whether the endowment’s conditions were being fulfilled.
The mütevellî was responsible for collecting and growing the vakıf’s income and using the funds to maintain the foundation’s buildings, pay its employees, and fulfil the conditions of the endowment. Once a year, the principal officers and employees of the vakıf met to audit themselves and could request the mütevellî’s dismissal. The state, through the local kâdî or a specially appointed inspector, also audited the accounts of each vakıf. “The aim of all these precautions,” Inalcik writes, “was to ensure that the institution continued to fulfil its proper function.”
The Temlik System
An important factor in the foundation of urban vakıfs was the institution of temlik (تملیك) — the royal grant of property rights. Statesmen or Palace women could apply to the sultan with a project for a charitable undertaking and receive from him the freehold of a piece of land, in some cases including several villages. They then bequeathed these lands to the charitable enterprise, assuring not only their own salvation in the next world but a continuous livelihood for their descendants, who were appointed mütevellîs. Inalcik notes that “the second and third generation descendants of Ottoman notables frequently enjoyed a comfortable retirement as mütevellîs of vakıfs, forming a kind of a class of absentee landlords.”
Scale of the Vakıf System in Istanbul
According to an official survey of 1546, there were 2,517 vakıfs founded by non-royal persons in Istanbul, with 1,600 new vakıfs added in the following half-century. To meet the expenses of his foundations alone, Mehmed II built markets (260 shops in Galata and 783 in Istanbul, not counting those in and around the bedesten), thirteen bathhouses, a number of dyehouses, bakehouses, warehouses, candleworks, oil-presses, and fifty-four mills. The revenues assigned to Hagia Sophia alone produced a yearly income of thirteen thousand gold ducats.
Inalcik summarizes: “The system of vakıfs created the cultural and commercial complexes in Istanbul.”

Istanbul: The Model Ottoman City
Inalcik presents Istanbul as the paradigmatic Ottoman city, the fullest expression of the imâret system and the model that lesser cities imitated. After the conquest of 1453, when the population had fallen to between thirty and forty thousand, Mehmed II undertook a deliberate programme of repopulation and construction:


Repopulation: He induced refugees to return by restoring their property and guaranteeing freedom of worship. He ordered provincial governors to send four thousand families from Rumelia and Anatolia. He forcibly settled merchants and artisans from conquered cities — Amasra (1459), Trebizond (1461), Karaman (1470s), Caffa (1475) — in different quarters of the city.


Founding of Imârets: In 1459, he assembled the empire’s leading men and required each to create an imâret in any part of the city he wished. The grand vizier Mahmud Paşa and other viziers constructed fine complexes in the centre and around the Golden Horn.


Food Supply: The provisioning of Istanbul became a major factor in creating an integrated imperial economy. The city’s ovens consumed 250 tons of wheat daily by the mid-seventeenth century, and 2,000 food-carrying ships arrived each year. The state regulated everything from producer to retailer to prevent profiteering and inflation.


By the census of 1477, Istanbul had 16,324 families (about 80,000-100,000 people). By the first half of the sixteenth century, the population reached four hundred thousand — larger than any city in Europe. Inalcik notes that “40 per cent of the population was non-Muslim” and that “in the mid-seventeenth century there were in Istanbul 152 Mosques, 126 medreses, a hundred caravanserais and about a thousand mansions which sultans, pashas and lesser persons had founded.”

Ottoman Turkish Glossary















































































































































Ottoman TurkishTransliterationModern TurkishEnglishعمارتimâretimaretPublic foundation / soup kitchen complexوقفvakfvakıfPious endowmentوقفیهvakfiyevakfiyeDeed of endowmentمتولیmütevellîmütevelliTrustee of a vakıfناظرnâzirnazırSuperintendent / inspectorسورsûrsurCity wallمحلهmahallemahalleNeighbourhood / quarterامامimâmimamPrayer leader / community headكتخداkethüdâkâhya / kethüdaSecular representative of a quarter or guildچارشوçârşûçarşıMarket streetبذستانbedestenbedestenCovered market / vaulted market hallخانhânhanInn / caravanseraiكاروانسرایkârvânsarâykervansarayCaravanseraiجامعcâmicamiFriday mosqueكليةkülliyyekülliyeMosque complexحمامhamâmhamamBathhouseقاضیkâdîkadıIslamic judgeمدرسهmedresemedreseTheological schoolدارالشفاdârüşşifâdarüşşifaHospitalتملیكtemliktemlikRoyal grant of property rightsییغیت باشیyiğitbaşıyiğitbaşıGuild internal affairs officerاحیahîahiFraternal guild leader
Key Points from Inalcik

The imâret was the nucleus of every Ottoman city: a complex of mosque, medrese, hospital, hostel, and commercial buildings whose revenues came from vakıf endowments.
The mahalle was organized by religion and ethnicity; each had its imam (for Muslims) or priest/rabbi (for non-Muslims) and its kethüdâ (secular representative).
The bedesten was the defining sign of a major city; Evliya Çelebi divided cities into those with and without bedestens.
The vakıf system was autonomous under Islamic law but the Ottoman state controlled and audited all endowments to ensure they fulfilled their purposes.
Istanbul was the model: deliberately repopulated by Mehmed II, built through imârets ordered from state officials, and sustained by a centralized provisioning system that integrated the entire empire.
The physical structure — sur, mahalle, çarşı, bedesten, han, cami/külliye, hamam — was replicated in Ottoman cities from Sarajevo to Bursa to Damascus.

concepts/court-and-government.title: Ottoman Court and Central Government under Suleiman the Magnificent
concepts/court-and-government.content:
Ottoman Court and Central Government under Suleiman the Magnificent
The Ruling Institution
The government of the Ottoman Empire in the time of Suleiman the Magnificent (سلطان سليمان قانونى) was built upon what Albert Howe Lybyer termed the “Ruling Institution” — a single, integrated slave-family that simultaneously served as the empire’s government, army, court, and nobility. Almost all of its members were recruited as slaves from Christian subjects, converted to Islam, trained through a rigorous educational system, and advanced purely on merit. The institution was a despotism centered on the sultan, but one heavily circumscribed by the Sacred Law (شريعت, Şeriat) of Islam, which functioned as a rigid, unamendable constitution.
The sultan was head of state in multiple capacities: as the supreme military commander wielding actual power through his kullar (قُلَّر, “slaves”); as the caliph (خليفه, Halife), defender and executor of the Sacred Law; as the successor to the Byzantine Caesars, inheriting their imperial splendor; and as the heir of ancient Turkish traditions of absolute obedience to the ruler who led and fed his people. All institutions of the empire — the Ruling Institution, the Moslem Institution, the Christian and Jewish ecclesiastical organizations, and even the foreign colonies — were “extended downward” from the divinely appointed sovereign at the top.
The Imperial Household
The sultan’s household was divided into three principal subdivisions: the outside service (بیرون, Birun), the inside service (اندرون, Enderun), and the harem (حرم, Harem). The outside service was composed of grown men and Ajem-oghlans, the inside service of white eunuchs and pages, and the harem of black eunuchs and women. In time of peace the first two subdivisions were in attendance at the principal palace built by Mehmed II on the acropolis of ancient Byzantium (the site of the modern Topkapı Palace); the harem was permanently located in the Old Palace in the center of the city.
The grounds of the principal palace were extensive: within the first gate lay a large open space used as a parade ground on state occasions; within the second gate stood the palace buildings proper, a beautiful garden, and an exercise ground for the pages. Members of the outside service — except the gardeners — did not ordinarily pass beyond the second gate. In time of war, practically the entire outside service and the principal officers of the inside service accompanied the sultan; no women from the harem were taken on campaign.
The total personnel of the imperial household is estimated at between ten and fifteen thousand individuals, of whom only a few (the Ulema learned men) were not under the complete control of the Ruling Institution.
The Harem (حرم همايون)
The harem in Suleiman’s time was far less prominent than it would become in later centuries, yet it demands attention because of the great influence of two of its ladies: Roxelana (خُرَّم, Khurrem) and Suleiman’s daughter Mihrmah.
The guard and order of the harem palace were committed to forty or more black eunuchs under the Kizlar Aghasi (قزلر آغاسی, “general of the girls”). This Agha was held in great honor and administered many religious endowments (اوقاف, evkaf), particularly those of the Holy Cities of Mecca and Medina.
The greatest lady of the harem, while she lived, was the sultan’s mother, the Valide Sultan (والده سلطان). She received great deference from her son and exercised general authority over all his women. Next in importance was the mother of the sultan’s eldest son, and after her the mothers of other sons — mothers of daughters held far less consideration. Each favored lady had her own suite of apartments, a business staff under a Kiaya (كَهْيَا, “steward”), and personal servants. Sons lived with their mothers during childhood, received careful education in letters and arms, and were sent out at the appropriate age as provincial governors (sancak beyleri). Daughters were married young to high officials.
Suleiman’s mother lived until far into his reign. The mother of his eldest son, Mustapha, held the next place until Roxelana — made a legal wife — supplanted her. Suleiman seems not to have visited his harem very often. His daughter Mihrmah, wife of Grand Vizier Rüstem Pasha, was especially dear to him.
The Inside Service (اندرون)
The inside service of the principal palace was supplied by five chambers (اوده, oda) of pages under the control of white eunuchs, together with doorkeepers. The head of this service was the Kapu Aghasi (قپو آغاسی, “general of the gate”), a white eunuch who managed many religious endowments and had the right to speak to the sultan whenever he wished. The Kapuji-bashi (قپوجی باشی, head doorkeeper), also a white eunuch, commanded a company of twenty or more white eunuchs who guarded the second gate.
The pages have been described from the educational point of view in Lybyer’s account of the palace school. Nearest the sultan’s person were the pages of the Has Oda (خاص اوده, Inner Chamber), of whom there were thirty-nine (the sultan himself being the fortieth). Their chief officer was the Has Oda-bashi. The pages of highest rank were the Silihdar (سله‌دار, who carried the sultan’s weapons outside the palace), the Chokadar (چوقه‌دار, who carried his garments), and the Sharabdar (شرابدار, cup-bearer).
The second group of pages constituted the Khazineh Odassi (خزینه اوده‌سی, treasury chamber), sixty or seventy strong, under a white eunuch — the inside Khazinehdar-bashi. These pages cared for all treasures in the palace, made payments, and kept accounts. A second Khazinehdar-bashi managed the financial affairs of the inside service outside the palace walls.
The Kiler Odassi (كیلر اوده‌سی, pantry chamber), under a white eunuch called the Kilerji-bashi, cared for the sultan’s bread, pastry, and game, and also controlled the kitchen service of the palace. The remaining two chambers — the Large and the Small (or the Old and the New) — were wholly concerned with the education of the pages, under the general direction of the Ikinji-Kapu-oghlan (ایكی نجی قپو اوغلانی, eunuch of the second gate).
The entire personnel of the inside service amounted to six to eight hundred persons. Eunuch officers maintained severe discipline, exact obedience, and perfect order among them all.
The Outside Service (بیرون)
The outside service was far more numerous than the inside. Beginning with those closest to the sultan, its groups were:
Learned Associates — Chiefly from the Ulema (علماء). The sultan’s Hoja (خواجه, teacher) was a religious adviser held in great esteem, often advanced to high judicial office. Next came two Imams (ائمه, preachers) with muezzins, then the Hekim-bashi (حكیم باشی, chief physician) with ten or more associates, the Munejim-bashi (منجم باشی, chief astrologer), and the Jerrah-bashi (جراح باشی, chief surgeon).
Kitchen Service — Under the oversight of the Kilerji-bashi, comprising bakers, scullions, cooks, confectioners, tasters, and musicians, each fifty to one hundred strong. Allied companies of tailors, shoemakers, furriers, and goldsmiths were employed exclusively in the palace. Many menial servants — scullions, wood-cutters, water-carriers — were Ajem-oghlans (عجم اوغلان, “foreign boys” in training).
Body-Guards — Three corps: the Muteferrika (متفرفه, Noble Guard), one to two hundred of the choicest page-school graduates, who followed immediately after the sultan on horseback and defended him in battle; the Solaks (صولاق, Janissary archers), about one hundred and fifty who marched on foot beside him with bows ready; and the Peiks (پَیْك, halberdiers), about one hundred men, taken over from the Byzantine emperors, who ran before the sultan when he rode.
Palace-Guards — The Kapujis (قپوجی, gatekeepers), three to four hundred Ajem-oghlans who watched the outside gates; the Chaushes (چاوش, ushers/marshals), about one hundred, who acted as marshals on Divan days, dressed the ranks at war, served as state messengers, and — being often multilingual renegades — as interpreters and envoys; and the Bostanjis (بوستانجی, gardeners), about four hundred Ajem-oghlans who cared for the gardens and rowed the sultan’s caiques. The Bostanji-bashi seems to have been the only adult man besides the sultan who resided within the inner palace.
Stable Service — Extremely important in a cavalry nation. The sultan kept two hundred horses for his own use, tended by a hundred men, and four thousand for his retinue, tended by two thousand. A thousand or more Bulgarian Voinaks tended herds on the domanial pastures. The Emir-al-Akhor (امير آخور, grand equerry) and the second equerry oversaw saddlers, camel-drivers, and muleteers, and controlled all domanial pastures and forests.
The Rekiab-Aghalari (ركاب آغالری, “generals of the imperial stirrup”) were a special group of officers: the head gardener, head gatekeeper, grand equerry, second equerry, and the Mir-Alem (مير علم, standard-bearer). The Mir-Alem had charge of the imperial standards and the six horsetails (طوغ, tuğ) borne before the sultan, distributed standards and horsetails to beylerbeys and sancakbeys (thus granting them investiture), ranked first among household officers in relation to the government, and commanded the military music.
Additional groups included the tent-pitchers (Mihter-bashi), the Veznedars (وزنه‌دار, weighers of money), and the masters of the hunt — heads of dog-keepers, falconers, vulturers, gerfalconers, and hawkers. Grand Vizier Ibrahim was chief falconer at the time of his promotion.
The Umena (امنا, intendants) exercised clear governmental functions: the Shehr-emini (شهر امينی, intendant of imperial buildings), the Zarabkhaneh-emini (ضرابخانه امينی, intendant of mints and mines), and others. These were exceptions, made for convenience, to the general rule of separating household from governmental officials.
Court Ceremonies
The early Ottoman sultans maintained simplicity of life down to the time of Murad II. Mehmed II, after the conquest of Constantinople, appropriated Byzantine imperial forms and ceremonies. His Kanuns (قانون, laws) dealt extensively with matters of rank and ceremony. By Suleiman’s time the Kanuni Teshrifat (قانون تشريفات, Law of Ceremonies) had become a sizeable collection, standing on a par with the Law of Fiefs, the Law of Egypt, and the Law of Fines and Punishments. It regulated the color, shape, and material of robes and turbans, the order of precedence on every occasion, and the proper observances for each event. It was as much an officer’s duty to wear the correct costume and appear in the right place at the right time as to attend to the business of his position.
All classes of the household, all high officers of government, and all separate bodies of troops were clearly distinguished by costume or head-dress or both.
Ceremonial occasions were numerous:

The Divan met four times weekly (Saturday through Tuesday)
The sultan rode to Friday mosque in magnificent state
Janissaries were paid quarterly with ceremony
Ambassadors were received on such occasions to impress them with wealth and power
The two great festivals of Bairam (عيد, Id) — one closing Ramadan, the other seventy days later — involved the ceremonial kissing of the sultan’s hand by all officials
Extraordinary ceremonies celebrated the birth of children, the circumcision of princes, the marriage of princesses, accessions to the throne, and the sultan’s going forth to war

The greatest of Suleiman’s celebrations was the circumcision of his sons Mustapha, Mehmed, and Selim in 1530 — twenty-one successive days of display, feasting, games, and formal presentation of gifts.
Ogier Ghiselin de Busbecq, ambassador from Emperor Charles V, left vivid descriptions of Suleiman’s court: the sultan seated on a low ottoman covered with costly rugs, surrounded by a sea of turbaned heads in dazzling robes of gold, silver, purple, and silk; the absolute silence and order of thousands of men; the Janissaries standing “so steady and motionless” that Busbecq could not tell whether they were “human beings or statues”; the magnificent cavalry with horses from Cappadocia and Syria, trappings sparkling with gold and jewels, riders in cloth of silver and gold, bows and painted arrows from Babylon, jewelled scimitars, and steel maces.
The Viziers and the Grand Vizierate
Ottoman writers represented their government under the figure of a tent supported by four lofty pillars: the Viziers (وزراء, Vüzera), the Kaziaskers (قاضى عسكر, military judges), the Defterdars (دفتردار, treasurers), and the Nishanjis (نشانجی, chancellors). The pillars did not bear equal weight, however: from Suleiman’s time the grand vizier carried so much greater a burden that he might be compared to a central pillar supporting the entire tent.
The word vizier (وزير) means “burden-bearer” — lifting from the sovereign’s shoulders the burden of state. In Suleiman’s reign there were ordinarily four viziers, that being a sacred number. All bore the title pasha (پاشا). Ordinary viziers had no regular responsibilities beyond their function as councillors; they enjoyed great incomes from regular and irregular sources and kept large establishments modeled on the sultan’s.
Under Suleiman the office of grand vizier (صدر اعظم, Sadr-ı Azam) reached the climax of a notable development. From being first among equals, the grand vizier became a personage far above his fellow-viziers — a difference not merely in degree but in kind, typified by the fact that in reporting to Suleiman after the Divan, no one spoke but the grand vizier.
This development resulted from Suleiman’s willingness to entrust vast power to chosen instruments. Ibrahim (Pargalı İbrahim Paşa) first held his master’s confidence for thirteen years. Later Rüstem (Rüstem Paşa) came to full power, supported by Roxelana and Mihrmah. In Suleiman’s last years he left nearly everything to Ali and to Sokollu Mehmed Paşa. The grand vizier thus came practically to wield the sovereign power of the state. This was a dangerous development: it made it easy for later, weaker sultans to withdraw into the harem, while the grand vizierate itself became prey to harem intrigue.
Ten special prerogatives marked the grand vizier’s dignity: he kept the imperial seal; held his own Divan at his palace; was escorted by the Chaush-bashi and all Chaushes; received weekly state visits from the Kaziaskers and Defterdars; went in solemn Friday procession to mosque; inspected the city and its markets; and was honored at Bairams with official felicitations. Customary ceremonies alone absorbed much of his time, to say nothing of vast administrative and judicial burdens.
Seven grand viziers served Suleiman in forty-six years. Four ended their service at death, two were deposed and executed, three were simply deposed. All but one (Piri Mehmed Paşa) were Christian renegades who had risen as slaves to the highest honor of the empire.
The Defterdars (Treasurers)
The great labor of accounting for the receipts and expenditures of the Ruling Institution was under the care of two principal Defterdars, one for Rumelia (دفتردار روم ايلى) and one for Anatolia (دفتردار آناطولی), aided by two of lower rank — one for Aleppo and the southwest, one for the Danubian countries. The principal defterdars were men of great position, with large incomes and households and the right of audience with the sultan on revenue matters.
Under them were twenty-five bureaus (قلم, kalem), instituted by Mehmed the Conqueror, each with a chief (خواجه‌گان, Khojagan) directing clerks of various grades. Between these and the defterdars were several intermediate officials, of whom the most important were the two Rusnamehjis (روزنامه‌جی, book-keepers). The total personnel of the treasury department numbered more than eight hundred.
The bureaus covered:

The Greater Book-keeping Bureau (central accounting)
The Head Bureau of Accounts (tithes, taxes, munitions, garrisons, intendants)
The Anatolian Accounts Bureau (domanial lands, Aegean garrisons, pensions)
The Cavalry Control Bureau (inner service salaries, Kapujis, stables, Spahis of the Porte)
5-6. Separate bureaus for Spahis and Silihdars
The Holy Cities Accounts Bureau (Mecca/Medina endowments, mosque salaries)
The Capitation Tax Bureau (جزیة, jizyeh — tax on adult male non-Muslims)
The Tributes Bureau (taxes in kind, grain stores, army supplies)
The Treasury Chancery Bureau (diplomas, firmans concerning treasury)
The Lesser Book-keeping Bureau (head Kapujis, stewards, marine)
The Infantry Control Bureau (Janissaries and auxiliary corps)
The Lesser Endowments Bureau (hospitals, soup-kitchens, asylums)
14-15. Greater and Lesser Fortress Bureaus (garrison records for Danube, Albania, Morea)
The Mine Leases Bureau (mines, gypsy tribute, Moldavian/Wallachian tribute, customs)
The Salaries Bureau (fleet captains, Crimean Khan)
The Domanial Leases Bureau (revenues for harem ladies and high officials)
The Head Leases Bureau (Danubian domains, rice fields, salt works, fisheries, forests)
The Holy Cities Leases Bureau (Anatolian side)
21-23. Regional leases bureaus for Constantinople, Brusa, Euboea
Kaffa Leases Bureau
The Dates Bureau (dating all public documents)

Supplementary bureaus covered confiscations, animal taxes, and Christian churches and monasteries.
The system was characterized by decentralization of management: each bureau received specific revenues and paid them out for specific purposes, subject only to inspection and supervision by the first bureau. This was typical of despotic administration — entrusting definite duties to definite groups with sufficient revenues, then leaving them to themselves, intervening only when things went wrong.
Taxation and Suleiman’s Income
A distinction was drawn between legal taxes (authorized by the Sacred Law) and arbitrary taxes (depending on the sultan’s will). The early Islamic system recognized only three taxes: the ‘ushr (عشر, tithe on land), the kharâj (خراج, land tax payable in money, with a share of produce), and the jizyeh (جزية, poll tax on adult male non-Muslim subjects). Additional impositions — customs, tolls, market dues — were tolerated by necessity but discouraged by legists.
The Ottoman system inherited all of these and added Seljuk and Byzantine imposts. Much land was retained as imperial domain; the feudal system (تيمار, timar) was applied to lands conquered from Christians; and the conquests in Syria, Mesopotamia, and Egypt were left under their old regulations with Turkish cadasters.
Until Mehmed II’s time revenues were administered directly; malversations led him to introduce tax-farming (التزام, iltizam). Large regions were sold by the treasury to high kullar, who resold them by sections, sometimes repeatedly. The amount wrung from taxpayers might easily be double what the government received.
The strongly conservative Ottoman tendency made revenues extremely inelastic. Treaties with Western nations were commercially favorable to the latter, limiting customs revenue. As the value of money declined, fixed payments declined too. Stationary taxation was supplemented by booty (the “Prophet’s fifth” went to support the Moslem Institution), tribute from Moldavia, Wallachia, Transylvania, Ragusa, Venice (for Cyprus), and after 1547 from Austria for Hungary, confiscations of executed officials’ property, and the estates of kullar who died without children.
Suleiman’s Revenue: Junis Bey, chief interpreter, estimated the regular income at about four million ducats around 1530-1537 (1,300,000 from Anatolia and Greece, 1,600,000 from Egypt, 700,000 from Syria, 150,000 from Mesopotamia, 250,000 from farms, islands, and customs). Later estimates, including mines, salt works, tributes, and other sources, suggest six million ducats early in the reign, rising to seven or eight million toward its close. The bullion value of six million ducats is less than fourteen million dollars; with purchasing power perhaps five times modern levels, the regular revenue was equivalent to less than seventy million modern dollars — a small sum for so great an empire. But this excludes revenues devoted to the Moslem Institution and those assigned by feudal grant to local government, which probably doubled the total.
Expenses grew particularly for the fleet and the increasingly splendid household. Suleiman felt the need of money and, through Rüstem, exacted payments from officials at their appointment — a fatal precedent that opened the door to systematized corruption.
The Nishanji (Chancellor)
The chancery department (ديوان همايون قلمی) had not reached the same stage of development as the treasury. The Nishanji-bashi (نشانجی باشی), often called simply the Nishanji, was clearly the chief. His duty was to authenticate firmans (فرمان, imperial decrees) sent to the provinces by tracing at the head of each document the sultan’s tuğra (طغرا), or official calligraphic signature.
A description from about 1537 says the Nishanji engrossed the ordinances and commands of the prince, revised writings in consultation with the pashas to ensure clarity (like a keeper of the seals), and received seven thousand ducats of revenue from fiefs, with many slaves and lesser recorders under him.
In the later Ottoman government the Reis Effendi (رئيس افندی, “Chief of the Men of the Pen”) — minister of foreign affairs, secretary of state, and chancellor — displaced the Nishanji at the head of the chancery as the sultan withdrew into the palace and foreign relations grew more complex. Under the Reis Effendi were three bureaus (the Beylik, Tahvil, and Ruus kalems) employing about one hundred and fifty clerks.
The personnel of the chancery and treasury likely changed in Suleiman’s time from Christian-born to Moslem-born scribes. The bureaucratic machinery produced vast quantities of paperwork — laws, ordinances, diplomas, nominations, deeds, grants, pay orders, receipts, reports, addresses, petitions — and the practice of demanding private fees for each act of writing or signing was already present.
The Divan (ديوان همايون)
The Divan (ديوان, imperial council) was the supreme administrative and judicial body of the empire. It held long sessions four times each week in time of peace (Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday). Its members represented all great components of the Ruling Institution, and through the two Kaziaskers (military judges) it also represented the Moslem Institution. The Divan was thus not strictly a part of the Ruling Institution alone, but the capstone of both institutions — the body that gave final unity, under the sultan, to the organization of the empire.
Former sultans had presided. Suleiman did not — a departure for which he has been blamed. He freed himself from a great burden but created a serious rift in both institutions. He retained control through a grated window in the wall (kafes, قفس); not knowing when he might be listening, councillors always had to speak as if he were present.
The Sessions: Soon after sunrise, officials gathered at the palace, passing the second gate in inverse order of rank. The grand vizier sat in the middle of a long sofa. On his right sat the other viziers, and beyond them the Nishanji. On his left sat the two Kaziaskers, and beyond them the Defterdars. The Beylerbeys of Anatolia and Rumelia sat beyond the viziers. The Agha of the Janissaries had a place; the chief interpreter was often needed; the Reis Effendi sat on the floor at the grand vizier’s left.
Great questions — proposals of ambassadors, the condition of provinces, the desirability of war — were discussed briefly. Lesser matters were decided individually or referred to officials in attendance. Lawsuits were presented by masters of petitions (Teskerejis, تذكره‌جی); parties might appear to plead their own cases. All business was done with despatch. Decisions were briefly formulated and recorded. After the sultan signified approval at the close of a Divan, decisions were irrevocable.
A simple meal was served to all in attendance. Order was absolute; any disturber was immediately bastinadoed.
After the session, officials who had the right of audience went to meet the sultan. The grand vizier alone reported on the day’s decisions; the sultan usually approved as made, sometimes mitigating a decision or dictating an ambassador’s reply.
The Divan was not a legislative chamber but a combination of a cabinet and a supreme court. It was the highest court in the land, not so much a court of appeal as a court of first instance, with cognizance of all civil and criminal cases from any part of the empire. It was a training school for judges, administrators, and statesmen, and its members influenced legislation by providing the information and drafting on which new Kanuns were based.
The Sultan as Legislator: Kanun and Şeriat
The law that demanded obedience in the Ottoman Empire was fourfold: the Sheri (شريعت, Sacred Law of Islam); the Kanuns (قوانين, written decrees of the sultans); the Adet (عادت, established custom); and the Urf (عرف, the sovereign will of the reigning sultan).
The Sheri was above the sultan and unchangeable by him. It was a written constitution for the Ottoman Empire, not subject to amendment but capable of slight modification by judicial and juristic interpretation. The Sheri was based on the Koran (قرآن), supplemented by traditions of Mohammed’s legal decisions (حديث, Hadith), the decisions of the early caliphs, and the interpretations of early judges. The Hanafi school (ابو حنيفه), the accepted teacher of all Turkish peoples, was the foundation.
Suleiman charged Sheik Ibrahim Halebi of Aleppo with preparing a new code, the Multeka ol-ebhar (ملتقى الأبحر, “Confluence of the Seas”), finished before 1549. It remained the foundation of Ottoman law until the nineteenth-century reforms.
The Kanuns were issued in accordance with the principle that “the Imam has the right to make all civil and political regulations demanded by prudence, circumstances, and the public welfare.” They dealt with military, financial, feudal, criminal, and police law, and the law of ceremonies. In the fields of feudal law and ceremonies — non-existent in the early Islamic state — the sultans had a free hand. In all other fields Kanuns were strictly supplementary and administrative, bound not to transgress the positive commands of the Sacred Law.
Kanuns were issued separately to meet special circumstances. A collection of them constituted a Kanun-nameh (قانون نامه, book of laws). Suleiman’s Kanun-nameh proper — chiefly compiled by the great Mufti Ebu Su’ud (ابو السعود افندی) — contained ordinances on land tenure, taxes on subject Christians, and regulations of the feudal system. The only complete body he published was the Kanun-nameh Misr (قانون نامه مصر, constitution for Egypt), issued in 1525.
Suleiman was rightly called Kanuni (قانونى, the Legislator) by comparison with his predecessors, who were men of the sword. He issued a large number of Kanuns dealing especially with timars (fiefs), rayahs (subjects), ceremonies, and criminal and market regulations. His legislation was hindered by the conservatism of Islam, which held that old ways were best, and by the weakness inherent in despotic legislation — the lawgiver’s distance from those affected made true adaptation impossible.
A devout Moslem whose piety increased in old age, Suleiman took his duty of enforcing the Sheri seriously. If he did not enact measures directly to increase the common welfare, his attempts to regulate the tax and tenancy systems lightened the people’s condition. He used severe measures against extortion and strove by market and police regulations to maintain justice, fairness, and order.
Provincial Administration: Beylerbeys and Sancakbeys
Local government and the command of the feudal Spahis were cared for by officials belonging to the sultan’s slave-family, who brought to their posts a number of Spahis of the Porte, pages, Ajem-oghlans, and their own slaves, proportioned to their rank.
The hierarchy of local officials:

Subashis (سوباشى, captains): In time of peace, governors of towns, with enough Janissaries and Azabs to police the locality.
Alai Beys (آلاى بك, colonels): Ready with a company of two to five hundred troops to move as needed.
Sancak Beys (سنجاق بك, district governors): Governed important cities and held superior rule over a number of towns and their surrounding districts.
Beylerbeys (بكلربكى, “lord of lords”): Had authority over all the beys of their region. Two were ancient and principal: the Beylerbey of Rumelia (European provinces), who resided in Constantinople in peacetime, and the Beylerbey of Anatolia (western Asia Minor), who spent much time in his dominions.

In Suleiman’s time there were ten beylerbeys in total: the two ancient ones plus those of Karamania, Amasia, Avandole, Syria, Mesopotamia, Egypt, Hungary, and Temesvar. The latter eight, who had fewer feudal troops and greater need of them at home, were summoned to campaigns only when the war was in their region.
Incomes were provided by the assignment of fiefs (تيمار, timar) proportioned in size to each officer’s importance. Each beylerbey had his own mufti, reis effendi, and defterdar with clerks, who advised him, recorded his decisions, administred revenues from estates assigned to his household, and kept accounts of fief-holders in his dominion. Each sancak bey had a similar group on a lesser scale.
In time of war, this local government scheme drew together the feudal Spahis into a perfectly organized territorial army for each region. The army of Rumelia under its beylerbey had the right of the battle-line when fighting in Europe; the army of Anatolia had the right when fighting in Asia. The enrolled feudal troops of Europe numbered about fifty thousand and those of Asia about thirty thousand — figures that should be doubled or tripled to account for additional horsemen brought by each Spahi. About half remained on duty at home to protect the provinces.
This system — which prevented the accumulation of power in local hands — meant that no beylerbey or sancak bey could hope to rebel successfully. All were “strangers and foreigners in the countries they ruled,” holding their positions by the most insecure tenure. The empire was not destined to break up into small independent states so long as its two great institutions were consistently maintained.
The Ruling Institution as a Whole
The Ottoman Ruling Institution was, in its most essential aspect, a government. Its form was a despotism centered in the sultan, but greatly circumscribed by the Sheri — a rigid constitutional law grounded in strong religious belief and intense national conservatism. It maintained public order, defended the empire, and enlarged its possessions by conquest. A large proportion of its energies was devoted to obtaining and distributing the means of its own support, keeping its own machinery in order, and maintaining its authority. The idea of labor for the public welfare was not present.
It kept itself in power by being organized as an army: a standing army of cavalry and infantry with artillery, commissary, and transport services, controlling a much larger feudal and irregular army. Through the feudal army it kept the countryside in subjection; by garrisons it held the towns quiet.
It was also a great court and household, maintaining the pomp and ceremony due to an imperial state. Suleiman was called the Legislator and the Conqueror, but beyond both titles, the Magnificent — he shone as head of the court.
All its members were set off as a nobility by exemption from taxation and special jurisdiction, but nobility was not hereditary — a feature that prevented the entrenchment of families and kept every ambitious member a devoted slave, an indefatigable learner, and a dauntless warrior. “Every man was his own ancestry.”
The most characteristic feature was that its recruits were almost all drawn from children of Christian parents, and that they were expected to become Muslims before advancement. This was both a missionary enterprise and a practical method of obtaining single-hearted servants.
The next most characteristic feature was its educational quality. The Christian slaves were acquired young and trained with the greatest care — body, mind, and spirit. The immediate object was to fit boys for the sultan’s service in war and government, but they were also trained to adorn his ceremonies and to live by the principles of Islam. The results were well-nigh incredible: the men who guided Suleiman’s empire to unexampled glory were sons of peasants and herdsmen, of downtrodden Christian subjects, of unlettered and half-civilized parents. They became educated, cultured, and polished men.
“You are his slave and he is your lord… The Ottoman government… seems to have attained during the sixteenth century the highest degree of perfection of which its constitution was capable.”
— Robertson

concepts/culture-and-learning.title: Ottoman Culture and Learning
concepts/culture-and-learning.content:
Ottoman Culture and Learning
The Medrese (مدرسه) System
The first Ottoman medrese was created in Iznik in 1331, when a converted church building was assigned as a medrese to the famous scholar Davud of Kayseri. In later years, when an Ottoman sultan wished to establish a new medrese, he would invite scholars from the old Anatolian cultural centres — Konya, Kayseri, Aksaray — or from Persia, Turkestan, Egypt or Syria. During the formative period of the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries, Ottoman ulema travelled to Egypt, Persia or Turkestan to complete their education under the great scholars of those lands. For the study of Koranic exegesis and jurisprudence they went primarily to Egypt and Persia; for mathematics they went usually to Samarkand.
A student, according to Taskopruzade, must study all the sciences, since they complement each other in forming a single whole. The essential element in an Islamic education was the muderris (مدرس — professor), a man of recognized authority in the religious and spiritual sciences. It was the muderris himself who was important and not the institution.

The Hierarchy of Medreses
Ottoman medreses fell into two major categories:
The Haric (خارج — Exterior) Medreses
These gave preparatory instruction in the “Fundamentals of Knowledge” — Arabic and the intellectual sciences:
(a) Ibtida-yi Haric (Lower Medreses): Taught the rudiments of Arabic grammar and syntax, logic, scholastic theology, astronomy, geometry and rhetoric. Known as “Tajrid medreses” after Sayyid Sharif’s commentary on Nasir al-Din of Tus’s al-Tajrid, also as “medreses of twenty” since the muderris received twenty akces daily.
(b) “Medreses of Thirty” or “Miftah medreses”: Named after their chief textbook, al-Sakkaki’s Miftah al-ulum on rhetoric. Gave instruction in rhetoric and the literary sciences. Most medreses of twenty and thirty were in the provinces.
(c) “Medreses of Forty” and “Medreses of Fifty”: Founded by princes, royal ladies or viziers in Istanbul, Edirne and Bursa. Gave elementary instruction in the interpretation of the Miftah, intermediate scholastic theology based on Adud al-Din’s al-Mawaqif, and higher jurisprudence based on al-Marghinani’s al-Hidaya.
The Dahil (داخل — Interior) Medreses
These gave instruction in “Higher Knowledge” — the religious sciences:
(a) Ibtida-yi Dahil (“Medreses of fifty”): Founded by sultans’ daughters, princes or viziers. Taught jurisprudence (al-Hidaya), the principles of jurisprudence (al-Taftazani’s al-Talwih), and Koranic exegesis (al-Zamakhshari’s al-Kashshaf).
(b) Tetimme or Musile-yi Sahn: Mehmed the Conqueror’s eight preparatory medreses.
(c) Sahn-i Seman or Semaniye: The highest level — Mehmed the Conqueror’s eight medreses where students studied jurisprudence, Koranic exegesis or scholastic theology, rhetoric, and related subjects, receiving specialized training.
The Sahn-i Seman (صهن ثمن — Eight Courts)
Between 1463 and 1470, Mehmed the Conqueror built the Conqueror’s Mosque in Istanbul and around it established the eight famous medreses known as the Sahn-i Seman or the Semaniye. These stone medreses, fine examples of Ottoman architecture, were built on either side of the mosque. There were eight higher medreses for specialized studies and eight lower medreses which prepared students for these. The daily pay of the muderris in charge of each was fifty akces, equal to about one gold ducat. Each medrese had nineteen rooms and one classroom. Fifteen rooms were allocated to specialist students known as danismends, who each received two akces daily from the endowment income and food from the hospice.
At this period, the Semaniye medreses were the highest ranking educational institutes in the empire. Below them came the Darulhadis medrese which Murad II had founded in Edirne, and below this the medreses which earlier sultans had established in Bursa.
The Suleymaniye Medreses
Suleyman I made an important change in the hierarchy. Around the Suleymaniye mosque in Istanbul (built 1550-56), he established four general medreses and two more for specialized studies — one devoted to the science of hadith (traditions of the Prophet) and the other to medicine. To these he gave the highest rank, thus establishing the hierarchy of medreses which was to continue until the end of the empire. All the hundreds of medreses throughout the empire were categorized according to these eleven grades.
By the seventeenth century there were as many as ninety-five medreses in Istanbul alone; by the nineteenth century their number had risen to 170. The medrese was always supported by a vakif (وقف — pious foundation) and was usually one component in a complex of mosque, hospice and other charitable institutions.

The Ulema (علماء) — The Learned Class
The ulema were recruited from the medreses. They had the dual role of interpreters and executors of Islamic law — the muftis performing the first of these duties and the kadis the second. There was a rigid hierarchy of ulema — muderrises, muftis and kadis — and a strict system of promotions.
The muderrises of haric medreses or graduates of the Semaniye could become kadis of small towns. The senior kadiships, at three hundred akces or more, reached the most elevated posts and were known as mollas. A muderris at the Semaniye could become a molla earning five hundred akces, with the possibility of promotion to kadi of Istanbul and later kadiasker. The highest ranks in the bureaucracy, not only the religious and legal professions, were open to them; many became viziers and attained positions of political power.
The seyhulislam (شيخ الإسلام) was the head of the ulema, appointed by royal warrant. The Kanunname of Mehmed the Conqueror placed his rank on a level with that of the grand vizier, and protocol required that he should receive the greater respect. His duty was to issue fetvas — written answers based on standard religious authorities to any problem falling within the scope of the seriat.

Intellectual Figures
Taskopruzade Ahmed (1495-1561)
Taskopruzade Ahmed (تاشكوپرى‌زاده) was the Ottoman encyclopaedist and medrese teacher best known for his Sakaik al-numaniya, a biography in Arabic of 522 Ottoman sheikhs and ulema, and Mawduat al-ulum, an encyclopaedia of the sciences. His concept of knowledge and division of the sciences provides a starting point for any study of Ottoman learning. He recognized four stages of knowledge — spiritual, intellectual, oral and written — and divided the spiritual sciences into theoretical rational sciences (theology, natural sciences, mathematics), practical rational sciences (ethics, political science), theoretical religious sciences (Koran, traditions, exegesis, theology, jurisprudence) and practical religious sciences.
In his religious views Taskopruzade inclined to mysticism. Following al-Ghazali, he maintained that contemplation is a necessary complement to the spiritual sciences, and that the scholar who studies only the exoteric religious sciences is “a poor man, excluded from the greater realities.”
Kinalizade Ali (c. 1510-71)
Kinalizade was a prominent Ottoman scholar and moral philosopher, best known for his work Ahlak-i Alai (علاقى اخلاق — The Alai Ethics), a treatise on practical philosophy and ethics based on the Islamic and Greek philosophical traditions. He served as a kadi in several major cities and was an important figure in the intellectual life of the sixteenth century.
Kâtip Çelebi (1609-57) — Hacı Halife
Kâtip Çelebi was the greatest Ottoman encyclopaedic scholar and a secretary in the imperial council. His bibliographic work Kashf al-Zunun (كشف الظنون — The Removal of Doubt) remains a standard reference book for Islamic manuscripts to this day. He wrote on history, geography, philosophy and the principles of the calendar, and represents the bureaucratic tradition of secular learning as distinct from the medrese-centred scholarship of the ulema.
The most important Ottoman writings in practical and useful fields — literature, language, calligraphy, law, history, philosophy, geography, surveying and agriculture — were the work of professional secretaries like Kâtip Çelebi, who together with the slaves educated in the royal Palace played a vital role in the creation of Ottoman culture.
Other Notable Scholars
Mehmed al-Fanari (1350-1431): Traditionally regarded as the first seyhulislam of the empire. He founded the Ottoman medrese tradition and specialized in the intellectual sciences; his work on logic was an essential part of medrese courses until the last days of the empire. As a follower of the Konya school of Ibn al-Arabi, he was censured by the Arab ulema in Egypt.
Molla Husrev (d. 1480): Ottoman scholar and jurist who became muderris, kadiasker and seyhulislam under Murad II and Mehmed II. His works on jurisprudence became standard textbooks in Ottoman medreses.
Ibn Kemal (Kemal Pasazade) (1468-1534): A typical broad-ranging Muslim scholar who composed over a hundred religious treatises and left a monumental ten-volume history of the Ottoman Empire. Many of his works were concerned with shiism, at that time a question of vital importance; he used religious arguments to prove the legality of holy war against the Shia.
Ebussuud Efendi (1490-1574): Seyhulislam under Suleiman the Magnificent, famous for his fetvas harmonizing the seriat with the kanun and for condemning the Melami heretic Hamza Bali.
Ali Kuscu (d. 1474): Mathematician and astronomer who worked at the Samarkand observatory under Ulugh Beg. Mehmed II invited him to Istanbul, inaugurating a brilliant era in Ottoman mathematics. He wrote classic works on arithmetic and astronomy.
Molla Lutfi (d. 1494): Muderris under Bayezid II, distinguished as a mathematician and theologian. His free thought and open mockery of superstition angered conservative ulema, who accused him of heresy; he was beheaded on the Atmeydani in Istanbul in 1494. Some ulema regarded the incident as slander, and public opinion came to regard him as a martyr to truth.

Ottoman Historiography
Ottoman historiography developed in two parallel streams. Side by side with folk histories written in simple Turkish, Palace poets and writers in the service of Mehmed the Conqueror composed works in high literary Persian. It did not take long for Palace histories to appear, written in Turkish but imitating the ornate Persian style, and in time a peculiarly Ottoman school of historiography came into being.
Early Chroniclers
Asikpasazade (1400-84): One of the earliest Ottoman historians, author of the Tevarih-i Al-i Osman (تاريخ آل عثمان — The History of the House of Osman). His work is written in simple Turkish and reflects the gazi and dervish traditions of the early frontier period, preserving many popular legends and epic motifs.
Nesri (d. c. 1520): Another early Ottoman historian whose Cihannuma (جهان‌نما — The World-Mirror) is a comprehensive history of the Ottoman state up to the reign of Bayezid II. Nesri is quoted by Inalcik describing how Mehmed the Conqueror “created Istanbul.”
Later Chroniclers
Selaniki Mustafa Efendi (d. c. 1600): A prominent late-sixteenth-century historian whose Tarih-i Selaniki covers the reigns of Selim II and Murad III. His chronicle is a crucial source for the administrative, social and economic history of the Ottoman Empire in the period of transformation.
Na’ima (Naima) (1655-1716): The first official Ottoman court historiographer (vak’anuvis). His Tarih-i Na’ima covers the years 1591-1659 and is one of the most important works of Ottoman historiography. He was influenced by Ibn Khaldun’s philosophy of history, which he introduced to Ottoman intellectual circles.
Ibn Kemal: Also wrote a monumental ten-volume history of the Ottoman Empire.

Ottoman Poetry — Divan Edebiyatı (ديوان ادبياتى)
Classical Ottoman literature developed as a Palace art. Most poets and writers had some connection with the Palace; Bayezid II’s account books show that about twenty poets regularly benefited from the sultan’s largesse. Classical Ottoman literature drew its main inspiration from Persian, and Persian language and literature were studied in Mevlevi tekkes since the ulema forbade their study in the medreses.
Major Poets
Baki (1526-1600): Known as “Sultan of the Poets” (سلطان الشعارا — Sultan al-Su’ara), Baki was the greatest lyric poet of the classical age. A native of Istanbul, he was a close friend of Sokollu Mehmed Pasha and enjoyed the patronage of Suleiman the Magnificent and Selim II. His Divan is the supreme achievement of Ottoman lyric poetry.
Fuzuli (c. 1494-1556): The most famous mystical poet of the Ottoman tradition, writing in Azerbaijani Turkish, Persian and Arabic. A native of Karbala (in Ottoman Iraq), his works include the Divan, the Leyla ve Mecnun (ليلى و مجنون) mesnevi, and the Hadikat al-Suada (حديقت السعدا — The Garden of the Blessed). His poetry is deeply infused with the sufi mystical tradition.
Nedim (1681-1730): The greatest poet of the Tulip Age (1718-30), Nedim’s poetry reflects the pleasure-seeking, worldly atmosphere of the court of Ahmed III. His style broke from the high Persianate tradition toward a simpler, more Turkish idiom.
The Mevlevîs were in the first rank of Ottoman musicians and poets from the sixteenth century onward, creating their own music and literature entirely derived from Mevlevî tradition.

Libraries and the Culture of the Book
The library had an important place in Ottoman society. The Ottomans founded libraries in mosques, hospitals and tekkes and collected private libraries in their own residences. Many private book collections found their way to vakif libraries, since it was considered meritorious to leave books to a pious foundation. The library was a single unit in a vakif complex, established in a stone room or separate building. The vakfiye stipulated how the books were to be preserved and used and appointed a librarian paid from vakif funds.
These libraries still preserve more than two hundred thousand manuscripts from all lands and epochs of the Islamic world, forming the richest source for the history and culture of Islam.
Ottoman writers used Arabic in their religious and legal works, but by the fourteenth century they had already begun to make translations into Turkish — at first useful or instructive works on history, politics, morals, etiquette, astrology, natural history and gemmology, made for the sultans or influential men of state. Two popular books, dating from 1449, are still among the most widely read Turkish works: the poem Muhammediye (The Book of Muhammed) and the prose work Envar al-Asikin (The Lights of the Lovers) of the Yazicizade brothers, who belonged to the Bayrami sect.

The Patronage System
Ottoman culture remained essentially a Palace culture. From the fifteenth century, Palace architects erected buildings in a distinctive Ottoman style throughout the empire, from Konya to Sarajevo. There grew up in the Palace an Ottoman artistic style and aristocratic way of life which the sultan’s slaves diffused to all corners of the empire. Classical Ottoman literature and architecture developed as Palace arts.
The patronage of the sultans was crucial. Mehmed II lamented that although he had established a great empire, his realms contained no ulema comparable with those of other lands; among native ulema he could take pride only in Molla Husrev and Hocazade. He actively sought to remedy this by converting eight churches into medreses after the conquest and by establishing the Sahn-i Seman. Suleyman I similarly raised the highest-ranking medreses around his mosque complex.
The reforming grand viziers Koca Ragib, Halil Hamid and Mustafa Resid were all professional bureaucrats from the scribal class. The bureaucrats represented the political and secular interests of state as against the ulema who represented the seriat, and were generally responsible for introducing traditional Near Eastern principles of administration to the empire.
The medrese was a self-governing unit within a vakif, itself an autonomous institution. The mutevelli (trustee) of the vakif complex entrusted to the muderris the funds allotted to the medrese; the muderris selected students, disbursed funds to students and servants, and administered the medrese.

The Intellectual Sciences
Following al-Ghazali, the Ottoman medreses maintained that the intellectual sciences (logic, mathematics, astronomy) contained essential elements for all the sciences — they accustomed the mind to correct thinking and thus helped to reveal divine truths. The result was the early adoption of the intellectual sciences into the curricula of Ottoman medreses.
In the fifteenth century, under the patronage of Mehmed II who attached great importance to these sciences, the Ottomans achieved real distinction in mathematics and astronomy within the Islamic world. The mathematical genius was Musa Pasha, called Kadizade, whose commentaries on Euclid formed part of medrese courses until the latest period. His student Ali Kuscu inaugurated a brilliant era in Ottoman mathematics.
Mehmed II also reopened the famous controversy between al-Ghazali and Ibn Rushd (Averroes) over the relationship between religion and philosophy, inviting the two great theologians Ala al-Din of Tus and Hocazade of Bursa to write treatises on the subject. Hocazade’s work, defending the seriat against the claims of philosophy, was judged the superior and maintained its reputation in the Islamic world until the present day. While Averroism was studied in Italy and became a major factor in Renaissance thought, a thoroughgoing scholasticism established itself in Ottoman medreses.

Key Ottoman Turkish Terms









































































































Turkish (Modern)Ottoman ScriptMeaningMedreseمدرسهIslamic school / collegeMüderrisمدرسProfessor / teacher at a medreseDanışmendدانشمندAdvanced student / specialist studentUlemaعلماءThe learned class / religious scholarsŞeyhülislamشيخ الإسلامHead of the ulemaMüftîمفتىJurisconsult / issuer of fetvasKadıقاضىIslamic judgeKazaskerقاضىعسكرMilitary judgeFetvaفتوىLegal opinion based on seriatMollâملاHigh-ranking kadiSahn-ı Semanصهن ثمنThe eight highest medreses of Mehmed IISüleymaniyeسليمانيهThe medrese complex of Suleiman the MagnificentVakıfوقفPious foundation / endowmentKanunnameقانوننامهSultanic law codeDivan edebiyatıديوان ادبياتىClassical Ottoman court poetryMesnevîمثنوىNarrative poem in rhyming coupletsHâriçخارج”Exterior” / preparatory medrese categoryDâhilداخل”Interior” / higher medrese categoryTetimmeتتمهPreparatory medrese attached to the Sahn

concepts/devsirme-and-palace-school.title: The Devsirme and the Palace School (Enderun)
concepts/devsirme-and-palace-school.content:
The Devsirme and the Palace School (Enderun)
The Ruling Institution as Slave-Family
Perhaps no more daring experiment has been tried on a large scale upon the face of the earth than that embodied in the Ottoman Ruling Institution. Its nearest ideal analogue is found in the Republic of Plato, its nearest actual parallel in the Mameluke system of Egypt. The Ottoman system deliberately took slaves and made them ministers of state; it took boys from the sheep-run and the plow-tail and made them courtiers and the husbands of princesses; it took young men whose ancestors had borne the Christian name for centuries, and made them rulers in the greatest of Mohammedan states, and soldiers and generals in invincible armies whose chief joy was to beat down the Cross and elevate the Crescent. It never asked its novices, “Who was your father?” or “What do you know?” or even “Can you speak our tongue?”; but it studied their faces and their frames and said, “You shall be a soldier, and if you show yourself worthy, a general,” or, “You shall be a scholar and a gentleman, and if the ability lies in you, a governor and a prime minister.”
The members of this system — numbering perhaps eighty thousand in Suleiman’s time — were, as long as they lived, at once slaves, proselytes, students, soldiers, nobles, courtiers, and officers of government. Every one who belonged to the Ruling Institution in any capacity from gardener to grand vizier, save only the members of the royal family, bore the title of kul (قول, slave) of the sultan. The power of the sultan over the lives, persons, and property of the kullar, and his right to their absolute obedience, derived directly from the idea of slavery. The very word despot means by derivation the master of slaves, and it was only over his kullar that the sultan’s power was despotic in the fullest sense.
Recruitment: The Devsirme
Entrance to the system came by the door of slavery, which was open regularly only to Christian boys from ten to twenty years of age. The preferred ages were between fourteen and eighteen. Four methods were employed for obtaining recruits: capture, purchase, gift, and tribute. Of these, the devşirme (دوشیرمه, “collecting” or “gathering”) — the tribute of children — is the most famous, though it furnished perhaps less than half of the annual recruits. The annual levy of tribute boys averaged about three thousand in the sixteenth century.
The Levying Process
The devşirme was accomplished by a regular process. Normally every four years, but oftener in case of need, a body of officials — described by contemporaries as more skilled in judging boys than trained horse-dealers in judging colts — was sent out by the government to the Christian provinces. The whole of the Balkan Peninsula, Hungary, the western coast of Asia Minor, and the southern and eastern shores of the Black Sea were included in the territory visited; the strongest and ablest youths came from the mountain regions inhabited by Albanians and the Southern Slavic peoples.
The recruiting officers were commissioned each to bring in a certain number apportioned to them out of a total determined at the capital. There was no principle of tithing, and no fixed proportion or number of boys was levied from each village or family. A regular procedure was followed: the officers obtained from the Christian priest of the village a list of the boys he had baptized between the ages of twelve and twenty years; all these were brought before the officers, who selected the best. Parents who had strong and well-favored sons might lose them all, while those who had weaklings would lose none. On leaving each village, the officer took with him the boys he had selected and delivered them to the capital.
Reaction of the Subject Population
The grief at parting was often a heart-breaking thing to witness; the mother whose son was taken by force to an unknown life among enemies of all that she had been taught to hold dear would hardly have suffered more at the death of her son. Yet she might hope to see him one day in possession of great wealth and power. Parents who wished to keep their sons would sometimes marry them in tender years, since married boys were ineligible; those who had means bought exemption from the recruiting officers. On the contrary, many parents were glad to have their sons chosen, knowing that they would escape from grinding poverty, receive a first-rate training suited to their abilities, and enter upon the possibility of a great career. Some parents came to regard the process as a privilege rather than a burden; Turkish parents envied them the opportunity, and sometimes tried to evade the regulations by paying Christians to take their Moslem sons and declare them as Christian children, so that they might be enrolled as the sultan’s slaves.
Selection and Classification
All recruits were brought before trained officials, carefully registered, and divided into two classes. Those who best satisfied the criteria of bodily perfection, muscular strength, and intellectual ability — about one in every ten of the whole number — were chosen for a superior quality of training, especially on the intellectual side. These regularly became pages (iç oğlan, ايج اوغلان, “inside youth”) and kapıkulu (قپوقولی) Spahis of the Porte, and the ablest rose to the great offices of army and government. The remainder were destined for a different education, mainly physical: they became acemi oğlan (عجمی اوغلان, “foreign youth” or apprentice Janissaries) and later Janissaries. But the ablest of these might also rise to positions as Spahis of the Porte and even as generals and officers of state. Merit was recognized everywhere and regularly led to promotion.
The Missionary Motive
Although almost every member of the governing group in the Ottoman Empire had been born a Christian, it was absolutely necessary for his advancement that he should profess the Moslem faith. The fundamental rule was very simple: every member of the Ruling Institution must have been born a Christian and must have become a Mohammedan.
The missionary purpose stood out clearly in the words attributed to Kara Khalil Chendereli, the traditional founder of the Janissary corps: “The conquered are slaves of the conquerors, to whom their goods, their women, and their children belong as a lawful possession; in converting the children to Islam by force, and in enrolling them as soldiers in the service of the faith, one is working for their happiness in this world and their eternal salvation.”
Conversion was not ordinarily forcible. The Ottomans were too wise to believe that the best results could be accomplished by such means. Their policy was rather to throw every difficulty in the way of remaining a Christian, and to offer every inducement to make the Moslem faith and system seem attractive. The educational scheme involved complete isolation from Christian ideas of every sort and complete saturation in all the ideas of Mohammedanism — religious, moral, social, and political. Even those whose education was mainly physical were isolated from Christians in a strict Moslem environment. “The Turks rejoice greatly when they find an exceptional man,” wrote one observer, “as though they had acquired a precious object, and they spare no labor or effort in cultivating him.”
Yet not all who turned Moslem did so without mental reservation. It was possible to hold fast to an inward belief in the superiority of Christianity through many years spent in the sultan’s service. The grand vizier Rustem told Busbecq, after offering him great rewards if he would turn Moslem, that he believed in the salvation of those of other faiths. A deli (scout) in his service confided to a French gentleman that while he pretended to follow Mohammedanism, he was a Christian at heart. The probability is that large numbers of the sultan’s slaves were merely nominal Mohammedans in religious belief, though they necessarily followed the larger part of the Moslem scheme of life.
The Palace School: Enderun
The Enderun (اندرون, “inside” or “interior”) — the palace school — was the most remarkable educational institution of its age. Those selected for the higher training, to the number of perhaps two hundred annually, or twelve to fifteen hundred in all, were taken into three palaces of the sultan as iç oğlanlar (pages). Three or four hundred were in the palace at Adrianople, a like number in one at Galata, and from five to eight hundred in the principal palace at Stamboul (the Saray-ı Cedid, or Topkapı Palace). These were all handsome boys, physically perfect, and of marked intellectual promise. A Venetian writer recorded that the pages included Bulgarians, Hungarians, Transylvanians, Poles, Bohemians, Germans, Italians, Spaniards, a few French, many Albanians, Slavs, Greeks, Circassians, and Russians.
The Colleges and Their Curriculum
The pages were organized into chambers (odalar, اوده لر). The two principal schools at the main palace were the Yeni Oda (New Chamber) and the Eski Oda (Old Chamber). Above these stood the chambers of personal service — the Seferli Odası (Campaign Chamber), the Kiler Odası (Pantry Chamber), the Hazine Odası (Treasury Chamber), and at the summit the Has Oda (خاص اوده, Private Chamber), containing thirty-nine gentlemen of the bedchamber who were the elite of all, chosen by selection after selection.
Menavino, who passed through the school, described the course of study in the Yeni Oda, which contained from eighty to a hundred boys: “When a boy has remained five or six days in that school, they set him to learning the alphabet. There are four teachers in the school. One drills the boys in reading during their first year. Another teaches the Koran in the Arabic language, giving explanations of the different articles of their faith. After this a third teaches books in the Persian tongue, and some write a little, but they do not teach writing willingly. A fourth teaches Arabic books, both vulgar and literary.”
The chief object of the course was to teach reading and writing for the purpose of giving inspection into the books of law and religion, especially the Koran. Arabic was taught to enable the boys to inspect the writings of the judges and to have knowledge of religion; Persian to give them quaint words and handsome and gentle deportment. They were not taught logic, physics, metaphysics, mathematics, or geography, but they received thorough instruction in Turkish history and the various Kanun-nameh (قانون نامه) — the imperial law codes — of the sultans. Most of the teachers were Anatolian Turks, chosen for imparting better pronunciation and more orthodox religious views.
Physical and Military Training
The pages were trained in the art of war, the use of all sorts of arms, and good horsemanship. Suleiman took especial delight in watching their cavalry evolutions, and occasionally summoned a page who pleased him, conversed with him, and dismissed him with presents. By old Oriental custom, every page was taught some handicraft useful in his master’s service, intended to provide for his own support in case of need.
Discipline and Daily Life
Discipline was severe but kept within bounds. A page could be beaten on the soles of his feet with no more than ten strokes, and not more than once on any one day. The boys were organized in groups of ten, watched carefully by eunuchs both day and night. Absolute obedience, modest behavior and decorum, and good manners were taught with great insistency. Rewards in the form of pay were given from the start. Menavino recorded: “These boys have a daily allowance of two aspers during the first year, three during the second year, four during the third year, and thus their allowance increases each year. They receive scarlet garments twice a year, and some robes of white cloth for the summer.”
Graduation and Placement
On reaching the age of twenty-five, every page was sent out from the school. Those from the Has Oda passed at once to places in the Muteferrika (متفره, Noble Guard) or to governorships of towns. The majority passed into the regular cavalry, the kapıkulu sipahileri (Spahis of the Porte). Those who left the school were honored by a ceremony of farewell: the sultan personally commended each one, gave him encouragement for good conduct, presented each with an embroidered coat and one of his most beautiful horses, and often a gift in money. The young man, with all the presents he had received during his stay, was escorted to the great gate, where he mounted his horse triumphantly and departed from the palace forever.
The Harem as Educational Space
The imperial harem partook of the characteristics of the schools of pages.
