Turkish (Modern)Ottoman ScriptMeaningEsnafاسنافGuilds / Tradesmen / ArtisansAhiاخىAhi brotherhood member; guild leader in early AnatoliaLoncaلونجهGuild councilNarhنرخOfficial fixed price; price controlIhtisabاحتسابMarket regulation / hisba law enforcementMuhtesibمحتسبMarket inspector enforcing ihtisabKethudaكتهداGuild warden / steward; external representativeSeyhشيخSpiritual leader of the guildYigitbasiيڭيت‌باشىGuild foreman; internal affairs officerEhl-i hibreاهل خبرهExpert craftsmen; quality inspectorsKalfaقلفاSalaried foreman; journeymanUstaاوستهMaster craftsmanYamakياماقDependent guild; subordinate craft groupGedikكدكMonopoly right to operate a shopFutuwwaفتوتIslamic chivalric code; guild ethicsMudarabaمضاربهCommercial partnership / investmentTuccarتاجرLarge-scale merchant (not under ihtisab)BedestanبزستانCovered market / cloth marketKadıقاضىIslamic judge; oversaw guild registration and disputesKanunnameقانوننامهSultanic law code; tax regulations

concepts/internal-politics-and-foreign-p.title: Internal Politics and Foreign Policy During the Reign of Bayezid II (1481–1512)
concepts/internal-politics-and-foreign-p.content:
Internal Politics and Foreign Policy
Overview
The reign of Bayezid II (1481–1512) is a case study in the inseparability of domestic politics and foreign policy in the early modern Ottoman Empire. Unlike his father Mehmed II, who ruled with an iron hand, Bayezid was a sultan who ascended the throne through the patronage of court factions and who remained, for the entirety of his reign, constrained by the forces that had elevated him. The central fact of his foreign policy — the fourteen-year captivity of his brother Cem (Jem) in Christian hands — was simultaneously a domestic threat, a diplomatic weapon, and a financial hemorrhage. Every major foreign policy decision Bayezid made between 1481 and 1495 was shaped by the fear that Cem might return, and after Cem’s death in 1495, by the consequences of having deferred expansion for so long.
The Janissaries as Kingmakers
Bayezid’s Accession
The Janissaries were the decisive force in Bayezid’s accession, and they never let him forget it. When Mehmed II died unexpectedly in May 1481, the grand vizier Karamani Mehmed Pasha and the chief judge Manisali Chelebi — both partisans of Cem — attempted to conceal the sultan’s death and summon Cem to Istanbul. Their plot failed because the janissaries, learning the truth, seized rude transports, crossed to Istanbul, beheaded the grand vizier, and paraded through the streets shouting “Long live Bayezid!”
The janissaries did not act out of pure loyalty. They were driven by a network of marriage alliances: the agha of the janissaries, the beylerbeys of Rumelia and Anatolia, and several European pashas were all related to Bayezid by marriage. These were graduates of the Palace School, members of the Ruling Institution, who saw in Bayezid a conservative alternative to Mehmed II’s centralizing policies. They installed Bayezid’s son Korkud as regent to hold the throne, and when Bayezid arrived, they extracted explicit concessions:

1,000 akçes to each sipahi and 300 to each janissary
Increased daily pay of 5 akçes for sipahis and 3 for janissaries
A promise that viziers would be appointed only from the janissary officers, court sipahis, and palace pages
Immunity for the pillaging and violence the janissaries had already committed

Bayezid assented to all but the last, spurring his horse through the crowd without reply.
The Gedik Ahmed Crisis (1482)
The most dramatic demonstration of janissary power came in November 1482. Bayezid, nursing a personal grudge against Gedik Ahmed Pasha dating from the 1473 campaign against Uzun Hasan, had the great general imprisoned. Gedik Ahmed had risen from the ranks of the common janissaries — he was not a Palace School graduate — and was the most popular commander in the empire. The reaction was instantaneous:

The janissaries, sipahis, and silihdars (sword-bearers) marched on the palace
They forced the outer gate and had Bayezid at their mercy
They demanded Gedik Ahmed’s release, the dismissal of those who had counseled his arrest, and fulfilment of the donatives promised before the Battle of Yenişehir
They accused Bayezid of favoring timar-holders and peasants over the soldiers who had built the Ottoman state

Bayezid capitulated. Gedik Ahmed was not only freed but promoted to vizier with special authority. Mustafa Pasha (Bayezid’s maternal uncle) was imprisoned, and Rustem (the janissary agha and Bayezid’s son-in-law) fled. The episode demonstrated that Bayezid was not an absolute sovereign but was often at the mercy of the court and the army.
Yet Bayezid was patient. Later that same year, after Cem had fled to Rhodes and the immediate threat receded, Bayezid had Gedik Ahmed seized at a banquet in Edirne and executed. The charges against him were many: he had criticized Bayezid’s military leadership in 1473; he had protested abandoning the Otranto campaign; he was too popular with the troops; he opposed the treaty with Rhodes that amounted to paying tribute for Cem’s imprisonment; and he was outspoken, especially when drunk. Some janissaries rebelled at the execution, but they were crushed. Bayezid was slowly moving toward personal control, but the pattern was set: janissary power was a permanent constraint on Ottoman foreign policy.
Post-Cem Unrest
After Cem’s death in 1495, with the restraining hand of the Cem threat removed, the janissaries became increasingly restive. In the summer of 1496, a major outbreak occurred in Istanbul between janissaries and sipahis. Neither Bayezid nor his officials dared intervene for some time, even as officials were being robbed. The riot was eventually quelled and some agitators executed, but the message was clear: prolonged peace was intolerable to the professional soldiery. This pressure was a significant factor in the deterioration of relations with Venice that culminated in the war of 1499–1503.
The Palace School vs. the Ghazi Tradition
A fundamental tension ran through Bayezid’s court: the division between palace-trained pashas and commanders who had risen through the ranks of the janissaries and the ghazi tradition.
The Palace School (Enderun), established and expanded by Mehmed II, produced a cadre of administrators and commanders who were loyal to the sultan as an institution rather than to any particular faction. These men were typically of devşirme origin, educated in the palace, and imbued with an esprit de corps that transcended regional and ethnic loyalties. The ghazi tradition, by contrast, emphasized frontier warfare, personal valor, and independence from centralized control.
Mehmed II had relied heavily on the Palace School graduates and the ulema, but this alienated the ghazi elements. Bayezid, having spent twenty-four years as governor of Amasya — an old ghazi center — was seen as sympathetic to the ghazi tradition, yet he was placed on the throne by Palace School men.
This tension erupted during the Egyptian war. Hersekoglu Ahmed Pasha — son of the last Duke of Herzegovina, educated in the Palace School, and Bayezid’s son-in-law — was appointed beylerbey of Anatolia and sent to campaign against the Mamluks. But when he took command, Karagöz Pasha (a Karamanian) and Khizrbeyoglu Mehmed Bey (Prince Ahmed’s preceptor) refused to cooperate, each considering himself superior to Hersekoglu. The reason was clear: to Anatolian-born officers like Karagöz, Hersekoglu was a Christian convert, a representative of the Ruling Institution that was centralizing authority in Istanbul at the expense of local power. When battle was joined near Adana, the two offended officers withdrew without fighting, and Hersekoglu was captured.
The same fault line appeared among Bayezid’s sons. Selim (later Selim I) was loved by the janissaries — he was a member of the Bektashi order, the traditional janissary religious order — while Ahmed was fat, pleasure-loving, and favored by the Palace School graduates and the high officials. When Bayezid tried to make Ahmed his successor, the janissaries refused: “Ahmed is fat and cannot lead us in war,” they said. They preferred Selim, who would maintain the empire and pay them liberally.
The Role of the Ulema and Their Partisanship for Cem
The ulema represented the settled, civilized, orthodox face of Ottoman society — the theologians, jurists, and educators who staffed the mosque-schools (medreses) and the judicial system. In the ghazi-ulema dichotomy that defined early Ottoman statecraft, the ulema were the counterweight to the frontier warriors. They were conservative, learned, and oriented toward the established Islamic centers of the older Seljuk and Abbasid traditions.
Bursa was their stronghold — dar al-‘ulema, “the city of theologians” — and Bursa was Cem’s power base. When Cem occupied Bursa in 1481, the ulema of the city supported him. The deputies sent to negotiate with Bayezid on Cem’s behalf — Ahmed Chelebi (son of Khudayi Shukr), and the mollas Ayas and Hamdi Chelebi — were all from the ulema. They proposed dividing the empire: Bayezid taking Europe, Cem taking Asia. Bursa would have been the capital of this Anatolian empire.
The grand vizier Karamani Mehmed Pasha, who plotted to put Cem on the throne, was himself a product of the ulema — born in Karamania, educated in a medrese, a descendant of Jalal al-Din al-Rumi. He was the leader of the “Moslem Institution,” the civil administration of theologians and lawyers who wished to re-establish an oriental monarchy. His co-conspirator, Manisali Chelebi, was the head of the ulema.
After Cem’s defeat, Bayezid punished the ulema who had supported him. At Bursa, when the janissaries wanted to sack the city and execute the dervishes who had aided Cem, Bayezid asked that Bursa be “given to him” — but each janissary had to be bribed first. Some dervishes were executed anyway.
During the succession crisis at the end of Bayezid’s reign, the poets and theologians supported Korkud, the scholarly son. Korkud was steeped in theology and philosophy, but the janissaries deemed him unfit to rule. The alignment was consistent: the ulema and literati preferred the contemplative, the soldiers preferred the martial.
The Succession Struggle and Foreign Exploitation
The Three Sons
By the last decade of Bayezid’s reign, three sons remained in contention: Ahmed, Korkud, and Selim. Each cultivated a different constituency:

Ahmed (governor of Amasya): Bayezid’s favorite, supported by the high officials, the grand vizier Ali Pasha, and Hersekoglu Ahmed Pasha (Ahmed’s brother-in-law). He was amiable and regal but not generous — his parsimony cost him janissary support.
Korkud (governor of Attalya, later Manisa): A poet and scholar, supported by the ulema and theologians. He had briefly been regent in 1481 and claimed priority as a former sultan.
Selim (governor of Trebizond): Cunning, ruthless, energetic, and generous. Loved by the janissaries and a member of the Bektashi order.

Foreign Exploitation
The succession struggle drew in foreign powers:
Korkud’s flight to Egypt (1509): Feeling squeezed by his brothers and humiliated by Ali Pasha’s seizure of his lands, Korkud sailed from Attalya in May 1509 to seek aid from the Mamluk sultan of Egypt. He was received royally in Alexandria and Cairo, but Bayezid sent word to Egypt that any support for Korkud would be considered an attack on the Ottoman Empire. Egypt, already in a disastrous war with the Portuguese, backed down.
Ahmed’s alliance with Persia (1511–1512): After being passed over for the succession, Ahmed married one of his daughters to Shah Ismail of Persia, while one of his sons donned the red hat of the Safavid heretics. This was a direct appeal to the Kızılbaş (Shiite) elements in Anatolia. The result was that in the final struggle, Selim controlled Europe with Tartar support, while Ahmed controlled Asia with Persian aid. The Safavid heresy that Selim would later crush so brutally had been, in its origins, intertwined with Ottoman dynastic politics.
Selim’s Tartar alliance: Selim married the widow of his brother Mahmud, who was the daughter of a Tartar khan. This gave him a reserve of Tartar cavalry that proved decisive.
Venetian and European intelligence: Throughout the succession crisis, Venetian bailos and merchants in Istanbul sent detailed reports to Venice on the movements of the princes, the health of Bayezid, and the shifting balance of power. The Venetians understood that the succession question directly affected Ottoman foreign policy — a militant sultan like Selim would be far more dangerous than Bayezid.
The Financial Dimension
The Cost of Cem
The Cem affair was an immense financial burden. By the treaty of December 1482, Bayezid agreed to pay the Knights of St. John 45,000 Venetian ducats annually for Cem’s confinement, plus a like sum for the past year — 90,000 ducats in the first year alone. When Cem was transferred to the Pope in 1489, the payments continued. Between 1482 and 1495, Bayezid paid well over half a million ducats for his brother’s imprisonment.
These payments were deeply unpopular. Gedik Ahmed Pasha had protested that paying for Cem’s imprisonment was “prostituting the dignity of the Ottoman state.” The treaty with Rhodes was a bitter pill: Bayezid, the sultan of a rising empire, was paying tribute to a small crusader order.
Bayezid’s letter to Pope Alexander VI in 1494 suggested — in veiled terms — that Cem should be killed, offering 300,000 ducats for the deed. Cem died naturally (or was poisoned) in February 1495, and Bayezid then faced demands from France, Naples, and the Pope for the promised sum. The body was held for ransom: 5,000 ducats was demanded for its release. Bayezid eventually retrieved Cem’s remains in 1499 and interred them at Bursa.
Donatives and Pay Increases
The financial demands of the soldiery were constant. Bayezid’s accession cost donatives of 1,000 akçes per sipahi and 300 per janissary, plus daily pay increases. Before the Battle of Yenişehir, Bayezid renewed the promises his son Korkud had made. After Gedik Ahmed’s imprisonment crisis, he was forced to pay the bonuses again.
By 1496, Bayezid’s annual income was estimated at 2,400,000 ducats, with about 3,000,000 ducats in the treasury. His army included 8,000 janissaries, 8,000 sipahis, and roughly 115,000 other troops, plus a fleet of about 250 ships. The cost of maintaining this establishment was enormous, and the Egyptian war of 1485–1491 was described as “very costly in men” — losses estimated at 100,000.
Tribute and Trade
Bayezid used tribute payments as diplomatic tools. The Ragusans paid an annual tribute, reduced to 3,000 ducats on Bayezid’s accession. Venice was relieved of the 10,000 ducat annual tribute in the 1482 treaty — a concession Bayezid made to keep Venice neutral on the Cem question. The Knights of St. John and the Pope received the largest payments: 45,000 ducats annually for Cem.
Bayezid was also conscious of commercial interests. He reduced Venetian customs duties from five to four percent, granted favorable treaties to Florence, and carefully managed the Ragusan trade route. His position on the Bosphorus made him “undoubtedly the wealthiest monarch of his age.”
Marriage Alliances
Bayezid’s political sagacity in arranging marriages was one of the keys to his success. The faction that placed him on the throne was bound together by family ties:

Kasım, agha of the janissaries, was related to Bayezid by marriage
Rüstem, janissary pasha, was Bayezid’s son-in-law
Hersekli Ahmed Pasha, beylerbey of Rumelia, was Bayezid’s son-in-law
Sinan, beylerbey of Anatolia, was related by marriage
Ishak Pasha and Daud Pasha, the viziers in Istanbul, were Palace School graduates

After Bayezid’s accession, he continued this pattern. Hersekoglu Ahmed Pasha became one of the most important figures of the reign, serving as grand vizier, admiral of the fleet, and twice being captured by the Mamluks. His relationship with Bayezid — he was both son-in-law and brother-in-law — made him uniquely trusted.
In 1489, Bayezid gave three of his daughters in marriage in a single grand celebration in Istanbul, each to a great lord. One was a Persian, Ahmed Mirza, a grandson of Uzun Hasan — a marriage that tied Ottoman interests to Persian dynastic politics and would later be used as a pretext for intervention in Persian affairs.
Alaeddevlet of Sulqadr, the border prince who repeatedly switched sides between the Ottomans and Mamluks, gave his daughter to the son of the Mamluk general Uzbey, cementing his defection from the Ottoman cause.
In the succession struggle, marriage became a weapon: Selim married the Tartar khan’s daughter (his brother Mahmud’s widow), giving him a reservoir of steppe cavalry; Ahmed married his daughter to Shah Ismail of Persia.
Fratricide and Succession Law
The Ottoman practice of fratricide — the execution of all male rivals by the successful heir — had been in place for at least a century before Bayezid. It set the stakes of inheritance high and virtually guaranteed civil war at every succession.
Bayezid’s response to Cem’s envoys who proposed a division of the empire — “Kings have no relatives” — illustrated the logic of the system. Yet Bayezid did not execute Cem when he had the chance, perhaps because he feared the reaction of the ulema, perhaps because he was genuinely reluctant to kill his brother. Instead, he offered Cem a pension of one million akçes and retirement in Jerusalem. Cem refused.
The system had direct foreign policy consequences. European powers understood that a defeated prince was a valuable asset. The Knights of St. John, the Pope, the King of France, Hungary, Naples, and Venice all tried to use Cem as a lever against the Ottomans. The mere existence of a rival claimant constrained Ottoman foreign policy for fourteen years.
At the end of Bayezid’s reign, the system produced another civil war. Bayezid wanted Ahmed to succeed, but the janissaries wanted Selim. Bayezid was forced to abdicate on April 24, 1512. Selim was girded with the sword of Osman. Thirteen days later, on May 26, Bayezid died en route to Demotika, almost certainly poisoned on Selim’s orders. The reason was political: Selim could not cross to Asia against Ahmed with Bayezid still living in Europe, and he needed the treasury to pay the janissaries.
The Role of the Grand Vizierate and Vizieral Factions
The grand vizierate under Bayezid was a revolving door, reflecting the shifting balance of court factions:



























































Grand VizierTenureFactionNotesKaramani Mehmed Pashauntil May 1481Ulema / CemKilled by janissariesIshak Pasha1481–1482Palace SchoolFather-in-law of Gedik Ahmed; replaced after Gedik Ahmed’s executionDaud Pasha1482–1497Palace School / MilitaryLed the 1487 Egyptian campaignHersekoglu Ahmed Pasha1497–1498Bayezid’s son-in-law / Palace SchoolFormer Duke of Herzegovina’s sonIbrahim Pasha1498–1499Bayezid’s former teacherFifth-generation vizier familyMesih Pasha1499–1501Palace SchoolHersekoglu Ahmed Pasha1501–1503(second term)Ali Pasha1503–1511Ahmed’s factionFavored Ahmed over Selim; killed at Sarmusaklu fighting the Kızılbaş
The viziers were not merely administrators but active participants in the dynastic struggle. Ali Pasha, the grand vizier from 1503, was a staunch partisan of Prince Ahmed. He opposed Selim at every turn and was largely responsible for the policy of trying to install Ahmed. When Bayezid wanted to abdicate in favor of Ahmed in 1511, the viziers counseled delay because they feared Selim’s revenge — an accurate fear.
Hersekoglu Ahmed Pasha, though Ahmed’s brother-in-law, was more cautious. During the janissary riot of September 1511, when a mob came to his palace, he coolly appeared at his door, asked why they had come, and told them to present their grievances to Bayezid. This action may have saved the capital from a general uprising.
The viziers were also deeply involved in foreign policy, sometimes acting independently of Bayezid. During the prelude to the Venetian war, the viziers (especially Ibrahim Pasha and Hersekoglu) conducted the negotiations with Andrea Zanchani, offered opinions on French and Imperial power, and ultimately convinced Bayezid to proceed with the attack on Corfu when he wanted to recall the fleet.
Intelligence and Espionage
Bayezid maintained an extensive intelligence network in Europe, focused primarily on tracking Cem and monitoring the plans of Christian princes.
Ottoman Agents
The most notable Ottoman agent was Assam Bey, who carried Cem’s pension to Pope Alexander VI in 1494, along with Bayezid’s letter suggesting Cem’s elimination. He was robbed at Sinigaglia by Giuliano della Rovere, who stole both the money and the correspondence.
Bayezid sent kapujibaşı Mustafa to Rome in 1490 with 120,000 ducats (three years’ pension) and holy relics — the lance that pierced Christ’s side and the sponge that quenched his thirst — to negotiate directly with the Pope.
Ottoman ambassadors were regularly deployed to European courts. Iskender Pasha, Bayezid’s former preceptor, was sent to Venice in 1482 to confirm the treaty and to negotiate about Cem — offering the Morea as a reward if Cem fell into Venetian hands. The same Iskender Pasha, as sanjakbey of Bosnia, led raids into Dalmatia and Hungary throughout the 1480s and 1490s.
The Venetian Network
The Venetian bailo (resident ambassador) in Istanbul was effectively an intelligence officer. Bayezid refused to accept a Venetian bailo for most of his reign, considering the office “an official spy.” When Andrea Gritti — the wealthiest Venetian merchant in Istanbul — served as an unofficial representative, his reports to Venice were detailed and accurate. He warned Venice in January 1498 that the viziers were not inclined to peace, that a great fleet was being prepared, and that Bayezid had the means to wage war.
Venetian consuls and merchants in Chios, Corfu, Modon, Alexandria, and other ports sent a constant stream of reports that were summarized in the diary of Marino Sanuto. These dispatches tracked:

Shipbuilding at Gallipoli, Ismid, Salonica, Negroponte, and Valona
Grain shortages and trade embargoes
Troop movements and army sizes
The health and mood of the sultan
The ambitions of the princes

Intercepted Communications
Bayezid intercepted letters between Cem and his supporters. In 1485, through Venice, he learned that Cem in France was corresponding with his mother in Egypt. Bayezid sent for Cem’s mother and family, but the Mamluk sultan refused to surrender them.
The Venetian bailo Hironimo Marcello was caught sending secret letters describing Ottoman naval preparations and was expelled; Bayezid notified Venice not to replace him.
Counter-Intelligence
During the succession crisis of 1511, Bayezid closely guarded the Persian ambassador to prevent him from learning of the dissension among the princes. He feared that if Shah Ismail knew how divided the Ottomans were, he would actively aid the Shia heretics in Anatolia.
Conclusion
The intersection of internal politics and foreign policy during Bayezid II’s reign reveals a sultan who was never truly master in his own house. He was placed on the throne by the janissaries and a marriage alliance of Palace School graduates; he was forced to cede to their demands at swordpoint; he paid humiliating tribute to Christian powers for fourteen years to neutralize his brother; he fought an indecisive and costly war with Egypt while fending off crusade threats; and he was finally deposed by his own son at the head of the janissaries he could no longer control or satisfy.
The pattern that Fisher describes — “the soldiers and the officials at the Porte, who had begun to show their spirit and their influence in the reign of Mehmed II, became in reality all-powerful” — was a direct consequence of the Cem crisis. Bayezid’s conservatism and caution kept the empire intact, but at the price of empowering the very factions that would dominate and weaken it in the following century. As a contemporary Venetian observer wrote: “They are the ones who dominate and lord it over the country.”
Key Sources

Fisher, Sydney Nettleton. The Foreign Relations of Turkey, 1481–1512. Illinois Studies in the Social Sciences, Vol. XXX, No. 1. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1948.
DaLezze, Donado. Historia Turchesca (1300–1514).
Sanuto, Marino. I Diarii. 58 vols.
Thuasne, L. Djem-Sultan, fils de Mohammed II, frère de Bayezid II (1459–1495).
Gritti, Andrea. Relazione de Andrea Gritti oratore straordinario a Bajezid II (1503).

concepts/law-kanun-and-seriat.title: Ottoman Law — Kanun and Seriat
concepts/law-kanun-and-seriat.content:
Ottoman Law — Kanun and Seriat
The legal system of the Ottoman Empire under Suleiman the Magnificent was a dual structure that balanced two distinct bodies of law: the Şeriat (شرع, Sheri or Sacred Law of Islam) and the Kanun (قانون, the written decrees of the sultans). These were supplemented by Adet (عادت, established custom) and Urf (عرف, the sovereign will of the reigning sultan). This arrangement gave the Ottoman state a flexible and durable legal framework, one that Lybyer describes as the empire’s “written constitution” — a constitution not subject to amendment by the sovereign, but capable of limited evolution through judicial and juristic interpretation.
The Four Sources of Ottoman Law
Lybyer identifies four distinct layers of law operating within the Ottoman Empire:
Şeriat — the Sacred Law of Islam, based on the Qur’an, supplemented by the hadith (traditions of the Prophet’s legal decisions and sayings), the decisions of the early caliphs, and the interpretations of early judges. It was first formulated by Abu Hanifa, the earliest of the four great orthodox Islamic legal schools, whose code became the accepted teaching tradition for all Turkish peoples. The Şeriat was above the sultan and unchangeable by him. It served as a written constitution for the empire, and any attempt by the sovereign to transgress it was understood to absolve the Moslem community from allegiance.
Kanun — the written decrees of the sultans, issued in accordance with a formula of the Sacred Law that recognised the ruler’s right to make all civil and political regulations demanded by prudence and the public welfare. Kanuns dealt with military, financial, feudal, criminal, and police law, as well as the law of ceremonies. Within the fields of feudal law and the law of ceremonies — matters entirely non-existent in the early Islamic state — the sultans had a free hand; in all other areas their Kanuns were strictly supplementary and administrative.
Adet — established custom, corresponding primarily to the unwritten regulations under which the Turks of the steppe lands had lived. Adet supplemented the Sacred Law and the Kanuns in matters they did not cover, and differed from district to district. It carried such binding force that to the unlettered citizen it formed an indistinguishable whole with the Sacred Law and the Kanuns, seeming almost a feature of the natural order.
Urf — the sovereign will of the reigning sultan, the seat and organ of sovereignty. When expressed and written, Urf became Kanun and annulled all contradictory Kanuns and Adet. However, against the Şeriat, Urf had no force; any attempt to exercise it in transgression of the Sacred Law was an invitation to disaster and justified revolution.
The Sultan as Legislator
Under the Ottoman system, the sole power to issue legislation rested in the sultan — but this power was sharply limited by the supremacy of the Sacred Law. Suleiman’s authority as legislator rested on his position as caliph, head of the Islamic state, defender and executor of the Sacred Law. The Sacred Law recognised no power of legislation in the head of state, since God through Mohammed had legislated once for all; it entrusted to him only the functions of administration and justice, to be exercised subject always to the prescriptions of the Law.
Suleiman was named Kanuni (قانونی, the Legislator) by comparison with his predecessors, who had been men of the sword rather than of the pen. By his accession, the feudal system, taxation, land tenure, and criminal law had come into great disorder. Egyptian affairs were in wild confusion after the Mamluk conquest. Suleiman therefore issued a large number of Kanuns dealing especially with timars (fiefs), rayahs (subjects), ceremonies, criminal and market regulations, and comprising a constitution for Egypt — the Kanun-nameh Misr (قانوننامه مصر), issued in 1532.
Lybyer emphasises that Suleiman’s legislation was doubly hindered: first by the conservatism of his people and his religion, which believed the old ways were best, and second by the weakness inherent in despotic legislation, in which the distance of the lawgiver from the subjects affected made true adaptation to circumstances impossible. Most of his laws professed to restore a former better state of affairs; they likely did little more than formalise a confused body of practice that had grown up gradually.
The Kanun-nameh of Suleiman and Mufti Ebu Su’ud
The best-known collection of Suleiman’s laws was compiled by the Mufti Ebu Su’ud (ابو السعود), one of the most distinguished ornaments of Suleiman’s reign. Ebu Su’ud had passed through all the stages of advancement among the Ulema and had served as Kaziasker for eight years before being appointed Mufti. He wrote a great commentary on the Qur’an and assembled the most famous Kanun-nameh (قانوننامه, book of laws) of Suleiman.
Lybyer cautions against thinking of a Kanun-nameh as resembling the codes of Theodosius or Justinian. The Kanun-nameh of Suleiman as arranged by Ebu Su’ud was not a unified body of law but a collection of Kanuns issued at different times, dealing chiefly with:

Land tenure and hereditary tenancy — laws concerning the tapu (hereditary lease of land), fallow fields, uncultivated lands, the relationship of tenants and absent tenants, and the rules for when a spahi (fief-holder) died or was dispossessed before delivery of the lease
Taxation — laws on the ground tax, the tithe (öşür), the fifth, taxes on vineyards, mills, green produce, honey, bees, sheep, the fold-tax, the half-hide-tax (bride-tax), the hide-tax, the obligations of subjects, and the tax on prisoners and slaves
The feudal system — regulations for fief-holders of both large and small fiefs, laws concerning the intendants of the fiscus and receivers of taxes, regulations for the Beylerbeys, Sanjak Beys, and Kapuji-bashis, and rules governing lawsuits between spahis and non-Moslems or between two spahis
Criminal and police law — judge’s fees, the contents of judge’s berats (diplomas of appointment), laws concerning fugitives, wandering hordes, wagoners, and irregular cavalry
Imperial foundations and vakfs — regulations concerning the religious endowments that constituted perhaps one-third of the empire’s land
Procedural and judicial matters — rules concerning lawsuits over land, the timing of harvest for legal purposes, and the dating and registering of fiefs

The Kanun-nameh begins at folio 27 of the Vienna manuscript (Fluegel No. 1816, Imperial Library, Vienna) and runs through folio 60, containing over forty distinct laws. It covers the full range of relations between the state, the land, and the subject population.
Şeriat and Kanun: The Relationship
The relationship between the two bodies of law was hierarchical, not parallel. The Şeriat was supreme; the Kanuns were subordinate and supplementary. The judges applied the Sacred Law first — as given in the Qur’an, the traditions of Mohammed, codified by the school of Abu Hanifa, and interpreted in collections of fetvas — and then applied the Kanuns of the sultans and the local customs of the regions in which they served.
Lybyer notes that the distinction between sacred and secular law corresponded inversely to the distinction in the West: to the canon law of the West corresponded the Şeriat, and to the civil or national law of the West corresponded the Kanuns. But the Şeriat had wider sway in sixteenth-century Turkey than the canon law ever had in the West. It dealt with a far larger field, its judges sometimes administered the Kanuns as well, and they had the support of the national government — whereas the rival courts of the great officials had ordinarily a very limited jurisdiction.
The practical regulations of the Sacred Law went by the Arabic name fikh (فقه); it included both jurisprudence and positive law. In the Ottoman Empire of the sixteenth century, the Şeriat held the place of overwhelming preeminence in legal matters, both in usefulness and in honour. Its practical precepts to the full extent of their formulated scope were the private law of the land; its judges were of equal or greater authority than the high officers of government.
The Mufti and the Fetva
Among the Ulema (علماء, learned men) who had completed the law course, a distinct body — the müftis (مفتون) — served as jurisconsults rather than judges. One mufti was assigned as associate to the judge of every important city, to the number of about two hundred in all, while others were counsellors for the Beylerbeys and Sanjak Beys. Appointed for life and living in retirement, they had no initiative of action. When a judge, Bey, or private citizen submitted a written question involving a learned knowledge of the Sacred Law, the mufti’s duty was to give an answer that applied the Sacred Law to the matter concerned. These answers, called fetvas (فتوى), were extremely concise, unaccompanied by reasoning, and prepared and sealed in solemn form.
When a judge or Bey proposed to his mufti a question touching a pending law case, the mufti’s response ordinarily settled the case. Since there was no class of professional lawyers in the Ottoman Empire, the muftis were a necessary and highly useful body.
The Şeyhülislam: Supreme Guardian of the Law
The mufti of Constantinople became par excellence the Mufti (مفتى). Mohammed II had assigned him the title of Şeyhülislam (شيخ الإسلام, the Ancient of Islam), which in later times became his ordinary designation. Suleiman definitively constituted the Şeyhülislam the head of the Ulema, outranking all officials of government except that he yielded place to the grand vizier on ordinary occasions. He was nearly the equal of the sultan himself, since he was the expounder and representative of the Sacred Law, which stood above the sultan.
Sixteenth-century Western observers compared the Mufti with a cardinal, but more often with the Pope of Rome. The comparison was apt in dignity and in the magnitude of the matters with which he dealt. He alone could proclaim that war should be begun, and send out preachers to declare it holy and incumbent on all Moslems. He was frequently consulted by the sultan on the conformity of proposed Kanuns with the Sacred Law. In his hands rested the ultimate responsibility of pronouncing that a sultan had transgressed the Sacred Law and ought to be deposed.
Lybyer draws a direct parallel between the Mufti’s power and that of the United States Supreme Court: the Mufti was the final earthly authority in interpreting the Sacred Law, exercising the power of defending the constitution. The fetvas of the muftis amounted in practice to a body of legislation intermediate between the Şeriat and the Kanuns, partaking of the sacred character of the former while being of a modern and practical nature derived from recent application to actual cases.
Ebu Su’ud’s role in the execution of Suleiman’s eldest son Mustapha illustrates the Mufti’s enormous moral authority. Suleiman, seeking to satisfy religious scruples before ordering his son’s death, presented a hypothetical case to the Mufti: a merchant left a slave in charge of his property, wife, and children; the slave embezzled and plotted against the family. Ebu Su’ud answered that such a slave deserved to be tortured to death — and this fetva, as Busbecq records, greatly influenced Suleiman’s decision.
The Judicial Hierarchy
The judges belonging to the Ulema corps had jurisdictions based on territory, covering the entire empire to an even wider extent than did the administrative government. The tribunals of the judges were competent for all kinds of cases — civil or criminal, whether covered by the Şeriat, the Kanuns, Adet, or none of these. However, they were not competent to try all persons: the kullar (sultan’s slaves), the Seids (descendants of the Prophet), and members of foreign colonies had their separate systems of justice.
The judges were carefully graded in five principal classes:
Kaziaskers (قاضي عسكر) — the two chief judges of the empire: the Kaziasker of Rumelia (Europe) and the Kaziasker of Anatolia (Asia). They ranked next to the viziers in the Divan, had first right of audience with the sultan at the close of each meeting of the council, and until Suleiman’s reign had held all the authority over the Ulema that later came to the Mufti. They controlled the appointment of all judges of other classes, subject to confirmation by the sultan. In the Divan and as “Pillars of the State,” they were among the four loftiest supports of Ottoman government, alongside the viziers, the defterdars (treasurers), and the nishanjis (chancellors).
Greater Mollas — in six groups: the Kaziaskers themselves; the judge of Constantinople; the judges of Mecca and Medina; the judges of Adrianople, Brusa, Cairo, and Damascus; and the judges of the three suburbs of Constantinople (Galata, Scutari, Eyub) and of Jerusalem, Smyrna, Aleppo, Larissa, and Salonica — seventeen judges in all.
Lesser Mollas — the judges of ten cities of second rank, including Marash, Baghdad, Sarajevo, Sofia, Belgrade, Konya, and Diyarbekir.
Mufettishes — five special judges dealing with disputes over vakf (religious endowment) lands.
Kazis (قاضى) — the vast majority of judges, about four hundred and fifty, stationed in smaller cities. About two hundred in Europe and the Crimea were under the Kaziasker of Rumelia; about two hundred and twenty-five in Asia and thirty-six in Egypt were under the Kaziasker of Anatolia.
Naibs (نائب) — inferior judges in several groups: judges of villages, lesser judges of cities, temporary substitutes for higher judges, and inspectors of public morals.
The Kaziaskers
The two Kaziaskers (قاضي عسكر, judge of the army) held a pivotal position in Ottoman law. Their titles reveal their original function as judges of the armies of Rumelia and Anatolia — offices they continued to exercise in time of war. In this capacity, appeals came up to them from the Subashis and Sanjak Beys in matters touching the kullar.
Under Mohammed II, it was provided that cases concerning Moslems in Constantinople should come before the Kaziasker of Rumelia, and those concerning non-Moslems before the Kaziasker of Anatolia. Their power had been extended to include the headship of all judges of their respective regions and the appointment of all judges. Each had a large corps of subordinate officials. They held court in their own houses, in addition to their duties in the Divan and at the palace gate.
Court Procedure and Appeals
In every court a single judge sat, with his clerks and other subordinates. Cases were presented by the parties themselves, and decisions were usually rendered immediately in very concise form. The judge cooperated with the Subasi (subashi) of the city, who brought summoned persons before the judge and executed the sentences — an arrangement akin to the ecclesiastical courts of the West, which could condemn but left execution to the secular arm.
Appeal went up through the judicial hierarchy: from the Naibs to the Kazis, to the Mollas, and finally to the two Kaziaskers. The highest courts were those of the Kaziaskers, the grand vizier (who was actual head of the Moslem Institution as substitute for the sultan, and whose court was the highest court of appeal for ordinary civil cases), and the Divan (the imperial council, which tried capital cases of great officials).
Costs and fines were moderate and fixed by Kanun, but they constituted a large part of the income of judges and their subordinates. The judges were salaried; some had in addition large irregular earnings. The judges also attended to all the notarial work of the empire.
Ottoman Turkish Key Terms

































































Ottoman TurkishModern TurkishEnglishشرع (شريعت)ŞeriatSacred/divine law of IslamقانونKanunSultanic secular decree/lawقانون نامهKanunnameBook or collection of lawsقاضیKadıJudgeقاضی عسكرKazaskerMilitary judge (lit. “judge of the army”)مفتىMüftüJurisconsult, expounder of Sacred Lawشيخ الإسلامŞeyhülislamChief of the Ulema, supreme MuftiفتوىFetvaFormal legal opinion of a muftiعرفÖrfSovereign will of the sultanعادتÂdetEstablished customفقهFıkıhPractical jurisprudence of the Sacred Law
Significance
Lybyer’s analysis of Ottoman law reveals a system that was both rigid and flexible — rigid in its supreme commitment to the unchanging Sacred Law, flexible in the space it allowed for sultanic legislation in areas the Sacred Law did not touch. The dual structure of Şeriat and Kanun, held in balance by the Mufti’s power of constitutional interpretation, gave the Ottoman Empire a stability that outlasted many other early modern states. But Lybyer also notes the system’s fundamental weakness: the idea of the changelessness of the Sacred Law was essentially hostile to progress. While considerable flexibility was possible in the application of the law to particular cases, the law itself could hardly change. The Ulema, who had built and maintained the Ottoman legal structure, also held the empire nearly stationary while the rest of the world moved on.

concepts/maritime-infrastructure.title: maritime-infrastructure
concepts/maritime-infrastructure.content:
Ottoman Maritime Infrastructure and Naval Practice
Overview
The Ottoman navy developed from a modest raiding fleet based at Gallipoli into the dominant galley navy of the Mediterranean under the classical age. Its growth was driven by three imperatives: the struggle with Venice for control of the Aegean and Adriatic; the need to secure the Dardanelles and the sea route between Rumelia and Anatolia; and, after the conquest of Egypt and Syria (1516-17), the defence of the Red Sea and the Indian Ocean against the Portuguese. The navy was both a war-fighting instrument and the enforcer of the empire’s closed economic system, particularly in the Black Sea, which after 1475 became an Ottoman lake.

1. The Imperial Arsenal (Tersane-i Amire / ترسانه عامره)
The chief naval dockyard and administrative centre of the Ottoman fleet was the Tersane-i Amire (Imperial Arsenal), located on the Golden Horn (Haliç) at Kasımpaşa, Istanbul. Under Mehmed II it was expanded into a major industrial complex. After the destruction of the Ottoman fleet at Lepanto (1571), the Arsenal’s ceaseless labour rebuilt the navy over a single winter, enabling Venice to make peace in 1573 — testament to the Arsenal’s productive capacity.
The Arsenal was administered by the tersane emini (arsenal superintendent), who oversaw shipbuilding, provisioning, and the supply of naval stores (timber, hemp, sailcloth, pitch, and iron). The grand vizier retained the right to visit and inspect the Arsenal at any time.
From the second half of the 16th century, the kapudan-i derya (grand admiral) presided over a separate naval council at the Arsenal, dealing with matters of the fleet, lawsuits, nominations, appointments, and dismissals.
Secondary dockyards existed at Gallipoli (Gelibolu), Sinop on the Black Sea, Suez on the Red Sea, and later at Basra on the Persian Gulf.

2. Shipbuilding and Vessel Types
Ottoman naval architecture reflected the Mediterranean galley tradition, adapted and scaled up for imperial requirements.
Kadırga (قاديرغا) — The standard Ottoman galley, a narrow, shallow-draft, oar-and-sail warship powered primarily by rows of oarsmen (slaves, prisoners, or levend volunteers) and carrying a lateen sail. Kadırgas were the backbone of the Aegean and Mediterranean galley fleet. They were fast, manoeuvrable in coastal waters, and could be beached for landing operations.
Baştarde (باشترده) — A larger, heavier galley, sometimes described as a “great galley” or bastarda. These were built for flagships and carried heavier armament, including cannon mounted on the prow. During the Venetian war of 1499-1502, Bâyezid II’s Ottomans constructed two warships of 1,800 tonilato, the largest then known — likely baştarde-type vessels built to overawe Venice.
Kalyon (كاليون) — The sailing galleon, a later introduction. At the Battle of Lepanto (1571) the Ottoman fleet was still composed overwhelmingly of galleys, which proved ineffective against the tall ships of the Christian allies capable of firing powerful broadsides. The Ottoman navy adopted the kalyon only very late and with great difficulty. By 1607 Sir Thomas Sherley observed that one English warship could defeat ten Turkish galleys. Technological conservatism around the sailing ship marked the beginning of Ottoman naval decline.
Other vessels: The fleet included smaller craft — kalita (light galley), pergende, firkate (frigate), and şayka (river/coastal vessel). A Danube river fleet of eighty to a hundred light vessels was maintained as early as 1442. The Cossacks, from the 1590s, used small, agile şayka fleets to raid the Black Sea coast with devastating effect, a challenge the Ottoman galley navy could not meet.

3. Main Ports and Naval Bases
Istanbul — The imperial capital and home of the Tersane-i Amire. The Golden Horn served as the main fleet anchorage. The arsenal complex at Kasımpaşa provided the empire’s largest concentration of shipbuilding capacity, naval stores, and dock labour.
Gelibolu (Gallipoli) — The earliest Ottoman naval base. Under Bayezid I the Ottomans built a fortress and an inner harbour enclosed by a wall at Gallipoli, mooring a small raiding fleet there. In 1416 the Venetians burned the Ottoman fleet at Gallipoli and tried to destroy the base. By 1442 the Ottomans maintained sixty ships there. Under Mehmed II, Gallipoli’s role shifted to secondary status after the Arsenal at Istanbul was built, but it remained an important outport for the Dardanelles. After 1453 the fortresses at Çanakkale (the twin castles) and the fortification of Bozcaada (Tenedos) secured the straits.
İzmir — A major Aegean port and commercial hub. In the later 16th and 17th centuries it became the leading export port for Anatolian goods and a centre of English and French Levant trade. Its strategic position made it an important staging post for the Aegean fleet.
Trabzon (Trebizond) — After the conquest of the Comnene empire in 1461, Trebizond became the main Ottoman naval base on the eastern Black Sea, controlling the sea route to the Caucasus and linking with the Crimean khanate.
Kefe (Caffa) — The Genoese colony of Caffa in the Crimea was conquered in 1475. It became the principal Ottoman port in the northern Black Sea and a key node for the grain, slave, and fish trades with the steppe. Ottoman customs registers for 1490 show seventy-five vessels calling at Caffa in four months — Greek, Italian, Russian, and Muslim — linking it with Istanbul, Trebizond, Sinop, Azov, and İzmit.
İskenderiye (Alexandria) — The great Egyptian port and the western terminus of the Red Sea-Indian Ocean spice route. Venetian merchants bought up to twelve thousand quintals of spices annually in Alexandria between 1560 and 1564. The Istanbul-Alexandria convoy route was the empire’s most valuable maritime artery.
Süveys (Suez) — After Selim I’s conquest of Egypt (1517), Suez was developed as the Ottoman Red Sea arsenal. Selim ordered a fleet built there to expel the Portuguese from the Indian Ocean. In 1538 a fleet of thirty ships sailed from Suez against Diu. Suez remained the base for Ottoman Indian Ocean operations throughout the 16th century.
Other ports: Sinop (Black Sea arsenal and shipbuilding centre), Antalya (southern Anatolia), Avlonya/Vlorë (Albanian coast, launch point for the Otranto expedition of 1480), and Basra (second Indian Ocean base after 1546).

4. The Aegean and Mediterranean Galley Fleet
The Mediterranean war fleet grew from thirty galleys in the 1470s under Mehmed II to ninety-two galleys by the end of his reign. Under Süleyman I the fleet reached its apogee: 110 galleys were sent to assist Francis I in 1543, and the fleet wintered in Toulon — a demonstration of Ottoman reach in the western Mediterranean.
The Aegean islands were gradually incorporated. Rhodes was taken from the Knights of St John in 1522, securing the route between Istanbul and Alexandria. Chios was annexed in 1566, Cyprus conquered from Venice in 1570-71.
The galley fleet operated on a seasonal cycle: fitting out in spring at the Istanbul Arsenal, summer campaigning in the Aegean or eastern Mediterranean, and wintering (often beached and covered) at principal bases. Convoys of merchant ships (often fifty vessels or more) sailed from Istanbul to Alexandria escorted by warships, the kapudan-i derya himself accompanying them into the Aegean.

5. The Kapudan Pasha and the Naval Organization
Kapudan-i derya / قپودان پاشا — The Grand Admiral of the Ottoman fleet. In the glossary of Inalcik’s work, the title is defined as “the admiral of the sea, the grand admiral of the Ottoman fleet.”
In the classical period the kapudan-i derya was initially a military commander elected from the sea captains and corsair leaders. With the appointment of Hayreddin Barbarossa in 1533 as kapudan-i derya and beylerbeyi of Algiers, the office was elevated. The beylerbeyilik of Algiers was created to unify all naval forces against Charles V, and Barbarossa united under his administration Algiers and thirteen sanjaks on the shores and islands of the Mediterranean.
From the second half of the 16th century, as the fleet grew in importance, the kapudan-i derya came to be chosen from among the viziers and gained the right to sit in the imperial council. He also presided over a separate naval council (the kapudan paşa divanı) which dealt with naval matters, lawsuits arising in the fleet, and all nominations, appointments, and dismissals of naval personnel.
The kapudan-i derya’s authority extended not only over the fleet but also over the coastal sanjaks of the Mediterranean and Aegean, including parts of the Morea, the Archipelago, and later Cyprus. This vast maritime province was known as the Kapudanlık or Cezayir-i Bahr-i Sefid (Islands of the White Sea, i.e. the Mediterranean).
Supporting officers included:

Tersane emini — superintendent of the Arsenal
Tersane kethüdasi — steward of the Arsenal
Kapudan kethüdasi — admiral’s deputy
Reis — captain of an individual vessel or squadron


6. The Barbary Corsairs and Ottoman Naval Power
The Barbary corsairs of North Africa — based at Algiers, Tunis, and Tripoli — were integral to Ottoman naval strength in the western and central Mediterranean. The greatest of them, Hayreddin Barbarossa (Hayreddin Pasha, originally the corsair Hızır Reis, called Barbarossa by Europeans), was appointed kapudan-i derya in 1533 after his conquest of Algiers. Under him the Ottoman fleet defeated the combined Christian fleet under Andrea Doria at Preveza in 1538, leaving the Ottomans undisputed masters of the Mediterranean for several decades.
The corsair tradition was inherited from the gazi frontier ethos of the early Ottoman beylik. Uruç Reis (Barbarossa’s elder brother), Turgut Reis (Dragut), Piyale Pasha, and Kılıç Ali Pasha were all products of this Mediterranean frontier. The corsair economy — raiding Christian shipping, capturing slaves and booty — was both self-financing and strategically useful, projecting Ottoman power far beyond the reach of the imperial fleet alone.
After the 1590s, however, the central government began to lose control over its North African provinces. Naval forces from Tripoli, Tunis, and Algiers no longer formed a regular part of the sultan’s fleets, but acted as corsairs on their own initiative, sometimes even making separate treaties with European powers.

7. The Black Sea as an Ottoman Lake (after 1475)
The transformation of the Black Sea into an Ottoman lake was one of Mehmed II’s most enduring strategic achievements. In 1454, the year after the conquest of Istanbul, the Ottoman fleet sailed into the Black Sea and compelled the Genoese colonies, the Comnene kingdom of Trebizond, and Moldavia to pay tribute.
The decisive step came in 1475 with the conquest of the Genoese colony of Caffa and the fortresses of Azov. In 1484 Kilia and Akkerman at the mouths of the Danube and Dniester were taken. With the Crimea becoming an Ottoman vassal state under the Giray khans, the entire littoral was under Ottoman suzerainty. The sultan forbade Italian ships from entering the Black Sea, and foreign shipping was closely inspected at Istanbul and Gallipoli.
The Black Sea trade became an internal Ottoman economy: grain, fish, oil, salt, slaves, and timber flowed from the northern coasts to Istanbul, the Aegean, and the Balkans. Ottoman subjects — Armenians (especially those from Caffa), Greeks, Jews, and Muslim Turks — displaced the Italian merchants.
This monopoly held until the late 16th century, when Cossack raids from the Dnieper began to threaten Ottoman shipping. From the 1590s the Cossacks intensified their attacks, burning Sinop in 1614 and Yeniköy on the Bosphorus in 1625. Between 1637 and 1642 they held Azov. The Black Sea, the lifeline of the Ottoman economy, was no longer secure.

8. The Red Sea Fleet and Indian Ocean Expeditions
The Ottoman entry into the Indian Ocean was a response to Portuguese expansion. When the Portuguese destroyed a Mamluk fleet at Chaul in 1509, the Mamluk sultan appealed to the Ottomans, who sent shipbuilding materials and craftsmen to Suez.
After the Ottoman conquest of Egypt (1517), Selim I ordered the construction of a fleet at Suez to drive the Portuguese from the Red Sea and the Indian Ocean. The Ottoman admiral Selmân Reis repulsed Portuguese attacks on Jidda in 1517 and 1525, then advanced on Yemen and Aden.
In 1538 a fleet of thirty ships sailed from Suez against the Portuguese at Diu in northern India. The expedition failed because the local Muslim ruler of Gujarat refused to cooperate, fearing Ottoman domination. Nevertheless, the Ottomans successfully subjugated Yemen and Aden, and made Basra a second base for Indian Ocean operations after 1546.
In 1552 an Ottoman expedition failed to oust the Portuguese from Hormuz. But the empire continued to receive spices directly from India and Indonesia throughout the 16th century. As late as 1554-64 Venetian purchases of twelve thousand quintals of spices annually in Alexandria matched pre-Vasco da Gama levels. Twenty ships laden with spices arrived at Jidda every year.
In 1585, an Ottoman fleet from Suez captured Portuguese factories on the East African coast, and the Prince of Mombasa recognized Ottoman suzerainty. These gains were short-lived: superior Portuguese and Spanish forces drove the Ottomans back, and by the early 17th century the Dutch and English dominated the Indian Ocean with their superior ships.

9. Naval Logistics and Provisioning
The galley fleet required prodigious logistical support. A galley of 150 oarsmen consumed enormous quantities of fresh water, biscuit, and salted provisions for a summer campaign. Naval timber came chiefly from the forests of the Black Sea coast (especially around Sinop) and the mountains of southern Anatolia. Hemp for ropes and sails was produced in the Balkans and Anatolia.
The extraordinary taxes levied to finance the fleet caused widespread discontent. After Lepanto, provincial military forces did everything in their power to avoid participating in naval campaigns. By the late 16th century the empire had, in Inalcik’s words, “exceeded its material capabilities.”
The kürekçi (oarsmen) were a persistent problem. While slaves and prisoners-of-war rowed some galleys, the fleet relied heavily on levend volunteers and provincial levies. The introduction of sail over oar in European warships after Lepanto rendered the Ottoman logistical model obsolete.

10. Decline of the Ottoman Navy in the Late 16th Century
Inalcik identifies several causes for the decline of Ottoman naval power:
Technological stagnation: The Ottoman fleet at Lepanto (1571) was still composed entirely of galleys, ineffective against the tall, broadside-firing ships of the Christian allies. The arrival of the Dutch and the English in the Mediterranean at the end of the 16th century accelerated this disadvantage. One English warship, a contemporary observed, could defeat ten Turkish galleys. The Ottoman navy adopted the new sailing ships only very late and with great difficulty.
Fiscal overstretch: The combined wars with Austria and Safavid Iran (1578-1606) coincided with the naval rebuilding after Lepanto. The extraordinary taxes levied to finance the fleet caused widespread unrest. Provincial timar-holders and sipahis evaded naval service.
Loss of control over North Africa: Naval forces from Tripoli, Tunis, and Algiers ceased to be regular elements of the sultan’s fleet and became independent corsairs. English and Dutch pirates — sometimes cooperating with the Algerian corsairs — began raiding Ottoman merchant fleets.
Cossack raids on the Black Sea: From the 1590s, Cossack fleets of small, fast şayka boats raided the Black Sea coast with impunity. They burned Sinop (1614), Yeniköy on the Bosphorus (1625), and held Azov (1637-42). The Ottoman galley fleet could not effectively engage these agile raiders. The Black Sea, once the secure heartland of the Ottoman economy, was now a zone of insecurity.
By the opening of the 17th century, Ottoman naval supremacy was a memory. The empire could still project power in the eastern Mediterranean, but it could no longer contest the open sea against a determined European coalition.

Key Figures and Admirals















































NameRolePeriodSignificanceHayreddin Barbarossa (Barbaros Hayreddin Paşa)Kapudan-i derya, Beylerbeyi of Algiers1533-1546Defeated Andrea Doria at Preveza (1538); established Ottoman dominance in the MediterraneanTurgut Reis (Dragut)Admiral, Bey of Tripolid. 1565Captured Tripoli; key figure in Mediterranean campaignsPiyale PashaKapudan-i derya1550s-1570sVictor at Djerba (1560); commanded fleet at siege of Malta (1565)Kılıç Ali PashaKapudan-i derya1571-1587Rebuilt fleet after Lepanto; commanded at the reconquest of Tunis (1574)Selmân ReisOttoman admiral, Red Sea1517-1525Repulsed Portuguese attacks on Jidda; advanced on Yemen and AdenGedik Ahmed PashaGrand vizier, admiral1470s-1480Led the Otranto expedition (1480); established Ottoman bridgehead in southern Italy

Key Battles













































BattleDateResultGallipoli (Venetian attack)1416Venetian fleet destroys Ottoman naval forceNavarino1499Ottoman naval victory over VenicePreveza1538Barbarossa defeats Andrea Doria; Ottoman mastery of the MediterraneanDjerba1560Piyale Pasha defeats Spanish/Christian fleetLepanto (İnebahtı)1571Crushing defeat of Ottoman fleet by Holy League; 200 of 230 ships lostDiu (India)1538Ottoman expedition fails to oust PortugueseMombasa1585-89Temporary Ottoman control of East African coast

Ottoman Turkish Terms


















































TermScriptMeaningTersane-i Amireترسانه عامرهImperial ArsenalKapudan-i deryaقپودان پاشاGrand AdmiralKadırgaقاديرغاGalleyBaştardeباشتردهGreat galleyKalyonكاليونGalleon (sailing ship)Tersane emini—Arsenal superintendentCezayir-i Bahr-i Sefid—Islands of the Mediterranean (naval province)Kürekçi—Oarsman

Source
This page is derived from Halil Inalcik, The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age 1300-1600 (London: Phoenix Press, 1973), covering the development of the Ottoman navy, the Imperial Arsenal, naval administration, the Black Sea and Red Sea fleets, and the decline of Ottoman naval power in the late 16th century.

concepts/markets-and-agriculture.title: Ottoman Markets and Agriculture
concepts/markets-and-agriculture.content:
Ottoman Markets and Agriculture
بخش اول: نظام بازار — The Ottoman Market System

The Çarşı (چارشى) and Bedesten (بدستان) System
The physical organization of the Ottoman market followed a pattern that had been established in the Islamic Near East for centuries. At the heart of every major Ottoman city stood the bedesten (بدستان) — a monumental stone building with iron doors, domed roof, and thick walls designed to resist both fire and pillage. Orhan Gazi constructed the first Ottoman bedesten in Bursa in 1340, establishing a trading centre that remains the commercial heart of the city to this day. Mehmed the Conqueror built the great bedesten of Istanbul after 1453, with 118 shops and storerooms inside the central structure and 984 shops in the surrounding markets, forming the nucleus of what is today known as the Covered Market (Kapalıçarşı — قپالى چارشى).
The bedesten served not merely as a marketplace but as a secure repository for valuable goods, money, and jewellery. Its doorkeepers, night-watchmen and brokers operated under direct government control. Around the central bedesten, streets of shops branched out in every direction, each street or quarter occupied exclusively by members of a single craft or by merchants selling the same type of goods. These market streets — the çarşı (چارشى) — were either roofed with stone vaults, as in Istanbul, or remained as lanes of open shops shaded by trees.
Evliya Çelebi, the great seventeenth-century traveller, divided Ottoman cities into two categories: those with a bedesten and those without. The bedesten and its surrounding markets were the economic engine of the Ottoman city, where local and long-distance merchants congregated, valuable goods accumulated, and the commercial life of the city pulsed. All large Ottoman towns in the Balkans — Tatar Pazarjik, Plovdiv, Sarajevo, Sofia, Skopje, Monastir, Serres, Salonica — grew around bedestens.

The Narh (نرخ) System: State Price Controls
The Ottoman state exercised tight control over market prices through the narh (نرخ) system. Prices were not determined by supply and demand but by administrative decree, established through a formal procedure. Prominent members of the community and the ehl-i hibre (اهل خبره — expert inspectors from the guilds) would meet in the presence of the local kadi (قاضى). They established the amount and quality of raw materials, added labour costs, and fixed a market price that allowed a profit of approximately 10 per cent — or 20 per cent for highly skilled labour. Sometimes the sultan himself inspected the registers of Istanbul market prices.
The purpose of the narh system was threefold: to prevent profiteering and exploitation of consumers; to ensure stable prices for essential goods, particularly foodstuffs for Istanbul; and to maintain social and political stability by guaranteeing that the urban population — especially the Janissaries and other kapıkulu troops — could afford basic necessities.
Food prices were fixed at the point of production, not just at the point of sale. Only state-authorized merchants could buy produce, and strict measures were taken to prevent smuggling. Provisions arrived at specified weigh-houses, whence they were distributed to representatives of the trade guilds. Large merchants and ship-owners who were caught smuggling or profiteering had their goods or ships confiscated.

The Muhtesib (محتصب) and İhtisab (احتساب) — Market Inspection
The enforcement of market regulations was entrusted to the muhtesib (محتصب), an officer derived from the old Islamic institution of hisba (حسبه) or ihtisab (احتساب). The Koran commanded that for the good of the Islamic community, the authority should actively support generally accepted social standards and prohibitions. In practice, hisba regulations mainly concerned trade and aimed at preventing fraud and profiteering, fixing fair prices in the markets, and controlling weights and the quality of merchandise.
The muhtesib constantly patrolled the markets to ensure enforcement of these laws. He brought violators before the local kadi, who could order flogging or fines. He stamped certain materials — timber, tile, cloth — according to their standard and prohibited the sale of unstamped materials. The Ottoman state reviewed the ihtisab laws to which the guilds were subject on the accession of each new sultan, indicating the importance attached to this institution.
Merchandise for the city market had to enter through particular gates, was carried through certain streets to specified markets or caravanserais, and was sold under the supervision of officials on duty. Valuable goods had absolutely to be sold through special brokers under government control. After goods had been weighed in the presence of guild representatives and taxed according to quantity, permission was given for their sale.

بخش دوم: نظام زراعت — The Agricultural System

Land Tenure: Miri Arazi, Mülk, and Vakıf
The Ottoman land system in the classical period rested on the principle that all agricultural land belonged to the state. This was miri arazi (ميرى اراضى — crown land), and it constituted approximately 87 per cent of all agricultural land in 1528. The state’s absolute control over land was essential to the functioning of the timar system (تيمار), and the Ottoman government, following the example of earlier Muslim states, announced that all rural agricultural land was miri — the property of the state.
The exceptions were mülk (ملك — freehold property) and vakıf (وقف — pious endowment) lands. Mülk lands were private property, usually granted by the sultan to distinguished individuals. Vakıf lands were endowed in perpetuity for charitable purposes — to support mosques, medreses, hospitals, imarets (soup kitchens), caravanserais, and other public institutions. The deed of endowment (vakfiye — وقفيه) was drawn up before a kadi, entered in his register, and confirmed by the sultan. Once endowed, only God had proprietary rights over a vakıf, so even if governments or states changed, the continuance of the public service was theoretically guaranteed.
These exceptional lands were contested throughout Ottoman history. When the state was weak, the area of land held as mülk or vakıf expanded; when a strong central authority emerged, private property rights and vakıfs were abolished and state control re-established. Bayezid I and especially Mehmed the Conqueror were famous for this type of reform. When Mehmed reviewed all land holdings in about 1470, he established the principle that all vakıfs that had not received the sultan’s sanction, or whose buildings or purpose no longer existed, should revert to the state. In this way over twenty thousand villages and farms became miri lands.

The Çift-Hane (چفت خانه) System: The Peasant Family Farm
The basic unit of agricultural production was the çift-hane (چفت خانه) — the peasant family farm. A çift (چفت) was a unit of agricultural land varying according to place from 60 to 150 dönüms (a dönüm being roughly one-quarter of an acre), a size deemed sufficient to support a single peasant family. The çift was indivisible: on the peasant’s death, his sons worked the holding jointly, but the unit itself could not be broken up.
The peasant who worked a çift had the status of a hereditary tenant. He enjoyed usufructuary rights — the right to work the land and pass it to his sons — but he could not sell it, grant it as a gift, or transfer it without permission. The land belonged ultimately to the state, and through the state to the sipahi (سپاهى — timar-holding cavalryman) who had the right to collect fixed revenues from it. This was what Inalcik called “fragmented possession”: the state, the sipahi, and the peasant all had simultaneous rights over the land.
The peasant paid two main taxes to the sipahi: the tithe (öşür — عشر, one-tenth of the crop, usually collected in kind) and the çift resmi (چفت رسمى), a fixed annual cash tax of twenty-two akçes for a full çift. Christians paid this tax under the name ispence (اسپنجه). The çift resmi was originally in lieu of labour services — the hay, fodder, wood, and services that the peasant had owed the Byzantine fief-holder. The Ottomans commuted these services for fixed cash payments wherever possible.

Taxation of Agriculture: Öşr, Haraç, and Cizye
Agricultural taxation rested on three principal pillars:
Öşr (عشر — tithe): The tithe on agricultural produce was the basic land tax, levied at one-tenth of the crop on Muslim peasants, though rates varied according to district and local custom as recorded in the kanunnames (قانون نامه) of each province. The tithe was usually collected in kind — grain, grapes, livestock — and stored in the sipahi’s barn.
Haraç (خراج): A land tax levied on non-Muslim peasants, technically a different category under Islamic law. The distinction between öşr and haraç was rooted in the şeriat but in practice the Ottoman fiscal system often blended them.
Cizye (جزيه): The poll tax on non-Muslim subjects, collected not by the sipahi but directly by the central Treasury. In the classical period the cizye was a fixed annual payment per adult male, but during the financial crises of the late sixteenth century it was increased four- or fivefold.
In addition to these, the peasant faced a variety of incidental taxes: marriage taxes, fines for minor crimes (half going to the sipahi and half to the sanjak beyi), and the çift bozan akçesi (چفت بوزان آقچهسى — farm-breaker’s tax), a compensation payment owed by a peasant who abandoned his land to enter a craft in a town.
The timar system was characterized by detailed fiscal registration. Immediately after the conquest of a region, and subsequently every twenty or thirty years, a commissioner called the il yazıcısı (ايل يازيجىسى — clerk of the district) was sent to determine the sources of revenue. He recorded the name of every head of family in each village and the approximate amount of land he owned, listing the total sum to be realized from the tithe, the çift resmi, and other taxes. These registers guided the kadi in case of dispute, and the timar was an indivisible and unalterable unit.

The Role of Agriculture in the Ottoman Economy
Agriculture was the foundation of the Ottoman economy. The overwhelming majority of the population — the reaya (رعايا — tax-paying subjects) — were peasants working the land. State revenues depended principally on agricultural taxes: the tithe on grain, the çift resmi, and the cizye. In 1528, state revenues totalled approximately 477 million akçes, of which a substantial portion came from agricultural taxation. About half the state’s income from the provinces was distributed as timars to the sipahi cavalry; the rest went to the central Treasury or was reserved as has (خاص) for the sultan, princes, and high officials.
The agricultural surplus fed the cities, especially Istanbul. The capital’s ovens alone consumed 250 tons of wheat daily in the mid-seventeenth century. Wheat, rice, sugar and spices came from Egypt; livestock, cereals, edible fats, and hides from the Black Sea region; cereals and hides from Thessaly and Macedonia; wine and Mediterranean products from the Morea and the Aegean islands. The Dobrudja became the granary of Istanbul, with hundreds of villages established and state grain-storehouses constructed at the ports.
This provisioning system was not left to the market. The need to feed Istanbul, the Palace, and the army was a major factor determining the rigidly monopolistic character of the Ottoman economy, with strict governmental controls on the trade of necessities. The state regulated everything from the distant producer to the retailer in Istanbul, linking the empire’s various areas of production to a single centre and creating an integrated — if centrally directed — economy.

بخش سوم: بحران و انحطاط — Crisis and Decline

The Price Revolution (نرخ طوفانى) and Its Impact on Agriculture
In the mid-sixteenth century, vast quantities of Mexican and Peruvian silver flooded the European market, causing the great Price Revolution. By the 1580s, European silver coins — especially Spanish reals — inundated the Ottoman market. A contemporary reported that “one of the main items of trade going to Turkey are Spanish reals sent by the chestful.” Within a short time, Ottoman prices doubled.
The impact on agriculture and rural society was devastating. Fixed-income groups — timar-holding sipahis, kapıkulus, and those who derived their incomes from vakıfs — were suddenly impoverished. The akçe was debased repeatedly in panic measures that only worsened the situation. In 1534 the central Treasury’s annual income had reached five million gold ducats, but by 1591 it was worth only half its original value, since most taxes were levied in akçe.
The debasement of the currency and the doubling of prices made it impossible for sipahis to maintain themselves on their timar incomes. The çift resmi, fixed in akçe since the time of Mehmed the Conqueror, lost most of its real value. Sipahis abandoned their timars rather than go on long campaigns that had become too costly. The state, desperate for cash to pay the Janissaries and the newly hired sekban musketeers, brought timar lands under direct Treasury control and farmed out their revenues to tax-farmers.
Depreciation brought in its train counterfeit coin, speculation, soaring interest rates, usury, and profiteering. The financial crisis was compounded by the long war with Austria that began in 1593, which drained the Treasury. The government increased taxes — the cizye was raised four- or fivefold — and converted the extraordinary levies called avarız (عوارض) into regular annual cash taxes. Money taxes became the principal source of state revenue, a development that revolutionized the tax system but placed an enormous burden on the peasantry.

The Celali (جلالى) Rebellions and Rural Devastation
The financial and military crises of the late sixteenth century produced the Celali rebellions (Celali İsyanları — جلالى ايصرانلرى), a period of anarchy and devastation that transformed Anatolia between 1595 and 1610.
To pay the expanding Janissary corps and the new sekban and sarıca infantry equipped with firearms, the state authorized provincial governors (beylerbeyis) to raise local taxes for the enlistment of sekban troops. This encouraged minor commanders to operate independently and plunder the people on their own account. When the state could not pay these troops after a campaign, or discharged them, bands of armed and unemployed men roamed the countryside, collecting provisions and money by force. The situation in Anatolia resembled France after the Hundred Years War, with companies of discharged soldiers pillaging the land.
The government proclaimed them Celalis — rebels against the state. Sipahis whose timars had been confiscated, landless peasants, and nomads seeking plunder joined the rebel forces. Under the leadership of Kara Yazıcı (قاره يازيجى), the rebel army numbered some twenty thousand men by 1598. After Kara Yazıcı’s defeat in 1602, the brigands dispersed throughout Anatolia. Between 1595 and 1610, these armed bands wrought havoc across the Anatolian countryside.
The consequences for agriculture were catastrophic:

Flight and migration: The richer Anatolians fled to the Balkans, the Crimea, Iran, and the Arab lands. Peasants abandoned their villages en masse.
Abandoned land: Fields lay fallow, and the area under cultivation shrank dramatically.
Famine: Hunger and famine followed the disruption of agricultural production.
Revenue collapse: The Treasury lost its rural sources of revenue as tax-paying peasants disappeared.
Permanent transformation: Janissaries were stationed in the provincial towns and cities, where they joined the ulema, guild masters, and merchants as a new influential class. They acquired large tracts of state lands by extortionate tax-farming, reducing the villagers on these lands to the status of sharecroppers.

The Celali period came at a time of great financial crisis and dragged the empire into a decline from which it never fully recovered. In 1607 the English ambassador wrote from Istanbul: “As far as I can see, the Turkish Empire was in great decline, almost ruined.”

The Decline of the Timar System and Its Impact on Agricultural Production
The timar system (تيمار) had been the backbone of both Ottoman military organization and agricultural administration. In the classical period, the timar-holding sipahi lived in the village of his timar, enforced the state’s land laws, maintained order, and collected the prescribed taxes in accordance with the kanunname. The peasant knew exactly what taxes were due, the sipahi had a secure fixed income, and the state had a cavalry army that cost it nothing in cash. This system began to disintegrate in the late sixteenth century.
Several factors drove the decline:
Military obsolescence: The timar-holding cavalry, armed with bow, sword, shield, and lance, was a mediaeval army whose day passed when it encountered German infantry equipped with firearms. The sipahis considered firearms unbecoming to their chivalric sense; the state therefore expanded the Janissary corps (from 16,000 in Suleyman’s time to 37,000 by 1609) while the number of sipahis collapsed — from at least 87,000 in Suleyman’s reign to 45,000 by 1609, and only about 8,000 by 1630 according to Koçi Bey.
Financial crisis: The Price Revolution wiped out the real value of fixed cash incomes from timars. Sipahis found they could no longer afford to campaign; they abandoned their timars or sold their rights.
Corruption and usurpation: Many timars were acquired by courtiers and converted into private property or vakıfs. Others were granted to the reaya in return for bribes. As the system of fragmented possession broke down, the state’s control over land weakened.
Tax-farming (iltizam — التزام): To raise cash, the state brought timar lands under direct Treasury control and farmed out their revenues to tax-farmers (mültezim — ملتزم). Unlike the sipahi, who had a long-term interest in the productivity of his timar, the tax-farmer was interested only in maximum short-term extraction. This shift devastated the peasantry.
Flight from the land: As taxes increased, security collapsed during the Celali period, and the economic position of agriculture deteriorated, peasants fled the land in ever greater numbers, seeking refuge in cities, joining bandit bands, or migrating out of Anatolia entirely.
The result was a profound transformation of Ottoman rural society. The small, independent peasant family farm — the çift-hane — gave way to large estates (çiftliks — چفتلك) owned by absentee landlords and worked by sharecroppers. The disciplined, state-regulated timar system was replaced by a chaotic and oppressive regime of tax-farming and local strongmen. The productivity of Ottoman agriculture declined, the state’s tax base shrank, and the empire entered a long period of economic and demographic stagnation.

Summary
Inalcik’s analysis of Ottoman markets and agriculture reveals a system that was highly organized, tightly controlled by the state, and remarkably stable during the classical period (1300–1580). The market system — with its bedesten-centred bazaars, its narh price controls, its muhtesib inspectors, and its guild-based distribution — ensured the provisioning of cities and the maintenance of social order.
