The agricultural system — with its state ownership of land (miri arazi), its çift-hane peasant farms, its detailed fiscal registration, and its timar-based extraction of revenue — provided the economic foundation for the empire’s military and political power.
The crisis of the late sixteenth century — driven by the Price Revolution, military change, currency debasement, and the Celali rebellions — shattered this system. The narh controls could not contain inflation, the timars collapsed under fiscal pressure, the peasants fled the land, and the agricultural economy was transformed from a state-regulated system of small family farms into a regime of tax-farming, latifundia, and rural exploitation. The Ottoman Empire never fully recovered the economic dynamism of its classical age.

concepts/mehmeds-building-program.title: Mehmed II's Building Program
concepts/mehmeds-building-program.content:
Mehmed II’s Building Program
The Vision for a New Capital
When Mehmed II entered Constantinople on the morning of May 29, 1453, he found a city gutted by war, its population shattered, its aqueducts broken, its churches looted, and its great houses standing empty. Kritobulos writes that the sultan “viewed its size and situation, the magnificent and beautiful churches, the many large and fine public buildings… the situation of the harbor and shipyards” and then “saw the vastness of the damage, the desolation of the houses, and the utter ruin and destruction” — and his soul ached for it. Yet almost at once, he resolved to make the city not merely habitable again but the greatest capital in the world: “so that as a city favorably situated on land and sea, it might be his imperial residence.”
Tursun Beg captures the same moment from the Ottoman perspective: “He looked upon Istanbul with contemplation. He saw clearly that its water, air, mountains, roads and plains were very beautiful. But because it was not filled with trustworthy people and had not been adorned with the Islamic faith, it remained tangled and disheveled like the locks of a delicate beauty.” And then: “A love arose in the Sultan’s heart — to make it his throne.”
The building program that followed was not a single campaign but a sustained effort spanning decades, carried out in distinct phases — immediate repopulation after the conquest, a second wave of settlement and construction around 1458–1459, and ongoing public works that continued through the 1460s. It encompassed every dimension of urban life: water supply, commerce, worship, defense, governance, and habitation.
Repopulation of the City
The most urgent problem was population. The city had been depopulated long before the conquest — Kritobulos notes Constantine XI’s speech in which he admits the city is “almost entirely depleted of inhabitants” — and the sack had scattered or enslaved the remaining population.
Mehmed’s first act, recorded by Kritobulos in Book 1, was to settle the captives he had received in the distribution “together with their wives and children, on the seashore of the city by the harbor, as seamen.” He then proclaimed that any captive who could pay his ransom might live in the city, granting them tax exemption and houses.
The second phase, described in Book 2 (1455), was more systematic. From the Peloponnesians he had brought to the city, he selected “all those who seemed more distinguished or who knew some craft” and settled them in Constantinople itself, while the rest were quartered in the surrounding villages with grain, oxen, and implements. He sent to Amastris on the Black Sea and “transplanted from its inhabitants the greater and wealthier portion.” From the Armenians under his rule, he brought “the wealthiest and those outstanding in skill, wealth, craftsmanship, and other knowledge, including even the merchant class.”
The third and largest phase came in 1458–1459 (Book 5). Mehmed issued proclamations throughout Asia and Europe commanding “that all those who had gone from Constantinople, whether as captives, or by emigration, or by any other means” were to be brought back. Many were found in Adrianople, Philippopolis, Callipolis, and Bursa — “intelligent and very useful people” who had grown wealthy in exile but were now “transplanted here, giving some houses, others sites to build on.” He also moved the entire population of both Phocaea towns in Asian Ionia, and sent Zaganos with forty ships to Thasos and Samothrace to bring their inhabitants.
Tursun Beg, who personally helped register the arriving settlers, confirms: “The task of settling the people flocking to Istanbul from all around and of registering the houses and shops in the city was given to his uncle Hacı Ali Bey.” He adds that Mehmed brought “those captured in war from the infidel lands he had conquered by the sword and settled them around Istanbul. He created villages and agricultural fields. Such that no empty place remained, and every part became lively.”
Kritobulos sums up the spirit of this effort: “Such special love had taken hold of his soul for the city and for its repopulation and restoration to its former happy condition.”
Restoration of the Roman Aqueducts
One of the great technical achievements of the building program was the restoration of the city’s water supply. The Roman aqueducts, particularly the Valens aqueduct (Bozdoğan Kemeri), had fallen into ruin during the Byzantine decline.
Tursun Beg gives the fullest account: “It turned out that in the former prosperous age of Istanbul, water had been brought from a distance of six or seven days’ journey. Old aqueducts were found, which had pierced through mountains and, via arches and bridges over valleys suitable to the terrain, had conveyed rivers. But all these works had been ruined through neglect and the forces of nature.”
Mehmed “had learned engineers and masters brought in, and repaired and renewed the collapsed arches and lost channels. In this process many springs were discovered along the routes and channeled into the system, and the waters of all the plateaus were brought to the city in a rushing river.” The water was then “distributed to the palaces, bathhouses, and neighborhoods.” At one suitable place, Tursun adds, “he had forty fountains built on one arch.”
Kritobulos confirms this more briefly in Book 2: the sultan “had abundant water brought into the city through aqueducts.”
The Grand Bazaar
Mehmed’s commercial centerpiece was the covered bazaar, described by Kritobulos in detail: “He also had a very large and very beautiful bazaar built in the middle of the city, near the royal palace, which was fortified on the outside with a very strong stone wall, while inside it was divided into sections by very beautiful and large halls, and its roof was made of fired tile and translucent stone (porcelain).”
This structure — the original Bedesten (the Cevahir Bedesten, or Old Bedesten) — became the nucleus of what would grow into the Grand Bazaar. Tursun Beg adds that Mehmed “had great bedestens, marketplaces, bazaars and caravanserais built in the city.” The bazaar was not merely a commercial space but a fortified stone structure that anchored the city’s economic life.
The Fatih Mosque Complex
On the highest hill of the old city, the site of the Church of the Holy Apostles, Mehmed built his great imperial mosque, known today as the Fatih Mosque. Kritobulos describes the ambition: “selecting the most beautiful and most elevated site in the center of the city, commanded that a church be erected there which should vie in height, beauty, and size with the largest and most beautiful churches ever built in the city.” He ordered an immense quantity of marble, precious stones, pillars, and materials “as much iron, copper, lead, and every other necessary material in great abundance.”
Tursun Beg’s description is more detailed: “At that place he built a great mosque in the majestic manner of Hagia Sophia, which incorporated all the art of the Hagia Sophia Mosque, having more and lacking nothing.” He adds that Mehmed “adorned the mosque’s gallery by appointing teachers of beautiful voice and knowledge, hafizes, and muezzins.”
Around the mosque, Mehmed built eight madrasas — the Sahn-ı Seman (Eight Courtyards) — “in these madrasas were taught the fine arts, tafsir, hadith and usul, the rational and traditional sciences.” Beside them, hospitals were built “for rich and poor alike,” and “on one side he also had a great imaret constructed, providing two meals daily to the madrasa scholars, the poor, and the local residents.”
Kritobulos describes how the sultan himself supervised the work: “He himself carefully supervised his own buildings — namely, the church and the imperial palace — diligently assembling not only all the immense materials necessary for the construction, but also very costly, precious, and very beautiful materials, and summoning from everywhere the most excellent craftsmen.”
Grand vizier Mahmud Pasha built his own mosque complex alongside the sultan’s, described as “a vast and wonderfully beautiful church erected on an elevated site in the city, which gleamed with bright stones and marbles, boasted beautiful great columns, was finely adorned with paintings and sculptures, and glittered with much gold and silver.” Around it he built “hospices, inns, and baths very handsomely.”
The Old Palace and Topkapı Foundations
The first palace Mehmed built after the conquest was the Old Palace (Eski Saray), on the site of what is now Istanbul University. Kritobulos records in Book 1 that immediately after the conquest, Mehmed “selects the most beautiful site in the middle of the city, to build an imperial palace there.” This was the Old Palace, begun in 1454 on the Forum Tauri.
Later, in Book 5, Kritobulos describes the decision to build a second palace: “Moreover, he commanded that an imperial palace also be built on the promontory of ancient Byzantium projecting into the sea, which should be in every respect superior and more admirable than previous ones in form, size, magnificence, and gracefulness.” This was the foundation of what would become the Topkapı Palace.
Tursun Beg’s account of the Topkapı foundation is more colorful. After the initial repair works, he says, Mehmed “commanded a new palace to be built for his own sultanate and residence” on a site “opposite Galata, at the corner of the Castle of Constantinople,” a place that “overlooks the tomb of the blessed Ayyub al-Ansari, the wharf, Tophane, and the whole of the Golden Horn.” He “brought skilled architects and engineers from the Arabs, Persians and Rum” and built “a lofty palace” adorned with fine works, surrounded by a wall “with towers in Turkish and Frankish style, triangular and circular,” and turned the space between the walls into “vineyards, gardens, orchards and rose-gardens” with “fountains, pools and conversational pavilions.”
Public Baths
Kritobulos makes clear that bath-building was part of the program: “he also had magnificent and beautiful baths built.” He commanded his chief officers “to erect baths, inns, bazaars, and as many and as beautiful workshops as possible.” Mahmud Pasha’s complex, too, included baths.
Tursun Beg confirms that the water brought by the restored aqueducts was distributed to “bathhouses” among other destinations, and notes that in the Eyüb Sultan complex, a bathhouse was built.
The Eyüb Sultan Complex
Perhaps the most symbolically charged of all Mehmed’s constructions was the mausoleum and mosque complex at Eyüb, outside the land walls on the Golden Horn. Tursun Beg records that Mehmed, “outside the walls of Istanbul, over the head of the chief of martyrs, the blessed Abu Ayyub al-Ansari… had a very beautiful tomb built, and beside it a madrasa and a bathhouse.” Abu Ayyub al-Ansari was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who had died during the first Arab siege of Constantinople in the 7th century; the discovery of his tomb at the time of the conquest was taken as a divine sign.
Tursun adds: “Because of these, the people flocked to that place and built houses and mansions around the tomb. A pleasant town came into being, to which those seeking peace and rest come by boat from the sea and by horse and on foot from the land, conversing and visiting.”
Shipyards on the Golden Horn
Mehmed understood that sea power was essential to holding his new capital. Kritobulos writes that he ordered “the construction of very many and very beautiful shipyards for the accommodation of ships and naval equipment, and the building of very strong and large houses for the storage of weapons, artillery, and other such things.” Beyond the Golden Horn shipyards, he “ordered warships to be built everywhere throughout his entire empire, knowing that naval power and dominion over the sea were necessary for him.”
In his speech before the final assault on Constantinople (Book 1), Mehmed had already made the strategic case for naval supremacy: “land and sea united under one empire would soon make it very great.” The shipyards on the Golden Horn — expanding on the existing Genoese arsenal at Galata — became the heart of the Ottoman navy.
The Dardanelles Fortresses: Kilitbahir and Çimenlik
To secure the sea approaches to his new capital, Mehmed built twin fortresses at the narrowest point of the Dardanelles. Tursun Beg records: “On the Mediterranean side, below Gallipoli, in a narrow strait called Eceovası, on both sides of the water he built two facing castles. He named one Kilitbahir [Lock of the Sea] and the other Sultaniye [later known as Çimenlik]. In the manner of Boğazkesen [Rumeli Hisarı], he installed awe-inspiring cannons on these as well, such that they would not let an unauthorized bird fly from the Mediterranean side.”
Kritobulos, in his dedicatory letter, refers to these as “the very useful and necessary guard posts and watchtowers at the Hellespont” — part of a broader system of fortifications that also included towers at the harbor of the Chersonese and at the Bosporus. Tursun adds: “Thus the routes by which the enemy might come from either side were closed, and Istanbul became a land of safety.”
He also records that Mehmed “additionally, opposite the mouth of the harbor of Istanbul, on the Anatolian side, among the rubble stones in the sea, had a strong castle built and cannons installed.”
The Palace at Edirne
Even before the conquest of Constantinople, Mehmed had built a magnificent palace at Edirne (Adrianople), the old Ottoman capital. Kritobulos describes it in Book 1: “Near Adrianople, right on the bank of the River Hebrus opposite the city, he built a magnificent imperial palace, which was adorned with lustrous stones and translucent marbles, richly gilded and silvered inside and out, and furthermore embellished with sculptural and painted works and various other precious works made with great care and skill. And around it he planted gardens adorned with all kinds of plants and cultivated trees, and with early-bearing fruit trees.”
This palace — known as the Edirne Palace or Saray-ı Cedid-i Amire — served as Mehmed’s residence when he was not in Constantinople, particularly during the summer, and hosted many of the major councils and diplomatic receptions described by Kritobulos.
Roads, Bridges, and Urban Infrastructure
Beyond the major monuments, Mehmed attended to the practical infrastructure of the city. Kritobulos in Book 2 describes his orders to restore “the bridges over the gulf of Athyra and Rhegion, which had been damaged by time and had collapsed,” and “the other roads and the bridges leading to the city… wherever they were dangerous and difficult to travel, by leveling them, paving them with hewn stones and covering them with gravel.” He also built inns and hostels along these roads for travelers.
Tursun Beg notes that “narrow streets were turned into wide thoroughfares” and that “many more charitable works came into being, all of which cannot be enumerated here.”
Mahmud Pasha’s Contribution
Kritobulos gives special attention to the building activities of Mahmud Pasha, the grand vizier and governor of Europe, who “had a vast and wonderfully beautiful church erected on an elevated site in the city,” surrounded by hospices, inns, baths, “splendid and costly houses” with gardens, and “had abundant water piped into them.” Mahmud was following “the supreme will of the ruler, and at the same time himself adorning the city according to his ability.”
Tursun Beg likewise notes that the Pasha “commanded his emirs and viziers not to lag behind in the repair and restoration of the city. They, too, built madrasas, mosques and imarets.”
The Transformation of the City
By the time Tursun Beg was writing, the transformation was complete: “Within a few years the city became so prosperous, adorned, and orderly that: Every corner is Paradise, every garden is Iram, every fountain is Kawsar, every river is honey.” And in lines that capture the spirit of Mehmed’s enterprise:

“Look at the monuments — what remains from those who have passed.
If good, they are praised; if bad, they are blamed.
Strive for good, leave a work as far as your power reaches,
So that it may live; your name [remains] forever with the Eternal.”

Mehmed’s building program was not merely a reconstruction of a damaged city but a deliberate act of urban creation — transforming a depopulated Byzantine remnant into the capital of a new world empire, one that could rival Baghdad, Cairo, and the great cities of the past. Kritobulos captures this ambition in his dedication: Mehmed was determined “to make the city in every respect the most flourishing and most powerful, such as it once was in power, wealth, glory, sciences, crafts, and all other fine arts and masterpieces, public and private buildings and monuments.”

concepts/military-organization.title: Ottoman Military Organization Under Suleiman the Magnificent
concepts/military-organization.content:
Ottoman Military Organization Under Suleiman the Magnificent
The Military Aspect of the Ruling Institution
The Ottoman government had been an army before it was anything else. Like the Turkish nations of the steppe lands, the Ottoman nation was “born of war and organized for conquest.” Fighting was originally the first business of the state and governing the second. After the capture of Constantinople the necessity of administering immense territories transferred the preponderance to the governmental aspect, but even in Suleiman’s time the two great functions of the Ruling Institution were very closely united. War carried practically the whole government into the field. So completely was the government an army that the more important judges, who did not belong directly to the Ruling Institution, were taken into the field. Suleiman on his last campaign had 48,316 men under pay. Acceptance of the sultan’s pay signified that the recipient was a kul (قول, slave of the sultan). Army and government were one: war was the external purpose, government the internal purpose, of one institution composed of one body of men.
The Janissary Corps
The body of regular infantry known as Yeniçeri (يڭیچرى, “new troops”), which the West called Janissaries, stood in the Western imagination for the sultan’s entire slave-family, though in the sixteenth century it formed not more than a fourth of the whole number. Its members were physically trained beyond comparison with their intellectual education, kept in poverty and hence comparatively irresponsible, and in time of peace largely idle — liable to act as an organized and dangerous mob. Yet in battle they drew up an invincible line behind which the person of their sovereign was safe; their devotion to him was the greater because they were in a special sense his kullar and because he was inscribed in one of their odas and received his pay regularly.
Size and Organization
The Janissaries under Suleiman numbered between twelve and fourteen thousand. They were organized in messes of ten; ten messes constituted an orta or oda (اوطه, chamber), of which there were 165 in Suleiman’s time. Each orta had its own officers promoted from its ranks, and above all the ortas was a graded set of officers under the Agha of the Janissaries (Yeniçeri Ağası, يڭیچری آغاسی). This official had never been a Janissary himself but came through the colleges of pages. He acted as a sort of minister of war for the corps, aided by his Kiaya (lieutenant), his chief Yaziji (scribe), and a bureau of clerks who directed enrolment, pay distribution, promotions, location, supply, and clothing. The Agha was well paid and of great authority, outranking all other generals, though occasionally obliged to yield precedence to two generals of cavalry whose corps were older.
Pay and Privileges
Each Janissary received from three to eight aspers per day. They were clothed once a year by the sultan in coarse blue cloth. They had their residence in two great barracks in Constantinople; married men lived at home in various parts of the city. They contributed a daily sum to a common fund for living expenses, with a steward and a cook providing necessities. Every hundred men transported a tent in the field. They fought on foot as musketeers, halberdiers, or scimitar-wielders. Every three men led a horse to carry clothing. In old age or when service no longer pleased the sultan, they were stricken from the roll and sent as hissarlis (castle guards), while deposed officers were sent as castellans with a feudal grant equal to their previous pay.
They were punishable only by their own officers; not even the grand vizier had direct jurisdiction over them. They were immune from taxation, and they were constantly drained of their ablest men by promotion to the regular cavalry. One hundred and fifty of their best bowmen were detailed as Solaks (solak, صولاق), the sultan’s foot-guards who marched beside him wherever he went.
Discipline
The Janissaries kept marvellous order in camp and were completely obedient to their officers except at crises. They had a strong sense of maintaining their privileges. Busbecq records how the grand vizier Rustem warned him that in war time the Janissaries were “masters, so that not even Solyman himself had control over them, and was actually himself afraid of receiving violence at their hands.” Suleiman succeeded on the whole in keeping them in hand, and they never revolted against him.
The Sipahis of the Porte
The regular cavalry were all included under the general name of Sipahis (sipahi, سپاهی, horsemen), but the name was also applied to one of the four divisions into which Osman’s corps of riders had been organized after the model of the cavalry of the Caliph Omar I. Their organization was older than that of the Janissaries. The members were not organized into a single body, they had high pay, and they were in the presence of excellent opportunities to acquire wealth and rise with rapidity; they appear never to have caused Suleiman any special trouble.
The Six Divisions
The four corps — divided into six regiments — were:

Spahis (Spahi-Oghlan, سپاهی اوغلان) — most honored and best paid; each had to bring five or six armed slaves on horseback.
Silihdars (silahdar, سلحدار, weapon-bearers) — less pay than Spahis; each brought four or five horsemen.
Ulufajis (ulufeci, علوفه‌جی, paid troops) — in two divisions, left and right; each furnished two or three horsemen. Recruited from pages, Janissaries, and occasional promotions from irregular troops.
Ghurebas (gureba, غربا, Foreign Legion) — in two divisions, left and right; least pay and small honor; members came alone, often not even as Ottomans.

Each corps and each division was organized separately with its own general who supervised all its affairs. The actual members of the four corps counted from ten to twelve thousand men, but since most had to bring from two to six additional horsemen, the total force assembled was from forty to fifty thousand.
Battle Formation
In time of battle the regular troops drew up to protect the sultan: the Janissaries were aligned in front, the Spahis proper on the right, the Silihdars on the left, and the Ulufajis and Ghurebas in the rear.
The Feudal Sipahis
Outside the towns, the greater part of the European dominions and a large part of Asia Minor were granted in fief to Moslems who were for the most part not kullar of the sultan. They collected the revenues and exercised seigniorial jurisdiction in their estates, and were officered by the sultan’s kullar.
Land Tenure: Timars, Ziamets, and Khasses
The estates were of three classes:

Timars (تیمار) — yearly revenue under twenty thousand aspers
Ziamets (زیامت) — yearly revenue from twenty to one hundred thousand aspers
Khasses (خاص) — yearly revenue over one hundred thousand aspers

Every fief-holder must appear in person when summoned to war. A Timarji with income reaching six thousand aspers must bring an armed horseman, and another for each additional three thousand aspers. Holders of larger fiefs began at ten thousand aspers and added a horseman for each additional five thousand. In the sixteenth century this service was strictly exacted and the fief-holders were held to residence on their estates.
Heredity and Central Control
One son of a small fief-holder had a right to the fief; not more than three sons of a large fief-holder were entitled to small fiefs. The sons of kullar in high position might receive fiefs large in proportion to the rank of their father, by which they were honorably conveyed from the Ruling Institution into the Moslem population.
In 1530 Suleiman issued a Kanun attaching the granting of all fiefs above a certain size to the central government. Each holder of such a fief must obtain a teskereh (document) from Constantinople to have good title. Only those holding by teskereh were entitled to be called Spahis; others were known as Timarjis or Timariotes. The feudal Spahis of Anatolia were more under the authority of the governor, less well paid, and not so highly esteemed as those of Europe. Through this system the country gentry were kept under good control, the accumulation of estates was prevented, and the territorial army was automatically increased in proportion to the increase of the empire by the granting of new fiefs in newly-conquered lands.
Officers of the Feudal Sipahis
Local government and the command of the feudal Sipahis was cared for by officials who belonged to the sultan’s slave-family:

Subashis (subası, subaşı, captains) — in peace, governors of towns with Janissaries and Azabs for police
Alai Beys (alay beyi, آلای بكی, colonels) — ready with two to five hundred troops to move as needed
Sanjak Beys (sancak beyi, سنجاق بكی) — governed important cities and districts
Beylerbeys (بكلربكی, lords of lords) — supreme over all Beys of a region; the Beylerbey of Rumelia resided in Constantinople in peace; the Beylerbey of Anatolia spent much time in his dominions

The enrolled feudal troops of Europe numbered about fifty thousand, and those of Asia (including Anatolia, Karamania, Amasia, and Avandole) thirty thousand. These numbers should be doubled or tripled to allow for the additional horsemen all Spahis were required to bring. About half remained on duty at home in time of war. The feudal troops were brave and eager but lacked the physical strength and practice of fighting in squadrons of the regular troops.
Auxiliary Troops
Azabs (azab, عزب)
Irregular infantry, numbering perhaps ten thousand in peace and forty thousand in war. Some served in garrisons and some with the fleet. In siege and battle they were sent forward to break the enemy’s charge or to fill moats with their own bodies. Survivors were rewarded generously; the rest were believed to pass at once by a martyr’s death to heavenly reward.
Akıncıs (akıncı, آقنجی)
Irregular cavalry numbering perhaps thirty thousand in peace, mainly near the European frontier where they made a living by raiding. They received no pay in peace or war but gathered booty and slaves and hoped for promotion. With the Azabs, their numbers were greatly augmented in war by volunteers, many of whom were criminals and ruffians. These irregular troops were the terror of invaded lands, carrying fire, rapine, and sword over wide areas, while the regular army was held under iron discipline.
Topçus (topçu, طوپجی, artillerymen)
Under the Topçu-başı (طوپجی باشی, chief of artillery), who received seventy aspers per day with a Kiaya and secretary. About two thousand Topçus served under him with six to ten aspers per day, all on foot. They managed the empire’s artillery train.
Cebecis (cebeci, جبه‌جی, armorers)
Under the Cebeci-başı (جبه‌جی باشی, chief armorer), who received sixty aspers per day with a Kiaya and secretary. About fifteen hundred Cebecis served under him with seven to twelve aspers per day, all on foot when the sultan took the field. They maintained the army’s weapons and armor.
Müteferrika (متفرفه, Noble Guard)
A body of from one to two hundred of the choicest graduates from the page schools and sons of high officials. Among them in 1575 were brothers of the Voivodes of Wallachia and Moldavia. The Müteferrika followed immediately after the sultan on horseback and in time of battle were ready to defend him to the end.
Other Corps
The army also included Arabacıs (عرباجی, wagoners) — three thousand under an Arabacı-başı; musicians and trumpeters under a Mihter-başı (مهتر باشی); transport personnel; commissaries; and tribute contingents from the Crimean Tartars, Moldavians, and Wallachians. The Deli (دلی, “crazy”) company of scouts served as advance skirmishers.
Weaponry
The Janissaries carried muskets, halberds, and scimitars. The Spahis of the Porte were armed with bows and painted arrows, light spears (painted green), jewelled scimitars, steel maces, and shields (targes) proof against arrows. The Silihdars were the weapon-bearers. The cavalry wore distinctive headdresses with fluted peaks of fine purple silk, often adorned with black plumes. On campaign the Janissaries alone among the regular troops carried firearms — their primary weapon being the musket — while the rest of the army relied on bows, spears, scimitars, and maces. The artillery corps (Topçus) operated the sultan’s cannon and siege train, in which the Ottomans were recognized masters of their age.
Discipline and Ardor
Contemporary observers were strongly impressed with the wonderful discipline and intense zeal for fighting seen among the Ottomans. The silence, order, and cleanliness of the camps, absolute obedience enforced by severe punishments, submissiveness to long marches, hard labor, and scanty food, the eagerness for battle and recklessness of life — these presented a perfection of discipline that seemed miraculous.

“The Turks come together for war as though they had been invited to a wedding.”
“The Great Turk is the best obeyed by his subjects of all the lords that I know.”
“Their military discipline has such justice and severity as easily to surpass the ancient Greeks and Romans.”

The discipline was summed up by one observer: “They keep the divinest order in the world.”
The Supreme Command
The sultan was commander-in-chief of the entire army — standing, feudal, and irregular. When the army was assembled for a great campaign, it gathered about him. On the march and in camp every body had its place with reference to him; in formation of battle he was the central point about which the whole vast display was organized. His kullar gathered about him in devotion of body and soul, going forth under his leadership against the infidel or the heretic.
In 1529 Suleiman issued to Ibrahim a commission as Seraskier (سرعسكر, general of the army) which placed the entire Ruling Institution, the Moslem Institution, the Ottoman nation, and all subject nations under his command. Four years later Ibrahim added the title of Sultan to that of Seraskier in issuing his daily orders. Suleiman, realizing the empire could have but one head, executed Ibrahim. He appointed no more Seraskiers with such exalted powers, but himself led the army when assembled as a whole. The campaign of Szigeth was the thirteenth he directed in person.
The Indivisibility of the Army
The essential oneness of the army, based on the sultan’s ownership of the standing body and its attachment to his person, was a fact that ultimately set bounds to Ottoman conquest. The empire, bordered by Persia on the east and Austria on the west, had only one great army and could conduct only one serious war at a time. If war was in progress on one frontier and conditions became intolerable on the other, peace had to be made on whatever terms could be had. As Lybyer concludes: “The sultan had but one arm; it was a long arm and a strong one, yet it could reach only a fixed distance, and it could strike but one blow.”
Relationship Between Military Service and Land Tenure
The timar system represented the most systematic link between land tenure and military obligation. The empire’s conquered territories were parceled into fiefs (timars, ziamets, khasses), each granting the holder the right to collect revenues from the lands and peasants in exchange for military service. This was not feudal in the Western sense — the fiefs were not hereditary fiefdoms but conditional grants revocable by the sultan, and they were administered by the sultan’s own kullar as officers. The fief-holders (Timariotes and Zaims) constituted a class of country gentry described by Lybyer as “honest, sober, true to the Moslem faith and to the sultan, better in morals than the kullar if not so able of intellect, the substantial middle class of the empire.” Through this mechanism the army was fed, the countryside governed, and the tax revenues collected, all in a single integrated system. The devşirme (child levy) system that supplied the kapıkulu corps was the other great pillar connecting service to the state with the state’s claim on persons — just as timars represented the state’s claim on land.

concepts/moslem-institution-ulema.title: The Moslem Institution (Ulema) of the Ottoman Empire
concepts/moslem-institution-ulema.content:
The Moslem Institution (Ulema) of the Ottoman Empire
Definition and Central Thesis
Lybyer’s second great pillar of the Ottoman state was the Moslem Institution — the religious, legal, and educational hierarchy based upon and inspired by the Mohammedan religion. It stood parallel and complementary to the Ruling Institution. Whereas the Ruling Institution was the engine of conquest, administration, and war — recruited from Christian-born slaves — the Moslem Institution was rooted in the free-born Moslem population and encompassed the ulema (learned men), judges, jurists, teachers, clergy, and the entire apparatus of Islamic law, education, and worship.

“The structure of the Moslem Institution of the Ottoman Empire … was and remains to the present time wholly different from that of any of the Christian ecclesiastical organizations. As a mere church it claimed far less place and influence than they do, but in other aspects it reached out far more widely.”
— Lybyer, ch. VII

The Moslem Institution included all those Mohammedans in the Ottoman Empire, outside of the Ruling Institution, who were lifted above the level of the ordinary believer. What united them was a special relationship to the Mohammedan religion — sometimes based on birth or piety, but usually established by intellectual training in the Book and the Law of Islam. The sultan was the head of this institution, just as he was head of the Ruling Institution; the two met at the apex and interacted at every level.
The fundamental difference: members of the Ruling Institution were drawn almost exclusively from Christian families, while members of the Moslem Institution came even more exclusively from Mohammedan families. Between them arose a rivalry of tendency and influence that was to become extremely harmful to the Ottoman state.

Financial Support: The Vakıf (وقف) System
A vast proportion of Ottoman land — perhaps one-third — was set aside as vakf (وقف, religious endowment). Much was endowed by sultans; in such cases the imperial treasury could use none of the revenue. Other parcels were endowed by private individuals, the treasury receiving its customary revenues as before while the surplus was devoted to purposes specified by the donor.
Each tract was assigned to a particular object, with a Muteveli (مُتَوَلّى, administrator) and a Nazir (ناظر, inspector) appointed to manage it. High officials such as the grand vizier, the Mufti, the Kapu Agha (قپو آغاسی), or the Kizlar Agha (قزلر آغاسی) were often put in charge ex officio. In time, these dignitaries organized the properties under their charge by holding private Divans and appointing Mufettishes (مُفَتِّش, special judges) with staffs of subordinates and travelling inspectors.
Three classes of vakıf:

Regular vakıfs of mosques — supported mosques and their associated institutions
Charitable vakıfs — endowments for schools, libraries, hospitals, bridges, fountains, caravanserais, public baths, and dervish convents
Customary vakıfs of mosques — investments of mosque funds, governed by Kanun (قانون) rather than Sheri (شرع)

The chief material unit was the mosque (جامع, cami). Each great mosque was a large house of worship surrounded by smaller institutions — colleges, law schools, hospitals, insane asylums, and soup-kitchens. The income from its vakıfs supported all of these. The revenue of Aya Sofia (آیا صوفیا) alone was estimated at two hundred thousand ducats a year.
Many of the sultan’s kullar (قول, slaves) provided inheritances for their sons by setting apart lands in vakıf, with the desired persons as administrators who would retain a portion of the income. The vakıfs as a whole supported all official members of the Moslem Institution, except that judges derived much of their income from fees and fines.
The Ulema (عُلَما, learned men) possessed the noble privilege of immunity from taxation. Their property was not subject to confiscation and, since they were not kullar, passed by inheritance to their relatives. These privileges gave the learned class the prestige of nobility and great financial advantage.

The Educational System
Like the Ruling Institution, the Moslem Institution contained an educational system that was of its essential structure. From the time of Mohammed II, the great majority of members — all who expected promotion — were required to pass through it, bearing collectively the name Ulema (عُلَما, learned men).
Three grades of schools, supported by vakıfs and attached to mosques:

Mektebs (مكتب, primary schools) — called okumak-yerleri (اوقوماق ییرلری, “reading-places”) in the 16th century — taught Arabic reading and writing and the Koran
Medreses (مدرسه, ordinary colleges) — gave a course of ten studies resembling the Seven Liberal Arts of the West
Higher medreses (law schools) — of university grade, teaching the sciences connected with the Koran and the Sheri (شرع, Sacred Law), including both law and theology

Education was free where primary schools existed; some students were even fed and lodged. Students in medreses were partially supported, and those in law schools received a sufficiency. The system probably gave better opportunity for education to Moslem boys in the 16th century than was afforded to Christian children in any land until a much later date. However, its conservatism — no change in methods or subjects from century to century — was eventually to become harmful.
Medreses were very numerous. The mosque of the Conqueror (Fatih, فاتح) had eight; that of Suleiman (Süleymaniye, سلیمانیه) had five. Suleiman set the gradations in their final form.
Stages of advancement:

Softa or Sukhta (سوخته, “inflamed with desire for learning”) — a beginning student
Danishmend (دانشمند, “master of arts”) — a graduate; appears in early sources as “Talisman.” Those content to teach primary school or attend to ecclesiastical duties needed study no further
Mulâzim (ملازم, candidate) — graduates of the higher law course, examined personally by the Mufti
Muderis (مدرس, professor) — those who sought appointment as teachers in medreses, receiving large salaries that increased as they rose

Three classes of Muderis:

Muderis of Constantinople (قسطنطنیه)
Muderis of Adrianople (ادرنه) and Brusa (بروسه)
Muderis of other cities

Constantinople’s roughly four hundred Muderis were in ten grades, distinguished by the subjects they taught. Those who wished to reach the higher judgeships were obliged to pass through all ten grades — so long a process as regularly to bring a man to gray hairs before he reached the top. This led to circumvention: the sons of Ulema were inscribed as Muderis while very young, with substitutes hired to teach in their places. By thirty or forty they could thus attain high position. This practice, combined with the immunities and privileges of the Ulema, led over time to great accumulations of wealth in a few families.

Clergy (Ruhban), Seids (Seyyidler), and Dervishes (Dervişler)
The Clergy
The clergy of the Ottoman nation were of no great education and possessed less influence than the priests of any other religion. They were in five classes:



































TurkishOttoman ScriptRoleŞeyh (Sheik)شیخPreacherHatib (Khatib)خطیبLeader of Friday servicesİmam (Imâm)امامLeader of daily prayersMüezzin (Muezzin)مؤذنOne who intones the call to prayerKayyım (Kaim)قائمCaretaker of the mosque
Seids (Seyyidler / Emirs / Sherifs)
The Seids (سادات, also known as Emirs — امرا — or Sherifs — شرفا) were a class apart. They were not properly members of the Ulema unless they passed through the schools like others. Their distinction rested on a real or assumed genealogy tracing their ancestry back to the Prophet Mohammed. They alone were privileged to wear a green turban (yeşil sarık, یشیل صارق).
They constituted the only hereditary nobility among the Ottomans — their privileges were personal rather than financial: they were not to be struck on penalty of severe punishment, and they had their own justice. Great honor was shown to two notable Seids:

Mir Alem (میر علم, the sultan’s standard-bearer) — regularly a Seid, had precedence of all army officers
Nakib ol-Eshraf (نقیب الاشراف, chief of the Seids) — ranked second in the Moslem Institution; at Bairam ceremonies had precedence even of the Mufti. Appointed by the sultan for life, he held despotic authority over all Seids, with a staff of officers and clerks in the capital and provinces

Dervishes
Dervishes (درویش) were not members of the Ulema. They represented in Islam the monks, hermits, and begging friars of Christianity. Through them heresies spread, uprisings were concocted, mobs gathered, and holy war was preached. On more than one occasion they endangered the power of the government. Many were honest and God-fearing; others were scarcely more than tramps and wandering thieves. Clergy, Seids, and dervishes represented the merely religious side of the institution; they never grew to possess permanent influence and power in the state.

Jurists and the Mufti (Müfti / Şeyhülislam)
The Muftis (Müftiler)
Among the Ulema who had finished the law course, those who chose to become counsellors and jurists rather than take up the active judicial career constituted a distinct body: the muftis (مفتى), held in high esteem. One was assigned as associate to the judge of every important city — about two hundred in all — while others were counsellors for Beylerbeys (بیگلربیگ) and Sanjak Beys (سنجق بیگی). Appointed for life, they lived in retirement with no initiative of action.
When a judge, Bey, or private citizen confronted a case involving learned knowledge of the Sacred Law, they submitted to a mufti a question in writing (usually a hypothetical case). The mufti, after careful consideration in light of the law books of the school of Abu Hanifa (أبو حنیفة), gave an answer: a fetva (فتوى). These answers were extremely concise and unaccompanied by reasoning, prepared and sealed in solemn form. When a judge or Bey posed a question touching a pending law case, the mufti’s response ordinarily settled the case. Since there was no class of professional lawyers, the muftis were a necessary and very useful body.
The Mufti of Constantinople: Şeyhülislam (شیخ الإسلام)
In ordinary cities the mufti ranked after the judge. But in Constantinople, where the sultan and his officers frequently presented questions touching matters of the highest public importance, the mufti of Constantinople became par excellence the Mufti. Mohammed II assigned him the title of Sheik ul-Islam (شیخ الإسلام, “Ancient of Islam”), which in later times became his ordinary title. The Mufti was not regularly chosen from among his fellows but was usually advanced by the sultan from the active judicial service. He had the right to appoint and promote all other muftis of the empire. Suleiman created the Fetva-khaneh (فتوىخانه) — a special bureau to assist the Mufti in preparing decisions.
Suleiman constituted the Mufti the head of the Ulema. He outranked all officials of government, yielding place only to the grand vizier on ordinary occasions. He was almost the equal of the sultan himself, since he was the expounder and representative of the Sacred Law, which was above the sultan. Bayezid II was accustomed to stand to receive the Mufti and give him a seat above his own.

“The Turk shows his [Mufti] the greatest reverence of any man in his realm, because he represents justice and the image of God.”
— Early in Suleiman’s reign

Sixteenth-century Westerners compared the Mufti with the pope. His powers included:

Sole right to proclaim that war should be begun and to declare it holy and incumbent on all Moslems
Frequent consultation by the sultan on the conformity of proposed Kanuns (قوانین) with the Sacred Law
The ultimate responsibility of pronouncing that a sultan had transgressed the Sacred Law and ought to be deposed

The Mufti exercised a function similar to the highest office of the Supreme Court of the United States: the power of defending the Constitution. Urf (عرف, sovereign will) was subordinate to Sheri (شرع, Sacred Law); the sultan, who embodied the former, could not override the Mufti, who represented the latter.
The power of the Mufti illustrated:


Selim I and the forced conversion of Christians (c. 1515): Selim decided to order all Christians to accept Islam on pain of death. The Mufti Jemali (جمالی) intervened decisively, showing the Greek Patriarch that the Sacred Law provided that Christians who had accepted Mohammedan rule and agreed to pay kharâj (خراج) and jizyeh (جزیه) were to be left unmolested. The argument prevailed, and the Christians were not disturbed.


Suleiman and the execution of Mustapha (1553): Suleiman consulted the Mufti Ebu Su’ud (ابو السعود) by means of a hypothetical case — a merchant who left a slave in charge of his household, only to have the slave plot against his wife and children. The Mufti answered that such a slave deserved to be tortured to death. This greatly influenced Suleiman in ordering the execution of his eldest son.


The fetvas of the muftis amounted in practice to a body of legislation intermediate between the Sheri and the Kanuns — partaking of the sacred character of the former while being, like the latter, of a modern and practical nature. However, nothing radical could ever be attempted. In reality, the muftis occupied the most influential position in the Moslem Institution and perhaps in the Ottoman state:

“Usually inferior to judges and officers of government in income and display, and giving no direct impulse to affairs, they nevertheless wielded the greatest continuous power in the state — the quiet, steady, almost changeless, almost irresistible, force of Mohammedanism.”

The muftis’ authority rested on (1) the acceptance by the entire Moslem population of the absolute supremacy of the Sacred Law, and (2) the recognition that they who had acquired learning in the Law by long years of arduous labor were the persons through whom that supremacy was to be maintained. They were the conservative, regulative force in the Ottoman state, contributing largely to the empire’s durability. But their work was done only too well — the idea of the changelessness of the Sacred Law was essentially hostile to progress, and the Ulema held the Ottoman mind nearly stationary while the rest of the world moved on.

The Judicial System
The Kadis (Kazılar / Judges)
The judges belonging to the corps of the Ulema had territorial jurisdictions that covered the whole empire — even extending to the Crimea and North Africa, though these were under vassal governments. The tribunals were competent for all kinds of cases, whether civil or criminal, whether covered by the Sheri, the Kanuns, Adet (عادت, custom), or none of these.
However, they were not competent to try all persons:

Kullar (قول, members of the Ruling Institution) had their own justice
Seids had their own jurisdiction under the Nakib ol-Eshraf
Foreign colonies had separate systems
Subject Christians had their own ecclesiastical tribunals for matters among themselves
Special courts handled certain vakıf cases, though presided over by the regular judicial body
Fief-holders had seigniorial jurisdiction in certain matters

Five classes of judges, carefully graded:





























ClassDescriptionGreater Mollas (Mollalar)The highest tier: Kaziasker of Rumelia, Kaziasker of Anatolia, judge of Constantinople, judges of Mecca and Medina, judges of Adrianople/Brusa/Cairo/Damascus, judges of the three suburbs of Constantinople and of Jerusalem/Smyrna/Aleppo/Larissa/Salonica — 17 in allLesser MollasJudges of ten second-rank cities: Marash, Bagdad, Bosna-serai, Sofia, Belgrade, Aintab, Kutaia, Konia, Philippopolis, DiarbekrMufettishes (مفتش)Five special judges for vakıf administration under the Mufti, grand vizier, and Kizlar AghaKazis (Kazılar)The vast majority — about 450 stationed in smaller cities. About 200 in Europe under the Kaziasker of Rumelia, about 225 in Asia and 36 in Egypt under the Kaziasker of AnatoliaNaibs (نایب)Inferior judges of villages, temporary substitutes, and a notable group in the 16th century acting as inspectors of public morals
The Kaziaskers (قاضیعسكر, Military Judges)
The Kaziaskers (Kaziasker of Rumelia and Kaziasker of Anatolia) were the two highest judicial officers. Originally their function was to judge the armies in time of war — hence their title “judge of the army” — but their authority had been extended to include the headship of all judges in their respective regions and the appointment of all judges, subject to approval of the pashas. In the Divan, and as “Pillars of the State,” they ranked next to the viziers. They had the first right of audience with the sultan at the close of each Divan, and until the reign of Suleiman they had held all the authority over the Ulema that later came to the Mufti.
Their jurisdictions were divided by Mohammed II: cases concerning Moslems came before the Kaziasker of Rumelia; those concerning non-Moslems before the Kaziasker of Anatolia.
Court Procedure
In every court a single judge sat, with clerks and other subordinates. Cases were presented by the parties themselves, and decisions were usually rendered immediately and in very concise form. The judge cooperated with the Subashi (subası) of the city, who brought summoned persons before the judge and executed sentences — an arrangement similar to ecclesiastical courts of the West, which condemned but left execution to the secular arm.
Hierarchy of appeal:

Lower judges (Kazis, Naibs)
Higher judges (Mollas)
Kaziaskers
Grand vizier
The Divan

Costs and fines were moderate, fixed by Kanun, but constituted a large part of judicial income. Judges were salaried; some had large irregular earnings. The judges also attended to all notarial work of the empire.
The highest courts were:

The Kaziaskers’ courts — held at the Divan, at the palace gate, and in their own houses
The grand vizier’s court — the highest court of appeal for ordinary civil cases, also a court of first instance where justice was refused to no one
The Divan — tried capital cases of great officials, usually in the absence of the accused

The Law Administered
The law which the judges administered was:

The Sacred Law (Sheri/Şeriat, شریعت) — primarily as given in the Koran and the traditions of Mohammed, codified by the doctors of the school of Abu Hanifa, and interpreted in collections of fetvas
The Kanuns (قوانین) of the sultans
Customs (Adet, عادت) and immunities of the regions
Equity — “the good sense and prudence of judges trained in reasoning supplies and decides many things that are not written” (Postel)

The only resemblance to Anglo-Saxon case law was the use of fetvas. Since decisions were rendered very briefly, no extended reports were possible, and the absence of printing (not introduced into Turkey until the 18th century) made general use of judicial precedents practically impossible. Ottoman justice depended more heavily upon the integrity of judges than does Anglo-Saxon justice.
Non-judicial law enforcement: The Muhtesibs (محتصب, lieutenants of police) exercised functions of market and police judges but did not belong to the Ulema. Accompanied by soldiers and attendants, they patrolled streets and inspected markets, inflicting punishment on the spot for infringements.
Quality of Ottoman Justice
Western observers were divided. Some praised the speed and simplicity of Ottoman courts compared with European ones. The grand vizier and Divan were considered the only incorruptible courts. Christian subjects may have received unfair treatment — they were not allowed to testify, and some Moslems considered it nearly a meritorious religious act to turn a case against a Christian by false testimony. Nevertheless, judges were well-paid, highly honored, and carefully inspected by honest men sent out annually by the Kaziaskers. In at least one respect the courts were highly commendable: there was a minimum of “law’s delay.” Cases were always decided promptly, in clear and simple terms.

The Moslem Institution as a Whole
The Moslem Institution represented and maintained the entire system based on the life and work of the Prophet Mohammed. This system claimed to be sufficient for all sides of temporal and eternal life — for individuals and for the state. Its power extended over the whole empire, even beyond the limits of political control.
It was firmly grounded in the allegiance, the fundamental beliefs, and the affections of the entire Moslem-born population. All Moslems of the empire were in a sense members of the institution. In the 16th century, any one of them might hope to see his son mount to a very high place within the organization, since industrious study combined with native ability was all that was demanded. The Moslem Institution was fundamentally democratic.
Basis of membership:

By birth: the Seids (descendants of Mohammed)
By piety and religious service: the dervishes
By learned knowledge of the Sacred Law: all positions of public influence and importance

The three highly-honored classes of teachers (Muderisler), jurists (Müftiler), and judges (Kazılar) were trained in the same superbly-planned educational system, the same text-books, the same ideas. Whether in Constantinople or Cairo, the Crimea or Algiers, Budapest or Bagdad, old, grave, wise, and learned professors, jurists, and judges taught, interpreted, and enforced the same wide-reaching and changeless Sacred Law.
Professors, jurists, and judges alike were, in all that they did and throughout their lives, fundamentally teachers. They taught all the Moslems of the empire, from the young child to the aged sultan. Every important officer of administrative government had a judge and a mufti at his elbow. The sultan himself had always a spiritual adviser — his Hoja (خواجه, teacher) — to whom he showed great deference.
There was an aspect in which the Moslem Institution, based upon the Moslem population of the empire, fitted the government as hand fits glove — and the figure can be pressed further: the hand (the Ruling Institution) is of much the same efficiency with or without the glove, while the glove is useless without the hand; furthermore, the hand may live to wear a succession of gloves.

Relationship Between the Moslem Institution and the Ruling Institution
Lybyer devotes his entire Chapter VIII to comparing the two great institutions.
Likenesses

Both were constructed from old, well-seasoned materials with origins lost in prehistoric obscurity
Both were founded on ideas, not racial descent — belief, not blood, was the test of membership
Both were self-perpetuating through education, with lifelong educational systems adapted to their character (the Ruling Institution trained physically and mentally; the Moslem Institution neglected physical education for greater intellectual training)
Both rose to an apex, through the Divan and grand vizier, in the sultan — though the ideas by which they were joined to their head were in striking contrast

Differences













































AspectRuling InstitutionMoslem InstitutionRecruitmentChristian-born slavesFree-born MoslemsLoyaltyTo one man, the sultan (servile devotion)To the Sacred Law (reasoned allegiance)SpiritLess independent of personal authorityIndependent; a Mufti would firmly oppose the sovereign where a grand vizier would scarcely ventureConservatismRelatively progressive; readily adopted new ideasExtremely conservative; change was of the essence of oppositionRoleActive — fought and governedContemplative — thought and judgedStructural stabilityArtificial; recruitment by capture/tribute ran against human nature; no hereditary propertyNatural; voluntary recruitment from an increasing population; free, with full family rightsSource of powerDownward from the sultan’s authorityUpward from the people, attached almost artificially to the sultan
Interactions
The two institutions taught each other some evil qualities. Luxury, venality, and unnatural vices — all strongly discountenanced by the Sacred Law — were fostered in the Ruling Institution by the conditions of the system and came to be charged against the Moslem Institution as well. Conversely, the conservatism and resistance to progress of the Moslem Institution came to characterize the Ruling Institution, as Ulema taught the palace pages and princes — eventually giving the Ruling Institution a changelessness that resisted improvement.
Each contributed to the other’s power. The Ruling Institution defended the Moslem Institution by the sword, carried out judicial decisions, and protected its revenue. The Moslem Institution kept the Moslem population obedient and submissive to the sultan’s authority, teaching that the sultan was divinely appointed and always to be obeyed so long as he did not transgress the Sacred Law.

“Accordingly the two institutions, so long as they acted in harmony, were absolutely impregnable in their position among the Moslems of the empire.”

Relative Power
The Moslem Institution’s power grew cumulatively. It gained lands and wealth continually through new vakıfs and never lost any. It held all means and methods of intellectual development. The Ruling Institution, by contrast, lost relatively — sultans constantly gave away state lands as endowments for new mosques and colleges. By the time of Suleiman’s reign, the Moslem Institution had overtaken its rival.
Suleiman’s gifts of great mosques, numerous colleges, and vast endowments — his arrangement of the cursus honorum leading up to the Mufti — and the personal leaning of his later years toward the influence of the Ulema, settled permanently the preponderance of the Moslem Institution.
Yet the Moslem Institution could never destroy its rival. It had become too unworldly to live unsupported by a secular power — “a strong but tender hand, which must always wear a glove.”

“Bound together closely in an alliance which neither enjoyed, but which was necessary for the preservation of both, the Ruling Institution and the Moslem Institution constituted the twofold inner framework of the Ottoman Empire, to which it owed all its might and energy, its grandeur and repute, its continuity and durability.”


Key Terms in Ottoman Turkish Script























































































































































































































EnglishOttoman TurkishTransliterationMoslem Institution——Learned men / ScholarsعُلَماUlemaSacred Lawشرع / شریعتSheri / ŞeriatImperial decree / lawقانونKanunReligious endowmentوقفVakf (pl. evkaf)JudgeقاضیKazi / KadiMilitary judgeقاضیعسكرKaziaskerChief Muftiشیخ الإسلامŞeyhülislam / Sheik ul-IslamLegal opinionفتوىFetvaJurisconsultمفتىMüfti / MuftiCollege / schoolمدرسهMedresePrimary schoolمكتبMekteb”Reading place”اوقوماق ییرلریOkumak-yerleriProfessor / teacherمدرسMuderisMaster of arts / graduateدانشمندDanishmendUndergraduateسوختهSofta / SukhtaCandidate (graduate)ملازمMulâzimHigher judgeملاMollaInferior judgeنایبNaibSpecial judge (vakıf)مفتشMufettishAdministrator (vakıf)مُتَوَلّىMuteveliInspector (vakıf)ناظرNazirRuling Institution slaveقولKul (pl. kullar)Teacher / adviserخواجهHoja / HocaPreacherشیخŞeyh / SheikFriday prayer leaderخطیبHatib / KhatibDaily prayer leaderامامİmamCaller to prayerمؤذنMüezzin / MuezzinMosque caretakerقائمKayyım / KaimDescendant of the Prophetسادات / سیدSeid / SeyyidChief of the Seidsنقیب الاشرافNakib ol-EshrafStandard-bearerمیر علمMir AlemDervish / monasticدرویشDerviş / DervishPolice lieutenantمحتصبMuhtesibPious foundation bureauفتوىخانهFetva-khanehCustomعادتAdetSovereign willعرفUrfPoll-tax on non-MoslemsجزیهCizye / JizyehLand tax on non-MoslemsخراجHaraç / KharâjTeacher of the sultanخواجهHoja / HocaFriday mosqueجامعCami

concepts/mughal-ottoman-compared.title: The Ottoman and Mughal Empires Compared
concepts/mughal-ottoman-compared.content:
The Ottoman and Mughal Empires Compared
Lybyer’s Appendix IV to The Government of the Ottoman Empire in the Time of Suleiman the Magnificent offers a systematic comparison of the Ottoman and Mughal imperial structures. His central argument is that the Mughals possessed far greater material wealth but fundamentally weaker institutions, which explains why their empire lasted barely 250 years while the Ottomans endured for six centuries.
Synchronous Foundations
The two empires were born at nearly the same moment: Baber won the Battle of Panipat in April 1526, establishing Mughal rule in India; Suleiman won the Battle of Mohács in August 1526, extending Ottoman power deep into Europe. But their trajectories diverged sharply. The third Battle of Panipat in 1761 marked the virtual destruction of the Mughal Empire, while the second Battle of Mohács in 1687 was merely the first step of the Ottoman retreat. Baber’s descendant Aurangzeb offers the closest comparison with Suleiman, but Aurangzeb died a century after Suleiman.
Material Wealth
The Mughals had five to ten times as many subjects as the Ottomans and a proportionally larger income — Akbar’s annual revenue was estimated at 200 million, Aurangzeb's at 450 million. Suleiman’s revenue was not the tenth part of Akbar’s, and Louis XIV’s was not the tenth part of Aurangzeb’s. The Mughal court was correspondingly more splendid: gold and silver, gems, silks, and muslins were far more abundant in India. Akbar removed internal tolls two centuries before France did, making India a single economic unit. His conversion of the land-tax from a one-third share of crops in kind to a money system was a step the Ottomans have never been able to take. Despite this, the Mughal empire was the weaker structure.
The Slave-Administration
The decisive Ottoman advantage was the Ruling Institution — a systematic slave-administration recruited through the devşirme, where boys were taken from Christian families, converted, trained in the palace school, and became the sultan’s personal property. They owed everything to him, could pass nothing to their children, and were bound by loyalty that was impersonal and institutional. The Mughals never developed anything comparable. Baber’s followers were free tribesmen bound by personal loyalty and open-handed generosity. Mughal high officers were Persians, Afghans, Rajputs, and Turks serving as vassals rather than slaves. A rare exception was the Nawab of Farrukhabad, Muhammad Khan, who ran a miniature devşirme — seizing 100-200 Hindu boys a year, training them as slave-administrators and musketeers, marrying them to slave daughters, and forbidding them from building masonry houses. But Lybyer stresses that the abundant records of the Ottoman slave-system contrast strikingly with the near-absence of such records in the Mughal case.
Succession and Fratricide
Mehmed II’s law code permitting fratricide was brutal but effective — it eliminated rival claimants and prevented civil wars at each succession. The Mughals had no such law, and every succession was a civil war. Baber’s sons fought each other. Akbar’s sons drank themselves to death, but Jehangir rebelled against his father. Shah Jahan’s four sons — Dara, Shuja, Murad, and Aurangzeb — fought until Aurangzeb killed all three and imprisoned their father for seven years. After Aurangzeb’s death, two more sons were slain. Lybyer’s judgment: “The Ottoman method was more practical if less humane.”
The Royal Family
Ottoman sultans were born of anonymous slave-mothers, and their daughters’ names have scarcely survived. Mughal imperial ladies are historically well-known individuals. Gul-Badan, daughter of Baber, wrote memoirs. Nur Jahan, wife of Jehangir, effectively ruled India — “in all but name, undisputed sovereign of the empire,” she portioned over 500 girls from her private purse and could lead armies. Mumtaz Mahal, wife of Shah Jahan, inspired the Taj Mahal. Jehan-Ara and Raushan-Ara, daughters of Mumtaz Mahal, were active partisan figures in the succession wars. Bernier described Raushan-Ara processing on a “stupendous Pegu elephant” with sixty elephants following, accompanied by hundreds of attendants. This contrast reflected the deeper institutional difference: the Ottoman system was impersonal and systematic; the Mughal remained dynastic and personal.
Religious Policy
The Ottomans brought Islam to conquered lands as the faith of the ruling class. Conversion was a steady public machinery — the devşirme produced generations of committed Muslims in high office. The Mughals entered an India where Islam had existed for eight centuries. Baber defeated the Moslem Lodi sultans of Delhi and the Hindu Rajput confederacy in successive battles; both groups were equally conquered subjects. Akbar adopted a policy of equal treatment unparalleled in contemporary Europe — he abolished the jizyeh (poll-tax on non-Muslims), forbade the enslavement of captives, ended child marriage and sati without full consent, removed internal tolls, and treated Brahmans and sheiks alike. Only Aurangzeb reimposed the jizyeh and tried to run a genuinely Islamic state, a policy that opened the cracks in the empire. The Ottoman sultan was the head of all Moslems, with a şeyhülislam whose fetvas could bind even the sultan. The Mughals had no such figure. Their Moslem subjects had no emotional loyalty to the house of Timur, which had defeated rather than liberated them.
Government and Administration
The Mughals never developed a grand vizier on the Ottoman pattern because the imperial house never abandoned personal government. Humayun bizarrely divided the ministries by the four elements: Khaki (earth, agriculture and buildings), Hawai (air, the wardrobe and kitchen), Ateshi (fire, artillery and war material), Abi (water, canals and drinks). The emperor presided over the council himself, unlike the Ottoman divan presided over by the grand vizier. The Nawabs (provincial governors) were almost little emperors, supported by jagir land grants analogous to Ottoman timars. But the Mughal system contained nothing corresponding to the Ottoman Ruling Institution’s rigid separation of the imperial household from the government, its systematic training of pages, its merit-based advancement, its discipline, and its institutional memory independent of any individual emperor.
The Army
The Mughal emperor’s personal army of 12,000-15,000 paid infantry and up to 40,000 paid cavalry roughly matched the Janissaries and Sipahis of the Porte in number but not in political importance. The mass of the army was feudal cavalry, estimated at 200,000 to 400,000 men. Artillery was strong under Baber and Humayun — Baber chained 700 guns together at Panipat, the same tactic Selim I used at Chaldiran. But the Mughal army declined into a bloated, inefficient horde. Aurangzeb’s Deccan campaign was a migration rather than an army: for each fighting man there were at least two camp-followers, and the camp was reportedly 20 to 30 miles in circumference with as many as 5 million souls. Lybyer writes: “A battle in 1526 between Baber and Suleiman would have been a worthy contest, but the army of Aurangzeb would probably have been defeated easily by the Ottoman troops which bit the dust before Prince Eugene.”
The Ultimate Weakness
The deepest structural flaw was the relationship between the ruling house and its Moslem subjects. Suleiman’s Moslems were bound to the house of Osman by centuries of shared history, conversion, and warfare. The Mughals’ Moslem subjects remembered the glories of lost dynasties, felt humiliated at being reduced to the level of Hindus, and had no loyal attachment to the house of Timur. “There was ever an absence of solidarity between the house of Timur and those Moslem subjects who had not come into India in the service of that house.” When Aurangzeb’s successors faced the Maratha revolt, there were few who would sacrifice their fortunes or be faithful unto death. Lybyer ends with a quotation from Aurangzeb’s letter to his old teacher, who had taught him only Arabic grammar and law: “Was it not incumbent upon my preceptor to make me acquainted with the distinguishing features of every nation of the earth, its resources and strength, its mode of warfare, its manners, religion, form of government? … Did you ever instruct me in the art of war, how to besiege a town, or draw up an army in battle array? Happy for me that I consulted wiser heads than thine on these subjects!” — a single anecdote that captures the entire institutional weakness: the Ottoman system was designed to produce rulers and administrators through the palace school; the Mughal system left princes to learn statecraft on their own initiative or not at all.

concepts/nasihatname-tradition.title: The Ottoman Nasihatname Tradition
concepts/nasihatname-tradition.content:
The Ottoman Nasihatname Tradition
Origins and Genre
The Ottoman nasihatname (نصيحت نامه, “book of counsel”) tradition grew out of a deeper Islamic and Persian literary heritage reaching back to the eleventh century. The earliest known Turkish work on government, the Kudatku Bilik of Yusuf Khass Hajib (written in 1069-70 for the Karakhanid court), established a pattern of advice-giving to rulers that would echo through Ottoman letters for centuries. The Persian tradition contributed the Siyasetname of Nizam al-Mulk — the most famous grand vizier of the Seljuk Empire, assassinated in 1092 — and the Qabusname, a mirror for princes composed by the Ziyarid ruler Kay Kavus. These works shared a conviction that good government followed from the personal virtue, piety, and wisdom of the ruler and his ministers, and that the health of the state could be diagnosed by comparing present conditions to an idealized golden age.
The Ottoman version of this genre emerged in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries as the empire’s expansion slowed and signs of institutional strain became visible. Where the earlier Persian mirrors had been primarily philosophical, the Ottoman nasihatname turned increasingly practical, specific, and critical. The genre became the principal vehicle through which Ottoman statesmen expressed their anxieties about what they perceived as decline from the age of Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent — the standard against which later conditions were invariably measured.
The Major Authors
The first great Ottoman nasihatname was the Asafname of Lütfi Pasha (d. 1564), who served as grand vizier under Suleiman for four years before being dismissed. In his work, Lütfi Pasha drew directly on his administrative experience, offering concrete advice on the behavior of the grand vizier, the management of the treasury, and the proper appointment of officials. He urged that vacancies in the zi’amet and timar system be given to deserving persons and that grand viziers abstain from granting large fiefs to their own dependents — a warning that would be repeated by every subsequent author.
Kınalızade Ali Efendi (d. 1572), a scholar of the ulema rather than a bureaucrat, wrote the Ahlak-ı Ala’i, which blended Islamic ethics with statecraft. His work is notable for its systematic treatment of the “circle of equity” (daire-i adalet) — the idea that justice produces prosperity, prosperity produces subjects, subjects produce soldiers, soldiers protect the state, and the state requires justice. This circular model became the conceptual framework of the entire nasihatname tradition.
Koçi Bey (d. c. 1650), a Bosnian-born official of the palace, wrote the most influential of all Ottoman reform treatises, the Risale, presented to Sultan Murad IV around 1630. Unlike earlier authors, Koçi Bey concerned himself little with theory. His work offered numerous definite, practical suggestions for reform based on detailed comparisons between the empire under Suleiman and the conditions of his own day. He identified specific abuses: the inflation of timar grants, the corruption of alay beys, the decay of the devshirme system, and the encroachment of the ulema on administrative positions. European and Turkish historians alike have drawn heavily on Koçi Bey for their discussions of Ottoman decline.
Katib Chelebi (Hajji Khalifa, d. 1657), the great bibliographer and encyclopedist, contributed a brief but incisive study focused on redressing the financial situation. His Düstur ül-Amel (The Rectification of Practice) analyzed the fiscal crisis of the mid-seventeenth century and proposed remedies for the budget deficits that plagued the treasury.
Sarı Mehmed Pasha (d. 1717), the author of the Naṣā’iḥ ül-Vüzera ve’l-Ümera (Book of Counsel for Viziers and Governors, also known as the Kitab-i Güldeste), represents the culmination of the tradition. Writing around 1715, he had served as chief defterdar seven times under Sultan Ahmed III, making him uniquely qualified to discuss financial administration. His work is divided into nine chapters covering the grand vizier, official positions and bribery, the treasury, the janissaries, the condition of the rayas (subject peasants), frontier warfare, avarice and liberality, friendship and calumny, and the state of the zi’amet and timar system.
Characteristic Themes
Several themes recur across the nasihatname corpus. First, the idealization of the past: every author compared the present unfavorably with the reign of Suleiman the Magnificent (1520-1566), which served as a fixed reference point for what the Ottoman system ought to be. Second, the diagnosis of decline as moral and administrative rather than structural: corruption, bribery, and the appointment of unqualified men were seen as the root causes, not any inherent weakness in the system itself. Third, a deep conservatism: the proposed reforms invariably aimed at restoring old practices rather than creating new ones. As Wright observed, “renovation rather than evolution was his aim.”
The tradition was by no means confined to literature. Both sultans and viziers attempted to implement the reforms urged by these authors — notably Sultan Murad IV and the Köprülü viziers. Sarı Mehmed Pasha himself, writing about the same reforms he had tried to carry out as defterdar, gave his book a practical urgency absent from purely academic works.
Significance
The nasihatname tradition provides the single most important body of internal Ottoman criticism of the empire’s institutions. The authors were not outsiders but men who had served at the highest levels of government — Lütfi Pasha as grand vizier, Koçi Bey as a palace intimate, Sarı Mehmed Pasha as chief treasurer. Their works offer an insider’s perspective on the processes that contemporaries identified as decline, even if modern historians have questioned whether the phenomena they described truly represented decay or rather a transformation of the Ottoman state into a different kind of polity.
The genre also illustrates the limits of Ottoman reform thinking. Bound by the Sacred Law (şeriat) and an intensely conservative political culture, the nasihatname writers could imagine only restoration, not transformation. They never questioned the fundamental structure of the Ruling Institution or the sultan’s authority, nor did they propose innovations in statecraft drawn from European models. Their advice was directed inward, toward the recovery of a lost purity — and in that orientation lay both their moral force and their practical weakness.

concepts/roads-and-monetary-system.title: roads-and-monetary-system
concepts/roads-and-monetary-system.content:
Ottoman Roads and Monetary System
Overview
The Ottoman Empire of the classical age (1300-1600) maintained an extensive road network connecting the imperial capital Istanbul to the furthest reaches of its domains, and administered a bimetallic monetary system centred on the silver akçe. The road system, praised by the historian Konstantin Jireček as the finest since the Roman Empire, was built on Roman foundations and sustained by a sophisticated network of caravanserais, imârets, and zâviyes funded through pious endowments (vakıf). The monetary system, anchored by the akçe — the standard silver coin first minted at Bursa in 1327 — provided a stable medium of exchange through most of the 16th century before being overwhelmed by the flood of American silver and the consequent price revolution of the 1580s.

1. The Empire’s Road Network (شبکه راه‌ها)
Istanbul was the terminus not only of sea lanes but also of the great caravan routes that bound the empire together. Three principal highways connected the Balkans to Istanbul, while Anatolian and Arab routes radiated eastward and southward.
1.1 The Balkan Highways
Three great roads linked the Balkans to the capital:

The Via Egnatia — The ancient Roman road from the Adriatic ports of Albania (Durazzo, Valona) through Ohrida (Ohrid), Monastir (Bitola), and Salonica (Thessaloniki) to Istanbul.
The Great Military Road — The most important artery of the European provinces, running from Belgrade through Sofia and Plovdiv (Philippopolis) to Edirne (Adrianople) and finally Istanbul. This was the route of imperial campaigns into Hungary and central Europe.
The Danubian Road — Running from the lower Danube region through the Tundza valley and Edirne, connecting the northern frontier to the capital.

The Ottomans kept the Roman road between Istanbul and Belgrade in constant repair, improving it with roughly hewn stone and building entirely new sections. Wagons could travel from Belgrade to Istanbul in one month; in 1566, Selîm II covered the same distance in fifteen days on horseback.
1.2 The Anatolian and Eastern Routes
The caravan route from Iran and Anatolia reached Istanbul through Bolu, Izmit (Nicomedia), and Gebze. The road from Ankara joined this artery near Izmit.
