concepts/ruling-institution-slave-family.title: The Ruling Institution as Slave-Family (Kapıkulu)
concepts/ruling-institution-slave-family.content:
The Ruling Institution as Slave-Family (Kapıkulu)
Definition and Central Thesis
Lybyer’s central thesis is that the Ottoman state was built upon two great institutions: the Ruling Institution (the sultan’s personal slave-administration) and the Moslem Institution (the ulema and Sacred Law). Of these, the Ruling Institution was the engine of war, government, and expansion. It comprised the sultan and his family, the officers of his household, the executive officers of government, the standing army (both cavalry and infantry), and a large body of young men being educated for future service. The most vital and characteristic features of this institution were, first, that its personnel consisted — with few exceptions — of men born of Christian parents or the sons of such; and second, that almost every member of the Institution came into it as the sultan’s slave, and remained the sultan’s slave throughout life no matter to what height of wealth, power, and greatness he might attain.

“Perhaps no more daring experiment has been tried on a large scale upon the face of the earth than that embodied in the Ottoman Ruling Institution. Its nearest ideal analogue is found in the Republic of Plato, its nearest actual parallel in the Mameluke system of Egypt.”
— Lybyer, ch. II

The Ruling Institution deliberately took slaves and made them ministers of state; it took boys from the sheep-run and the plow-tail and made them courtiers and the husbands of princesses; it took young men whose ancestors had borne the Christian name for centuries, and made them rulers in the greatest of Mohammedan states. It never asked its novices “Who was your father?” or “What do you know?” or even “Can you speak our tongue?”; but it studied their faces and their frames and said, “You shall be a soldier, and if you show yourself worthy, a general,” or, “You shall be a scholar and a gentleman, and if the ability lies in you, a governor and a prime minister.”
The Sultan as Despot
The sultan was the head and center of the institution in every aspect. He gave it its unity, its vigor, and its propelling force. Although his despotic power was limited in many directions (by the Sacred Law, by custom, by the Moslem Institution), it knew no limits with regard to the members and mechanism of this institution. The person, the fortune, the property, and the life of every member lay in his hand. The very word despot (from Greek δεσπότης) means by derivation the master of slaves, and it was only over his kullar (قول, slaves) that the sultan’s power was despotic in the fullest sense.
The absolute character of the sultan’s authority was an element of great strength, but it contained also the possibility of great danger. The sultan held the position of Deity toward his slaves, and needed the omniscience and benevolence of Deity to exercise his power wisely and justly. The only position in the entire scheme filled by the uncertain lot of heredity was the sultanate itself.
The Slave-Family: Kapıkulu
Every one who belonged to the Ruling Institution in any capacity — from gardener to grand vizier, save only the members of the royal family — bore the title of kul (قول), or slave, of the sultan. Nor was this title a mere form: with few exceptions, all members entered the system as actual slaves, and there was nowhere along the line of promotion any formal or real process of emancipation. The power of the sultan over the lives, persons, and property of the members of the institution, and his right to their absolute obedience, bore every mark of having been derived from the idea of slavery.
The whole number in the system in Suleiman’s time is estimated at about eighty thousand. Since losses by war were sometimes tremendous, the average annual renewal required was as much as one-tenth, or between seven and eight thousand.
The institution contained several component parts capable of separate existence:

The Janissaries (يڭیچرى, yeniçeri, “new troop”) — the permanent infantry, representing the brute force of the system and its most dangerous element.
The Spahis of the Porte (قپوقولی سپاهی لری, kapıkulu sipahileri) — the permanent cavalry, more numerous than the Janissaries, better educated, and less dangerous because of greater opportunities.
The hierarchy of governing officials — the viziers, beylerbeys, defterdars, and nishanjis.
The acemi oğlanlar (عجمی اوغلان, “foreign youth”) — apprentice Janissaries.
The colleges of pages — training many of the Spahis of the Porte and most of the officers of government.

Recruitment: The Four Methods
Entrance to the system came by the door of slavery, which was open regularly only to Christian boys from ten to twenty years of age, with preferred ages between fourteen and eighteen. Four methods were employed:
1. Capture (War Captives)
By the Sacred Law, the sultan was entitled to one-fifth of all captives taken in war. His officers chose, as his share, such young men as seemed suitable for a place in the system. Since by special Ottoman regulation the sultan’s fifth belonged to the church, he paid twenty-five aspers to the church for each slave he took. Captives came from the irregular raids in Austria, the corsair expeditions of the Mediterranean, and the quasi-slave-farms of the Caucasus. The raided belt around the empire — stretching across Austria-Hungary, Southern Poland and Russia — furnished a continuous supply.
2. Purchase
The sultan’s officers purchased youths in the public slave-market of Constantinople. These came from the captives that the Tartars of the Crimea took in great numbers, from the Caucasus region, and from Mediterranean corsair expeditions.
3. Gift
The sultan received a large number of boys as gifts, since it was well known that no presents were more acceptable. Those who desired his favor kept a lookout for suitable youths.
4. Tribute: The Devşirme (دوشیرمه)
The devşirme — the tribute of Christian children — is the most famous method, though it furnished perhaps less than half of the annual recruits. It produced, however, the ablest and highest officials of the system; its youth bore the same relation to those obtained by capture as cultivated fruits of the garden do to those gathered in the woods. The annual levy averaged about three thousand in the sixteenth century.
The Devşirme Process
The levying was accomplished by a regular process. Normally every four years, but oftener in case of need, a body of officials — described by contemporaries as more skilled in judging boys than trained horse-dealers in judging colts — was sent out by the government to the Christian provinces. The whole of the Balkan Peninsula, Hungary, the western coast of Asia Minor, and the southern and eastern shores of the Black Sea were included; the strongest and ablest youths came from the mountain regions inhabited by Albanians and the Southern Slavic peoples.
The recruiting officers were commissioned each to bring in a certain number apportioned out of a total determined at the capital. There was no principle of tithing, and no fixed proportion or number of boys was levied from each village or family. A regular procedure was followed: the officers obtained from the Christian priest of the village a list of baptized boys between the ages of twelve and twenty; all were brought before the officers, who selected the best. Parents who had strong and well-favored sons might lose them all, while those who had weaklings would lose none.
Selection Criteria
All recruits were brought before trained officials, carefully registered, and divided into two classes:
Those who best satisfied the criteria of bodily perfection, muscular strength, and intellectual ability (about one in ten of the whole number) were chosen for a superior quality of training, especially on the intellectual side. These regularly became iç oğlanlar (ايج اوغلان, palace pages) and kapıkulu Spahis of the Porte, and the ablest rose to the great offices of the army and government.
The remainder were destined for a different education, mainly physical. They became acemi oğlanlar (apprentice Janissaries) and later Janissaries. But the ablest of these might also rise to positions as Spahis of the Porte and even as generals and officers of state. Merit was recognized everywhere and regularly led to promotion.
Reaction of the Subject Population
The grief at parting was often heart-breaking. The mother whose son was taken by force to an unknown life among enemies of all she held dear would hardly have suffered more at his death. Yet she might hope to see him one day in possession of great wealth and power. Parents who wished to keep their sons would sometimes marry them in tender years (married boys being ineligible); those with means bought exemption from the recruiting officers.
On the contrary, many parents were glad to have their sons chosen, knowing they would escape from grinding poverty, receive a first-rate training suited to their abilities, and enter upon the possibility of a great career. Some parents came to regard the process as a privilege rather than a burden. Turkish parents envied them the opportunity, and sometimes tried to evade the regulations by paying Christians to take their Moslem sons and declare them as Christian children, so that they might be enrolled as the sultan’s slaves.
The Slave Status and Its Implications
Whether captured, purchased, presented, or levied, the young men who entered the system were the sultan’s slaves — his personal property for life. They were never allowed to forget the responsibilities of their condition.
No Inheritance Rights
If a kul was executed (a common fate for those who fell from favor), all his property went to the sultan. When one of the sultan’s slaves died leaving children, the sultan sealed up the property and took the tenth part for himself before distributing the rest to the children — the nine-tenths being given by favor rather than right. If the slave had no sons or daughters, the sultan took the whole estate.
No Marriage Without Permission
The Janissaries were not supposed to marry, although the rule was not strictly enforced. The Spahis of the Porte probably married late, if at all, giving the whole system a monastic aspect. High officials kept harems, but their children were ordinarily few. Frequent wars, the danger of execution, and confiscation of property put a check on family establishment.
Absolute Dependence
They must to the end of their days go where the sultan chose to send them, obey his slightest wish, submit to disgrace as readily as to promotion, and — though in the highest office of state — accept death by his order from the hands of their humblest fellow-slaves. The viziers of Selim I carried their wills in their bosoms; the heads of seven are said to have fallen at his command.

“Being all slaves by condition, and slaves of a single lord, from whom alone they hope for greatness, honor, and riches, and from whom alone on the other hand they fear punishment, chastisement, and death, what wonder that in his presence and in rivalry with each other they will do stupendous things?”
— Venetian observer Andrea Gritti, 1503

The Harem and Eunuchs
Suleiman’s harem contained about three hundred women, kept in a separate palace well fortified and guarded. All its inmates except his children were purchased or presented slaves. These women, brought for the most part from the region of the Caucasus, including some of the fairest female captives of many lands, were nearly all daughters of Christians. Khurrem (Roxelana) herself was a Russian.
The imperial harem partook of the characteristics of the schools of pages. There were two odalar for recruits of the harem, where they were taught housework, sewing and embroidery, manners and deportment. Those with a taste for music and dancing learned those accomplishments; those who were studious learned to read and write. All were carefully instructed in the system of Islam. Like the pages, nearly all of them passed out of the palace at the age of twenty-five, being given in marriage to Spahis of the Porte or to other officials. Thus the harem served as a training-school of slave-wives for the sultan himself and for the most highly honored of his kullar.
The royal family itself was part of the slave system. The mothers of the sultan’s children were slaves; the sultan himself was the son of a slave; his daughters were married to men who bore indelibly the title of kul.
Eunuchs
Although the Sacred Law strongly disapproved of the employment of eunuchs, that unfortunate class was thought too useful to be dispensed with entirely. Some were white, brought mainly from the Caucasus region; the great majority were negroes brought from Africa. Tribute children seem rarely to have been made eunuchs. Several important offices of state among the “men of the pen” were held by eunuchs, and now and then one rose to high place in the army or administration.
Other Ottoman Slave-Families
Every great officer of the Ottoman court built up a slave-family after the model of the Ruling Institution. The grand vizier had a very large establishment; the viziers somewhat smaller ones; the governors of provinces had households in proportion to their incomes; each adult prince kept a miniature government.
The grand vizier Ibrahim, Iskender Chelebi the defterdar, and Rustem Pasha each maintained educational systems, standing armies, courts, and governing establishments modeled on the sultan’s. Such imitation might easily become a danger, but a prompt remedy ordinarily existed: every such household was strictly personal, gathered about a living man who was himself a slave of the sultan. Let him show the least movement toward treason, and his head would be removed, his property would come to his master, his household would be incorporated with the central slave-family.
Moslem private citizens also kept slave-families as numerous as they could afford, emulating the missionary and educational character of the greater households, but never daring to attempt imitation of the military features. The whole Ottoman system discouraged great accumulations of wealth so effectively that private citizens could never hope to compete with the power of officials and the sultan.
Relation to the Mamluk System
The Mamluk system of Egypt (1250-1517) is the nearest actual parallel to the Ottoman Ruling Institution. Like the Ottoman system, the Mamluks recruited their ruling class from purchased slaves (primarily from the Caucasus and the steppe lands), trained them in a rigorous military and religious education, and placed them in complete control of the state. However, the Ottoman system differed crucially in that the sultan’s own authority remained supreme above his slaves, whereas the Mamluk system became a self-perpetuating military aristocracy in which even the sultan was chosen from among the slave-officers. The Ottoman system subdued and outlived the Mamluk system, incorporating Egypt after 1517 while adapting the Mamluk governing structure under Ottoman pashas.
Relation to the Crimean Tartars
The Crimean Khanate served as a vital external source of slaves for the Ottoman system. The Tartars conducted regular slave-raiding expeditions into the steppe lands north of the Black Sea — Southern Poland, Russia, and the Caucasus — and the captives were sold in the slave-markets of Caffa (Kefe) and Constantinople. The Crimean khans, though vassals of the Ottoman sultan, maintained their own dynastic government and did not themselves participate in the devşirme system; their contribution to the Ruling Institution was through the market, supplying many of the purchased slaves who entered the sultan’s service, particularly from the Caucasus region.
Character of Ottoman Slavery
Ottoman slavery was a very different institution from that which Anglo-Saxons have practised. In it there could ordinarily be no color-line, and therefore no ineffaceable distinction. Where difference in color existed, it counted for nothing, by old Islamic custom.
Nor did the fact of slavery impart any indelible taint. Islam knew slave and free, but it knew no intermediate class of freedmen. Private citizens had the power of emancipation and often exercised it as a meritorious act. The slave who was set free was immediately in possession of full rights. Slavery had therefore no inherent quality; it was merely an accidental misfortune from which complete recovery was possible. The idea of Aristotle — that some men are born to be slaves — was wholly absent.
The Moslem religion teaches kindness and benevolence to all but armed enemies of the faith. Moslem masters, in constant personal association with persons whose condition led them to strive to please, were apt to become very friendly toward them. Emancipation was common, and not only the sultan but many of his subjects did not hesitate to give their daughters in marriage to worthy slaves. A slave was often beloved above a son — a son possessed a character which was more or less a matter of chance, but a slave had been selected.
Distinct from Classical and Plantation Slavery
Ottoman slavery was fundamentally distinct from classical (Greco-Roman) slavery in several respects:

Race and color: Roman slavery often carried ethnic and cultural stigmas that persisted even after manumission; Ottoman slavery had no color-line and no hereditary taint.
Assimilation: The Ottoman system was a machine for assimilation and conversion, actively incorporating slaves into the ruling nation; classical slavery tended to maintain a permanent servile class.
Occupational range: Ottoman slaves could and did reach the highest offices of state — grand vizier, general, governor; in classical and plantation slavery, such ascent was virtually impossible.
No chattel permanence: In plantation slavery (particularly the Atlantic system), the slave condition was lifelong, hereditary, and racially defined. In the Ottoman system, the children of kullar were free Moslems and could not inherit their father’s position in the Ruling Institution. The system was self-consuming: it did not perpetuate a slave caste.

Thus the Ottoman Ruling Institution was, as Lybyer concludes, “a wonderful fabric of slavery” — perhaps the largest, most powerful, and most honorable slave-family in history, better provided for and rewarded, more obedient and more contented, than any other the world has known.
The Missionary Motive
Although almost every member of the governing group in the Ottoman Empire had been born a Christian, it was absolutely necessary for his advancement that he should profess the Moslem faith. The fundamental rule was very simple: every member of the Ruling Institution must have been born a Christian and must have become a Mohammedan.
The missionary purpose stands out clearly in the words attributed to Kara Khalil Chendereli, the traditional founder of the Janissary corps:

“The conquered are slaves of the conquerors, to whom their goods, their women, and their children belong as a lawful possession; in converting the children to Islam by force, and in enrolling them as soldiers in the service of the faith, one is working for their happiness in this world and their eternal salvation.”

Mohammed the Conqueror expressed the idea poetically: “Our empire is the home of Islam; from father to son the lamp of our empire is kept burning with oil from the hearts of the infidels.”
The Requirement of Conversion
Conversion was a principal object of the system, and favor and promotion waited as rewards upon acceptance of the Moslem faith. Yet conversion of the neophytes seems not ordinarily to have been forcible. The Ottomans were too wise to believe that the best results could be accomplished by such means. Their policy was rather to throw every difficulty in the way of remaining a Christian, and to offer every inducement to make the Moslem faith and system seem attractive. The educational scheme involved complete isolation from Christian ideas of every sort and complete saturation in all the ideas of Mohammedanism — religious, moral, social, and political. Even those whose education was mainly physical were isolated from Christians in a strict Moslem environment.
Sincerity of Conversion
Not all who turned Moslem did so without mental reservation. The grand vizier Rustem told Busbecq, after offering him great rewards if he would turn Moslem, that he believed in the salvation of those of other faiths. A deli (scout) in his service confided to a French gentleman that while he pretended to follow Mohammedanism, he was a Christian at heart. The probability is that large numbers of the sultan’s slaves were merely nominal Mohammedans in religious belief, though they necessarily followed the larger part of the Moslem scheme of life.
The Two Great Institutions Compared
Lybyer’s framework requires understanding the Ruling Institution in contrast to the Moslem Institution. The Ruling Institution consisted of men born of Christian parents who became slaves of the sultan; they wielded the sword, the pen, and the scepter, conducting the whole of government except the rendering of justice in matters controlled by the Sacred Law. The Moslem Institution consisted of men born of Moslem parents, born and brought up free; they were the educators, priests, jurisconsults, and judges who embodied and maintained Mohammedan learning, religion, and law.
As Marcantonio Barbaro, Venetian Bailo from 1568 to 1573, first discerned clearly: “The arms and the forces are wholly reposed in the hands of persons all born Christians” while “the administration of the laws is all solely in the hands of those who are born Turks.”
Key Terminology





















































































TermOttoman ScriptMeaningkulقولslave of the sultankapıkuluقپوقولیslave of the Portedevşirmeدوشیرمه”collecting” — the child tributeiç oğlanايج اوغلانpalace pageacemi oğlanعجمی اوغلان”foreign youth” — apprentice Janissaryyeniçeriيڭیچرى”new troop” — Janissaryenderunاندرون”interior” — palace school and inner servicebirunبرون”outside” — the outer palace servicehas odaخاص اوده”private chamber” — elite pagesmuteferrikaمتفرهNoble Guardbostancıبستانجیgardener (palace service corps)kanun-namehقانون نامهbook of lawsodalarاوده لرchambers (of pages or Janissaries)dar-ul-harbدار الحربland of war (non-Moslem territory)dar-ul-Islamدار الإسلامland of Islam

concepts/silk-route-under-ottoman.title: The Silk Route under the Ottoman Empire
concepts/silk-route-under-ottoman.content:
The Silk Route under the Ottoman Empire
The Silk Route (İpek Yolu / ايپك يولو) was the economic backbone of the early Ottoman Empire. Under Ottoman dominion in the fourteenth through sixteenth centuries, the overland caravan routes linking Iran to Anatolia and the Mediterranean shifted decisively westward, making Bursa the foremost international emporium for raw Persian silk in the Mediterranean world. The Ottoman state’s control over these routes, its regulation of the silk trade through customs and the bedestan system, and the role of Venetian, Genoese, and Florentine merchants in Bursa together created a commercial system that sustained Ottoman prosperity until the rise of European maritime routes and Ottoman-Persian conflicts disrupted it.
The Shift of Trade Routes to Western Anatolia
With the establishment of the Mongol Empire in the thirteenth century, Anatolia became the highway for east-west trade. Italian merchants from the maritime republics met caravans from the Far East and Iran at Ayas on the Cilician coast, Trebizond on the Black Sea, and inland at Sivas and Konya. An imperial highway linked Tabriz (تبريز) to Konya through Erzurum (ارض روم), Erzincan (ارزنجان), and Sivas (سيواس). Twenty-three caravanserais from the Seljuk period still stand on the Sivas-Konya road, attesting to the density of this traffic.
The fall of the Ilkhanid Empire in Iran in the fourteenth century and the simultaneous rise of the Ottomans in western Anatolia caused the commercial centre of gravity to move westward. The old emporia of Palatia, Altoluogo (Ephesus), and Smyrna (Izmir / ازمير) came under Ottoman control and were linked to Bursa. By extending his dominions eastward to Erzincan through Amasya (آماسيه) and Tokat (توقات), Bayezid I (يلدريم بايزيد) gained control of the Tabriz-Bursa caravan route. Persian silk caravans now abandoned the Trebizond route and travelled overland to Bursa instead. In the fifteenth century, Amasya and Tokat — situated on this route — became after Bursa the most important economic and cultural cities in Anatolia.
Bursa as the Primary Silk Centre and Entrepot
Bursa (بروسه) was the heart of the Ottoman silk trade. Already in 1333, Ibn Battuta described it as “a great city with fine bazaars and broad streets,” and Orhan Ghazi was noted as the richest of the Turcoman sultans in Anatolia. In 1340 Orhan constructed a bedestan (بيدستان) — an enclosed, fortified market for the sale of valuable goods — which laid the institutional foundation for Bursa’s commercial rise.
By the end of the fourteenth century, the German traveller Schiltberger compared Bursa’s silk trade and industry with that of Damascus (دمشق) and Caffa (كفه), recording that Persian silk was sent from Bursa to Venice (ونيزيك) and Lucca — then the centre of the European silk industry. Trade in Persian silk was the mainstay of Bursa’s development and prosperity. In the fifteenth century, as the European silk industry expanded greatly, Bursa became the international marketplace for the basic raw material of the industry: the extremely fine silk of Asterabad (استرآباد) and Gilan (گيلان) in northern Iran.
The scale of the trade was substantial. In Bursa, about a thousand silk looms worked through five fardelli (each approximately 150 kg) of raw silk per day. An average caravan brought about two hundred fardelli. The price of a fardello rose from fifty akces (آقچه) in 1467 to seventy in 1488 and eighty-two in 1494. In Italy, each fardello yielded a profit of seventy to eighty gold ducats. The customs receipts from silk in Bursa reflect both the volume and the volatility of this trade:

































YearCustoms Receipts (gold ducats)148740,000150833,000151243,000152113,000152317,000155724,000
The sudden decline after 1512 was a direct result of the Ottoman-Persian wars. Receipts recovered partially after the Peace of Amasya in 1555 but never regained their fifteenth-century peak.
The Tabriz-Bursa Route
The primary artery of the silk trade ran from Tabriz, the great commercial capital of northwestern Iran, across Anatolia to Bursa. The route passed through Erzurum, Erzincan, Sivas, and Tokat before descending to Amasya and then westward to Bursa. Caravans also followed a southerly branch from Tabriz through Van (وان), Bitlis (بتليس), Diyarbekir (ديار بكر), and Birecik (بيرجك), reaching Aleppo (حلب) rather than Bursa.
Security along these routes was maintained by the Ottoman state. Caravanserais (كاروانسرا) built by sultans and high-ranking statesmen dotted the roads at intervals of a day’s march, providing shelter, stabling, and provisions. The state also policed the routes. When in 1501 a load of Florentine silk was stolen near Foca (فوشه), the sultan immediately sent officers who recovered part of the load and compelled the local population to pay for the remainder — demonstrating the importance the Ottomans placed on maintaining the security and reliability of the silk route.
Venetian and Genoese Merchants in Bursa
For the Italian maritime republics, Bursa was the closest and richest market in which to purchase eastern goods and sell European woollens. Venetian and Genoese merchants from Constantinople and Galata (غلطه) regularly travelled to Bursa to trade. The Genoese had concluded a commercial agreement with the Ottomans as early as 1352 and enjoyed a long-term monopoly over alum production in the region — alum being essential for fixing dyes in the textile industry.
Florentine merchants became increasingly active in the Bursa market after Sultan Mehmed II granted new trade privileges to Florence in 1469, during the Ottoman-Venetian war of 1463-79. At that time some fifty Florentine houses were trading in the empire, and Bursa became an emporium for the sale of Florentine cloth to Anatolia and Iran, with Florentine traders buying Persian silk in return. The representative in Bursa of the Medici and other Florentine houses, Giovanni Maringhi, left detailed letters recording that in 1501 several silk caravans arrived in Bursa from Iran each year. His correspondence reflects the impatience with which the Italian merchants awaited the caravans, the rush to buy the goods, and the fierce competition among them.
In the broader Mediterranean context, Levantine trade remained dominated by Venice, whose merchants operated under capitulations (امتيازات / imtiyazat) that fixed customs duty at 2 per cent. The Venetian bailo resided in Istanbul, and Venetian galleys regularly carried Bursa silks, spices, and cottons to the markets of Europe.
The Ottoman Regulation of the Silk Trade
The Ottoman state regulated the silk trade through a carefully structured system. The bedestan in Bursa was the central clearing-house for all transactions in valuable goods. Here, raw silk imported from Iran was sold under the supervision of state-appointed brokers, doorkeepers, and night-watchmen. The government controlled weights, measures, and pricing, and customs duties (gumruk / گمرك) were collected on all silk entering the city.
At the level of production, the silk industry of Bursa operated through a hierarchically organised guild system. The silk merchants who dealt in raw silk formed a guild that acted as entrepreneur. They bought imported raw silk in the bedestan, gave it to the twisters’ guild to be worked into thread, passed it to the dyers’ guild to be coloured, and finally sold the prepared skeins to the weavers. These dependent guilds were known as yamaks (ياماق). The state typically supported the dominant merchant guild in disputes, since disruptions caused unemployment and a drop in tax revenues.
Customs receipts from silk were a significant source of state revenue. At their height in 1512, silk customs in Bursa yielded 43,000 gold ducats annually. The state also controlled the alum trade (essential for textile dyeing), granted as a monopoly to the Genoese and later to Venice for 25,000 gold ducats in 1546.
The Impact of Ottoman-Persian Conflicts on Silk Routes
The ongoing conflict between the Ottoman Empire and Safavid Iran had a direct and disruptive effect on the silk trade. The Safavids, who established Shiism as the state religion of Iran in 1501, presented both a political and ideological challenge to the Sunni Ottomans. The wars that followed — particularly the campaigns of Selim I (1514, Battle of Chaldiran), Suleyman I (1534-35, capturing Tabriz and Baghdad), and the exhausting war of 1578-90 — repeatedly interrupted the flow of Persian silk to Bursa.
The customs receipts table above shows the impact. Receipts fell from 43,000 gold ducats in 1512 to 13,000 in 1521, a drop of 70 per cent. This collapse was directly attributable to the disruption of the Tabriz-Bursa route by the Ottoman-Safavid wars. Although a partial recovery followed the Peace of Amasya (1555), which established a temporary boundary between the two empires, receipts reached only 24,000 gold ducats by 1557 — barely more than half the 1512 level.
The Ottomans attempted to address the problem by bringing the silk-producing regions themselves under their control. In the sixteenth century they tried to bring the north Persian centres of silk production at Shirvan (شيروان) and Gilan directly under Ottoman rule. Suleyman’s campaign of 1534-35 forced the local dynasts in Gilan and Shirvan to recognise Ottoman suzerainty. Later, during the war of 1578-90, the Ottomans annexed all the western provinces of Iran from the Caucasus to Nehavend, including the silk lands. However, these gains proved temporary, and the Safavids eventually reasserted control.
The Silk Trade through Aleppo and the Levant
After the Ottoman conquest of Syria and Egypt in 1516-17, Aleppo emerged as a rival centre of the silk trade. Silk caravans from Iran reached Aleppo through Erzurum and along the Euphrates valley, or more often from Tabriz through Van, Bitlis, Diyarbekir, and Birecik. With the conquest of the Mamluk lands, the Ottomans gained control of all outlets for Persian silk open to Europeans — every route from Iran to the Mediterranean was now in Ottoman hands.
Aleppo functioned as both a terminus for overland silk caravans and a distribution centre for the Levantine ports. European merchants, particularly the Venetians and later the French and English, established consulates and fondacos (merchant compounds) in Aleppo. The city’s bedestan and covered markets handled not only silk but also spices, dyestuffs, drugs, and Indian textiles. The Aleppo route thus complemented the Bursa route, providing a southern alternative that remained active even when the northern route through Anatolia was disrupted by war.
Damascus also played a role. The caravan route from Damascus to Bursa was described by Bertrandon de la Brocquiere in 1432. He joined a group of pilgrims and merchants returning from Mecca with a caravan of three thousand camels near Damascus, and after a journey of some fifty days reached Bursa, where he found Florentine merchants and Genoese from Pera who had come to buy spices.
The Bedestan and Caravanserai System
The institutional infrastructure of the silk route rested on two Ottoman institutions: the bedestan (fortified market) and the caravanserai. The bedestan served as a secure repository for valuable goods, with stone domes and iron doors that resisted both fire and pillage. It functioned as a central exchange where merchants could buy and sell silk under government supervision, and where state-appointed brokers facilitated transactions.
Caravanserais provided the physical security that made long-distance overland trade feasible. Along the major routes, these inns were spaced a day’s march apart — typically 30 to 40 kilometres. They offered accommodation for merchants, stabling for animals, storage for goods, and often a small mosque, bath, and market. Many were built as vakifs (وقف / charitable endowments), ensuring their perpetual maintenance. The most famous surviving examples from the Ottoman period include the Buyuk Han in Bursa and the Kurlu Han on the Istanbul-Bursa road.
The Decline of the Overland Silk Route
Several converging factors brought about the decline of the overland silk route in the late sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.
First, the Ottoman-Persian wars of 1578-90, while bringing temporary Ottoman gains, devastated the silk-producing regions of Iran and disrupted the caravans for decades. The cost of continuous warfare strained Ottoman finances and diverted state attention away from commercial infrastructure.
Second, the Portuguese discovery of the sea route to India around the Cape of Good Hope (1498) and their subsequent domination of the Indian Ocean began to redirect the spice trade away from the Levant. Although the overland spice trade proved surprisingly resilient — Venetian purchases in Alexandria remained at pre-Vasco da Gama levels into the 1560s — the long-term trend was toward maritime routes.
Third, European maritime powers — first Portugal, then England and Holland — developed direct sea routes to the sources of silk and spices. The English Levant Company, chartered in 1581, and the Dutch, who received capitulations in 1612, increasingly brought Asian goods to Europe by sea rather than through the overland Ottoman routes. The English attempt to establish a route through Moscow, Iran, and Hormuz, though initially blocked by the Ottomans, pointed toward a future in which the overland silk route would be bypassed entirely.
Fourth, the emergence of the Atlantic economy shifted the centre of European commercial gravity away from the Mediterranean. The rise of Antwerp, Amsterdam, and London as entrepots for Asian goods eroded the competitive position of Bursa, Aleppo, and the other Ottoman silk markets.
Fifth, the influx of cheap European silver from the Americas after the 1580s — the Price Revolution — disrupted Ottoman monetary stability and undermined the traditional foundations of the silk trade. Silver coins flooded the Levantine market, causing inflation that eroded the profits of both merchants and the state.
By the seventeenth century, Bursa had lost its status as the premier silk emporium of the Mediterranean, though its silk industry survived in diminished form. The great age of the overland Silk Route, in which Persian silk travelled from Tabriz to Bursa and on to Venice and Lucca, had passed. The Ottoman Empire, once at the centre of this trade, became progressively more dependent on European commercial intermediaries — a transformation that Inalcik identifies as a turning point in Ottoman economic history.
Key Terms in Ottoman Turkish





















































































TermOttoman TurkishMeaningIpek Yoluايپك يولوSilk Road / Silk RouteBursaبروسهBursa, the primary silk entrepotBedestanبيدستانFortified market for precious goodsGumrukگمركCustoms house or customs dutyAkceآقچهOttoman silver coinKervanكاروانCaravanKervansarayكاروانسراCaravanserai (roadside inn)VakifوقفCharitable endowment (pl. evkaf)YamakياماقDependent or subordinate guildImtiyazatامتيازاتCapitulations / commercial privilegesFardello(Italian)Bale of silk, approx. 150 kgTabrizتبريزTabriz, the Iranian silk sourceGilanگيلانGilan, silk-producing region of IranShirvanشيروانShirvan, silk-producing region of the CaucasusHalepحلبAleppo, southern silk entrepot

concepts/spice-routes-under-ottoman.title: Spice Routes Under the Ottoman Empire
concepts/spice-routes-under-ottoman.content:
Spice Routes Under the Ottoman Empire

The two principal arteries of the eastern spice trade before the Portuguese discovery of the Cape route. Gold: Red Sea route (Cochin/Calicut → Aden → Jidda → Suez → Cairo → Alexandria → Venice). Teal: Persian Gulf route (Cochin → Hormuz → Baghdad → Aleppo/Damascus → Mediterranean).
The Traditional Spice Route
Before the Portuguese discovery of the sea route around Africa, the spice trade between Asia and Europe flowed through two principal arteries of the Islamic world: the Red Sea route from India to Egypt and Syria, and the Persian Gulf route through Baghdad to the Mediterranean. Of these, the Red Sea route — known in Ottoman Turkish as Bahâr-ı Hind (India Spice Route) — was the most important. Spices from India, the Moluccas, Java, and Sumatra were carried by Indian and Arab merchant vessels across the Indian Ocean to the port of Aden at the mouth of the Red Sea. From there they were shipped northward to Jidda, the seaport of Mecca, or to the Yemeni ports of Mocha and Kamaran. At Jidda the cargoes were offloaded onto smaller vessels or loaded onto camel caravans for the journey across the desert to Cairo.
The classical itinerary was: Alexandria — Cairo — Suez — Jidda — Aden — Indian Ocean. European merchants, above all the Venetians, purchased their spices in Alexandria and Cairo, whence the goods had travelled up the Nile from the Red Sea ports. Every year some twenty ships, laden with spices, arrived at Jidda alone. Ottoman pilgrims returning from Mecca carried spices, dyes, and Indian cloth back with them, supplementing the organised commercial traffic.
Alexandria and Cairo as Spice Entrepôts
Alexandria was the great Mediterranean terminus of the spice trade. In 1554 the Venetians alone bought six thousand quintals of spices in Alexandria, and their annual purchase of twelve thousand quintals between 1560 and 1564 matched the volume of trade before Vasco da Gama’s discovery of the sea route to India. Cairo functioned as the inland clearing-house: spices arriving from Jidda via the Red Sea port of Suez were transported by caravan across the desert to Cairo, where they were stored, taxed, and re-exported either to Alexandria for European buyers or overland to Syria and Asia Minor.
Bursa, the early Ottoman capital, also remained a major spice market for another century after Istanbul became the capital. In 1487 annual customs receipts from saffron, gumlac, and pepper imported into Bursa reached about two thousand gold ducats. Florentine and Genoese merchants came to Bursa to buy spices. A wealthy Damascene merchant named Abû Bakr sold a single shipment of spices worth four thousand gold ducats in Bursa in 1500. The Bursa customs register of 1545 shows European merchants actively buying spices there, and by 1582 customs receipts on spices in Bursa had reached 7,250 gold ducats — four times what they had been in 1487.
The Ottoman Conquest of Egypt (1517) and Its Impact
The conquest of the Mamlûk Sultanate by Selîm I in 1516–17 brought Syria, Egypt, and the Hejaz under Ottoman control. This was a watershed for the spice trade. The Ottomans now held both termini of the eastern spice routes — the Red Sea ports and the Mediterranean emporia — and could regulate the flow of spices from India to Europe. The immediate strategic impetus was defensive: in 1509 the Portuguese had destroyed a Mamlûk fleet at Chaul, and the Mamlûk ruler had turned to the Ottomans for assistance. The Ottomans sent matériel and craftsmen to Suez to build ships. In 1517, while still in Cairo, Selîm I ordered the construction of a fleet at Suez to drive the Portuguese out of the Indian Ocean.
The conquest gave the Ottomans control over the customs revenues of Egypt and Syria, which together provided one-third of the entire imperial budget income in 1528. The Egyptian budget alone remitted half a million gold ducats annually to the imperial Treasury in Istanbul. Spices, sugar, and drugs for the Palace cost 13,866 ducats in 1532 — a sign of how deeply the spice trade was woven into the fabric of Ottoman state finance and court consumption.
Ottoman Customs and the Taxation of Spice
The Ottoman state extracted revenue from the spice trade through a layered system of customs duties levied at every major entrepôt: Alexandria, Cairo, Damascus, Aleppo, Bursa, and Istanbul. In 1562 customs levied at Damascus on spices brought by the pilgrim caravans rose to 110,000 gold ducats. European merchants bought spices at Damascus and exported them through Beirut, while large portions of the cargo were forwarded to Bursa and Istanbul and thence to the Balkans and beyond.
But the Ottoman customs regime was not merely extractive: it also shaped the geography of the trade. The high duties — which the English later complained made pepper prices in Ottoman ports three times as high as in directly-purchased Atlantic routes — reflected the Ottoman understanding of the empire as the gatekeeper of East-West commerce. The mukâta’a (tax-farming) system was widely applied to customs collections, and the rapacity of customs officials was noted by contemporaries. The Ottoman historian Âlî attributed the decline in the number of ships arriving in the Red Sea from India — a drop from twenty ships per year to three or four — primarily to the corruption and overreach of customs officials.
The Portuguese Diversion and Its Devastating Impact

The Cape Route (red) bypassing the traditional eastern routes (grey), with Portuguese fortified factories (red squares). Key milestones: Dias rounds the Cape (1488), da Gama arrives in Calicut (1498).
The Portuguese discovery of the sea route around the Cape of Good Hope, culminating in Vasco da Gama’s arrival at Calicut in 1498, was the single greatest disruption to the eastern spice trade since the rise of Islam. The Portuguese established a system of cartaz (naval passes) and fortified factories at Hormuz, Goa, Cochin, and Malacca, systematically interdicting Muslim shipping in the Indian Ocean and diverting the flow of spices around Africa to Lisbon.
By 1501 the Portuguese had already begun to transport spices directly from India to Europe. The official price of pepper in Edirne in 1501 was eighteen gold ducats a kantâr (about 56 kg), but in Pera the price could spike to twenty-seven gold ducats when new supplies failed to arrive — a direct consequence of Portuguese disruption of the traditional routes.
The impact on Ottoman customs revenues was severe. The spice trade through the Red Sea — which had furnished a major portion of Egyptian and Syrian customs income — contracted dramatically. Where twenty ships had arrived annually at Jidda from India, by the early seventeenth century only three or four came. The Ottoman treasury lost the customs revenue that had been one of the pillars of Egyptian fiscal surplus. The transit trade of spices through Bursa declined as the Portuguese undercut the eastern route prices in European markets.
Yet the damage was not immediate. The Portuguese failed, despite all their efforts, to sever the trade routes entirely. Throughout the sixteenth century spices continued to flow through the Red Sea and the Persian Gulf, and the Levantine trade proved remarkably resilient.
The Competition Between Venice and Portugal
The rivalry between Venice and Portugal for the spice trade defined the commercial politics of the sixteenth century. Venice had been the principal European distributor of eastern spices for centuries, buying in Alexandria and Cairo and selling throughout Europe. The Portuguese crown, by contrast, sought to monopolise the Atlantic route, selling spices in Antwerp and northern Europe at prices that undercut the Venetian supply.
The competition was fierce and closely balanced. In 1554 the Venetians bought six thousand quintals of spices in Alexandria, and between 1560 and 1564 their annual purchases of twelve thousand quintals matched pre-da Gama volumes. The Lisbon market underwent periodic crises as a result: in 1564 a Portuguese spy in Egypt informed his government that thirty thousand quintals of spices had arrived in Alexandria — a volume that threatened to collapse the Portuguese monopoly price. The fact that Venice could still obtain such quantities through Ottoman ports demonstrates that the Ottoman spice routes were far from moribund in the mid-sixteenth century.
Venetian merchants brought cloth to Istanbul and bought spices there as late as 1590. The Hungarian trade offers a telling illustration: in 1547 a Hungarian merchant brought kersey (a coarse woollen cloth) to Bursa and purchased 110 kantârs of spices, but by the mid-sixteenth century Hungary had begun to receive spices from the west — a sign that the Portuguese route was gradually displacing the eastern supply even in territories adjacent to the Ottoman Empire.
Attempts to Redirect Trade Through the Red Sea

Ottoman naval campaigns in the Indian Ocean: Selmân’s defense of Jidda and securing of Aden (1517-1538, green), the failed Diu expedition (1538, gold), and the East Africa campaign reaching Mombasa (1585, orange).
The Ottomans did not passively accept the Portuguese disruption of the spice trade. Under Selîm I and Süleymân I, the Ottoman state mounted a sustained naval campaign to challenge Portuguese dominance in the Indian Ocean and reassert control over the spice routes.
The Ottoman admiral Selmân repulsed Portuguese attacks on Jidda in 1517 and 1525, then advanced on Yemen and Aden, securing the southern approaches to the Red Sea. In 1538 the Ottomans sent a fleet of thirty ships from Suez to expel the Portuguese from Diu in northern India. The expedition failed — the local Muslim ruler of Gujarat, fearing Ottoman conquest rather than liberation, refused to cooperate — but the Ottomans successfully subjugated Yemen and Aden in the same period. By the 1550s the Ottomans controlled the entire Red Sea coastline and the Yemeni ports that commanded the sea route to India.
In the second half of the sixteenth century the Portuguese even permitted the sale of spices to the Near East in Hormuz, tacitly acknowledging that they could not completely sever the Red Sea and Persian Gulf routes. The Ottomans maintained a fleet at Suez throughout the century, and as late as 1585 an Ottoman fleet sailed from Suez to capture Portuguese factories on the East African coast, with the Prince of Mombasa recognising Ottoman suzerainty. These successes did not last: the Spanish-Portuguese Union under Philip II (1580) brought renewed naval pressure, and the Ottoman African project ended in failure.
After 1600 the Dutch and English, with superior ships and organisational capacity, came to dominate the Indian Ocean. By 1613 Dutch and English pirates were operating on the Red Sea itself. The English East India Company, founded in 1600, preferred to buy spices directly from India rather than through Ottoman intermediaries, since customs duties levied in Ottoman ports meant that pepper prices were three times as high in Near Eastern ports. From 1614 the English began to sell pepper from Java and Sumatra directly in Ottoman ports — a sign that the traditional direction of trade had reversed. Eventually none of the Indian trade with the West passed through the Near East.
Long-Term Economic Effects
The long-term economic effects of the Portuguese diversion of the spice trade were profound and cumulative. The Ottoman Empire lost control of the most lucrative branch of Eurasian commerce, and with it the customs revenues that had enriched the Egyptian and Syrian treasuries. The mukâta’a contracts for spice customs, which had generated tens of thousands of gold ducats annually, shrank to insignificance. The decline in shipping in the Red Sea — from twenty ships annually to three or four — reflected not just Portuguese interdiction but the structural shift of the world’s most valuable trade to the Atlantic.
The loss of the spice route’s customs revenues contributed to the broader fiscal crisis of the late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries. Egypt and Syria, which had provided one-third of the imperial budget in 1528, could no longer sustain that proportion. The Ottoman treasury became increasingly dependent on other revenue sources — the cizye (poll tax on non-Muslims), the avariz (emergency levies), and the manipulation of the coinage — all of which had distorting effects on the economy.
The diversion of Indian Ocean trade to the Atlantic also isolated the Ottoman Empire commercially. Where Bursa, Aleppo, and Cairo had once been central nodes in a global network of exchange — connecting India, Iran, Anatolia, and Europe — they became regional markets serving a contracting imperial economy. The rise of the Atlantic powers (Portugal, then the Dutch Republic and England) displaced the Ottoman Empire from the commercial revolution that would transform the world economy in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.
The spice trade’s decline was not, however, a simple story of Ottoman passivity. Inalcik shows that the Ottoman spice routes remained active throughout the sixteenth century, sustained by Venetian demand, the resilience of Muslim merchant networks, and periodic Ottoman naval offensives. The decline was gradual, contested, and intimately connected to the broader story of Ottoman naval power, fiscal administration, and commercial policy. By the early seventeenth century, however, the combination of Portuguese, Dutch, and English maritime supremacy had decisively shifted the centre of gravity of the Eurasian spice trade from the Red Sea and the Persian Gulf to the Cape of Good Hope and the Atlantic. The Ottoman Empire, which had once stood at the crossroads of the world’s most valuable trade, found itself on the margins of the new commercial order.

concepts/sufi-orders.title: Ottoman Sufi Orders (Dervish Orders)
concepts/sufi-orders.content:
Ottoman Sufi Orders (Dervish Orders / طريقت)
The Two Worlds of Ottoman Religious Life
Inalcik distinguishes two broad currents of religious life in the Ottoman Empire. Among the educated urban elite and the Palace, high Persian culture and the theosophical mysticism of Ibn al-Arabi (ابن عربى) prevailed. On the frontiers and among the Turcoman tribes, a popular Turkish culture of gazis and dervishes dominated, shaped by shamanist beliefs, tribal custom, and a form of Islam profoundly modified by Central Asian tradition.
The dervishes on the frontier, usually named baba, abdal or ahi, had been coming to Anatolia since the eleventh century with the waves of migrating Turcomans. They were the social and spiritual centres of the Turcoman tribes, like the old Turco-Mongol shamans. Arabic sources describe with amazement the hundred or so dervishes who came to Syria with Barak Baba in 1307: they hung bells and knucklebones around their necks, shaved their beards and let their moustaches grow, carried wooden swords or crooked cudgels, and danced to drums and pipes with vigorous movements.

Major Established Orders (Tarîkat)
Inalcik divides the religious orders of the Ottoman Empire into two main groups. The first group consisted of the established orders, with lodges supported by the income from vakifs which sultans or great men had founded, with a clearly defined organization and fixed rites and ceremonies. The most famous were the Nakşbendîs, the Mevlevîs, the Halvetîs, and their various branches. They usually settled in the cities and drew their novices from the upper ranks of society.
Nakşbendî (نقشبندى)
The Nakşbendî order was founded by Muhammad Nakshband (1317-89). It was among the most widespread of the established orders, maintaining close ties with the ulema and the Ottoman ruling class. The Nakşbendîs emphasized strict adherence to the Sharia alongside mystical practice, which made them generally acceptable to the orthodox ulema. They settled primarily in the cities and drew their membership from the upper ranks of society. The order’s influence grew steadily throughout the classical period, as its emphasis on sobriety, discipline, and conformity to Islamic law gave it a privileged position vis-a-vis the state.
Mevlevî (مولوى) — The Whirling Dervishes
The patron saint of the Mevlevî order was Maulana Jalal al-Din Rumi (1207-73), one of the world’s greatest mystical writers. He grew up in Konya, the Seljuk capital, where Ibn al-Arabi’s thought prevailed. He first achieved fame as a scholar and preacher, but at a certain stage in his life he became a saint, devoting his whole existence to mystic love. His lyrical verse tells how in mystical ecstasy he saw all differences of race, religion and creed disappear.
Rumi recognized music and dance — sema (سماع) — as the most effective means to attain mystic ecstasy. He was not a man to concern himself with rites and ceremonies, but in time a tarikat was founded in his name, taking its final form in the fifteenth century. The Mevlevîs became known as the “whirling dervishes” from their whirling dances in special dress.
When the order was founded its first centre was the “Seat of the Pir” in Konya, a central lodge built around Rumi’s tomb. Rumi’s successors sent representatives to other towns, approaching local governors and the ruling class to secure patronage and vakifs for the lodges. From the fifteenth century the Mevlevîs established themselves in many Ottoman cities as a tarikat appealing to the elite. All the Ottoman sultans, in particular Murad II, Bayezid II, Selim I and Murad III, took a close interest in the Mevlevîs. Murad II founded a large Mevlevî lodge in Edirne.
Mevlevî tekkes in the great cities acted as cultural centres and art academies. Since the ulema forbade the study of Persian in the medreses, the tekkes became centres for the study of Persian language and literature. Establishments called Dar al-Masnavi were founded with this as their sole function. The most famous commentaries on Rumi’s Masnavi were written in Turkey, usually in the light of Ibn al-Arabi’s philosophical system.
Mevlevî-ism established itself among Ottoman intellectuals, and particularly among the bureaucratic class who were imbued with Persian literary and cultural traditions. It was a major factor in creating classical Ottoman literature, which originally drew its main inspiration from Persian.
Halvetî (خلوتى)
The Halvetî order (from halvet, meaning spiritual retreat or seclusion) was among the most widespread of the established orders in the Ottoman Empire. It found particular favour among the Ottoman elite and ruling class. The order spread through a network of tekkes across the empire, and its branches proliferated throughout the classical period. The Halvetîs, like the Nakşbendîs and Mevlevîs, maintained lodges supported by vakif endowments founded by sultans and statesmen.
Kadirî (قادرى)
The Kadirî order, tracing its foundation to the great sufi Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani (d. 1166), was another of the established orders present in Ottoman lands. Like the other major orders, it maintained tekkes in the cities and drew members from across Ottoman society, though it is discussed less extensively in Inalcik’s treatment than the Nakşbendî, Mevlevî, and Halvetî orders, which he identifies as the three most famous.

The Bektaşî (بكتاشى) Order and Its Role
The Bektaşî order was to become the most important popular tarikat, gradually absorbing various other groups of dervishes — the abdal, kalenderî and haydarî — who had been preaching to the people since the fourteenth century.
The founder and patron saint was Hacci Bektas, a disciple of Baba Ishak who had led the rebellion of 1241. He was active in the second half of the thirteenth century in Seljuk Anatolia, on the Ankara-Kayseri trade route, an area with dense Turcoman population. In the Ottoman Empire, Bektasi-ism first became important when it spread among the Turcoman tribes and then when it became the tarikat of the Janissary corps.
Bektasi dervishes appeared on the Ottoman frontiers in Rumelia from the middle of the fourteenth century, adopting as their own Sari Saltuk, who had become the patron saint of the Ottoman gazis in Rumelia. By the fifteenth century, Bektasi-ism had established itself in the Janissary corps. Devsirme children sent to live in Turkish villages to learn Turkish and Islam tended towards popular forms of religion rather than sunni Islam. From the end of the sixteenth century Hacci Bektas was officially recognized as the patron saint of the Janissaries, and a Bektasi father permanently resided with the corps. The Bektaşî order and the Janissary corps became so inseparable that when a new dede was chosen as head of the order, he came to the Janissary barracks in Istanbul to be crowned by the aga of the Janissaries.
Bektasi-ism was particularly strong among Turcoman nomads and in the villages they had established. In the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries many of these nomads settled and founded villages. Bektasi-ism was a major factor in spreading Islam among the native Christian populations of Rumelia. The eclectic nature of this popular tarikat made Islam easily acceptable: it looked tolerantly on all religions, attached importance to the esoteric rather than externals, did not compel observance of Islamic rites such as ritual prayer and fasting, permitted wine-drinking, and allowed women to go unveiled and mix socially with men.
Bektasi-ism was a sect with beliefs composed of various elements drawn from a multitude of sources — from shamanism to the religious beliefs of the Balkan peoples. Shamanist influence is particularly clear in the ecstatic dances, and the ancient Turkish tradition of the ritual meal and remnants of shamanist stone and tree cults continued in Bektaşî rites. Women retained the position of freedom and equality with men which they had enjoyed in pre-Islamic Turkish society. Scholars have noted the influence of Christianity as well: the concept of the Trinity is reflected in the Bektaşî belief that God, Muhammad and Ali are one; bread, wine and cheese were offered to the novice in the ritual admitting him to the order; disciples confessed their sins to the seyh. From the sixteenth century celibacy was incumbent on dervishes living in tekkes.
The arrangement of the order was hierarchical, with the pir or dede at the head, below him the halifes or fathers, then the seyhs, and then the murids or muhibs. In the mid-seventeenth century Evliya Celebi wrote that there were seven hundred Bektaşî tekkes in the Ottoman Empire (likely an exaggeration), and at the beginning of the nineteenth century it was recorded that one-fifth of the population of Istanbul were Bektaşîs, with fourteen tekkes.

Heterodox and Secret Orders
The second group of religious orders were the secret orders, known usually as the Melâmîs or Melâmetîs. They sought not fame but blame and censure, avoided all ostentation and external organization, and their forms of worship were secret and esoteric. They established no links with the state and were opposed to authority. They lived off the fruits of their own labour, accepting no bequest or alms from the state.
Bayramî Order
The Bayramî order was a religious-social movement born of the upheaval after the Battle of Ankara (1402). Its founder was Hacci Bayram (d. 1430), a peasant from near Ankara and a Melâmî dervish. He wanted his disciples to subsist on the fruits of their labour; he and his followers tilled the fields together and disposed of the produce together. Murad II, sympathetic towards the dervishes, pardoned him and granted his disciples tax exemptions.
The Bayramî movement later split into two. One group accepted sunni Islam and state protection; its follower Aksemseddin became seyh to Mehmed the Conqueror and played an important part in the conquest of Istanbul. The other group remained faithful to the traditions of the Melâmîs, remaining a secret sect with links to the guilds in the towns.
Melâmîs and Hamzavîs
The Melâmîs formed a close-knit group around a kutb (pole) — a spiritual leader believed to be the centre of the universe. They organized secret meetings, tried the accused in their own courts, and had no wish to establish links with the state. They required their members to work at a trade and earn an honest living, condemning idleness.
In the sixteenth century, when the Melâmîs began to show Safavid leanings, the government pursued them ruthlessly. In 1529 the kutb Ismail Masuki was captured and with his twelve disciples executed on the Atmeydani in Istanbul. In 1561 a fetva of the seyhulislam Ebussuud condemned to death another Melami, Hamza Bali of Bosnia. Thereafter the Melâmîs were often known as Hamzavîs. Despite persecution, Melami-ism spread in the great cities and eventually even to the ruling classes.
Hurûfîs
The Hurûfî sect was founded by Fadlullah (d. 1394) in Iran. Fadlullah declared himself the manifestation of God and the Mahdi awaited by Muslims, Christians and Jews. He interpreted the Koran according to an ultra-esoteric system based on cabalistic interpretation of its letters, maintaining that “the world is eternal and creation is a continual process.” He did not believe in the hereafter.
Hurûfî-ism spread rapidly in Ottoman towns from the early fifteenth century. In 1444 the Hurûfîs formed a numerous sect in Edirne, and a Persian missionary had influence in the Palace. The sunni ulema reacted sharply; the missionary was burned and many followers had their tongues cut out. Violent persecution continued into the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, and a strong Hurûfî influence is apparent in Bektaşî thought.
The Kizilbaş (قزيلباش)
From the fifteenth century, extreme shiite doctrines from the Safavid order of Ardabil spread among the Turcomans, who became known as Kizilbaş — “red head” — from the red headdress they wore. The Kizilbaş movement was as much social and political as religious, expressing strong Turcoman opposition to Ottoman centralization. In 1511 the Kizilbaş under Sah Kulu raised a terrible rebellion in south-west Anatolia, and Selim I’s merciless repression and his victory at Chaldiran (1514) only temporarily halted the movement.
Kizilbaş beliefs were closely related to Bektaşî-ism, but the Turcomans were fanatical shiites, mixing tribal customs and shamanist beliefs. They recognized as heads of the tarikat the Celebis, claimed descendants of Hacci Bektas. The long struggle with the Kizilbaş strengthened the position of narrow sunni Islam in the Ottoman Empire.

The Tekke and Zaviye System
Dervishes founded innumerable zaviyes (زاويه — hospices) in newly conquered territories, which later became the nuclei of new Turkish villages. Turkish colonization rapidly followed conquests in Thrace as dervishes established these foundations. The government also established zaviyes near mountain passes, fords and bridges to maintain security on the roads, posting derbendcis (guardians of passes) at these locations.
The established orders had tekkes (تكيه — lodges) supported by the income from vakifs. Each order had its own standard and headdress, its own form of recitation and ceremony, and recognized a famous sufi, saint or companion of the Prophet as its patron.

Dervish Orders and the Guilds (Ahilik)
The early Ottoman ahi brotherhoods represented the intersection of guild organization and sufi spirituality. On Osman’s death, the meeting to choose his successor was held in the zaviye of Edebali’s nephew, Hasan the Ahi. Orhan and his son Suleyman later created hundreds of vakifs for these ahis and other dervishes in newly conquered areas, confirming the ahis’ important part in the establishment of the Ottoman state.
The futuwwa (فتوت) ethics which underlay guild organization shared common ground with sufi principles: absolute obedience, modesty, disdain for wealth, regarding greed and competition as the ugliest moral defects. The Melâmîs maintained particular links with the guilds, requiring their members to work at a trade.

Dervish Orders and Colonization
Dervishes played a critical role in the colonization of newly conquered territories. The Ottoman government encouraged Turks from Anatolia, sometimes by forcible deportation, to settle newly conquered lands, and dervishes founded innumerable zaviyes which became the nuclei of new Turkish villages. The Bektasi order was particularly active in spreading Islam among Balkan Christians; its tolerant and eclectic character made Islam easily acceptable to many Balkan peasants.
The Dobrudja region, where Sari Saltuk had been settled in 1261 with about forty Turcoman clans, remained throughout the Ottoman period a centre where Turcoman tribes, gazis and heretical dervishes thrived.

Conflicts with the Ulema
The relationship between the dervish orders and the orthodox ulema was often tense. The ulema regarded certain heterodox beliefs as heretical: the philosophy of monism (the unity of being — vahdet-i vucud), the esoteric interpretation of the Koran, latitudinarian attitudes toward the Sharia, and shiite tendencies all provoked condemnation. Seyh Bedreddin (d. 1416) was classed among the great scholars of Islamic law but was condemned as an extreme batinite who completely ignored the seriat.
The ulema also resented the influence of Ibn al-Arabi, whose works had many Ottoman commentators, though the seyhulislam Kemal Pasazade issued a fetva approving all his works and Selim I built a mausoleum over his tomb. In the sixteenth century, the observatory of Takiyuddin Mehmed was destroyed on the petition of the seyhulislam, who regarded astronomy as irreligious — a sign of the growing power of religious orthodoxy.
From time to time the government conducted investigations of heterodox groups and searched dervish lodges, forbidding wandering dervishes “who raised standards, played on the pipe and drum, and acted contrary to religion in all things” from travelling. The Kizilbaş faced systematic repression, and the Melâmîs and Hurûfîs were ruthlessly persecuted.

The Intellectual Mystical Tradition
Beyond popular dervish religion, mysticism was a main element in the thought of the Ottoman intellectual elite. This tradition goes back to the Seljuk period, when sultans welcomed scholars and mystics fleeing the Mongol invasion. Konya, Kayseri, Aksaray and Sivas became brilliant centres of mystical thought. The illuminism of al-Suhrawardi, the philosophy of Nasir al-Din of Tus, and above all the system of Ibn al-Arabi (d. 1240) — one of the greatest mystical theorists in the Islamic world — were prevalent in intellectual circles.
Ibn al-Arabi’s thought became a dominant influence in Turkish thought. His works had many Ottoman commentators: Davud of Kayseri, Kutbeddin of Iznik and Yazicizade Mehmed of Gallipoli in the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries, and Bali of Sofya (d. 1533) and Abdullah of Bosnia (d. 1660) in the sixteenth and seventeenth. Mehmed al-Fanari, founder of the Ottoman medrese tradition, clearly betrayed Ibn al-Arabi’s influence.

Key Ottoman Turkish Terms

concepts/the-janissaries-under-mehmed.title: The Janissaries Under Mehmed II
concepts/the-janissaries-under-mehmed.content:
The Janissaries Under Mehmed II
Origins and the Devsirme System
The Janissary corps (yeniçeri, يڭیچرى, “new troop”) was the standing infantry of the Ottoman sultans, raised through the devşirme (دوشیرمه, “collecting”) — the child levy system that took Christian boys from the Balkan and Anatolian subject populations, converted them to Islam, and trained them for state service. The devşirme was not a slave trade in the ordinary sense: the boys were selected for their physical and intellectual promise, raised in Turkish households to learn the language and faith, and then funneled into either the palace bureaucracy or the Janissary barracks. They became, in law and in fact, the personal property of the sultan — his kapıkulu (قپوقولی, “slave of the Porte”) — bound to him by a loyalty that owed nothing to clan, region, or aristocratic lineage.
The institution predated Mehmed. It was established during the reign of Murad I (1362–1389) and formalized under Murad II. But it was Mehmed II who transformed the Janissaries from a palace guard into the nucleus of a professional imperial army.
Mehmed’s Expansion of the Corps After 1451
When Mehmed II ascended the throne for the second time in 1451 — after a brief, turbulent first reign as a boy-sultan followed by his father Murad’s return — he moved immediately to consolidate power. Among his first acts was a thorough review of the state’s military and financial apparatus. Kritoboulos, the Greek historian of Imbros who wrote a panegyric life of Mehmed, describes the scene:

“Then he set about reviewing his entire empire, establishing law in the provinces of the nations under him, and removed some of the governors and installed others in their places, whom he considered superior to the others in military talent and intelligence, valor and integrity of soul.”

Kritoboulos then specifies Mehmed’s particular attention to the Janissaries:

“And then he inspected the military registers and troops, both cavalry and infantry, as well as the armies paid from the imperial treasury, but he devoted particular care to the imperial bodyguard, and also increased the forces, namely the so-called free infantry, and the troop around him, whom in their own language it is customary to call Janissaries, which means literally ‘new troop,’ knowing well that by this his power and the force protecting his life and the entire empire would increase.”

This passage, in Book I, section 26 of Kritoboulos’s history, captures Mehmed’s strategic vision. The Janissaries were not merely soldiers — they were the sultan’s personal instrument, a force answerable to no one but him, financed directly from the imperial treasury rather than through the timar (تیمار, land-grant) system that supported the provincial cavalry (sipahi, سپاهی). By expanding the corps, Mehmed was building a military establishment that would be loyal to the throne, not to the old Turkish aristocracy or the provincial notables.
The Janissary Discipline Incident
Mehmed’s relationship with the Janissaries was not one of simple patronage; it was forged through an iron discipline that he imposed early in his reign. Tursun Beg, the Ottoman bureaucrat and historian who served in Mehmed’s chancery, records a revealing episode that took place as the sultan set out on his first campaign after his second accession.
The Janissary corps lined the road in two armed ranks. When the sultan passed among them, they demanded a gift — a bonus for the campaign. Tursun Beg writes:

“The Janissary corps stood on the road, in two armed ranks. When the sultan came among them, they said: ‘This is our sultan’s first campaign — his servants require a gift.’ Yet the sultan’s gifts had already submerged the world in bounty. Raising his servants’ salaries was a matter that shone in his heart and constantly occupied his mind.”

What seems like a traditional request for a donative was, in Mehmed’s eyes, a provocation — a breach of military order, a test of his authority. Tursun continues:

“But their standing on the road in this improper manner and their impatient demanding of a grant did not sit well with his fine disposition, and he referred the matter to the court scholars (kapu uleması). When he encamped, a council (divan) was held. He commanded that their sergeants (yayabaşı) be brought in and said: ‘This rudeness is due to the deficiency of your wits,’ and having them subdued, gave each a hundred strokes of the rod, dismissed them, and drove them from his gate.”

The message was unmistakable. The men who had lined the road to pressure their young sultan were not punished collectively — their sergeants were publicly beaten and dismissed. Tursun concludes: “With this policy, the love of the sultan became established in the hearts of both those who [desired] justice and order and of others, and until the end of his reign they never set foot beyond the path of discipline.”
This was vintage Mehmed: he understood that the Janissaries’ power to make or break a sultan would grow with their numbers and prestige, and he met that challenge head-on, before they had tested him in war. The discipline held for thirty years.
The Janissaries in the Siege of Constantinople (1453)
When Mehmed’s massive army encamped before the Theodosian Walls in April 1453, the Janissaries were the elite strike force. Tursun Beg describes the camp: “The camp was set up according to Ottoman order, and the Janissary tents formed a circle, like the moon encompassing the new moon.”
During the siege itself, the Janissaries were assigned the sector of the wall at the Lycus Valley, the most critical point of assault. Tursun notes that as the siege progressed: “The gate-people and the Janissary corps had completed their duties, beaten down and demolished the areas assigned to them, and filled in and destroyed the deep moats.”
But it was in the final general assault of May 29, 1453, that the Janissaries played their decisive role. Kritoboulos gives the most detailed account. After the first waves of irregulars (azabs) and Anatolian troops had been repulsed with heavy losses, Mehmed held back his best soldiers:

“Emperor Mehmed, having seen that the troops engaged in the assault had suffered much in the battle and achieved no notable result, while the Romans and Italians fought strongly and triumphed in the combat, being greatly angered at this and not considering the matter tolerable any longer, brings forward all his troops, which he had kept for later: the best armed, the most daring, the most reliable, and by far more capable than the others in experience and strength — heavy infantry, archers, lancers, and the guard corps formed from the other troops and the so-called Janissaries, who constituted the flower of the camp.”

Then Mehmed himself led them forward: “He himself leads them, going at the very front, to the foot of the stone wall, as far as the ditch.” After the Genoese commander Giustiniani Longo was mortally wounded and his men abandoned their posts, the Janissaries and the other elite troops pushed through the palisade, entered the city through the Kerkoporta gate, and streamed into Constantinople.
Once inside, Kritoboulos records: “The Janissaries and other soldiers broke forcibly into the houses, without any order or plan.” The corps that Mehmed had expanded, disciplined, and personally led into battle had delivered him the greatest prize in Christendom.
From Slave-Soldiers to Political Force
Under Mehmed, the Janissaries evolved from a household guard of a few thousand into a standing army that, by the end of his reign, numbered perhaps 10,000–12,000 men. The expansion was deliberate: Mehmed needed a permanent, professional infantry to garrison his new capital, man the frontiers, and serve as a counterweight to the provincial cavalry and the old Turkish nobility.
But this expansion had a latent political logic. The Janissaries were, by design, detached from the networks of kinship and patronage that structured Ottoman society. They had no families in the normal sense — they were forbidden to marry until the later 15th century, and their children were excluded from the corps. They lived in barracks, received regular salaries from the treasury, and owed their advancement solely to the sultan’s favor.
Yet the very qualities that made them loyal also made them dangerous. The Janissaries were a corps with a strong corporate identity. They policed their own ranks, had their own traditions and ceremonies, and developed a keen sense of their collective rights — including the right to petition the sultan for better pay or conditions. The discipline incident at Mehmed’s accession showed that even as a young sultan, Mehmed understood the need to keep this corporate spirit in check. His solution was to mix ferocious punishment with generous reward: he raised their salaries (as Tursun notes, “Raising his servants’ salaries was a matter that shone in his heart”), gave them privileged access to the sultan’s person, and rewarded them lavishly from the plunder of Constantinople and later campaigns.
The Janissary Uprising After Mehmed’s Death
The structure Mehmed built was strong enough to contain the Janissaries during his lifetime, but it could not survive his death. When Mehmed died in 1481 — possibly poisoned, possibly from gout or complications of his incessant campaigning — the Janissaries immediately asserted themselves as kingmakers.
The succession pitted Mehmed’s two sons against each other: Bayezid II, the elder, who served as governor of Amasya, and Cem Sultan, the younger, who held the governorship of Karaman. Mehmed had reportedly favored Cem, but the Janissaries threw their weight behind Bayezid. When the grand vizier, Karamani Mehmed Pasha — a devout and capable administrator who had served Mehmed II loyally — attempted to control the succession and keep order in the capital, the Janissaries turned on him.
Karamani Mehmed Pasha was dragged from the vizieral palace and lynched by the Janissaries in the streets of Constantinople. His body was left in the public square. The uprising was a watershed moment: the corps that Mehmed had created as the sultan’s loyal instrument had, in the absence of a strong hand, become the arbiter of the succession. They demanded — and received — a substantial donative from Bayezid upon his accession, establishing a precedent that would haunt later sultans. The discipline that Tursun Beg had praised — “until the end of his reign they never set foot beyond the path of discipline” — shattered within days of the Conqueror’s death.
Legacy
