Mehmed II’s relationship with the Janissaries encapsulates the paradox of the institution he did so much to shape. He expanded their numbers, elevated their status, and relied on them as the cutting edge of his conquests. But the discipline he imposed was personal — it depended on his own authority, his own presence, his own willingness to have sergeants beaten in the dust. After him, the Janissaries became a praetorian guard in the full Roman sense: the power behind the throne, capable of making and unmaking sultans, demanding donatives at every accession, and eventually, by the 17th century, resisting the very military reforms that might have saved the empire.
In the century of Mehmed, however, the Janissaries were still the new troop — young, disciplined, loyal to a sultan who had beaten their officers and led them through the breach. They were, as Kritoboulos wrote, “the flower of the camp,” and with them, Mehmed conquered an empire.

concepts/timar-decline.title: The Decline of the Ottoman Timar System
concepts/timar-decline.content:
The Decline of the Ottoman Timar System
The Classical Timar System
The timar (تيمار) was the foundation of Ottoman provincial administration and military mobilization for nearly three centuries. Under this system, the state granted the right to collect taxes from a designated piece of land to a cavalryman (timariot or sipahi) in exchange for military service. The grant was not hereditary: it reverted to the state on the holder’s death and was reassigned by the central government. This prevented the emergence of an entrenched feudal nobility of the European type and kept the provincial cavalry directly dependent on the sultan.
The hierarchy of fiefs was carefully calibrated. A timar proper yielded an annual income of less than 20,000 akçe (aspers), supporting a single cavalryman. A zi’amet yielded between 20,000 and 100,000 akçe, and its holder (za’im) was obliged to bring additional mounted retainers (jebelü) proportionate to his income beyond the basic minimum (kılıç, “sword”). Above both stood the khass (خاص), the great fiefs held by beylerbeys and other high officials, with incomes from 100,000 to over 1,000,000 akçe. In each province the basic minimum for a timar was usually about 3,000 akçe, intended for the support and equipment of the holder in person, with additional income at the rate of one jebelü per 5,000 akçe of surplus revenue.
In the age of Suleiman the Magnificent, the enrolled feudal troops of Europe and Asia numbered roughly 80,000 men — a figure doubled or tripled when the additional retainers (jebelü) brought by each sipahi were counted. This feudal army was the sultan’s principal instrument for conquest, and the nasihatname authors — Koçi Bey, Lütfi Pasha, and Sarı Mehmed Pasha — all looked back on it as the engine of Ottoman greatness.
Signs of Decay
By the early seventeenth century, the timar system was showing serious signs of breakdown. The problem, as Koçi Bey and Sarı Mehmed Pasha diagnosed it, was twofold: corruption in the administration of grants and the flight of timariots from their military obligations.
At the local level, the alay beys (the intermediate officers responsible for maintaining the timar registers in each province) began to abuse their authority. Rather than awarding vacant timars to deserving and able warriors, they gave them to their own dependents, to freed slaves, or to fictitious persons whose names existed only on paper. A timar might be divided into shares (hisse), with the alay bey pocketing the income from the portion nominally separated off while continuing to occupy the land himself. As Sarı Mehmed Pasha wrote in the Ninth Chapter of his Naṣā’iḥ ül-Vüzera: “They take money beyond all bounds under the name of ‘petition money’ for the vacant timar, and give them to useless men.”
The phenomenon of the sepet timarı (“basket timar”) became a byword for abuse. The term referred to fiefs whose holders, instead of serving in the field, spent campaigns weaving wicker hurdles and carrying baskets in the trenches — and often did not even perform that service. Their berats (patents of appointment) were kept in a basket rather than on the official rolls. Koçi Bey, writing about eighty years before Sarı Mehmed, had already noted men who held as many as fifty timars; by Sarı Mehmed’s time, it was universally recognized that there were men who held five or six timars simultaneously. As the author grimly observed, “of such persons the majority exist in name but not in body.”
Military Consequences
The collapse of the timar system had direct military consequences. Timariots who actually existed and appeared on campaign began to evade their obligations. Men holding zi’amet yielding four, five, or even eight purses of aspers — enough to require them to bring multiple jebelü — went entirely alone, “put on an appearance of wretchedness” and were “good for nothing.” Many placed themselves “under protection” (himaye) of powerful officials, fleeing from combat as soon as battle was joined.
The result was a catastrophic decline in the quality and quantity of the Ottoman provincial cavalry. By the late seventeenth century, as Wright notes, the feudal army had become “for the most part untrained levies, of volunteers and adventurers, all of whom plundered friend and foe alike as they came and went through the land.” The reformed regiments of the late Köprülü period attempted to address this, but by the time of the disastrous siege of Vienna (1683) and the subsequent Great Turkish War, the feudal sipahi was no longer the decisive arm of the Ottoman military. The Janissary corps, once the elite of the infantry, had itself become a politicized and semi-hereditary caste whose primary loyalty was to its own privileges.
The Shift to Tax-Farming
The decline of the timar system was both cause and consequence of the empire’s fiscal transformation. As the value of the akçe eroded through repeated debasements, the fixed tax rates collected by sipahis became worth less and less. The central government, desperate for cash to finance prolonged wars, turned increasingly to the iltizam (tax-farming) system, which delivered immediate payments to the treasury in exchange for the right to collect revenues. By the early eighteenth century, with the introduction of the malikâne (life-term tax farm) in 1695, large portions of provincial revenue were being alienated permanently to urban financiers and office-holders, bypassing the timar system entirely.
Sarı Mehmed Pasha recognized this shift as destructive. In his Treasury chapter, he called for a complete return to the direct collection of taxes by appointed officials — a reform that would have restored the principle of the classical timar. But the system had already passed the point of no return. The landed revenues of the empire, once the basis of a military-fiscal state that mobilized cavalry through land grants, had become a patrimonial resource to be milked by the Istanbul elite. The janissaries, “useless against the enemy but for their private interests they could fight,” filled the void left by the feudal cavalry, and the empire’s military power dwindled to a shadow of what it had been under Suleiman.
Efforts at reform had been attempted before. In 1011 AH (1602-3), under Grand Vizier Yemişçi Hasan Pasha, imperial orders were issued to reclaim the “basket timars” and distribute them to deserving men. Later, under Nasuh Pasha, the zi’amet and timar holders of Rumelia were summoned to Edirne for inspection. In both cases, “the reform did not come to pass.” The entrenched interests of the alay beys, the provincial governors, and the urban notables were too strong. The timar system collapsed not in a single crisis but through a long, grinding process of attrition — what the nasihatname authors could only lament as decay, and what modern historians might recognize as the transformation of the Ottoman state from a conquest empire to a fiscally constrained agrarian monarchy.

concepts/trade-venice-genoa-europe.title: Ottoman Trade with Venice, Genoa, and Europe
concepts/trade-venice-genoa-europe.content:
Ottoman Trade with Venice, Genoa, and Europe
Ottoman Ticâreti — Venedik, Ceneviz ve Avrupa ile Münâsebetler

The Capitulations (Ahidnâmeler / İmtiyâzât) with Venice and Genoa
The framework of Ottoman trade with the Italian maritime republics rested on a series of commercial treaties known in Ottoman Turkish as ahidnâmeler or, later, imtiyâzât (capitulations). These were unilateral grants by the sultan, not bilateral treaties in the modern sense: the sultan conferred privileges on foreign merchants as an act of sovereign grace, revocable at his pleasure, and the grants applied only for his lifetime, requiring renewal by each successor.
The earliest recorded capitulation dates from 1352, when the Genoese — already established at Galata (Pera) across the Golden Horn from Constantinople — concluded a commercial agreement with the Ottoman ruler Orhan Gazi. This was a natural alliance: Genoa was Venice’s great rival, and the Ottomans exploited this rivalry skilfully. The Genoese received preferential access to Anatolian trade, including a long-term monopoly over alum production in the Manisa region — the principal source of alum for the European textile industry. The Genoese colonies at Foça (Phocaea) on the western Anatolian coast and the island of Chios became the primary ports of entry for Anatolian goods. In return, Genoese ships rendered the Ottomans vital military assistance, transporting Ottoman troops across the Dardanelles in 1421 and 1444, at times when Venetian naval power controlled the straits. During the siege of Constantinople in 1453, the Genoese of Galata observed a carefully calculated neutrality.
For Venice, the first major Ottoman capitulation came after the capture of Constantinople in 1453. Mehmed II (Mehmed the Conqueror) understood that the economic life of his new capital depended on continued commercial intercourse with the West. In the immediate aftermath of the conquest, he granted Venice a capitulation permitting a Venetian ambassador — the bailo — to reside in Istanbul and Venetian merchants to trade freely throughout the empire, on the condition that they pay customs duties. The rate was set at a remarkably low 2 per cent, a figure that reflected Mehmed’s desire to keep Venetian commerce flowing into the city.
The terms of these early capitulations were detailed and specific. They guaranteed the security of merchants and their goods, freedom of navigation, the right to worship, the right to settle disputes among themselves according to their own laws, and freedom from arbitrary taxation beyond the agreed customs. The Venetian bailo and the Genoese podestà in Galata functioned as both diplomatic representatives and judicial authorities over their respective mercantile communities.

The Venetian Bailo in Istanbul
The Venetian bailo (baylos in Ottoman usage) was the senior Venetian diplomatic and commercial representative in Istanbul, a position of extraordinary influence that lasted from the Byzantine era through the entire Ottoman period. The bailo resided in a palatial compound in Galata, the Palazzo del Bailo (later the Venetian Embassy), and exercised jurisdiction over all Venetian subjects in the Ottoman Empire.
The bailo’s functions were manifold: he represented Venetian interests at the Sublime Porte, negotiated the renewal of capitulations on the accession of each new sultan, adjudicated commercial disputes among Venetian merchants, maintained intelligence networks across the Levant, and reported regularly — in the famous relazioni — to the Venetian Senate on Ottoman political, military, and economic affairs. The bailo’s reports constitute one of the richest archival sources for Ottoman history in the sixteenth century; they were read with the utmost seriousness in Venice and often shaped the Republic’s strategic decisions.
During the 1463–1479 Ottoman-Venetian war, Mehmed II imprisoned all Venetian merchants and confiscated their goods, and the bailo was expelled. But with the return of peace, the bailo resumed his post, and his office continued without interruption into the seventeenth century. The bailo was assisted by a chancellor, interpreters (dragomans), and a staff of clerks. He oversaw the Venetian merchant colony in Galata, the Venetian fondaco (warehouse and trading compound), and the church of San Francesco. The bailo’s position was so central to Ottoman-Venetian relations that even periods of open war did not entirely sever the connection: messages continued to pass through intermediaries, and peace negotiations almost always began with the re-admission of the bailo.

Venetian Merchant Colonies in Galata
Galata — called Galata or Pera in European sources, and غلطه (Galata) in Ottoman Turkish — was not a single colony but a mosaic of Italian merchant communities, of which the Venetians were the most prominent after 1453. Under Byzantine rule, the Genoese had dominated Galata, but after the Ottoman conquest, Mehmed II confirmed the Genoese in their privileges while also granting the Venetians extensive commercial facilities.
The Venetian colony in Galata was organized as a self-governing community under the bailo’s authority. Venetian merchants resided in a designated quarter, the Fondaco dei Veneziani, which functioned as a combined warehouse, market, and residential compound. The colony possessed its own church (San Francesco), its own bakery, its own scales and measures, and its own court for civil matters involving Venetians.
The merchant population was not static. Venetian merchants came and went with the sailing seasons, but a core of permanent residents — fattori (factors) representing the great Venetian trading houses — maintained year-round operations. These factors cultivated relationships with Ottoman officials, Jewish and Greek intermediaries, and Muslim merchants. They extended credit, negotiated contracts, and arranged the shipping of goods to Venice via the regular convoys that sailed from Istanbul to the Adriatic.
The Venetian presence extended beyond Galata itself. Venetian merchants were active in Bursa, the great silk market of western Anatolia; in Edirne (Adrianople), the former Ottoman capital; in Salonica; and in the ports of the Morea such as Modon and Coron, which remained Venetian possessions well into the sixteenth century. But Istanbul — and specifically Galata — remained the nerve centre of Venetian Levantine commerce.

Genoese Trade in the Black Sea and the Loss of Caffa
The Genoese presence in the Black Sea preceded the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople by more than two centuries. From their fortified colony at Caffa (modern Feodosia) in the Crimea, and from their other bases at Tana (at the mouth of the Don), Trebizond, and Amasra on the Anatolian coast, the Genoese had dominated Black Sea commerce during the late Byzantine and Mongol periods. The trade was lucrative and varied: Caffa exported grain, fish, caviar, salt, wax, honey, and — most notoriously — slaves.
The slave trade was the backbone of the Genoese Black Sea economy. Tatar raids on Russian, Polish, and Circassian territories supplied a steady stream of captives, who were brought to Caffa and sold to Genoese merchants. From Caffa, the slaves were shipped across the Black Sea to Istanbul and the Mediterranean, where they were sold in the markets of Egypt, Italy, and the broader Islamic world. In pre-Ottoman times the Caffa slave trade was entirely in Genoese hands, and the revenues it generated were enormous.
The Genoese also traded in the products of the Pontic steppe and the Russian forests: furs (sable, fox, ermine), timber, iron, and honey, which they exchanged for Mediterranean goods — wine, olive oil, textiles, and spices — that they brought from Italy and the Levant.
With the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453, the Genoese position in the Black Sea became precarious. Mehmed II, having secured control of the Bosphorus and the Dardanelles, was determined to close the Black Sea to Italian merchants and develop the region as an integral part of the Ottoman imperial economy. The Straits were the key: by inspecting all shipping at Istanbul and Gallipoli, the Ottomans could regulate access to the Black Sea at will.
The decisive blow fell in 1475, when the Ottoman navy under Gedik Ahmed Pasha conquered Caffa, along with the other Genoese colonies at Azov, Taman, and Kerch. The Genoese were expelled, their commercial network dismantled, and their monopoly replaced by Ottoman subjects — Muslims, Greeks, Armenians, and Jews, particularly the Armenians of Caffa itself, who adapted to Ottoman rule and continued much of the transit trade under the sultan’s sovereignty.
In 1484 the Ottomans added Kilia and Akkerman at the mouths of the Danube, closing the last significant access points for Italian shipping to the Black Sea. Henceforth, Italian ships on the Black Sea were rare: a few Venetian vessels carrying wine from Crete or Chios, or ships based in the Crimea belonging to Italians who had become Ottoman subjects. The great Genoese and Venetian commerce of the Black Sea was effectively ended.

The Grain Trade
Grain — primarily wheat — was the most politically sensitive commodity in Ottoman trade with Europe. The wheat of Anatolia, Macedonia, Thrace, and Thessaly was vital not only to Istanbul but also to Venice and the entire Po valley. The Ottoman sultans used the grain trade as a deliberate instrument of policy.
During the reign of Bayezid I (1389–1402), the Ottomans blockaded the Bosphorus and restricted grain exports to pressure Venice in times of conflict. Mehmed II, before the siege of Constantinople in 1453, granted the Venetians permission to export wheat in order to lull them into inaction. After the conquest, however, he forbade the export of foodstuffs to Italy and subjected Italian ships to close inspection at Istanbul and Gallipoli. The Black Sea grain trade — which had once supplied Italy with Ukrainian and Crimean wheat — was redirected entirely to Istanbul. In 1600 the Palace kitchens placed a single order for two thousand kantârs of clarified butter from Caffa alone.
Throughout the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, the export of grain to Venice and the Italian states was permitted only intermittently, as a diplomatic concession. The principle was consistent: the needs of Istanbul — the imperial capital, the largest city in Europe — came first. Wheat surpluses could be exported, but only under license and with the payment of customs duties. This policy gave the sultan powerful leverage over Venice, for the Republic depended on Ottoman grain to feed its population and its colonial possessions in the Aegean and the Ionian Sea.
The Black Sea continued to supply Istanbul with vast quantities of grain throughout the classical age. Wheat, flour, tallow, clarified butter, cheese, and honey were shipped from the Crimea and the Kuban steppe through Caffa to the capital. To increase production, the Crimean Tatar aristocracy settled colonies of Russian prisoners on the steppe as agricultural labourers, and the semi-nomadic peoples of the northern Black Sea region also grew cereals for export. This steady supply was the foundation of Istanbul’s prosperity and a pillar of the Ottoman provisioning system (iaşe).

The Slave Trade
The slave trade was a major component of Ottoman commerce with the Black Sea region and, through it, with Europe. In the pre-Ottoman period, the Genoese at Caffa had dominated the trade, exporting slaves captured by the Tatars from Russian, Polish, and Circassian lands. After the Ottoman conquest of Caffa in 1475, the trade continued under Ottoman auspices, but now the slaves were primarily destined for the Ottoman market rather than for Italian buyers.
Caffa and its dependent ports — Azov, Kerch, Taman, and Kopa — became the principal markets for slaves in the Ottoman world. Captives were typically taken from Taman to Caffa, where they were exchanged for cloth and other goods brought by Anatolian merchants. The state derived a yearly income of eighteen thousand gold ducats from the slave trade in the mid-sixteenth century, and the tax on each slave was four gold ducats. Most slaves went to Istanbul, and some to Sinop and Inebolu on the Anatolian Black Sea coast.
The slave trade was not confined to the Black Sea. Antalya on the Mediterranean coast of Anatolia was also a centre of the slave trade, exporting white slaves to the south and importing Negro slaves from Africa. The Bursa customs registers also record the transit of slaves. Slaves were used in the Ottoman economy as domestic servants, agricultural labourers, soldiers (the Janissary corps was recruited through the devşirme system of child levy), and concubines. The slave trade thus intersected with nearly every aspect of Ottoman economic and social life.

Luxury Goods: Silk, Spices, and Fine Textiles
The luxury trade was the most profitable sector of Ottoman-European commerce. The great commodity was Persian silk, which passed through Ottoman territory on its way to the textile industries of Italy and, later, of France and England.
Bursa, the early Ottoman capital, was the entrepôt of the silk trade. Persian silk caravans from Tabriz travelled the overland route through Erzincan, Amasya, and Tokat to Bursa, avoiding the old route through Trebizond. In Bursa, Italian merchants — Venetians, Genoese, and Florentines — purchased the raw silk and shipped it to the looms of Venice, Lucca, and Florence. The profit margins were enormous: each fardello (about 150 kg) yielded a profit of seventy to eighty gold ducats in Italy. Around a thousand silk looms operated in Bursa, consuming five fardelli of silk per day. The price of silk rose steadily from fifty akçes per fardello in 1467 to eighty-two in 1494.
Spices were the second great luxury commodity. Despite Portuguese competition from the Cape route, the Ottoman spice trade remained robust throughout the sixteenth century. The Venetians alone bought six thousand quintals of spices in Alexandria in 1554, and their annual purchases of twelve thousand quintals between 1560 and 1564 equalled the volume before Vasco da Gama’s discovery. Spices arriving through the Red Sea and Persian Gulf routes were distributed through Aleppo, Damascus, Cairo, Bursa, and Istanbul. The customs registers of Bursa show that European merchants continued to buy spices there throughout the century, and by 1582 customs receipts on spices in Bursa had reached 7,250 gold ducats — four times the level of 1487.
Other luxury goods in the trade included: Bursa brocades and velvets, Ankara camlets (fine mohair fabrics), Anatolian carpets and rugs (especially the famous rugs of Uşak and Gördes), cotton textiles, mohair, opium, dried fruits, wax, and the fine woollens of Florence and Flanders that were imported into the Ottoman Empire in exchange.

The Impact of the Ottoman-Venetian Wars on Commerce
The relationship between Venice and the Ottoman Empire was characterized by a paradoxical intertwining of war and trade. Periods of open conflict — the wars of 1463–1479, 1499–1503, 1537–1540, and 1570–1573 — disrupted commerce severely, yet trade was never entirely extinguished, and peace treaties invariably restored commercial privileges.
During the long war of 1463–1479, Mehmed II imprisoned all Venetian merchants in the empire and confiscated their goods. At the same time, he actively encouraged Florence and Ragusa (Dubrovnik) to take Venice’s place in Ottoman commerce. In 1469, he granted new trade privileges to Florence, and some fifty Florentine houses were soon trading in the empire. By annexing Bosnia and Herzegovina in 1463, Mehmed opened a new overland trade route to Italy through Ragusa and Ancona, bypassing Venetian-controlled sea lanes. The war also forced Venice to relinquish Negroponte (Euboea), Scutari, and other Aegean and Albanian possessions, reducing the Republic’s eastern Mediterranean colonial empire.
The peace treaty of 25 January 1479 imposed harsh terms on Venice. The Republic surrendered Scutari and other fortresses, paid an indemnity of 100,000 gold ducats, and agreed to an annual tribute of 10,000 ducats for permission to trade in Ottoman domains. On the other hand, the sultan granted the Venetians freedom of trade and confirmed the bailo’s residence in Istanbul.
The war of 1499–1503 saw the Ottomans capture Lepanto (Naupaktos), Modon, and Coron, key Venetian bases in the Morea. The loss of these ports dealt a severe blow to Venetian commercial routes in the Ionian Sea. The wars of 1537–1540 and 1570–1573 continued the pattern: Ottoman territorial gains, Venetian commercial losses, and then a restoration of trade under renewed capitulations. After 1570–1573, when the Ottomans conquered Cyprus, Venice lost its last major colonial possession in the eastern Mediterranean, and the Republic’s commercial pre-eminence in the Levant began its irreversible decline.
Through all these conflicts, a pattern held: even at war, the Ottomans allowed trade to continue through intermediaries — Ragusan ships, Jewish merchants, and neutral Greeks — and peace treaties always contained detailed commercial clauses restoring Venetian privileges. Trade was too essential to both powers to be sacrificed entirely to military ambitions.

Trade Routes Across the Mediterranean
The Mediterranean trade routes connecting the Ottoman Empire to Europe formed a complex network shaped by politics, geography, and the shifting balance of naval power.
Istanbul to Venice
The primary sea route ran from Istanbul through the Sea of Marmara, the Dardanelles, and the Aegean, calling at the Venetian-held islands of Crete and Corfu, then across the Ionian Sea and up the Adriatic to Venice. Venetian convoys (mude) sailed this route on a regular schedule, protected by armed galleys. The outward journey carried European woollens, paper, glassware, and silver; the return voyage brought silk, spices, cotton, mohair, carpets, and wheat.
The Adriatic: Ragusa and Ancona
After the Ottoman conquest of Bosnia and Herzegovina in 1463, a new overland route opened through the Balkan interior. From Istanbul, the route passed through Edirne, Plovdiv, Sofia, and Sarajevo to the Adriatic port of Ragusa (Dubrovnik). From Ragusa, goods were shipped across the Adriatic to Ancona, a free port in the Papal States, and then forwarded to Florence and the rest of Italy. This route bypassed Venetian-controlled sea lanes and was actively promoted by Mehmed II as a counterweight to Venetian dominance.
Ragusa, though an Ottoman tributary paying an annual tribute of 12,500 gold ducats, prospered enormously as the intermediary of this trade. Ragusan merchants paid only 2 per cent customs duty, half the rate levied on Venice, and Ragusan ships carried Ottoman goods to Italy, the Adriatic ports, and even to London. During the Ottoman-Venetian wars, Ragusa acted as the essential commercial bridge between the belligerents.
The overland route was secured by a chain of Ottoman caravanserais and fortified stations, and the famous Vişegrad bridge across the River Drina — one of the masterpieces of sixteenth-century Ottoman architecture — was built along this very route. Settlements such as Sarajevo, Skopje, Foça, and Mostar grew into flourishing Ottoman cities thanks to their position on this international trade corridor.
The Aegean and the Levant
A separate network of sea routes connected Istanbul to the ports of the Aegean — Salonica, Izmir (Smyrna), and the islands of Chios, Lesbos, and Rhodes — and from there to the Levantine ports of Alexandria, Beirut, Tripoli, and Alexandretta. These routes carried the spice trade from Egypt and Syria, the silk trade from Aleppo, and the cotton, grain, and olive oil of western Anatolia.
Other Routes: Hungary and Poland
From the Black Sea ports of Caffa, Kilia, and Akkerman, overland routes ran northward through Moldavia to Lwow (Lemberg) in Poland and to the Hungarian plain. These carried Ottoman silks, spices, and cotton northwards, and brought back Polish and Hungarian woollens, Russian furs, and German metals. The wine trade was particularly important: Cretan and Morean wines were shipped to Kilia and then forwarded to Poland and Muscovy.

The Shift from Italian to French and English Dominance (Late 16th Century)
The last quarter of the sixteenth century witnessed a fundamental shift in the structure of Ottoman-European trade. The Italian maritime republics — Venice and, to a lesser extent, Genoa — which had dominated Levantine commerce for centuries, were gradually eclipsed by the Atlantic powers: France, England, and the Dutch Republic.
France
France obtained its first capitulations from the Ottoman sultan in 1569, following negotiations that had begun under Süleyman the Magnificent. The French capitulations of 18 October 1569 were the first to extend to a western monarchy the commercial privileges that had previously been granted only to Venice. They served as the model for all subsequent European capitulations. French consuls took up residence in Istanbul, Alexandria, Beirut, and Tripoli (in Lebanon). French ships bound for the Levant carried Normandy textiles, paper, and German hardware, returning with wool, cotton, silk, spices, perfume essences, drugs, mohair, and Anatolian rugs.
After the Ottoman-Venetian war of 1570–1573, France began to displace Venice as the leading European commercial power in the Levant. By the beginning of the seventeenth century, about a thousand French vessels were active in Levantine commerce, and the volume of trade had risen to thirty million livres — half of France’s total foreign trade. Marseilles became the great French emporium for Levant goods. Other European merchants, especially the English and the Dutch, initially traded under the French flag.
England
The English entry into the Levant trade was more gradual. Süleyman I had granted English merchants the right to trade freely in the empire as early as 1553, but the English did not exploit this privilege immediately, preferring to seek a route through Moscow and Iran to the spice markets of the Indian Ocean. In 1578, the Ottomans invaded Azerbaijan and Shirvan, gaining control of this northern route and cutting off the English option.
With Spain as a common enemy, the English and Ottoman governments saw the advantage of cooperation. Despite opposition from France and Venice, the sultan granted the English a capitulation in 1580, confirmed by a more extensive grant in 1583. On 11 September 1581, the Turkey Company was founded by royal charter; in 1592 it merged with the Venice Company to form the Levant Company, which would dominate Anglo-Ottoman trade for two centuries.
The Ottoman government gave the English preferential customs treatment: English merchants paid only 3 per cent duty, while the French and other foreigners paid 5 per cent (until 1673, when the French also secured the 3 per cent rate). The Dutch traded under the English flag until they received their own capitulation in 1612.
The English competed fiercely with France and Venice. English woollens — fine quality kerseys — were sold at low prices in Ottoman markets, while imports of English tin and steel became vital for the Ottoman arms industry. English pirates, sometimes in cooperation with the Algerian corsairs, raided Venetian and French merchant fleets, and Queen Elizabeth turned a deaf ear to complaints. Venetian trade fell sharply, and by 1630 French trade had declined by half, while the Levant Company dominated Ottoman markets. English consuls established themselves in Istanbul, Izmir, Aleppo, and Alexandretta.
By the end of the sixteenth century, the old pattern of Italian commercial hegemony in the Levant was broken. Ragusa, once a thriving intermediary, declined as the Ottoman economy oriented itself toward the Atlantic states. Venice, confined increasingly to the Adriatic, never recovered its former position. The capitulations, originally a tool of Ottoman statecraft to manage foreign merchants, became the instrument through which European powers progressively penetrated and eventually dominated the Ottoman economy.

Key Sources

Halil Inalcik, The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age 1300–1600 (1973), Chapter XIV: “The Ottoman Empire and International Trade”
Inalcik, “Bursa and the Commerce of the Levant,” Journal of Economic and Social History of the Orient, vol. III–2 (1960)
Inalcik, “Imtiyāzāt” in Encyclopaedia of Islam (2nd ed.)
F. Braudel, La Méditerranée et le monde méditerranéen à l’époque de Philippe II (1949)

entities/aden.title: Aden (Adan / 'Adan)
entities/aden.content:
Aden — ‘Adan (عدن) / Adan (عدن)
Aden (Arabic: عدن, ‘Adan; Ottoman Turkish: عدن, Adan) was the principal port of Yemen and a strategic chokepoint at the entrance to the Red Sea. Controlling the narrow Bab al-Mandab strait — the “Gate of Tears” — Aden commanded the maritime route between the Indian Ocean and the Red Sea, making it one of the most valuable commercial and strategic positions in the Ottoman-Portuguese struggle for the Indian Ocean.
Geography and Strategic Position
Aden is situated on a volcanic peninsula on the southern coast of Yemen, guarding the eastern shore of the Bab al-Mandab strait, the 30-km-wide passage connecting the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden and the Indian Ocean. Its natural harbor, sheltered by the extinct volcanic cones of Jabal Shamsan and the crater of Seerah, provided a safe anchorage for ships. The city controlled the sea lanes linking India, East Africa, and Southeast Asia to the Red Sea ports of Jidda and Suez.
Under Ottoman Rule
Aden was ruled by local Tahirid and Rasulid dynasties before the Ottoman expansion into Yemen. The city’s incorporation into the Ottoman sphere began in the context of the Portuguese threat. Inalcik records that after repulsing Portuguese attacks on Jidda in 1517 and 1525, the Ottoman admiral Selman Reis advanced from Jidda to subjugate Yemen and Aden. The Ottomans recognized that control of Aden was essential to protecting the Red Sea and the Holy Cities from Portuguese naval power.
The major Ottoman campaign came in 1538, when a large fleet was sent from Suez against the Portuguese. Inalcik describes how the fleet anchored at Aden, but the local Muslim ruler refused to cooperate, fearing that the Ottomans had come to establish their own rule rather than to assist him. The Portuguese usually avoided open confrontation with the Turks, and seeing this, the Ottomans passed to the offensive. Inalcik notes that the Ottomans successfully subjugated Yemen and Aden, bringing the port under direct Ottoman administration.
Yemen was organized as an Ottoman province (eyalet), with a governor (beylerbey) appointed from Istanbul, and Aden became the key Ottoman naval outpost on the Indian Ocean. The city was garrisoned with Ottoman troops and its fortifications were strengthened with artillery.
Role in Trade
Aden had been one of the great emporia of the Indian Ocean trade since antiquity. Under Ottoman rule, it continued to function as a major transshipment point for goods moving between India, East Africa, and the Red Sea. The city handled:

Spices (pepper, cinnamon, cloves) from India and Southeast Asia
Textiles from Gujarat, Bengal, and Coromandel
Coffee from the Yemeni highlands
Slaves, ivory, and gold from East Africa
Frankincense and myrrh from the Horn of Africa

The customs revenues of Aden were substantial, though the Portuguese presence in the Indian Ocean disrupted some traditional trade patterns. The Ottomans used Aden as a base for naval operations and as a center for intelligence gathering on Portuguese movements in the Indian Ocean.
Fortifications
The Ottomans fortified the volcanic crater and the harbor approaches with artillery batteries. The city walls were strengthened, and a citadel was maintained on the heights overlooking the harbor. The garrison included Janissaries and local levies, and the Ottoman fleet patrolled the Bab al-Mandab to challenge Portuguese shipping.
Key Connections

Jidda: Primary Red Sea counterpart; the Suez—Jidda—Aden route was the main Ottoman Red Sea axis
Suez: Northern terminus of the Red Sea route; Ottoman fleets sailing from Suez called at Aden
Bab al-Mandab: The strait that Aden commanded; controlling this passage was the key to the Red Sea
Yemeni highlands (Mocha, Zabid): Interior trade routes for coffee and other Yemeni goods
India (Calicut, Goa): Aden was the western terminus of Indian Ocean shipping before entering the Red Sea
East Africa (Mogadishu, Mombasa): Ottoman operations from Aden extended influence down the Swahili coast

entities/ahmed-iii.title: Ahmed III
entities/ahmed-iii.content:
Ahmed III — The Tulip Age Sultan
Ahmed III (r. 1703–1730) was the twenty-third Ottoman sultan, whose reign inaugurated the so-called Tulip Age (Lâle Devri), a period of cultural efflorescence, diplomatic opening to Europe, and architectural patronage. He acceded to the throne in 1703 following the deposition of his brother Mustafa II in the Edirne Vaq’ası.
Accession
The Edirne Vaq’ası (“Adrianople Happening”) of July 1703 was a rebellion by troops in Istanbul protesting the non-payment of salaries and the influence of the şeyhülislam Feydullah Efendi. The revolt spread rapidly, and the army sent from Adrianople to suppress it defected to the rebels. Mustafa II was deposed, and Ahmed III ascended the throne. One of the revolutionary leaders’ principal demands was the return of the sultan to Constantinople.
Patron of Reform Literature
Soon after his accession, Ahmed III commissioned a book of counsel from his defterdar, Sarı Mehmed Pasha, resulting in the Kitab-i Güldeste (or Naşa’iḥ ül-vüzera vel-ümera). Sarı Mehmed writes that “in the year 1115 [1703] when our majestic and august Padishah, H.I.M. Ahmed Khan, with prosperity and good fortune ascended the imperial throne, this humble author was in the service of the Exalted Government in the office of defterdar” and that “orders were issued for the due preparation and completion of the imperial donative.”
Reign
Ahmed III’s reign was marked by military reversals and cultural achievements. The Ottoman Empire suffered the disastrous Battle of Peterwardein (1716) against Prince Eugene of Savoy, leading to the loss of Temesvar and Belgrade. The Treaty of Passarowitz (1718) formalized these losses in the Balkans while securing some gains from Venice. In the east, the Ottomans recovered territories from the Safavids in the 1720s.
The Tulip Age saw the introduction of the first Ottoman printing press by İbrahim Müteferrika in 1727, the construction of elegant palaces and gardens along the Golden Horn, and the flourishing of poetry and the arts. Ahmed III himself was a poet and calligrapher.
Deposition
Ahmed III was deposed in 1730 by the Patrona Halil rebellion, a popular uprising fueled by resentment of the Tulip Age’s opulence and the government’s perceived weakness. He was succeeded by his nephew Mahmud I. He died in captivity in 1736.

entities/aleppo.title: Aleppo (Halep / Halab)
entities/aleppo.content:
Aleppo — Halab (حلب) / Halep (حلب)
Aleppo (Ottoman Turkish: حلب, Halep; Arabic: حلب, Halab) was one of the great commercial cities of the Ottoman Empire and the principal entrepot for trade between the Mediterranean world and Persia, Mesopotamia, and the Indian Ocean. Inalcik ranks Aleppo alongside Cairo, Istanbul, and Bursa as one of the four great markets where Indian goods and European merchandise were exchanged. Its conquest in 1516 was the first step in Selim I’s Mamluk campaign.
Geography and Strategic Position
Aleppo lies in northern Syria, on a plateau between the Mediterranean Sea (approximately 120 km west) and the Euphrates River (approximately 100 km east). The city sits at the crossroads of the ancient trade routes: the east-west route connecting Mesopotamia and Persia to the Mediterranean ports of Alexandretta and Antioch, and the north-south route linking Anatolia to Damascus and the Holy Cities. Its strategic position made it the natural commercial capital of northern Syria.
Under Ottoman Rule
Aleppo was the first major city to fall to the Ottomans in the Mamluk war. Inalcik records that Selim I marched first against Aleppo, and the city’s governor and people deserted to the Ottomans. On 24 August 1516, Selim destroyed the Mamluk army of Sultan al-Ghawri at Marj Dabiq, just north of Aleppo. The Mamluk sultan died on the battlefield. In the great mosque of Aleppo, in the presence of the Abbasid caliph al-Mutawakkil, Selim received the title “Servant of Mecca and Medina” — a declaration of Ottoman sovereignty over the entire Arab world.
After the conquest, Aleppo became the seat of a beylerbey (provincial governor) and the capital of the Aleppo eyalet, one of the most important provinces of the empire. The city’s existing Mamluk administrative elite was largely absorbed into the Ottoman system, and the city’s commercial infrastructure was preserved and expanded.
In the foreign relations context, Fisher notes that Aleppo was deeply involved in the border conflicts between the Mamluks and Ottomans before 1516. During the Cem Sultan affair (1481–1495), Aleppo served as a gathering point for forces loyal to the Mamluk sultan Kaitbay and as a staging ground for operations against the Ottomans. The governor of Aleppo was repeatedly called upon to provide troops and funds for Mamluk campaigns.
Lybyer notes that in the later 16th century, the Ottomans did not object to wintering in Aleppo (as opposed to the cold north), and Aleppo became a comfortable base for Ottoman military operations against Persia.
Role in Trade
Aleppo was the greatest commercial center of the Ottoman Empire in the Levant, perhaps equalled only by Cairo. Inalcik emphasizes that Indian goods—spices, textiles, indigo, and precious stones—were actively exchanged with European merchants in the markets of Aleppo alongside Cairo, Istanbul, and Bursa.
The city’s commercial importance rested on several pillars:

The Persian silk route: Raw silk from Iran passed through Aleppo on its way to the Mediterranean ports, where it was purchased by Venetian, French, and English merchants
The spice trade: Indian spices reached Aleppo via two routes: through Baghdad and the Syrian desert, and through the Red Sea ports of Jidda and Suez
The European trade: Venetian, Genoese, French, and later English and Dutch merchants maintained permanent trading colonies (fondacos) in Aleppo. The English Levant Company established a factory in the city in the late 16th century
Local manufacturing: Aleppo was famous for its soap (made from olive oil and laurel oil), textiles (silk and cotton blends), and leather goods

Inalcik notes that the customs duties levied in Ottoman ports like Aleppo were high, and when the English began buying directly from India after 1600, Aleppo’s position gradually began to erode. Nevertheless, throughout the 16th century, Aleppo was one of the wealthiest and most cosmopolitan cities in the Ottoman Empire.
Population and Society
Aleppo’s population under Ottoman rule was a rich mosaic of ethnic and religious communities: Sunni Arabs, Turks, Kurds, Circassians, Christians (Greek Orthodox, Syriac, Armenian, and later Maronite and Catholic), Jews, and a small Shiite minority. The city was a center of Islamic learning with numerous madrasas, and its Christian and Jewish communities played important roles in commerce and finance, particularly in the long-distance trade with Venice and Iran.
Fortifications and Urban Fabric
The city was dominated by the Aleppo Citadel, a massive fortified hilltop structure that had been used since antiquity. The Ottomans maintained the citadel and garrisoned it with Janissaries. The covered suqs (markets) of Aleppo, notably the Suq al-Madina, were among the largest and most elaborate in the Middle East. The Great Mosque of Aleppo (Umayyad Mosque) was a major religious and architectural monument, and it was here that Selim I had proclaimed his new title in 1516.
Key Connections

Alexandretta / Iskenderun: Mediterranean port serving Aleppo; the main outlet for Aleppo’s exports to Europe
Damascus: Rival and complement; together the two cities dominated Syrian trade
Baghdad: Eastern terminus of the desert caravan route connecting Aleppo to Mesopotamia and Persia
Bursa & Istanbul: Northern trade routes for Ottoman domestic consumption
Venice: Major European trading partner; Venetian merchants were a fixture in Aleppo’s markets
Persia (Tabriz): Source of raw silk; the Tabriz—Aleppo route was the major silk artery

entities/alexandria.title: Alexandria (Iskenderiyye / al-Iskandariyya)
entities/alexandria.content:
Alexandria — Iskenderiyye (اسكندريه) / al-Iskandariyya (الإسكندرية)
Alexandria (Ottoman Turkish: اسكندريه, Iskenderiyye; Arabic: الإسكندرية, al-Iskandariyya) was the principal Mediterranean port of Egypt and the western terminus of the spice trade routes from the Indian Ocean. Under Ottoman rule, Alexandria declined from its ancient eminence but remained the primary gateway between Egypt and Europe, handling the bulk of the transit trade in spices, textiles, and eastern luxury goods.
Geography and Strategic Position
Alexandria lies on the Mediterranean coast of Egypt, on a narrow strip of land separating the sea from Lake Mareotis. Its double harbor — the Great Harbor (east) and the Eunostos (west) — sheltered by the Pharos island, had made it the premier port of the eastern Mediterranean since antiquity. The city was connected to Cairo by the Nile River via the Mahmudiyya Canal and by road, making it the natural outlet for Egyptian and Indian Ocean goods to European markets.
Under Ottoman Rule
After the Ottoman conquest of Egypt in 1517, Alexandria became the main naval base for the Ottoman fleet in the eastern Mediterranean south of Istanbul. Inalcik notes that Christian corsairs operating from Rhodes, Cyprus, and Crete constantly menaced the sea route between Alexandria and Istanbul, and the conquest of Rhodes by Suleiman in 1522 was motivated in part by the necessity of securing this vital shipping lane.
The city’s customs house was one of the most lucrative in the empire. Inalcik records that in 1554 the Venetians alone bought six thousand quintals of spices in Alexandria, and their annual purchase of twelve thousand quintals between 1560 and 1564 matched the volume of trade before Vasco da Gama’s discovery of the sea route to India. A Portuguese spy in Egypt reported to his government in 1564 that thirty thousand quintals of spices had arrived in Alexandria.
Alexandria’s population under Ottoman rule was a cosmopolitan mix of Egyptians, Greeks, Venetians, French, English, and Jewish merchants who managed the European trading houses (fondacos).
Role in Trade
Alexandria was the Mediterranean terminus of the spice route: spices arriving at Jidda were shipped across the Red Sea to Suez, transported overland to Cairo, and then down the Nile to Alexandria for export to Europe. The city handled pepper, cinnamon, cloves, nutmeg, ginger, and other spices, as well as Indian textiles, Chinese porcelain, Yemeni coffee, and Egyptian agricultural products (flax, sugar, cotton).
Inalcik emphasizes that despite the Portuguese disruption of the Indian Ocean trade after 1498, the old Red Sea route via Alexandria continued to function throughout the 16th century, and the volume of spices reaching Alexandria actually equaled or exceeded pre-1498 levels for much of the century. Lisbon’s market suffered periodic crises as a result.
European merchants — particularly Venetians — maintained permanent agents in Alexandria. The city also served as a point of departure for pilgrims traveling to the Holy Land and as a transit point for Ottoman official correspondence and despatches between Istanbul and the provincial governors of Egypt.
Fortifications and Defenses
The city’s medieval walls and the Citadel of Qaitbay (built on the site of the ancient Pharos lighthouse) guarded the harbor. Under Ottoman rule, the garrison was maintained with Janissaries and local troops. The harbor was defended by artillery batteries, and the Ottoman fleet regularly patrolled the approaches against corsairs.
Key Connections

Cairo: Connected by Nile canal and road; most goods transiting Alexandria passed through Cairo
Istanbul: Primary sea route ran from Alexandria to the Ottoman capital; critical for grain shipments and tax remittances
Venice: Major trading partner; Venetian galleys called regularly at Alexandria for spices
Rosetta & Damietta: Secondary Nile Delta ports that competed with Alexandria in the 16th century
Suez: Red Sea counterpart on Cairo’s eastern side; together they formed the Cairo—Suez—Alexandria trade corridor

entities/athens.title: Athens
entities/athens.content:
Athens — آتنه (Atina)
Coordinates: 37°59’02”N 23°43’40”E
Athens (Ottoman Atina, آتنه), the ancient city of classical glory, came under Ottoman rule in 1456, three years after the fall of Constantinople. The city had been under Florentine rule through the Acciaiuoli dynasty since the late fourteenth century, and its capture by the forces of Mehmed II marked the end of Latin rule in Attica and the beginning of nearly four centuries of Ottoman administration. Unlike the great Ottoman capitals, Athens was a provincial center — a city living on its classical past but integrated into the administrative and economic structures of the Ottoman Balkans.
Geography and Strategic Position
Athens lies in the Attic basin, surrounded by the mountains of Hymettus, Pentelicus, and Parnitha, and facing the Saronic Gulf to the southwest. Its ancient port of Piraeus — though silted and reduced in importance by the fifteenth century — provided access to the Aegean sea routes. The city controlled the approaches to the Morea (Peloponnese) and the routes through the Isthmus of Corinth, making it a strategically significant but not militarily dominant outpost of the Ottoman Balkan system.
The Acropolis, the ancient citadel, dominated the city’s skyline. Its classical temples — the Parthenon, the Erechtheion, and the Propylaea — still stood largely intact in the fifteenth century, though they had been converted to Christian use under the Byzantines and Franks.
Ottoman Conquest
The Ottoman conquest of Athens was a direct consequence of Mehmed II’s campaigns to subdue the remnants of Byzantine and Latin rule in Greece. After the fall of Constantinople in 1453, the Despotate of the Morea — ruled by the brothers Thomas and Demetrios Palaiologos — became tributary to the sultan, but their internal conflicts invited Ottoman intervention. In 1456, the Ottoman commander Turahan Bey took Athens from the Florentine Acciaiuoli, who had ruled since 1388. The last Florentine duke, Francesco II Acciaiuoli, was executed, and Athens became an Ottoman possession.
The city was placed under the authority of the Beylerbey of Rumelia, though it was later transferred to the sanjak of Egriboz (Euboea/Chalkis). The Parthenon, which had been the Latin cathedral of Athens under the Franks, was converted into a mosque, and a minaret was added to its structure.
Under Ottoman Rule
Athens under the Ottomans was a kaza (judicial district) within the sanjak of Egriboz. The city’s population in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries was predominantly Greek Christian, with a small but significant Muslim community that included both converts and settlers from elsewhere in the empire. The Orthodox ecclesiastical hierarchy remained in place, with the Archbishop of Athens exercising authority over the Christian population.
The classical monuments of the city were treated with a degree of pragmatism: the Parthenon became the Great Mosque; the Erechtheion housed the harem of the Ottoman governor; and the Tower of the Winds, the Monument of Lysicrates, and other structures were incorporated into the fabric of Ottoman domestic and military architecture. Travelers in the early Ottoman period described Athens as a small town with perhaps ten thousand inhabitants clustered around the base of the Acropolis, its ancient glory faded but its buildings still standing.
Economic Life
Athens was not a major center of Ottoman trade or manufacture. Its modest economy rested on agriculture (olive oil, honey, wine), the production of silk cocoons, and small-scale textile production. The city’s port at Piraeus handled local coastal shipping but could not compete with the great emporia of Thessaloniki, Negroponte, or Patras. The Ottoman tax registers (tahrir defterleri) for Athens indicate a small but stable tax base, with revenues flowing to the imperial treasury through the Egriboz sanjak.
The City in the Fifteenth-Sixteenth Centuries
Athens lived in the shadow of its classical past. It was a city that had become a provincial backwater — admired by Western travelers for its ancient ruins but of only secondary importance to the Ottoman administration. The city’s significance increased somewhat in the later sixteenth century as European interest in classical antiquities grew and as travelers from Venice, France, and England began to visit and record the monuments.
Key Connections
Athens was administratively linked to Egriboz (Negroponte/Euboea) and, through it, to Istanbul. The city’s fate in the fifteenth century was tied to the struggle for the Morea between the Ottoman governors and the Palaiologos despots, and its capture preceded the final Ottoman conquest of the Morea in 1460. The Venetian presence in the Aegean — at Negroponte until 1470 and at Modon and Coron in the southern Morea — meant that Athens sat on the fluid frontier between Ottoman and Venetian spheres of influence in Greece.

entities/baghdad.title: Baghdad (Bagdad)
entities/baghdad.content:
Baghdad (بغداد / Bagdad)
Baghdad (Ottoman Turkish: بغداد, Bagdad) is the historic capital of Iraq (Mesopotamia), straddling the Tigris River at the strategic crossroads between Persia, Anatolia, Syria, and the Persian Gulf. Founded in 762 CE as the Round City of the Abbasid caliphs, it was for centuries the intellectual and commercial heart of the Islamic world. Under the Ottomans, Baghdad was the seat of a powerful provincial administration and a key frontier city on the empire’s eastern flank.
Geography and Strategic Position
Baghdad lies at approximately 33.3° N, 44.4° E, on both banks of the Tigris River in central Mesopotamia. Its position commands the water and land routes connecting the Persian Gulf to Syria and Anatolia. The city sits at the narrowest point between the Tigris and the Euphrates, making it the natural crossing point for east-west traffic. The routes from Baghdad to Tabriz, Aleppo, Damascus, and Basra made it the linchpin of trade across the central Islamic lands. Inalcik records that after the 1514 Battle of Chaldiran, the routes “to Azerbaijan, the Caucasus and Baghdad were open to the Ottomans” (Inalcik, p. 1532).
Under Ottoman Rule
Baghdad was taken by the Safavid Shah Ismail in 1504 during his early campaigns (Fisher, p. 4285). It remained under Safavid control until Suleyman the Magnificent’s campaign of 1534-35, when the Ottoman sultan “took Tabriz and Baghdad and annexed Azerbaijan and Iraq” (Inalcik, p. 1746). The shah’s governor in Baghdad had come to an agreement with the Ottomans, triggering the Persian war (Inalcik, p. 1743). The Treaty of Amasya (1555) formally confirmed Baghdad as an Ottoman possession (Inalcik, p. 1759-1760).
Suleyman’s boast captures the city’s symbolic importance: “In Baghdad I am the shah, in Byzantine realms the Caesar, and in Egypt the sultan” (Inalcik, p. 1858). Baghdad was administered as a salyane (salaried) province outside the timar system, meaning the sipahi cavalry-based land tenure did not apply there. Instead, the sultan “stationed Janissary garrisons” and appointed a governor, defterdar and kadi (Inalcik, p. 4641-4644). This arrangement preserved significant local autonomy.
In the seventeenth century, as central authority weakened, “real power in the capitals of distant provinces, such as Algeria, Egypt or Baghdad, passed into their hands, opening the way for the formation of a new ruling class in the provinces” (Inalcik, p. 5227-5228). The Janissaries of Baghdad came to dominate local politics, a pattern that produced the Mamluk dynasty of Baghdad in the 18th century.
Lybyer notes that Ottoman janissaries “did not object seriously to staying in Aleppo or Bagdad,” suggesting the city was considered a more desirable posting than the European front, but that this attitude “probably saved Vienna for Austria and lost Bagdad for Persia” (Lybyer, p. 3209-3210).
Role in Trade
Baghdad was the western terminus of the overland silk route from Persia and Central Asia, and the northern terminus of the Persian Gulf trade route from India. The city’s merchants handled spices, silks, cotton textiles, and precious goods flowing between the Indian Ocean, Persia, and the Mediterranean. The Ottoman conquest of Baghdad gave the empire control over the Persian Gulf approaches, and by 1546 Basra was developed as a second naval base after Suez for operations against the Portuguese (Inalcik, p. 1751-1754).
Key Connections

Tabriz: Direct route through the Zagros Mountains; the Safavid capital before and after Ottoman occupation
Basra: Southern water route to the Persian Gulf and India
Aleppo/Damascus: Western caravan routes across the Syrian desert
Mosul/Erzurum: Northern routes into Anatolia
Isfahan/Qazvin: Eastern routes into the Persian heartland
**Baghdad was also a major intellectual center; Fisher notes that many early Ottoman administrators were “trained in the schools of Baghdad” (Fisher, p. 146-147)

entities/barbarossa-hayreddin-pasha.title: Barbarossa Hayreddin Pasha (Khayr al-Din Barbarossa / Hayreddin Barbaros)
entities/barbarossa-hayreddin-pasha.content:
Barbarossa Hayreddin Pasha — Khayr al-Din Barbarossa (c. 1478–1546)
Barbarossa Hayreddin Pasha (c. 1478–1546), known in the West as Barbarossa (“Redbeard”) and in Turkish as Hayreddin Barbaros or Barbaros Hayreddin Paşa, was the greatest Ottoman admiral of the classical age. Born on the island of Lesbos (Midilli), he began his career as a Barbary corsair in the western Mediterranean before becoming the conqueror of Algiers and, ultimately, the kapudan-i deryâ (grand admiral) of the Ottoman navy under Sultan Suleiman I. Halil Inalcik’s account places Barbarossa at the center of the Ottoman naval revolution that transformed the empire into a Mediterranean superpower, culminating in his decisive victory over the Holy League at the Battle of Preveza in 1538.
Origins and the Barbary Corsair Years
Barbarossa was born on the island of Midilli (Lesbos), then under Ottoman rule, around 1478. His father, Yakup Ağa, was a former Ottoman sipahi of Albanian or Greek origin who had become a potter. Together with his elder brother Oruç Reis, Hayreddin took up privateering in the Mediterranean, operating from bases in the western Aegean and later the North African coast.
The Barbarossa brothers established themselves as the leading corsair captains of the Barbary Coast, raiding Christian shipping and coastal settlements. In 1516, Oruç and Hayreddin captured Algiers from the Spanish, expelling the Spanish garrison that had occupied the offshore Peñón fort. Oruç declared himself Sultan of Algiers and sought Ottoman protection, but was killed in 1518 during a Spanish expedition against Tlemcen. Hayreddin succeeded his brother as the leader of Algiers and as the foremost Muslim naval commander in the western Mediterranean.
Inalcik describes Hayreddin as the “famous Turkish corsair and conqueror of Algiers,” a figure whose reputation was already legendary before he entered Ottoman service. Recognizing the growing threat from Charles V and the Spanish-Habsburg naval buildup in the Mediterranean, Hayreddin formally submitted to Ottoman suzerainty in 1519, placing Algiers under the sultan’s protection. This act integrated the Barbary corsair fleet into the Ottoman imperial system — a step that would transform the balance of naval power in the Mediterranean.
Appointment as Kapudan-i Deryâ
The strategic context that brought Barbarossa to supreme command was the Habsburg-Ottoman rivalry of the 1530s. In 1532, while Suleiman was campaigning against the Habsburgs in Hungary, the Genoese admiral Andrea Doria — serving Charles V — captured Coron in the Morea from the Ottomans. Inalcik records that Suleiman, “realizing that he now had to open a second front in the Mediterranean, placed all Ottoman naval forces under the command of the famous Turkish corsair and conqueror of Algiers, Hayreddin Barbarossa, appointing him kapudan-i deryâ — grand admiral.”
This appointment in 1533 was a transformative moment for the Ottoman navy. Barbarossa brought with him the expertise, the crews, and the aggressive tactical doctrine of the Barbary corsairs — men who had spent their lives in galley warfare against the Spanish, the Knights of St John, and Italian maritime states. Inalcik notes that 1533 also saw the creation of the beylerbeyilik (province) of Algiers, conferred upon Barbarossa “in an attempt to unify all naval forces against Charles V.” As kapudan-i deryâ, Barbarossa united under his administration Algiers, which he himself had conquered, and thirteen sanjaks on the shores and islands of the Mediterranean, creating a unified naval command stretching across the Ottoman Mediterranean littoral.
Organization of the Ottoman Navy
Barbarossa’s achievement as grand admiral went beyond battlefield victories. He reorganized the Ottoman navy into a permanent, centrally-coordinated force capable of sustained campaigns far from home ports. Before his tenure, the Ottoman fleet had been largely a coastal defense force, assembled for specific campaigns and then dispersed. Under Barbarossa, the Imperial Arsenal (Tersane-i Amire) in the Golden Horn became a permanent shipbuilding and logistical hub, and the Mediterranean fleet was structured as a standing force.
Inalcik records that from the second half of the sixteenth century — a direct institutional legacy of Barbarossa’s reorganization — the kapudân-i deryâ presided over a separate council which dealt with naval matters and lawsuits arising in the fleet, and made nominations, appointments and dismissals. This gave the admiralty an institutional independence it had not previously possessed. The grand vizier retained the right to visit and inspect the Arsenal, but operational naval command was firmly in the hands of the kapudan pasha.
The thirteen Mediterranean sanjaks under Barbarossa’s administration — ranging from the coasts of the Morea and the Aegean islands to the North African littoral — supplied ships, crews, timber, and naval stores to the imperial fleet. This administrative unification was the foundation upon which Ottoman naval supremacy in the eastern and central Mediterranean was built.
The Franco-Ottoman Alliance and Mediterranean Campaigns
Barbarossa’s appointment coincided with the deepening of the Franco-Ottoman alliance. Since 1531, Inalcik records, the French had been “trying to persuade the sultan to attack Italy,” and now they sought a formal alliance. In 1536 this alliance was concluded, with Suleiman ready to grant the French freedom of trade within the empire. The alliance was secret in its political and military details, kept secret by both parties, and provided Francis I with a powerful counterweight to Charles V.
In 1537, Suleiman brought his army to Valona in Albania and besieged Venetian ports in Albania and the island of Corfu, where a French fleet assisted the Ottomans. In the Mediterranean theater, Charles V had captured Tunis from the Ottomans in 1535, but this setback was about to be reversed.
The Battle of Preveza (1538)
The climactic naval engagement of Barbarossa’s career came on 28 September 1538 at Preveza, off the coast of Epirus in western Greece. Pope Paul III had organized a Holy League — the Sacra Lega — uniting the naval forces of Spain, Venice, Genoa, the Papal States, and the Knights of Malta under the overall command of Andrea Doria. The League assembled a fleet of some 300 vessels, outnumbering the Ottoman fleet commanded by Barbarossa.
Inalcik’s account is characteristically concise and definitive: “Barbarossa defeated a crusader fleet under the command of Andrea Doria at Preveza, leaving him undisputed master of the Mediterranean.”
The victory at Preveza cemented Ottoman naval dominance in the Mediterranean for the next three decades. Barbarossa’s tactical brilliance — exploiting his galleys’ maneuverability, using the lee of the coast to negate the League’s numerical advantage, and forcing Doria into an indecisive engagement that turned into a rout — became legendary. After Preveza, no Christian fleet dared challenge Ottoman naval superiority in the eastern Mediterranean until Lepanto in 1571.
The Conquest of Tunis and the Nice Campaign
Despite Charles V’s capture of Tunis in 1535, Barbarossa had previously conquered Tunis in August 1533, the same year he became grand admiral. The city remained contested terrain between the Ottomans and the Spanish-Habsburgs throughout the 1530s.
In 1543, when Suleiman again marched into Hungary, he “sent a fleet of 110 galleys, under the command of Barbarossa, to assist Francis.” The Franco-Ottoman fleet besieged Nice, then under the control of Charles V’s ally the Duke of Savoy, and the Ottoman fleet wintered in the French port of Toulon — a deeply controversial arrangement that allowed Christian Europe to see Ottoman sailors and Muslim worship on French soil. In return, a small French artillery unit joined the Ottoman army in Hungary. Inalcik notes, however, that “this cooperation, however, was not particularly effective.”
Death and Legacy
Barbarossa Hayreddin Pasha died in 1546 in Istanbul, where his tomb stands to this day in the Beşiktaş district, near the shore from which his fleet sailed. He was succeeded by his lieutenant and protege, Turgut Reis (Dragut), who continued the tradition of Barbary corsair-admirals in Ottoman service.
Inalcik’s narrative makes clear that Barbarossa’s legacy was twofold. First, his victory at Preveza established a generation of Ottoman naval supremacy that made possible the empire’s projection of power across the Mediterranean, from the Adriatic to the coast of Spain. Second, his institutional reforms — the unification of the Mediterranean naval command under a single admiral, the creation of the Algiers beylerbeyilik, and the establishment of the kapudan pasha’s independent naval council — created an administrative structure that outlasted him.
The decline of Ottoman naval power after the 1590s, which Inalcik analyzes in detail — the failure to adopt the new ocean-going sailing ships that replaced galleys, the inability to face combined Christian fleets, the loss of control over the North African corsair provinces — only underscores by contrast the magnitude of Barbarossa’s achievement. Under his command, the Ottoman navy reached a peak of effectiveness that was never again attained. In the broader arc of Inalcik’s account, Barbarossa stands as the figure who transformed the Ottoman Empire from a land power with a supplementary navy into a true Mediterranean naval power, capable of contesting and defeating the combined fleets of Christendom.
Chronology

c. 1478 — Born on the island of Midilli (Lesbos)
1516 — Together with his brother Oruç, captures Algiers from the Spanish
1518 — Oruç killed; Hayreddin becomes leader of Algiers
1519 — Places Algiers under Ottoman suzerainty
1533 — Appointed kapudan-i deryâ (grand admiral); the beylerbeyilik of Algiers created; conquers Tunis
1535 — Charles V captures Tunis
1536 — Franco-Ottoman alliance concluded
1537 — Campaign against Venice; siege of Corfu
28 September 1538 — Decisive victory at the Battle of Preveza against Andrea Doria and the Holy League
1543 — Commands 110 galleys in support of France; siege of Nice; Ottoman fleet winters in Toulon
1546 — Dies in Istanbul; buried in Beşiktaş

entities/bayezid-ii.title: Bayezid II
entities/bayezid-ii.content:
Bayezid II
Bayezid II (1447–1512), known in Ottoman tradition as Sofu or Veli Bayezid (“the Pious” or “the Saint”), was the eighth sultan of the Ottoman dynasty, reigning from 1481 to 1512. The son of Mehmed the Conqueror, he succeeded his father after Mehmed’s sudden death in 1481, defeating his brother Cem in a civil war. His twenty-one-year reign has often been dismissed as “feeble and inglorious,” but Fisher argues it was a period of necessary consolidation — the empire absorbing Mehmed’s conquests while managing a complex web of diplomacy with Venice, the Mamluks, Safavid Persia, the Papacy, and the kingdoms of Europe.
Youth and Character
Bayezid served as governor of Amasya from 1455 to 1481 thirty years in one provincial post. At Amasya he developed into a scholarly, introspective prince who filled his days with music, poetry, philosophy, theology, astronomy, and cosmography. Unlike his warlike father, he was given to books and talk rather than arms. Ottoman historians called him Sofu or Veli — a religious devotee. He wore simple clothes, ate moderately, abstained from wine, and collected finely cut diamonds and hand-wrought metal objects. He was, however, a fine horseman and excellent marksman with the bow. He took part in the 1473 campaign against Uzun Hasan, leading thirty thousand Rumelian troops on the right wing at the Battle of the Euphrates, but he was never a magnetic military leader. After the battle, his troops were found in disorder by Gedik Ahmed Pasha, who publicly reproached him — beginning a feud that would end with Gedik Ahmed’s execution after Bayezid became sultan. He had eight sons: Abdullah, Shehin, Alem, Ahmed, Mehmed, Mahmud, Korkud, and Selim (the future Selim I).
The Succession Crisis
Mehmed II died unexpectedly in his camp at Maltepe in early May 1481, while preparing a campaign. The grand vizier Karamani Mehmed Pasha, hoping to bring Mehmed’s younger son Cem to the throne, concealed the sultan’s death, closed the city to the Janissaries, and sent slaves to notify Cem in Konya. The plot failed: the messengers were caught by the beylerbey of Anatolia, and the Janissaries learned the truth, crossed to Istanbul on any available transport, and lynched the grand vizier, parading through the streets shouting “Long live Bayezid!” The pashas in Istanbul — Ishak Pasha, Daud Pasha, the ağa of the Janissaries, and the beylerbeys of Rumelia and Anatolia — were all relatives of Bayezid by marriage. They raised Bayezid’s son Korkud as a regent and placed the weight of the Janissaries, the solaks (the sultan’s left-handed guards), and the palace pages behind Bayezid. Bayezid learned of his father’s death and rode from Amasya to Istanbul in eight days. At Ismid he halted, sent a trusted slave to confirm with his own eyes that Mehmed was truly dead, then proceeded to Üsküdar, where the pashas greeted him with galleys and barges. The Janissaries surrounded his barge demanding a donative of a thousand akçes per sipahi and three hundred per Janissary plus a daily pay raise. Bayezid agreed, landed, and rode through a cordon of soldiers who also demanded he appoint only Janissary officers, court sipahis, and palace pages as vizirs. He assented and spurred his horse to the palace. Seventeen days had elapsed between Mehmed’s death and Bayezid’s arrival in Istanbul.
The Civil War with Cem
Cem raised twenty thousand men at Konya, including feudal sipahis from Anatolia, asabs (irregular infantry), dervishes, tribesmen from Karamania and the Warsak and Turgud tribes, and the sipahis and timarjis assembled for Mehmed’s cancelled campaign. He marched to Bursa, where the Janissary garrison initially resisted but eventually recognized him, and Bursa opened its gates. Cem had money coined and the Friday sermon read in his name — the two prerogatives of a sultan. Astrologers had spread the rumor that Cem was destined to rule, and many Anatolians preferred him, believing him to be just. The citizens of Bursa, fearing for their property, sent a deputation to Bayezid led by Seljuk Hatun (a daughter of Sultan Mehmed I) and several mollas, proposing the empire be divided: Bayezid keeping the European half and Cem ruling Anatolia. Bayezid replied, “Kings have no relatives,” and prepared for battle. At the plain of Yenişehir on 20 June 1481, Bayezid exhorted his troops, renewed promises of money, and led them personally. He had also corrupted Cem’s chamberlain Yakub with a promise of a hundred thousand akçes and the position of beylerbey of Anatolia. The battle lasted until noon; Cem was decisively defeated. As the chronicler Neşri wrote, “The Karaman crows scattered like young from the nest when they saw the eagles of Bayezid.”
Cem fled through Eskişehir to Konya, then set out for Egypt with his family. Sultan Kaitbay of Egypt received him as a son. Bayezid pursued leisurely, reaching Konya four days after Cem’s departure, and sent Gedik Ahmed Pasha in pursuit as far as Ereğli, the Ottoman-Egyptian border. Bayezid invested his son Abdullah with the governorship of Karaman and returned to Istanbul.
The Gedik Ahmed Crisis
Bayezid had Gedik Ahmed Pasha arrested and imprisoned — partly from personal feud (Gedik Ahmed had publicly humiliated Bayezid at the Battle of the Euphrates) and partly on suspicion that he favored Cem. The Janissaries, sipahis, and silihdars (sword-bearers), who adored Gedik Ahmed as the ablest general in the empire, stormed the palace, threatening Bayezid. They demanded his liberation and the dismissal of his accusers. Bayezid, fearing the troops might turn to Cem, capitulated. Not only did he release Gedik Ahmed, but he made him vizir with special authority. Bayezid then sent Gedik Ahmed to subdue a revolt in Karamania led by Kasim Bey of the Karamanoglu family, which he did.
Cem in Europe and the Diplomatic Crisis
The most persistent foreign relations problem of Bayezid’s reign was the presence of his brother Cem in Christian hands. After leaving Egypt, Cem travelled to Rhodes, where the Hospitaller Grand Master Pierre d’Aubusson saw his value. D’Aubusson imprisoned Cem and negotiated an agreement with Bayezid: the Hospitallers would keep Cem securely in return for an annual payment of 45,000 ducats. Over the next decade, Cem was moved from Rhodes to France and then to the Papal court in Rome, where Pope Innocent VIII and later Alexander VI used him as a lever against Bayezid, threatening to release him at the head of a crusade. Bayezid’s agents paid the Pope, the Hospitallers, and the King of Naples to keep Cem imprisoned and out of active use. The crisis involved diplomacy with France (Charles VIII, who saw Cem as a weapon against the Papacy), Naples (King Ferrante, who housed Cem), the Mamluk sultan Qaitbay (who had offered Cem refuge), and the Venetian Republic. Cem died in Naples in 1495, possibly poisoned, while in the custody of Pope Alexander VI. His death freed Bayezid from the largest diplomatic burden of his reign.
Foreign Policy and Wars
Bayezid’s foreign policy was one of strategic consolidation. He made peace with Venice, which ended the long Ottoman-Venetian war inherited from Mehmed II, though hostilities flared again in 1499–1503 over Venetian holdings in the Morea. Relations with Hungary remained tense along the Danube frontier. The rise of Shah Ismail’s Safavid Empire in Persia posed a new ideological threat: Safavid propaganda spread among Anatolian Turkmen tribes, leading to the Şahkulu rebellion of 1511, a major religious and political uprising that nearly toppled Bayezid. The Ottoman-Mamluk rivalry simmered over control of Anatolian buffer states and the holy cities. Bayezid’s conquest of Kili and Akkirman (1484) secured the mouths of the Danube and Dniester, completing Ottoman control of the Black Sea coast.
Deposition and Death
In his final years, the succession struggle between Bayezid’s sons — particularly Selim (the future Selim I) and Ahmed — tore the empire apart. The Janissaries supported Selim, who was more warlike and resembled Mehmed the Conqueror. In 1512, Selim forced his father to abdicate. Bayezid died shortly afterward, possibly poisoned, on the road to his retirement in Demotika/Dimetoka.
Legacy
Fisher’s revisionist argument presents Bayezid not as a “slothful sultan” but as a ruler who understood that an empire strained by decades of relentless expansion needed a period of internal consolidation. His reign preserved the Ottoman state through a complex international environment, managing the Cem crisis through diplomacy rather than war, making strategic peace with Venice, and keeping the Safavid and Mamluk threats at bay. The foundations he laid made the even more dramatic conquests of his son Selim I — who would conquer Syria, Egypt, and the Holy Cities — possible.

entities/belgrade.title: Belgrade
entities/belgrade.content:
Belgrade — بلغراد (Nándorfehérvár)
Coordinates: 44°47’13”N 20°27’15”E
Belgrade, called Nándorfehérvár by the Hungarians and Belgrad (بلغراد) by the Ottomans, is the fortress city commanding the confluence of the Sava and Danube rivers. For much of the fifteenth century, it was the most formidable obstacle to Ottoman expansion into central Europe — a Hungarian-held bastion that withstood repeated Ottoman sieges until its eventual capture by Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent in 1521. Its possession was the key to control of the middle Danube basin and the gateway to Hungary.
Geography and Strategic Position
Belgrade occupies a high ridge at the junction of the Sava (flowing from the west) and the Danube (flowing from the north), creating a natural fortress defended by water on three sides. The city’s position commands both the riverine route from the Black Sea into central Europe and the overland Via Militaris from Constantinople through the Balkan passes. This strategic location made it the single most important military objective in the northern Balkans — the lock on the door to Hungary and beyond.
The Ottoman road from Istanbul to Belgrade ran through Edirne, Plovdiv, Sofia, and Nish, following the Maritsa and Morava river valleys. The city itself was heavily fortified with a double line of walls, a citadel on the hilltop, and the rivers protecting its approaches.
The Hungarian-Ottoman Contest for Belgrade
Belgrade passed to Hungarian hands in the early fifteenth century when the Serbian despot Stefan Lazarevic bequeathed it to King Sigismund of Hungary. From that point forward, it became the forward bastion of Christian Europe against Ottoman expansion. In 1440, Murad II tried unsuccessfully to dislodge the Hungarians from Belgrade; the failure of this siege demonstrated the strength of the city’s fortifications and the determination of its defenders.
After the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, Mehmed II turned his attention northward. In 1456, he laid siege to Belgrade with a massive army and fleet, but the fortress held. The relief force organized by John Hunyadi — the hero of the Hungarian frontier — broke the Ottoman blockade and inflicted a rare and stinging defeat on the Conqueror. The siege of 1456 became a legendary episode in both Ottoman and Hungarian histories; it marked the limit of Mehmed II’s northern expansion during his lifetime.
During the remainder of the fifteenth century, Belgrade remained in Hungarian hands, serving as the base for cross-Danube raids into Ottoman territory. John Hunyadi, the Regent of Hungary, had used Belgrade as his base against the Rumelian forces, and his son Matthias Corvinus maintained the fortress as the keystone of Hungary’s defensive system.
The Conquest of 1521
