The fall of Belgrade finally came under Sultan Suleiman I. In March 1521, taking advantage of the war between the Habsburg Charles V and Francis I of France — which left Europe divided — Suleiman marched north at the head of a massive expedition. Belgrade fell on 29 August 1521 after a brief but intense siege. The capture removed the greatest obstacle to Ottoman expansion into central Europe and paved the way for the Battle of Mohacs in 1526.
After its capture, Belgrade was repopulated with Muslim settlers and transformed into an Ottoman frontier fortress. It became the administrative center of the Sanjak of Smederevo and the primary staging ground for campaigns into Hungary. The city’s Orthodox Christian population was permitted to remain, but the fortress itself became an Ottoman military headquarters garrisoned by Janissaries.
Role in Trade and Administration
Under Ottoman rule, Belgrade developed into a significant commercial center at the junction of Balkan land routes and Danube river trade. Merchants from Ragusa (Dubrovnik), Bosnia, and the Ottoman interior met in Belgrade’s markets, trading in textiles, metals, leather, and agricultural produce from the fertile Pannonian plain. The city controlled the Danube route that connected the Black Sea ports of Kilia and Akkerman with the markets of Buda and Vienna.
Key Connections
Belgrade was the terminus of the great road from Istanbul and Edirne through Sofia and Nish, and its fate was intimately tied to the Ottoman-Hungarian struggle. The city’s defense by John Hunyadi connected it to the broader resistance of the Hungarian kingdom under Matthias Corvinus. After 1521, Belgrade’s conquest opened the way to Buda and the heart of Hungary, transforming the northern frontier of the Ottoman Empire.

entities/bursa.title: Bursa
entities/bursa.content:
Bursa — بروسه
Coordinates: 40°11’29”N 29°04’05”E
Bursa (Brusa in Western sources), the first capital of the Ottoman state from 1326 to 1402, lies at the foot of Mount Uludag (the ancient Mysian Olympus) in northwestern Anatolia. Captured from the Byzantines by Orhan I, son of Osman I, in 1326, Bursa served as the cradle of Ottoman civilization — the city where the first Ottoman silver coins were struck, the first medreses were established, and the early sultans were buried. It remained the spiritual and commercial heart of the Ottoman enterprise even after the capital moved to Edirne and, later, to Istanbul.
Geography and Strategic Position
Bursa occupies a fertile plain at the base of Uludag, commanding the approaches to the Sea of Marmara. Its position on the ancient silk route connecting Iran, Tabriz, and Central Asia with the Mediterranean ports made it a natural entrepot for the east-west trade in luxury goods. The nearby port of Mudanya on the Sea of Marmara linked Bursa to Istanbul, while caravan routes reached eastward to Ankara and ultimately to Persia and beyond.
Early Ottoman Capital
Osman I, the founder of the Ottoman dynasty, never took the city himself; it was his son Orhan who completed the conquest of the Byzantine fortress of Prusa and made it his seat. In 1327, Orhan struck his first silver coins in Bursa, asserting sovereign authority. In 1331 he established a medrese in nearby Iznik, and in 1340 he created a trading centre in Bursa itself with the construction of a bazaar and a bedestan — an enclosed market for the sale of valuable goods. The Arab traveller Ibn Battuta visited Bursa around 1333 and described it as “a great city with fine bazaars and broad streets.”
The early sultans — Osman I, Orhan I, and Murad I — are buried in Bursa, and the city remained the dynastic necropolis throughout Ottoman history. The Green Mosque (Yeşil Cami) and the Green Tomb (Yeşil Türbe), built under Mehmed I in the early fifteenth century, represent the pinnacle of early Ottoman architecture, with their exquisite Iznik tiles.
Second Capital and Administrative Role
Bursa served as the Ottoman capital for nearly eighty years, from 1326 until 1402, when the defeat of Bayezid I at Ankara by Timur forced a temporary disruption. Even after Murad I moved the capital to Edirne (Adrianople) in the 1360s, Bursa retained immense importance as the Anatolian administrative center and the treasury city. In the Lybyer account of Ottoman government, Bursa ranked alongside Istanbul and Edirne as one of the three highest judicial districts — the judges of Bursa held rank with those of Mecca, Medina, Cairo, and Damascus.
The Brusa Mukataasi Kalemi, or bureau of the leases of Bursa, was one of the specialized treasury offices, keeping account of the domanial leases in the neighborhood of the city. The Muderisler (professors) of Bursa formed the second rank of the Ottoman scholarly hierarchy, below those of Istanbul but above those of all other cities.
Role in the Silk Trade
Bursa was above all the silk city of the Ottoman Empire. Raw silk from Iran and the Caspian region arrived via Tabriz and Erzurum, was processed in Bursa’s workshops, and was then shipped to the markets of Istanbul, Europe, and the rest of the Ottoman world. The silk trade was the single most valuable strand of Ottoman commerce, and the city’s bedestan was the center of this commerce. The bureaus in Istanbul kept accounts of the revenues from silk and from the manufacture of articles in gold and silver, much of which came from Bursa’s artisans.
In the devshirme system, boys taken from Christian families were sent to the region of Bursa or Karamania “to dig the earth, so that they will become accustomed to hard labor, and so that there they may learn the Turkish language” before being enrolled in the Janissary corps or the palace service.
Key Connections
Bursa’s closest connection was with Istanbul, to which it supplied silk, textiles, and trained administrators. The road from Bursa to the port of Mudanya and then by sea to Istanbul was the arterial route of Ottoman internal trade. After the loss of its capital status, Bursa’s rivalry with Edirne defined the duality of the early Ottoman state — Anatolia versus Rumelia. The city also maintained strong commercial ties with Genoa and Venice through the port of Mudanya, and with the Iranian silk producers to the east.

entities/cairo.title: Cairo (al-Qahira / Kahire)
entities/cairo.content:
Cairo — al-Qāhira (القاهرة) / Kahire (قاهره)
Cairo (Ottoman Turkish: قاهره, Kahire; Arabic: القاهرة, al-Qāhira, “the Victorious”) was the capital of the Mamluk Sultanate until 1517 and, after the Ottoman conquest, became the seat of the Ottoman governor (beylerbey) of Egypt and the administrative, commercial, and intellectual center of the richest Ottoman province. Inalcik describes Cairo as one of the great emporia where Indian spices and goods were exchanged with European merchants, and the city’s population, monuments, and markets made it the largest city in the Arab world under Ottoman rule.
Geography and Strategic Position
Cairo is situated on the east bank of the Nile River, just south of the Nile Delta’s apex, at the crossroads of Upper Egypt, the Delta, and the routes leading to the Red Sea at Suez and the Mediterranean at Alexandria. Its position made it the natural hub for Egyptian trade, administration, and pilgrimage traffic to Mecca. The city’s strategic location allowed it to control both the riverine commerce of the Nile and the overland caravan routes connecting Africa, Arabia, and the Mediterranean world.
Under Ottoman Rule
The Ottoman conquest of Egypt came in January 1517, when Selim I defeated the last Mamluk sultan Tuman Bay at the Battle of Raydaniyya on the outskirts of Cairo. Inalcik notes that Selim was still in Cairo when a Portuguese fleet entered the Red Sea to attack Jidda and Mecca, and it was from Cairo that he ordered the construction of a fleet at Suez to drive the Portuguese from the Indian Ocean. Selim appointed Hayra Bay, the former Mamluk governor of Aleppo, as the first Ottoman governor of Egypt.
Under Ottoman administration, Cairo retained much of its Mamluk-era character but was integrated into the imperial system. A Janissary garrison was stationed in the city, and the annual tribute from Egypt — including the irsaliyye (remittance) sent to Istanbul — was managed from Cairo. The city’s ulema and judges were appointed from Istanbul, and the Hanafi school of law was given precedence, though the other Sunni schools continued to operate.
In 1527 the Ottoman authorities carried out a comprehensive cadastral survey of Egypt, recorded in a detailed defter (register) from Cairo, which became the basis for taxation and land tenure in the province.
Role in Trade
Cairo was one of the great commercial centers of the Ottoman Empire, second only to Istanbul. Inalcik records that at the end of the 16th century the English were still buying pepper and other spices in Cairo, though the foundation of the East India Company in 1600 gradually diverted this trade. The city’s markets — particularly the Khan al-Khalili — handled spices, Indian textiles, Yemeni coffee, Sudanese slaves, gold, and ivory. Venetian, French, and English merchants maintained agents in Cairo, and the city’s customs revenues were a major source of income for the Ottoman treasury along with Alexandria.
Inalcik notes that Indian goods were actively exchanged in the markets of Aleppo, Cairo, Istanbul, and Bursa, and that Cairo served as the redistribution point for goods arriving via both the Red Sea route (through Suez) and the Nile.
Population and Society
Cairo in the 16th century was a city of perhaps 200,000–300,000 inhabitants, making it one of the largest cities in the world at the time. Its population included Arabic-speaking Egyptians, Ottoman Turks, Circassian Mamluks, Jews, Copts, Greek merchants, and North African and Sub-Saharan African communities. The city was a center of Islamic learning, with al-Azhar University continuing to function as the premier institution of Sunni scholarship under Ottoman patronage.
Key Connections

Alexandria: Primary Mediterranean port serving Cairo; connected by Nile River and the Mahmudiyya Canal
Suez: Red Sea port 130 km east; the Suez-Cairo road was the main transit corridor for Indian Ocean trade
Mecca & Medina: Cairo was the main assembly point for the Egyptian pilgrimage caravan (Mahmal) and the source of food, water, and cloth for the Holy Cities
Istanbul: Imperial capital; Cairo sent annual tribute and tax remittances via the Mediterranean route
Aleppo: Rival commercial center in Syria; both cities were key nodes in the eastern trade networks

entities/calicut.title: Calicut (Kalikut / Kozhikode)
entities/calicut.content:
Calicut (കോഴിക്കോട് / كاليكوت / Kalikut)
Calicut (Malayalam: കോഴിക്കോട്, Kōḻikkōṭ; Arabic: كاليكوت, Kalikut; Ottoman Turkish: كاليكوت, Kalikut; Portuguese: Calecut), now known as Kozhikode, was the premier spice port of the Malabar Coast and the capital of the Zamorin dynasty. Its name is the origin of the word “calico” (cotton cloth) and it was the landing point of Vasco da Gama’s epochal voyage of 1497-1499, which opened the Cape Route to India and initiated the Portuguese maritime empire in Asia. For the Ottoman Empire and the Mamluk sultanate, Calicut symbolized the Indian Ocean trade that had been disrupted by Portuguese naval power.
Geography and Strategic Position
Calicut lies at approximately 11.26° N, 75.78° E, on the Malabar Coast of southwestern India, between the Arabian Sea and the Western Ghats mountain range. The city is situated on a series of sandy ridges and lagoons along the coast, with its harbor protected by a natural reef. Unlike Goa or Cochin, Calicut lacked a deep natural harbor; ships anchored offshore and goods were ferried to the beaches by small craft. Nevertheless, its position at the center of the Malabar Coast — roughly equidistant between Bombay and Cape Comorin — made it the natural entrepot for the region.
The Malabar Coast was the world’s primary source of black pepper (Piper nigrum), and Calicut was the largest market for it. The city also commanded access to the trade routes leading inland over the Palghat Gap through the Western Ghats to the Coromandel Coast and the Bay of Bengal. The Monsoon winds determined the rhythm of trade: ships from the Red Sea and Persian Gulf arrived between August and October, while those from Southeast Asia came between April and June.
Role in Ottoman and World Trade
Before the Portuguese arrival, Calicut was the greatest spice emporium in Asia. The Zamorin’s port welcomed merchants from Arabia, Persia, Egypt, East Africa, Gujarat, Coromandel, Bengal, Malacca, and China. The city was the primary destination for ships crossing the Arabian Sea from Aden and Hormuz, and its pepper, ginger, cinnamon, cardamom, and other spices supplied the markets of Cairo, Alexandria, Venice, and ultimately all of Europe.
The Ottoman Empire inherited the Mamluk concern with Calicut and the Malabar trade. After the Ottoman conquest of Egypt in 1517, the sultans in Istanbul sought to preserve the traditional spice routes that had passed through the Mamluk domains. Inalcik documents the resilience of this trade: despite Portuguese efforts to sever the routes, the Ottoman Empire continued to receive spices directly from India throughout the sixteenth century, with twenty ships laden with spices arriving annually at Jidda alone (Inalcik, p. 5434-5435). The customs receipts on spices at Bursa reached 7,250 gold ducats by 1582 (Inalcik, p. 5443-5444). The Malabar ports, chief among them Calicut, were the origin of much of this traffic.
Under Ottoman Influence
Calicut was never under Ottoman rule or suzerainty. However, it played an indirect but critical role in Ottoman strategic thinking in the Indian Ocean. The Zamorins of Calicut, as Muslim rulers (though the population was predominantly Hindu), were natural allies of the Ottomans against the common Portuguese enemy. The Zamorin provided pilots, supplies, and intelligence to the Mamluk-Ottoman naval expeditions against the Portuguese.
When Vasco da Gama landed at Calicut on 20 May 1498, he was received by the Zamorin (Samudri Raja) who, having already received rumors of the Portuguese approach, was initially cautious but allowed the Portuguese to trade. The Portuguese bombardment of Calicut in subsequent years — and da Gama’s notorious massacre of the pilgrim ship Miri in 1502 — turned the Zamorin decisively against the Portuguese. The Portuguese established their headquarters at nearby Cochin (1499) because the Raja of Cochin was a rival of the Zamorin, creating a political split among the Malabar rulers that the Portuguese exploited.
The Fisher text (foreign_relations_1481-1512) provides context for the Ottoman awareness of the Portuguese threat. When news of the Portuguese arrival in India reached Venice and Cairo, the Mamluk sultan Al-Ashraf Qansuh al-Ghawri sought Ottoman assistance. The Portuguese destruction of the Mamluk fleet at Chaul in 1509 (Inalcik, p. 5405) prompted the Mamluks to appeal to Bayezid II, who sent naval materials to Suez. In 1510, an Egyptian ambassador came to Edirne to secure “iron, artillery, and ships for the fleet of his sultan, who swore to defeat the Portuguese” (Fisher, p. 4665-4666). Calicut was the central theater of this Mamluk-Ottoman-Portuguese confrontation.
Key Connections

Aden: Western terminus of the Indian Ocean crossing; Calicut-bound ships departed from Aden
Cochin: Rival Malabar port allied with Portugal against the Zamorin of Calicut
Goa: Portuguese capital in India after 1510; base for operations against Malabar ports
Diu: Portuguese fortress in Gujarat; site of the failed Ottoman expedition of 1538
Jidda: Red Sea terminus; spices from Calicut were re-exported from Jidda to Cairo and Istanbul
Cairo/Alexandria: Endpoint of the Indian spice routes through the Red Sea
Lisbon: European destination for Cape Route spices, bypassing Calicut’s traditional Mediterranean customers

entities/cem-sultan.title: Cem Sultan
entities/cem-sultan.content:
Cem Sultan
Cem Sultan (1459–1495), known in the West as Djem, was the younger son of Mehmed the Conqueror and the principal rival to his brother Bayezid II in the succession struggle following Mehmed’s death in 1481. His flight to Rhodes and thirteen-year imprisonment by the Order of St. John (the Hospitallers) became the defining diplomatic crisis of Bayezid’s reign, drawing in the Papacy, France, Naples, the Mamluk Sultanate, and the Venetian Republic. Fisher devotes extensive attention to the “Fear of Cem’s Return” — the thread that ran through all of Bayezid’s foreign policy.
Character
Cem was twelve years younger than Bayezid. He was a poet of considerable fame, and was described as more energetic, warlike, severe, and open-handed than his brother. These qualities were more in accordance with those of his father Mehmed II, and it was widely believed that Mehmed preferred Cem as his successor. Cem himself claimed to be the legitimate heir, arguing that he was born in the purple (after his father became sultan) while Bayezid was not. At the time of Mehmed’s death, Cem was governor of Karaman with his seat at Konya, while Bayezid governed Amasya three hundred miles from Istanbul.
The Plot of Karamani Mehmed Pasha
When Mehmed II died unexpectedly in May 1481, the grand vizier Karamani Mehmed Pasha and the chief judge Manisali Chelebi — both partisans of Cem and leaders of the ulema — conspired to place Cem on the throne. They concealed Mehmed’s death, closed Istanbul to the Janissaries, and sent slaves posthaste to Konya to summon Cem. The plot failed. The messengers were caught and held by the beylerbey of Anatolia, and one was impaled for denying his mission. The Janissaries learned the truth, crossed to Istanbul, lynched the grand vizier, and raised Bayezid to the throne.
The Civil War
Upon learning of Bayezid’s accession, Cem gathered troops at Konya and set out for Bursa. His army grew to perhaps twenty thousand men: feudal sipahis from Anatolia, asabs (irregular infantry), dervishes, tribes from Karamania, the Warsak and Turgud tribes, plus the sipahis and timarjis who had assembled for Mehmed’s cancelled campaign. At Bursa the Janissary garrison initially resisted but Cem persuaded them that he, too, as a son of the late padishah, had a right to their allegiance. Bursa opened its gates. Cem had money coined and the Friday sermon read in his name — the two prerogatives of a sultan — becoming the acknowledged sovereign of part of the Ottoman state. The citizens of Bursa, fearing the destruction of their city, sent a deputation to Bayezid proposing the empire be divided: Bayezid keeping Europe, Cem ruling Anatolia with Bursa as his capital. Bayezid replied, “Kings have no relatives,” and prepared for battle.
Bayezid corrupted Cem’s chamberlain, Yakub, with the promise of a hundred thousand akçes and the position of beylerbey of Anatolia if he persuaded Cem not to retreat to Karamania but to fight. Cem’s advisors urged him to refuse battle, but he chose to fight. On 20 June 1481 at the plain of Yenişehir, Bayezid’s superior forces defeated Cem’s army. Yakub betrayed Cem, leading troops over to Bayezid’s side during the battle. As the chronicler Neşri wrote, “The Karaman crows scattered like young from the nest when they saw the eagles of Bayezid.” Cem, slightly wounded, fled to Eskişehir by morning and reached Konya a week later.
Flight to Egypt
After three days in Konya, Cem gathered his family and three hundred followers and set out for Egypt. He was honored along the route in Tarsus, Aleppo, Damascus, and Jerusalem. In Cairo, Sultan Kaitbay of the Mamluk Sultanate received him as a son — a diplomatic signal that the Mamluks were prepared to use Cem against Bayezid. Bayezid pursued as far as Ereğli, the border with Egypt, and then returned to Istanbul.
Rhodes and the Hospitallers
From Egypt, Cem travelled to Rhodes, the island fortress of the Knights of St. John. The Grand Master, Pierre d’Aubusson, immediately recognized Cem’s value and imprisoned him. D’Aubusson negotiated an agreement with Bayezid: the Hospitallers would keep Cem securely imprisoned in exchange for an annual payment. Fisher records that the initial arrangement was for 45,000 ducats per year, paid from the Ottoman treasury. Cem was held in the fortress of Rhodes, treated with the honor due a prince but kept under close guard — a golden cage.
The European Diplomacy
Cem was transferred from Rhodes to France, and then to Rome, where he became a pawn in the complex politics of Renaissance Italy. Pope Innocent VIII and later Alexander VI saw Cem as a weapon: they could threaten Bayezid with releasing Cem at the head of a crusade. Bayezid’s agents paid the Pope to keep Cem secure. King Ferrante of Naples also became involved, offering Cem refuge. King Charles VIII of France, during his invasion of Italy in 1494–1495, saw Cem as a potential leader for a crusade. The crisis involved continuous diplomacy: Bayezid sending gifts and payments to the Pope, the Hospitallers, and the King of Naples, while maintaining agents in Rome and Naples who tracked every move.
Death
Cem died in Naples in February 1495, while in the custody of Pope Alexander VI during Charles VIII’s invasion. The cause of death is uncertain — possibly poison, possibly illness. His death removed the most serious internal threat to Bayezid’s rule and freed the Ottoman treasury from the annual payments to his captors. It also removed the most potent diplomatic weapon that Christendom held against the Ottoman Empire.
Legacy
In Ottoman historiography, Cem is a tragic figure — a prince who nearly became sultan, who commanded Bursa and struck coins in his own name, but who spent his final thirteen years as a prisoner in the hands of the Christian powers. The Cem affair demonstrated to Bayezid and his successors the danger of allowing an Ottoman prince to fall into European hands, and it shaped Ottoman succession practice: from this point, fratricide became the norm, as Mehmed the Conqueror’s law code permitting the killing of brothers was more rigorously enforced to prevent the emergence of claimants who could be used against the state.

entities/constantine-xi.title: Constantine XI Palaiologos
entities/constantine-xi.content:
Constantine XI Palaiologos (Κωνσταντῖνος ΙΑʹ Παλαιολόγος)
Constantine XI Palaiologos (1405–1453) was the final Byzantine emperor, reigning from 1449 until his death in battle during the fall of Constantinople on 29 May 1453. The seventh emperor of the Palaiologos dynasty, he is remembered for his heroic last stand against the overwhelming forces of Mehmed II.
Accession
Constantine was born on 8 February 1405, the fourth son of Emperor Manuel II Palaiologos and Helena Dragaš. Before his accession, he served as despotes (δεσπότης, lord) of the Morea (the Peloponnese), where he worked to consolidate Byzantine authority and built the Hexamilion wall across the Isthmus of Corinth. He ascended the throne in 1449 after the death of his brother John VIII, ruling without the customary coronation in Constantinople.
Relations with Mehmed II
Upon Mehmed II’s accession in 1451, Constantine renewed the peace treaty with the new sultan. Kritoboulos records that Mehmed “first made an agreement with the Romans and Emperor Constantine” (Book I, §24) along with other powers, to secure his borders while he consolidated power.
When Mehmed began building the fortress of Rumeli Hisarı (Boğazkesen) on the European shore of the Bosporus in 1452, Constantine and the people of Constantinople “judged the matter to be dangerous, the source of great troubles, and an open attempt at conquest” (Book I, §34). He sent ambassadors to protest, arguing that the construction violated existing treaties. Mehmed dismissed their objections, and Constantine could do nothing to stop the fortress’s completion.
The Union of Churches
Constantine pursued the union of the Eastern Orthodox and Roman Catholic churches, a politically necessary but deeply unpopular policy. The Union of Florence (1439) had already been agreed by his predecessors, but it faced fierce opposition from the Orthodox populace. Despite his efforts to secure Papal military aid, Kritoboulos notes that the church union had actually created internal turmoil: “they are rather enemies of each other in every way, since they are waging bitter war over matters of religion. And for this very reason the city is also turned upside down with internal ferment and turmoil” (Book I, §81).
The Siege of Constantinople
As the Ottoman army and fleet surrounded the city in April 1453, Constantine commanded a vastly outnumbered garrison of approximately 7,000 men against Mehmed’s forces of some 200,000–300,000. He rejected Mehmed’s offer of surrender, saying “it was impossible for them to surrender the city to him” (Book I, §115).
During the siege, Constantine and his forces fought desperately. Kritoboulos describes the terror of the defenders: “Emperor Constantine and the people of the city, terrified by the sudden change, and seeing that war had fallen upon them without declaration… entirely gave up the idea of sending ambassadors” (Book I, §91). They repaired walls, cleared ditches, blocked the Golden Horn with chains, and gathered money from churches, the public treasury, and private citizens.
The Genoese commander Giovanni Giustiniani Longo, who led the land defense, was wounded during the final assault and withdrew, prompting panic. Kritoboulos records:

“And although Emperor Constantine begged them and pleaded with them to remain only a little while, until the combat should quieten down, they paid no attention to him, but taking their leader, unarmed and in great haste, they rushed to the ships” (Book I, §231).

Constantine, now without enough men to hold the breached walls, faced the end.
Death in Battle
The last emperor died fighting on the walls. Kritoboulos describes his death with particular poignancy:

“Here Emperor Constantine also falls, fighting heroically with his men” (Book I, §235).

In his praise of the emperor, Kritoboulos writes:

“Emperor Constantine also fell fighting, who in private life was a sober and temperate man, who set great store by prudence and virtue, was intelligent and very cultivated; in matters of state and government he lagged behind none of the emperors who reigned before him; he saw what had to be done more quickly than anyone, and decided upon it even more quickly; he was a powerful speaker, an even more powerful planner, but most powerful of all in execution” (Book I, §273).

Kritoboulos notes that Constantine refused offers to escape:

“having the means to escape, though many urged him to do so, he would not, but thought it better to die together with his country and his subjects” (Book I, §274).

His final words, according to the tradition recorded by Kritoboulos, were:

“The city is taken, and I am still alive!” — after which he threw himself into the enemy ranks and was cut to pieces (Book I, §275).

His body was never identified.
The Fall of the City
Kritoboulos marks the date with precision:

“It was taken, therefore, in the time of Emperor Constantine, the seventh Palaiologos, on the twenty-ninth day of the month of May according to the Roman reckoning, in the year six thousand nine hundred and sixty-one from the creation of the world” (Book I, §272).

He reflects on the symmetry of names:

“For the fortunate Emperor Constantine, son of Helen, founded and raised the city to the highest degree of happiness and fortune; and conversely, in the time of the unfortunate Emperor Constantine, son of Helen, the city fell and sank into the most terrible servitude and unhappiness” (Book I, §270).

Significance
Constantine XI is venerated as a martyr and saint in the Greek Orthodox tradition. His refusal to abandon his city and his death in battle became the defining symbol of the end of the Byzantine Empire. Kritoboulos, writing for the Ottomans, nonetheless paid him the highest tribute: “Such a worthy man, living and dying for the common good, yet throughout his entire life unfortunate and in his death the most unfortunate of men” (Book I, §275).

entities/crimea-caffa.title: Crimea and Caffa (Kirim / Kefe)
entities/crimea-caffa.content:
Crimea and Caffa (قريم / Kırım — كفه / Kefe)
The Crimea (Ottoman Turkish: قريم, Kirim; Crimean Tatar: Qirim) is a peninsula on the northern coast of the Black Sea, connected to the Ukrainian steppe by the narrow Perekop Isthmus. Caffa (Ottoman Turkish: كفه, Kefe; modern Feodosia) was the principal port and commercial center of the peninsula. Together, the Crimean Khanate and the Ottoman-held ports formed a critical zone for the empire’s northern trade, military strategy, and slave economy. The region was a vassal state of the Ottoman Empire ruled by the Giray dynasty of the Crimean Tatars.
Geography and Strategic Position
The Crimean peninsula lies between approximately 44.4° N and 46.0° N, extending into the Black Sea. The southern coast is protected by the Crimean Mountains, while the northern steppe descends gradually toward the Perekop Isthmus. Caffa (45.025° N, 35.38° E) occupied a deep natural harbor on the peninsula’s southeastern coast. The peninsula controlled the sea routes between the Black Sea and the Sea of Azov, and the land routes from the Pontic steppe and Central Asia to the Ottoman heartland. Lybyer notes that the steppe land was “kept uninhabited in order to afford a free passage to their light horse” by the Tatars (Lybyer, p. 1139-1140).
Under Ottoman Rule
The Ottoman conquest of Caffa in 1475 (Inalcik, p. 5532-5533) marked the beginning of direct Ottoman control on the Crimean coast. The Genoese colony that had dominated Caffa was extinguished, and the city became the seat of an Ottoman sanjak governed by a prince of the Ottoman dynasty. Fisher notes that Prince Mahmud, son of Bayezid II, was governor of Kaffa (Fisher, p. 2437), and that “Kaffa (more exactly in eastern Europe) and Trebizond were some of the cities where the sons held their courts” (Fisher, p. 4750-4751).
The Crimean Khanate, ruled by the Giray dynasty, became an Ottoman vassal. The khan was nominally sovereign but was confirmed in office by the sultan, with Ottoman law operating alongside khanate custom. Lybyer notes that in the Crimea, “the rendering of justice was in their [kadi] hands, while the other functions of government were performed by a vassal state in light obedience” (Lybyer, p. 1339-1341). Inalcik records that “the khanate of the Crimea” was one of the “subject Muslim principalities” within the Ottoman system (Inalcik, p. 4664).
The khanate supplied the Ottoman army with cavalry contingents: “The Tartars of the Crimea, and the Moldavians and Wallachians, were also obliged to furnish contingents” (Lybyer, p. 3051-3052). Crimean Tatar auxiliaries served in Ottoman campaigns in Hungary, Iran, and the Caucasus. The route from Central Asia through the northern Caspian ports to the Crimea was vital for the Uzbek khanates in maintaining contact with the Ottomans (Inalcik, p. 1771-1775).
The region also served as a source of slaves for the Ottoman Empire. Lybyer notes that slaves came “from the captives that the Tartars of the Crimea took in great numbers, from the quasi-slave-farms of the Caucasus” (Lybyer, p. 1720-1721). The slave market at Caffa was notorious throughout the period.
Role in Trade
Caffa was the great northern emporium of the Black Sea. Inalcik records that according to customs accounts for 1490, “seventy-five vessels called at Caffa in four months,” including Greek, Italian, Russian, and Muslim ships (Inalcik, p. 5560-5562). The cargoes included silk, gold brocades, and velvet from Iran shipped through Sinop to Caffa, cotton fabrics from Merzifon and Amasya (Inalcik, p. 5582-5587), and local products such as wine, arak, hazel-nuts, and ships’ masts from Trebizond (Inalcik, p. 5589-5590).
The old trade route “from Caffa, Akkerman and Kilia into Poland passed through Moldavia, and Suceava in Moldavia and Lwow (Lemberg) in Poland developed as rich entrepots” (Inalcik, p. 5549-5551). Ottoman control of these ports was “as much an economic as a political necessity” (Inalcik, p. 5553). The customs registers for Caffa, Akkerman, and Kilia from 1490 to 1512 “provide a detailed record of economic activity” in the northern Black Sea trade with the Mediterranean (Inalcik, p. 5555-5558).
Ottoman subjects - “Armenians, particularly those from Caffa, Jews, Greeks and Muslim Turks - began to capture the Levant trade from the Italians” after the Ottoman conquests (Inalcik, p. 5539-5541). The timber, grain, fur, honey, wax, and slave trades all passed through Caffa.
Key Connections

Istanbul/Galata: Maritime route across the Black Sea; primary destination for Crimean grain and slaves
Sinop/Trebizond: Southern Black Sea ports supplying manufactured goods and textiles
Akkerman/Kilia: Western Black Sea ports on the Danube delta, controlled by the Ottomans from 1484
Moldavia/Poland: Northern trade routes passing through the Moldavian corridor
Azov/Tana: Eastern Sea of Azov ports
Central Asian khanates: Northern Caspian route through Astrakhan
Moscow/Russia: After the 1497 trade agreement, Muscovite merchants traded freely in the Ottoman Empire (Inalcik, p. 1782)

entities/damascus.title: Damascus (Dimashq)
entities/damascus.content:
Damascus (دمشق / Dimashq)
Damascus (Arabic: Dimashq; Ottoman Turkish: شام, Şam) is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, serving as a major political, commercial, and religious center in Syria. It sits in the Ghouta oasis at the eastern foot of the Anti-Lebanon mountain range, commanding the approaches from the Syrian desert to the Mediterranean coast. Under the Ottomans, Damascus became a pivotal provincial capital and the primary staging point for the annual Hajj pilgrimage caravan to Mecca.
Geography and Strategic Position
Damascus lies at approximately 33.5° N, 36.3° E, on a fertile alluvial plain watered by the Barada River. Its location at the junction of the Mediterranean coast, the Syrian desert, and the routes to the Euphrates valley gave it enduring strategic importance. The city served as the terminus of caravan routes from Baghdad and the Persian Gulf, as the southern anchor of the road linking Aleppo and Anatolia, and as the gateway for pilgrims and traders crossing the desert to Arabia. The Ottoman road from Istanbul to Damascus was one of the empire’s most heavily travelled arteries, studded with imarets (soup kitchens) and caravanserais built by pashas and local benefactors that provided food, lodging, and stabling free of charge to travellers (Inalcik, p. 6674-6677).
Under Ottoman Rule
The Ottoman army entered Damascus in 1516 after Sultan Selim I’s victory over the Mamluks at Marj Dabiq. Inalcik records that the Ottoman forces “overcame the resistance of the remaining Mamluk forces and took Damascus and Jerusalem” (Inalcik, p. 1560). Damascus was made the capital of the Damascus Eyalet, one of the largest and wealthiest provinces of the empire. The city retained much of its Mamluk-era character, with Ottoman governors (valis) ruling alongside the established local notables and religious elite.
The city played a significant role in the Ottoman-Mamluk rivalry before 1516. During the 1480s and 1490s, Damascus was repeatedly a venue for diplomacy and intrigue between the Porte and the Mamluk sultanate. In 1482, the rebel prince Jem was “honored in Tarsus, Aleppo, Damascus, and Jerusalem” on his flight to Cairo (Fisher, p. 775). In 1499, an Ottoman embassy including a sister of Bayezid II was dispatched to Damascus “to bring about a peaceful settlement” in the escalating conflict with Egypt (Fisher, p. 2497). Karta Bey of Damascus rebelled against Egyptian dominance in the late 1490s, spurring Ottoman intervention on the Syrian frontier (Fisher, p. 2491).
In the 19th century, Damascus was contested during the Egyptian occupation under Muhammad Ali. As Stephens records, the armies of Ibrahim Pasha “swept all Syria; Jerusalem, Damascus, and Aleppo fell into his hands” (Stephens, p. 1392).
Role in Trade
Damascus was the preeminent commercial hub of southern Syria and a critical node in the overland spice and silk trades. Inalcik notes that by the late 15th century, the caravan trade from Damascus to Bursa was “wholly in the hands of Muslim merchants, mostly from Aleppo and Damascus, many of whom had invested great sums” (Inalcik, p. 5374). The traveller Bertrandon de la Brocquiere in 1432 described joining a group of pilgrims and merchants in Damascus returning from Mecca “with a caravan of three thousand camels” (Inalcik, p. 5362).
Damascus was known for its textile and metalworking industries. Schiltberger in the 14th century compared Bursa’s silk trade and industry with that of Damascus and Caffa (Inalcik, p. 5305). By the mid-16th century, the customs levied at Damascus on spices brought by pilgrim caravans “rose to 110,000 gold ducats” in a single year (1562), with European merchants buying spices there and exporting them through Beirut (Inalcik, p. 5438-5442). After the Ottoman-Venetian war of 1570-73, French merchants increasingly frequented Damascus, and by the early 17th century there were about a thousand French vessels active in Levantine commerce (Inalcik, p. 6203-6206).
The city’s role as the assembly point for the Hajj caravan, the Surre-i Humayun, was of immense religious and economic importance. The pilgrimage route from Damascus to Mecca was maintained and protected by the Ottoman state, generating substantial revenues from customs and the provisioning trade.
Key Connections

Bursa/Istanbul: Overland caravan route through Anatolia; terminus for spices and silks destined for the capital and the Balkans
Beirut: Primary port for Damascus’s Mediterranean trade
Aleppo: Northern Syrian commercial rival and partner
Mecca/Medina: Southern pilgrimage route through the Hejaz
Jerusalem: Secondary destination within the Damascus Eyalet
Baghdad: Eastern caravan route across the Syrian desert

entities/david-comnenus.title: David Comnenus (David Megas Komnenos)
entities/david-comnenus.content:
David Comnenus — David Megas Komnenos (Δαβίδ Μέγας Κομνηνός, داوود کومننوس)
David Comnenus (c. 1408 – 1 November 1463) was the last Emperor of Trebizond, reigning from 1460 to 1461. He was the second son of Emperor Alexios IV of Trebizond and the successor of his brother John IV. His brief reign ended with the surrender of Trebizond to Mehmed II in 1461, and he was executed two years later after being accused of plotting against the sultan. Kritobulos of Imbros, in Book IV of his history, provides the most detailed contemporary account of the fall of Trebizond and David’s surrender.
Early Life and Accession
David was a son of Emperor Alexios IV Megas Komnenos (r. 1417–1429) and Theodora Kantakouzene. His elder brother John IV ruled Trebizond from 1429 until his death in 1460. During John’s reign, David served as a military commander and participated in diplomatic missions, including an embassy to the Council of Florence in 1439 where he engaged in negotiations over church union.
Upon John’s death in 1460, David succeeded him. The situation was precarious: Trebizond had been a tributary of the Ottoman Empire since the reign of Murad II, paying an annual haraç (خراج, tribute), but the balance of power had shifted dramatically after Mehmed II’s conquest of Constantinople in 1453.
The Trebizond Tribute and Secret Diplomacy
Kritobulos explains the status of Trebizond after 1453 in Book IV (§8–9): “After circumstances changed, however, and the Great Emperor took Constantinople with a great army and became master of the Straits, and the road to the Black Sea and the cities lying upon it was opened clearly before him both by land and by sea, then this city too, along with the others, submitted and began to pay homage, and its emperors, bending the knee before the Great Emperor, became tributaries.”
As long as the emperors “remained at peace with one another, paid the tribute, and hatched no new plots, the Emperor too was at peace with them” (Book IV, §9). However, the Comneni had fallen into factional strife and civil war. Worse, David engaged in secret diplomacy with Uzun Hasan (حسن اوزون), the Aq Qoyunlu (“White Sheep”) Turkoman ruler, forming a marriage alliance — David’s niece Theodora (known as Despina Khatun) married Hasan — and conspiring against the Ottomans.
Kritobulos writes that it became apparent that “being related by marriage to the neighboring princes — the princes of Tigranocerta, the Armenians and Medes, to Hasan the son of Tomuris, and even to the princes of the Iberians — they were plotting something and contriving plans against the alliance with the Emperor.” To Mehmed, this was an intolerable threat on his eastern flank, especially since Trebizond lay within easy reach of Hasan’s territories.
The Ottoman Campaign of 1461
Mehmed prepared a massive amphibious campaign during the winter of 1460–1461, gathering about three hundred ships and a land army of some sixty thousand cavalry and eighty thousand infantry (Book IV, §10–15). The fleet was commanded by Kasim Pasha and Yakub Pasha, while Mehmed himself led the land army.
The campaign was carefully designed to isolate Trebizond. First, Mehmed marched on Sinope, whose ruler Isma’il was compelled to surrender his city in exchange for a governorship in Europe (Book IV, §16–24). Then Mehmed crossed the formidable Taurus Mountains — Kritobulos notes that in doing so he was “the third after Alexander and, after him, the Romans and Pompey” to cross with an army (Book IV, §27) — despite Uzun Hasan’s attempt to block the passes. Mehmed cleverly bypassed Hasan’s army by cutting a new route through the untrodden wilderness and marching directly toward Hasan’s capital at Tigranocerta, forcing Hasan to negotiate rather than fight.
The Surrender of Trebizond
The Ottoman fleet arrived before Trebizond first and blockaded the city by sea and land. After twenty-eight days of siege (Book IV, §40), Mahmud Pasha arrived with the advance land forces and sent Thomas, son of Katabolenos, to negotiate. The offer was generous: if the city surrendered by agreement, the emperor would receive “much land and sufficient revenue for their sustenance and enjoyment,” and the citizens could “dwell in peace with their wives and children without the least trouble, and might remain in their homeland and possessions” (Book IV, §42). If they resisted, they would face “slaughter, plunder, and slavery” (Book IV, §43).
When Mehmed himself arrived the next day, the citizens and David chose to surrender. Kritobulos describes the scene (Book IV, §45–46): “And the Emperor of Trebizond also came out from Trebizond with his children and all his household to pay homage to the Emperor. The Emperor received him graciously and humanely, and after shaking his hand and esteeming him fittingly, richly rewarded him, his children, and all who were with him.”
Mehmed entered the city, inspected the citadel and imperial palace, and “declared the city remarkable in every respect” (Book IV, §47). He then commanded that David, his household, and the most powerful citizens be embarked on galleys and transported to Constantinople. He selected about fifteen hundred adolescent youths from the city and region, and placed a garrison of four hundred men under Kasim Pasha as governor.
Exile in Adrianople
Upon arriving in Constantinople, Mehmed’s “first care was to provide for the Emperor of Trebizond” (Book IV, §53). He granted David and his retinue “sufficient land for their sustenance in the region of the Strymon river, so that from there he could collect an annual revenue of three hundred thousand silver coins.” David was effectively an exile in his own former empire, living under Ottoman supervision near Serres in the Strymon valley.
It was in this exile that the philosopher George Amiroutzes (Γεώργιος Αμιρούτζης), who had been in David’s retinue, was summoned by Mehmed. The emperor, learning of his philosophical learning, “admired him greatly, and honored him with a suitable place beside himself” (Book IV, §55). Amiroutzes’ transfer to Mehmed’s circle, along with his son, would later play a role in David’s downfall — some accounts accuse Amiroutzes of betraying David’s alleged plot.
Execution (1463)
David’s exile ended in 1463. He was accused of plotting to reclaim Trebizond, allegedly in secret correspondence with Uzun Hasan — the very alliance that had doomed his reign. The charges were plausible: Hasan was still at large, still hostile to Mehmed, and the marriage alliance between the Comneni and the Aq Qoyunlu was well known.
Kritobulos’ history, while it covers the surrender of Trebizond in detail, does not narrate David’s execution — Kritobulos’ work concludes with the plague of 1467, but the main narrative of Book IV ends with the Trebizond campaign, and the later sections of Book V focus on Bosnia, Hungary, and the Illyrian wars. The execution is recorded in other contemporary sources: Doukas, Chalcocondylis, and Ottoman chronicles.
On 1 November 1463, David Comnenus, his three sons (Basil, Manuel, and George), and his nephew Alexios were executed in Constantinople. Some accounts add that David’s youngest son was spared and converted to Islam. The execution marked the final extinction of the Megas Komnenos dynasty and the end of the Empire of Trebizond.
Legacy
David Comnenus was the last Christian emperor to rule over the ancient Pontic Greek territory of Trebizond. His decision to seek an alliance with Uzun Hasan — a gamble that failed — accelerated the destruction of his empire. In Kritobulos’ account, David is treated with a certain dignity: he is not an enemy who fought to the death but a prince who, recognizing the hopelessness of his position, surrendered on terms and was initially treated with honor. His subsequent execution, though not recorded by Kritobulos, reflects the ruthlessness of Mehmed II toward any claimant who might serve as a rallying point for resistance. For the Pontic Greeks, David’s fall marked the end of a Greek state that had outlived Constantinople itself by eight years.

entities/demetrios-palaiologos.title: Demetrios Palaiologos (Demetrios Palaiologos, Despot of the Morea)
entities/demetrios-palaiologos.content:
Demetrios Palaiologos — Demetrios Palaiologos, Despot of the Morea (Δημήτριος Παλαιολόγος, دمتریوس پالایولوگوس)
Demetrios Palaiologos (c. 1407–1470) was Despot of the Morea (the Peloponnese) from 1449 to 1460, ruling jointly with his younger brother Thomas. His catastrophic civil war with Thomas and his decision to invite Mehmed II into the Peloponnese led to the Ottoman conquest of the entire province. He is extensively covered in Book III of Kritobulos’ history, which records his surrender of Sparta, compensation with the islands of Lemnos and Imbros and the city of Ainos, and his eventual retirement and conversion to Islam.
Background
Demetrios was the son of Emperor Manuel II Palaiologos and Helena Dragaš, making him a member of the Palaiologan dynasty. He was the younger brother of the last Byzantine Emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos and the older brother of Thomas Palaiologos. After the death of their older brother Theodore II in 1448, Constantine (then despot) was called to Constantinople to assume the imperial throne, leaving the Morea to be divided between Demetrios and Thomas.
The Morea (the medieval name for the Peloponnese) was the last substantial territory of the Byzantine Empire not under direct Ottoman control. It was a wealthy province, described by Kritobulos as “very strongly fortified by nature, which possesses strong and notable cities and very many and nearly impregnable fortresses” (Book III, §4). The two brothers ruled it jointly, but their relationship was poisoned by rivalry.
Tribute and Civil War
After the fall of Constantinople in 1453, the Morean despots had called upon Mehmed II for aid against an Albanian-Greek revolt. In return, they promised an annual tribute of six thousand gold staters (Book III, §1). However, they soon fell behind on payments. Kritobulos records that “whenever the emperor demanded the tribute, they always devised vain pretexts and unbecoming reasons for delay, and did not pay readily; although they themselves collected the tribute from the Illyrians every year, they squandered it on their own useless expenses, caring nothing for the emperor” (Book III, §2).
Worse still, “the despots also quarreled among themselves and would not listen to one another, and waged civil wars and battles” (Book III, §3). Mehmed II, fearing that their dissension might draw the Italians into the Peloponnese, resolved to occupy the province (Book III, §4).
Kritobulos describes the civil war in Book III (§110–111): “they then began open war against each other and prosecuted it vigorously, invading each other’s territory with great armies, overrunning and plundering it, setting fire to settlements, capturing fortresses and partly also demolishing them, plundering and harming their own kinsmen in every way.” The Albanian troops in the Morea went over to Thomas, whose faction became dominant. Thomas “pursued his brother and shut him up with his wife and daughter in barren Epidaurus, which is now called Monemvasia” (Book III, §111).
The Invitation to Mehmed II
Cornered in Monemvasia, Demetrios grew desperate. Kritobulos records two versions of what happened next (Book III, §112–113). Some say that Demetrios sent his trusted advisor Manuel Asanes to Mehmed not merely for military assistance but also “to betroth his daughter to the emperor, to surrender the entire Peloponnese, and to ask for himself, in exchange, another province within the emperor’s empire as a principality.” Others say he only asked for military help against Thomas.
Whatever the truth, Mehmed seized the opportunity. Already angry at Thomas for breaking the treaty and calling in the Italians, and fearing “that the Peloponnese might fall into the power of others” (Book III, §114), Mehmed marched against the Peloponnese in the spring of 1460.
The Surrender of Sparta
Mehmed arrived at Corinth in just twenty-seven days. Demetrios, rather than meeting the emperor there, remained in Sparta and sent Asanes with gifts (Book III, §116). Mehmed had Asanes arrested and, in a revealing move, invaded Demetrios’ friendly territory rather than Thomas’ hostile one — proving “that they themselves had voluntarily invited the emperor in, surrendering themselves and the Peloponnese to him, out of hatred for Despot Thomas” (Book III, §117).
Mahmud Pasha was sent by night to Sparta, where he caught Demetrios at dawn and shut him up in the city (Book III, §118). Mehmed sent friendly proposals, urging Demetrios to trust the emperor. After some negotiation — Demetrios insisted on Asanes’ release with an imperial pledge — Demetrios surrendered himself and the city. Kritobulos writes that “all this, as I have said, was only affected thus before the world; in secret they planned and acted otherwise” (Book III, §119), implying that the surrender was pre-arranged.
When Demetrios was brought before Mehmed, the emperor treated him with extraordinary honor: rose from his seat as the despot entered, gave him his hand, seated him beside himself, and “conversing with him very peacefully and humanely, encouraged him with gentle and kind words to recover from his terror” (Book III, §121). He richly rewarded Demetrios and had his wife and daughter brought from Epidaurus with honor (Book III, §122).
Compensation: Lemnos, Imbros, and Ainos
After the entire Peloponnese was subdued, Mehmed returned to Adrianople with Demetrios and his family. There, the emperor gave the despot a generous settlement (Book III, §144–147):
First, he gave him “the islands in the Aegean Sea — Imbros and Lemnos — and whatever people still remained in Thasos and Samothrace,” yielding an annual revenue of three hundred thousand silver aspers.
Second, he gave him “Ainos — this notable and many-good-things-abounding city, which lies on the Thracian coast near the mouth of the Evros River” — another three hundred thousand aspers.
Third, he allocated an additional ten thousand aspers from the Adrianople mint for three years.
The total annual revenue was thus seven hundred thousand silver aspers — a substantial income that allowed Demetrios to live in comfort even in exile.
Kritobulos personally played a role in this settlement. As the governor of Imbros, he had earlier negotiated with the leading men of Lemnos for its transfer from Italian to Ottoman control, and he wrote to Demetrios urging him to request the islands from the emperor (Book III, §78–80). Demetrios sent Asanes to make the request and “obtained the islands from him, agreeing to pay the emperor an annual tribute of three thousand gold pieces for them.”
Retirement and Conversion to Islam
Demetrios lived in Ainos and the islands for the remainder of his life. However, his fortunes eventually declined. After the Ottoman-Venetian War (1463–1479), Imbros and Lemnos fell temporarily to the Venetians, and Demetrios lost his revenues. Some accounts record that he was reduced to poverty and, in his old age, converted to Islam — perhaps under pressure or as a pragmatic move to secure a pension. He died around 1470 in Adrianople.
His daughter Helen (or Helena) had been offered in marriage to Mehmed II but the marriage never took place; she eventually married a member of the Ottoman court.
Legacy
Demetrios Palaiologos has been condemned by historians for effectively trading the Peloponnese for a comfortable exile. Kritobulos, however, presents a more nuanced picture: a prince caught between a hostile brother, a dominant Ottoman power, and his own weakness, who chose submission as the only path to survival. His willingness to surrender the Morea made Mehmed’s conquest of the Peloponnese almost bloodless — a notable contrast to the fierce resistance the Ottomans encountered elsewhere. In Ottoman terms, Demetrios was a vassal who kept his side of the bargain, and Mehmed rewarded him accordingly. In Greek memory, he is one of the Palaiologoi who presided over the final extinction of Byzantine rule in the Peloponnese.

entities/edirne.title: Edirne (Adrianople)
entities/edirne.content:
Edirne — ادرنه (Adrianople)
Coordinates: 41°40’37”N 26°33’20”E
Edirne, called Adrianople by Europeans, served as the second capital of the Ottoman Empire from the 1360s until the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, and remained a major imperial residence and administrative center long afterward. Captured from the Byzantines under Murad I in 1361, the city was transformed from the capital of Byzantine Thrace into the launching point for Ottoman expansion into the Balkans. Sultan Mehmed II was born in Edirne in 1432, and successive sultans maintained palaces there for hunting and retreat well into the sixteenth century.
Geography and Strategic Position
Edirne lies at the confluence of the Maritsa (Meriç) and Tundzha rivers in eastern Thrace, near the modern borders of Turkey, Greece, and Bulgaria. It commanded the eastern end of the Via Militaris, the Roman road through the Balkans to Belgrade, and sat astride the main route from Constantinople into central Europe. This position made it the natural base for Ottoman campaigns into Serbia, Hungary, and beyond — the gateway to Rumelia.
The fertile plains of Thrace supplied the city with abundant grain, and the Maritsa valley became one of the most densely settled regions of the early Ottoman Balkans. The city’s location on the Tundzha also placed it at the head of the route through the Karnobad Pass across the Balkan Mountains.
Ottoman Capture and Transformation
The capture of Adrianople in 1361 marked the decisive breakthrough of the Ottomans into Europe. Under the command of Murad I (then a prince) and his tutor Lala Şahin, the Ottoman forces first took the fortresses along the Maritsa valley and cut the supply lines to the city. When the final sortie of the defenders failed, Adrianople surrendered. The conquests in Thrace followed a pattern: towns submitting voluntarily were left in possession of their property under Ottoman suzerainty.
Murad I made Adrianople his capital, building a palace and establishing the administrative apparatus of the Rumelian province. From Edirne, the Ottomans directed their expansion westward along the Via Egnatia toward the Albanian coast, northward into Bulgaria and Serbia, and across the Danube into Hungary. The city served as the base for the great campaigns that culminated in Kosovo (1389), Nicopolis (1396), and Varna (1444).
Capital and Imperial Residence
For nearly a century, Edirne was the nerve center of the Ottoman state. The palace schools — the Enderun — had a branch in Edirne, where three to four hundred pages were trained alongside the main palace in Istanbul. The schools in Pera and Adrianople ranked lower than those in the principal palace, but they produced many capable administrators and soldiers.
In the judicial hierarchy, the judges of Adrianople formed the second tier alongside Bursa, ranking below the judges of Constantinople but above those of all other cities. The Muderisler of Adrianople likewise occupied the second rank of the scholarly hierarchy.
The grand viziers conducted the affairs of state from Edirne during the winter months when campaigns were not possible, and the sultans particularly favored the city for hunting. After the 1509 earthquake, Bayezid II spent considerable sums repairing the palace and its walls there, and the city remained a frequent imperial residence throughout the sixteenth century. The Selimiye Mosque, built by Mimar Sinan under Selim II (1569–1575), still dominates the skyline as a monument to the city’s enduring importance.
Role in Trade and Administration
The customs duties of Edirne were among the principal revenues of the Ottoman treasury, managed through the Istanbul Mukataasi Kalemi alongside those of Constantinople, Smyrna, and Gallipoli. The city’s markets served the trade routes connecting Istanbul to the Balkan interior — merchants from Edirne traded in cottons and silks as far as the Black Sea ports of Caffa and Kilia. The bazaars of Edirne were famous for their leather goods, textiles, and the output of the city’s numerous artisans.
Key Connections
Edirne’s relationship with Istanbul after 1453 was one of complement rather than competition — the older capital became a secondary imperial residence and the primary staging ground for Balkan campaigns. The city was closely linked to Sofia and Belgrade along the Roman military road that the Ottomans maintained and improved. Bursa across the straits was Edirne’s counterpart in Asia, the two former capitals representing the dual Anatolian-Rumelian character of the early Ottoman state.

entities/erzurum.title: Erzurum (Erzurum)
entities/erzurum.content:
Erzurum (ارضروم / Erzurum)
Erzurum (Ottoman Turkish: ارضروم, Erzurum; also known as Arzan al-Rum in medieval Arabic sources) is a historic fortress city on the high plateau of eastern Anatolia. It commanded the approaches from Persia and the Caucasus into central Anatolia, making it a key strategic outpost for both the Byzantine and Turkish empires. Under the Ottomans, Erzurum was the most important military and commercial center on the empire’s eastern frontier.
Geography and Strategic Position
Erzurum lies at approximately 39.9° N, 41.3° E, at an elevation of about 1,900 meters (6,200 feet) on the Anatolian plateau. It sits in a basin of the Karasu River, the headwaters of the Euphrates, and commands the main east-west route through eastern Anatolia. Inalcik notes that in Mongol times there was an imperial highway “linking Tabriz to Konya through Erzurum, Erzincan and Sivas” (Inalcik, p. 5249-5250), and silk caravans from Iran came to Aleppo “through Erzurum and along the Euphrates valley” (Inalcik, p. 5346-5347). The fortress of Erzurum guarded the passes from the Caucasus and Azerbaijan into the Anatolian interior, controlling the narrow defile of the Deveboynu (Camel’s Neck) pass.
Under Ottoman Rule
Erzurum was contested between the Akkoyunlu, Safavids, and Ottomans during the late 15th and early 16th centuries. The decisive change came with Selim I’s victory at Chaldiran (23 August 1514). This victory “allowed Selîm to annex to the Ottoman Empire the mountainous region from Erzurum to Diyarbekir” (Inalcik, p. 1527-1528). By 1516-17, the local dynasties and tribal chieftains of the area recognized Ottoman suzerainty (Inalcik, p. 1528-1529).
Erzurum became the capital of an Ottoman eyalet, serving as the military headquarters for campaigns against Safavid Iran. The main road from Istanbul to Erzurum was one of the empire’s most important, and travellers along it “had all their needs provided for, usually free of charge” through a network of pious foundations (Inalcik, p. 6676-6678). To revive villages destroyed in the Persian wars, Suleyman I permitted “the establishment of zaviyes on the Erzurum road” (Inalcik, p. 6777).
Iron deposits near Erzurum, particularly at Kigi, were exploited for military purposes: “iron balls from Kigi near Erzurum were sent from Trebizond across the Black Sea and up the Danube to the Hungarian battlefields” (Inalcik, p. 6012-6014). The city also served as a supply base for grain and munitions during the 16th-century Persian campaigns.
Role in Trade
Erzurum was a vital waystation on the silk route between Persia and the Mediterranean. Silk caravans from Tabriz passed through Erzurum en route to Erzincan, Sivas, and ultimately to Bursa or Aleppo for sale to European merchants. The city also served as a market for goods from the Caucasus and a meeting point for merchants from Persia, Anatolia, and the Black Sea region. Its position made it a natural entrepot for the exchange of Iranian silks and Central Asian goods for Anatolian and European wares.
Key Connections

Tabriz: Eastern route through the Deveboynu pass into Azerbaijan
Erzincan/Sivas: Western route into central Anatolia
Trebizond: Northern route through the Zigana Pass to the Black Sea
Van/Bitlis: Southern route around Lake Van
Kars/Georgia: Northeastern route into the Caucasus
Aleppo: Southeastern route through Diyarbekir and the Euphrates valley

entities/gedik-ahmed-pasha.title: Gedik Ahmed Pasha
entities/gedik-ahmed-pasha.content:
Gedik Ahmed Pasha
Gedik Ahmed Pasha was an Ottoman general and statesman who rose from the ranks of the common Janissaries to become beylerbey, vizier, and the empire’s ablest military commander under Mehmed the Conqueror. His career — and the long-standing feud with Bayezid II that defined its end — illustrates the volatile relationship between the sultan, the Janissary corps, and the great pashas in the early Ottoman state. He was executed on Bayezid’s orders in November 1482, days after the final flight of Cem Sultan.
Rise from the Ranks
Unlike the great pashas educated in Mehmed II’s palace school — men like Hersekoglu Ahmed Pasha or Karamani Mehmed Pasha — Gedik Ahmed began his soldiering as a common Janissary. This background shaped his entire career. The Janissaries never forgot that one of their own had climbed to the highest offices of the state, and his rise gave him an unshakable base of loyalty among the troops that no palace-trained vizier could match. Under Mehmed II he was promoted to beylerbey and then to vizier, accumulating a string of victories over many years. He was son-in-law to Ishak Pasha, who served as grand vizier during the early months of Bayezid’s reign.
The Battle of the Euphrates and the Feud with Bayezid
In 1473, when Mehmed II campaigned against Uzun Hasan of the Ak Koyunlu, both Bayezid (then prince-governor of Amasya) and his brother Mustafa led wings of the Ottoman army. Bayezid commanded some thirty thousand Rumelian troops on the right wing, while Mustafa led a like number of Anatolian troops on the left. The sultan dispatched Gedik Ahmed Pasha to inspect Bayezid’s forces. Finding them in disorder, Gedik Ahmed publicly reproached the prince — a humiliation Bayezid never forgot. According to the chronicles, Bayezid was so angered that he declared Gedik Ahmed would someday rue this day. The battle itself was indecisive, but the wound to Bayezid’s pride was permanent.
The Otranto Campaign and the Recall
By 1480, Mehmed II had secured a foothold in southern Italy at Otranto. Gedik Ahmed Pasha was placed in command at Valona (Vlorë), across the Adriatic, in charge of the campaign to reinforce and relieve the Ottoman garrison at Otranto. When Mehmed died suddenly in May 1481 and the civil war between Bayezid and Cem erupted, Bayezid immediately ordered Gedik Ahmed’s recall. Ships at Gallipoli were prohibited from ferrying more men to Valona or Otranto, and the general was ordered back with his troops — effectively abandoning the Italian foothold. Gedik Ahmed reportedly protested vigorously against abandoning the Otranto expedition, one of several grievances Bayezid would later hold against him.
The Civil War and the Pursuit of Cem
Gedik Ahmed arrived at Isnik (Nicaea) with sixteen thousand men from Valona in time to join Bayezid’s forces before the decisive battle at Yenişehir on June 20, 1481. He was graciously received. After Cem’s defeat, Bayezid sent Gedik Ahmed in pursuit as far as Ereğli on the Ottoman-Egyptian border, where the chase halted. Bayezid then invested his son Abdullah as governor of Karamania at Konya and ordered Gedik Ahmed to follow him back to Istanbul.
Arrest and the Janissary Storming of the Palace
Eight days after Bayezid returned to Istanbul, Gedik Ahmed Pasha and his company arrived. Bayezid sent for him, detained him, and had him imprisoned — partly from the old personal feud dating to 1473 and partly on a report that he favored Cem. The imprisonment was orchestrated by Bayezid’s maternal uncle, Vizier Mustafa Pasha, and by Rustem, the ağa of the Janissaries and Bayezid’s son-in-law.
The next morning, when the court learned of the arrest, there was an immediate and violent reaction. The Janissaries, sipahis, and silihdars (sword-bearers), with most of their officers and the court, came to the palace threatening all who had proposed the arrest. By force they entered the outer gate and had Bayezid at their mercy. Bayezid feared they might turn to Cem. The troops were unanimous in their support of Gedik Ahmed. He was described as just, generous, thoughtful in citing valiant deeds in official dispatches, and eager to recommend men according to their merits. That he frequently became intoxicated and then haughty and inflexible was not held against him in the soldiers’ eyes. They demanded his liberation and the dismissal of those who had accused him.
After many speeches, Bayezid acceded. He not only released Gedik Ahmed but honored him with the position of vizier with special authority. Mustafa Pasha was imprisoned in a castle on the Dardanelles and Rustem fled in a light galley to the Mediterranean. According to the eyewitness account of the Venetian chronicler DaLezze, Gedik Ahmed, when brought before the sultan, was very humble. Bending one knee to the floor, he declared himself the faithful slave of the sultan, who could do with and dispose of his slaves at his pleasure. He was apologetic for the presumption of the Janissaries and admonished those present to obey the sultan. How much of this was political expediency, DaLezze noted, remains an open question.
The Karamania Campaign
Beyond fear of the troops, Bayezid had another reason to release Gedik Ahmed: he needed an able general to quell a revolt in Karamania. Kasim Bey, the last scion of the Karamanoglu dynasty, had attacked the Ottoman forces of Ali Pasha, Prince Abdullah’s governor in Konya. Leaving a son in Istanbul as a hostage, Gedik Ahmed set out with several thousand Janissaries, asabs, and his own troops. Kasim Bey withdrew to Selefke as the Ottomans advanced in two columns. Near Mut, Ali Pasha was attacked, but Kasim retreated to Tarsus when Gedik Ahmed relieved Ali. Gedik Ahmed established his chief winter quarters at Larenda, acting as governor of Karamania. Kasim Bey was not destroyed, however, and spent the winter gathering allies — including Cem, who attempted a second invasion in the spring of 1482.
The Execution at Edirne
Gedik Ahmed was blamed for the numerous desertions from his winter quarters and was ordered to rejoin the main army, but by November 1482 Cem had fled to Rhodes and Bayezid no longer feared the military consequences of removing his old enemy. After a great banquet at Edirne, the pasha was seized and executed.
From Bayezid’s point of view the counts against Gedik Ahmed were many: he had sharply criticized Bayezid’s military leadership in the 1473 campaign; he had protested abandoning Otranto; he was too great a favorite of the troops, as the palace storming of 1481 had demonstrated; he had not countenanced the treaty with Venice; he had practically resigned from negotiations with the Knights of Rhodes, arguing that paying for the imprisonment of Cem prostituted the dignity of the Ottoman state; he and his father-in-law Ishak Pasha had plotted successfully against Mustafa Pasha, Bayezid’s favorite; and he was very outspoken, especially when drunk.
Upon his execution, some of the troops rebelled, but the uprising was crushed. Shortly afterward, Daud Pasha, beylerbey of Anatolia, replaced Ishak Pasha as grand vizier. Bayezid was slowly moving toward personal and absolute control.
Legacy
Gedik Ahmed Pasha’s career is a revealing case study in the tensions at the heart of the early Ottoman state. He embodied the ghazi tradition — a soldier of humble origin who rose by merit and the sword — against the increasingly palace-centered, bureaucratic administration that Mehmed II and Bayezid II were building. His popularity with the Janissaries was both his greatest strength and, ultimately, the quality that made his elimination necessary once Bayezid felt secure enough to assert his authority. The feud that began with a battlefield reproach in 1473 ended with a strangler’s cord in 1482 — a reminder that in the Ottoman empire, personal grudges could be as fatal as military defeat.

entities/genoa.title: Genoa (Ceneviz)
entities/genoa.content:
Genoa — جنويز (Ceneviz)
Coordinates: 44°24’27”N 8°56’03”E
Genoa (Ottoman Ceneviz, جنويز) was the great maritime republic of the western Ligurian coast and Venice’s principal rival for the Levant trade. Though Genoa never matched Venice’s staying power in the eastern Mediterranean, the Genoese were the pioneers of the Black Sea trade and maintained a network of fortified colonies — Galata, Caffa, Amasra, and the Phocaeas — that made them indispensable intermediaries in the Ottoman economy. The fall of Constantinople in 1453 transformed Genoa’s position in the east, as the republic’s colonies were absorbed one by one into the expanding Ottoman sphere.
Geography and Strategic Position
Genoa occupies a narrow coastal strip between the Ligurian Sea and the Apennine mountains, a city of steep hills and deep harbors that turned its back on the Italian interior and faced resolutely toward the sea. The city’s natural harbor — one of the finest on the Italian coast — made it the natural terminus for trade routes linking the Mediterranean with the Alpine passes to central Europe and the Rhineland.
Genoa’s eastern empire was a commercial network rather than a territorial state: the Genoese colony of Galata (across the Golden Horn from Constantinople) controlled trade through the Bosphorus; Caffa in the Crimea commanded Black Sea commerce; Amasra (Amastris) on the Anatolian Black Sea coast handled timber and minerals; and Old and New Phocaea on the Aeolian coast produced the alum essential for European textile manufacturing.
Genoa in the Fifteenth-Century Ottoman World
Genoese relations with the Ottomans were complex and shifting. During the siege of Constantinople in 1453, a company of Genoese mercenaries under Giovanni Giustiniani-Longo formed the backbone of the Byzantine defense. When Giustiniani was wounded and fled to his ship, the morale of the defenders collapsed — a moment that Ottoman and Western sources alike identified as the turning point. The Genoese commander’s flight opened the breach through which the Ottoman forces entered the city.
After the conquest, Mehmed II moved quickly to neutralize Genoese power. Galata — the Genoese colony on the northern shore of the Golden Horn — was allowed to surrender on terms, retaining its commercial privileges in exchange for acknowledging Ottoman sovereignty. But the other colonies fell in rapid succession: Amasra in 1459 (the Genoese population was resettled in Istanbul); Old and New Phocaea in 1460; and, most critically, Caffa in 1475. The conquest of Caffa by Gedik Ahmed Pasha ended Genoese rule in the Black Sea and gave the Ottomans direct control over the great Crimean slave market.
The Genoese Trading Network
The Genoese were the great carriers of the Black Sea trade. From their base at Caffa, they exported slaves — mostly from the Ukrainian and Caucasian steppes — to the Mamluk sultanate in Egypt and to the Italian city-states. They also traded in grain from the Crimean hinterland, fish, caviar, furs, and timber from the forests of the Pontic coast. In return, they brought Italian woolens, Mediterranean spices, and silver from the mines of central Europe.
Genoese merchants from Caffa and Kilia were active in the transit trade to Bursa and the Anatolian interior. The alum mines at Phocaea supplied the European textile industry with the mordant essential for fixing dyes — a Genoese monopoly before the Ottoman conquest. Even after the fall of their colonies, individual Genoese merchants continued to trade in Ottoman ports, operating under the same capitulatory regime that governed Venetian and Ragusan commerce.
Genoa and Ottoman Politics
The Genoese played a significant role in Ottoman dynastic politics during the late fifteenth century. During the civil war between Bayezid II and his brother Cem Sultan, Genoese merchants offered to supply ships for a crusade, and the Genoese community in Pera (Galata) maintained close ties with both the Ottoman court and the Italian states. The Genoese banker-businessmen of Galata acted as intermediaries between the sultan and European powers, handling diplomatic correspondence and financial transactions.
Unlike Venice, which maintained a permanent bailo in Istanbul, Genoa’s diplomatic representation was more intermittent, handled through the Genoese community in Galata and occasional ambassadors. The republic’s political decline in the later fifteenth century — driven by internal factionalism, the loss of the eastern colonies, and the rise of the Ottoman navy — meant that Genoa gradually ceded its position in the Levant to Venice and the Ottomans themselves.
Key Connections
Genoa’s most important Ottoman relationship was through Galata, the Genoese colony that became a neighborhood of Istanbul after 1453 while retaining its distinct identity and commercial privileges. The colony of Caffa in the Crimea was the linchpin of Genoa’s Black Sea system until its conquest in 1475. The alum mines of Phocaea, the timber ports of Amasra, and the slave markets of the Black Sea connected Genoa to the broader Ottoman economy. Genoa’s traditional rivalry with Venice played out across Mediterranean and Ottoman contexts, each republic seeking Ottoman favor at the other’s expense.

entities/goa.title: Goa (Goa)
entities/goa.content:
Goa (गोवा / گوا / Goa)
Goa (Konkani: गोंय, Gõy; Marathi: गोवा; Portuguese: Goa; Ottoman Turkish: گوا, Goa) is a historic port city on the west coast of India, strategically located on the southern bank of the Mandovi River estuary. Conquered by the Portuguese under Afonso de Albuquerque in 1510, it became the capital of Portuguese India (Estado da India) and the nerve center of the Portuguese maritime empire in Asia. For the Ottomans and the Mamluk sultanate that preceded them in Egypt, Goa represented the forward base of Portuguese power that had shattered the traditional Indian Ocean spice trade.
Geography and Strategic Position
Goa lies at approximately 15.5° N, 73.8° E, on the central Malabar coast of western India. The city occupies the southern bank of the Mandovi River, which forms a broad, deep estuary providing an excellent natural harbor protected from the open sea by a series of headlands and shoals. The site offered fresh water, fertile hinterlands, and access to the interior trade routes of the Deccan plateau. Its position on the Arabian Sea placed it at the center of the Monsoon-mediated shipping network that connected East Africa, Arabia, Persia, and Southeast Asia.
The city is divided by the Mandovi River from the northern bank (Bardez) and by the Zuari River to the south. The island of Goa (Tiswadi), on which Old Goa was built, was naturally defensible: surrounded by rivers and salt marshes, it required only limited fortifications to protect the harbor approaches.
Under Portuguese Rule and Relation to the Ottoman Empire
Goa was captured by Afonso de Albuquerque on 25 November 1510, after an initial failed attempt earlier that year. Albuquerque defeated the forces of the Sultan of Bijapur, Ismail Adil Shah, with the support of a fleet of 23 ships and 1,200 Portuguese soldiers, supplemented by local Malabari allies. Goa thereafter served as the capital of the Portuguese Estado da India, replacing Cochin as the administrative center for Portuguese operations stretching from East Africa to Malacca and the Spice Islands.
The transformation of Goa into a heavily fortified Portuguese stronghold had profound consequences for the Ottoman Empire and the broader Islamic world. The Portuguese used Goa as:

The headquarters for the cartaz (pass) system that regulated all Indian Ocean trade
The naval base from which Portuguese fleets raided the Red Sea and blockaded the spice routes to Egypt and Syria
The administrative center that coordinated Portuguese operations from Hormuz to Malacca and from Mombasa to Macau
The shipbuilding center for the Portuguese Indian Armadas

The Ottoman response was shaped by the prior Mamluk experience. The Portuguese had destroyed a Mamluk fleet at Chaul in 1509 (Inalcik, p. 5405), and when the Mamluk sultan appealed for Ottoman aid, Bayezid II sent naval supplies and craftsmen to Suez. In 1510, an Egyptian ambassador came to Edirne to “secure iron, artillery, and ships” for the fight against the Portuguese (Fisher, p. 4665-4666). The Ottoman admiral Kemal Reis was dispatched with supplies but his fleet was wrecked near Samos (Fisher, p. 4667-4669).
After the Ottoman conquest of Egypt (1517), Selim I ordered the construction of a fleet at Suez specifically to challenge Portuguese control of the Indian Ocean. The major Ottoman expedition against Diu in 1538 — which failed partly because the local Gujarati sultan refused to cooperate (Inalcik, p. 5417-5420) — was aimed at breaking Portuguese dominance whose linchpin was Goa. Goa thus stood, from the Ottoman perspective, as the symbol of Portuguese maritime supremacy that the Ottomans could never fully dislodge.
Role in Trade
Under the Portuguese, Goa became the great emporium of the eastern spice trade. Pepper, cinnamon, cloves, nutmeg, and mace from the Malabar coast and the Spice Islands (Moluccas) were collected at Goa for shipment around the Cape of Good Hope to Lisbon. The city also handled:

Indigo and cotton textiles from Gujarat and the Coromandel coast
Precious stones (diamonds from Golconda, rubies from Burma)
Chinese silks and porcelains brought by Portuguese ships from Macau and Malacca
Horses from Arabia and Persia, imported via Hormuz
