Slaves from East Africa and Southeast Asia
Ivory and gold from Sofala and Mozambique

The Portuguese monopoly from Goa diverted much of the spice trade that had formerly flowed through the Red Sea and Persian Gulf to Ottoman ports like Alexandria, Beirut, and Aleppo. However, as Inalcik demonstrates, the Ottoman spice trade proved resilient: Venetian purchases in Alexandria between 1560 and 1564 matched pre-Cape Route volumes (Inalcik, p. 5428-5430), and the Lisbon market suffered periodic crises as a result.
Key Connections

Hormuz: Portuguese vassal state controlling the Persian Gulf; coordinated with Goa
Diu: Portuguese fortress in Gujarat; site of the failed Ottoman expedition of 1538
Cochin: Early Portuguese headquarters in India, later subordinate to Goa
Mombasa: East African port briefly under Ottoman suzerainty; opposed to Portuguese-controlled Mozambique and Goa
Lisbon: Metropolitan capital; Goa was the administrative link between Lisbon and all Portuguese Asian possessions
Suez: Ottoman naval base for Indian Ocean expeditions targeting Portuguese positions including Goa
Aden: Ottoman-controlled Red Sea port; the Ottoman-Portuguese naval confrontation for Indian Ocean dominance centered on the Aden-Goa axis

entities/hormuz.title: Hormuz (Hurmuz / Hürmüz)
entities/hormuz.content:
Hormuz — هرمز (Hürmüz) / Hurmuz (هرمز)
Hormuz (Persian: هرمز, Hormoz; Ottoman Turkish: هرمز, Hürmüz; Portuguese: Ormuz) was a fortified island city-state at the mouth of the Persian Gulf, controlling the narrow Strait of Hormuz — the sole maritime passage from the Indian Ocean into the Persian Gulf and the trade routes to Basra, Baghdad, and the Levant. In the sixteenth century it was the central node of the Portuguese cartaz system, which required all Indian Ocean shipping to purchase passes from Portuguese authorities at Hormuz. The island’s capture by the Portuguese in 1507 and the failed Ottoman expedition of 1552 to dislodge them marked a critical episode in the Ottoman-Portuguese struggle for the Indian Ocean.
Geography and Strategic Position
Hormuz sits on a barren, arid island approximately 8 km in diameter, located at 27.07° N, 56.46° E, just 8 km off the coast of modern Iran. The island’s position commands the narrowest point of the Strait of Hormuz — the 40-km-wide channel connecting the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and the open Indian Ocean. Unlike the verdant port of Bandar Abbas on the mainland, Hormuz Island is virtually waterless and treeless, with all supplies imported from the mainland. Its strategic value derived entirely from its command of the sea lanes: every ship entering or leaving the Persian Gulf had to pass within sight of its cannon.
The kingdom of Hormuz, which ruled both the island and substantial mainland territories on both the Persian and Arabian coasts, was the dominant maritime power in the Gulf before the Portuguese arrival. Its wealth came from the transshipment of Indian spices, Persian silk, Arabian horses, and pearls.
Role in Ottoman and World Trade
Hormuz was the choke point of the Persian Gulf trade route, one of the three great arteries connecting the Indian Ocean to the Mediterranean world (alongside the Red Sea route and the overland route through Iran). Until the Portuguese blockade, spices from India and Indonesia, silks from China and Persia, and precious goods from all over Asia passed through Hormuz for onward shipment to Basra, Baghdad, Aleppo, and the Mediterranean markets of Europe.
The Portuguese seizure of Hormuz in 1507 (secured by Afonso de Albuquerque in 1515) transformed it into a monopoly-controlled fortress. The Portuguese cartaz system, enforced from Hormuz, required all ships trading in the Indian Ocean to purchase a pass, pay duties, and call at designated Portuguese ports. This system directly threatened the Ottoman-controlled spice routes through the Red Sea and the Persian Gulf. Inalcik notes that in the second half of the sixteenth century the Portuguese even permitted the sale of spices to the Near East at Hormuz (Inalcik, p. 5402-5403), suggesting a pragmatic loosening of the blockade as they recognized the resilience of the Levantine spice trade.
Under Ottoman Influence
Hormuz was never under Ottoman rule, but it was a central object of Ottoman strategic concern. The Ottomans’ advance to the Persian Gulf came after Suleyman I’s campaign of 1534-35, when Baghdad and lower Iraq were annexed. By 1538 the Emir of Basra had submitted to the Ottomans, and by 1546 Basra was developed as a second naval base after Suez for equipping fleets against the Portuguese (Inalcik, p. 1748-1752).
The key event was the Ottoman expedition of 1552. Inalcik records: “In 1552 an Ottoman expedition failed to oust the Portuguese from the island of Hormuz which controlled the Persian Gulf” (Inalcik, p. 1752-1754). The expedition, involving a combined fleet from Suez and Basra, besieged the Portuguese fortress but ultimately failed to capture it. The chronology of Ottoman setbacks in 1552 also included the failure at Erlau in Hungary, as the empire struggled to sustain simultaneous wars in Europe, Iran, and the Indian Ocean (Inalcik, p. 9562-9564).
Later, in the 1560s, Hormuz figured in English commercial ambitions. English merchants, seeking to bypass Ottoman middlemen and obtain spices more cheaply, explored a route from Moscow through Iran to Hormuz. Inalcik notes: “A Turkish embassy, sent to the shah in 1562, aimed at preventing this diversion” (Inalcik, p. 6222-6224). The Ottomans successfully prevented the opening of this alternative route until 1578, when they invaded Azerbaijan and Shirvan and gained control of it.
Key Connections

Basra: Ottoman Persian Gulf base; launch point for expeditions against Hormuz
Bandar Abbas: Mainland Iranian port that rose to rival Hormuz after the English and Dutch entered the Gulf in the early 17th century
Strait of Hormuz: The strategic passage; control of Hormuz meant control of the Gulf
Muscat: Portuguese stronghold on the Arabian coast of the Gulf of Oman
Goa: Portuguese capital in India; the administrative center coordinating Portuguese strategy from Hormuz to Malacca
Baghdad: Ottoman provincial capital; economic hinterland for Hormuz-bound trade via Basra
Aleppo: Mediterranean terminus of the Persian Gulf trade route through which Hormuz goods reached European markets

entities/istanbul.title: Istanbul (Constantinople)
entities/istanbul.content:
Istanbul — Konstantiniyye (قسطنطينيه)
Coordinates: 41°00’49”N 28°57’18”E
Istanbul, known to the Ottomans as Konstantiniyye (قسطنطينيه) and colloquially as Istanbul (استانبول), is the city that straddles two continents at the crossroads of Europe and Asia. Conquered by Sultan Mehmed II on 29 May 1453 after a fifty-four-day siege, it was proclaimed the new Ottoman capital when the Conqueror entered the Hagia Sophia, converted it into a mosque, and declared “Hereafter my capital is Istanbul.” The city sits astride the Bosphorus strait, commanding the maritime routes between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean, and its Golden Horn harbor made it the natural center of Ottoman trade, administration, and imperial ideology.
Geography and Strategic Position
Istanbul occupies a triangular peninsula on the European shore of the Bosphorus, flanked by the Sea of Marmara to the south and the Golden Horn to the north. The Byzantine land walls — the strongest fortifications of the Middle Ages — protected the city on its western flank. The city’s position at the junction of the overland route from Europe (the Via Egnatia) and the sea routes from the Black Sea, the Aegean, and the Mediterranean gave it unrivaled strategic importance. The Bosphorus, only seven hundred meters wide at its narrowest, had been controlled by the Ottomans even before 1453 through the fortresses of Anadolu Hisari (built by Bayezid I) and Rumeli Hisari (built by Mehmed II in 1452), which together commanded the strait and allowed the sultan to regulate all shipping between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean.
Mehmed II considered himself the legitimate heir to the Roman Empire, and the new capital bridged the two continents of his domain. The natural deep-water harbor of the Golden Horn, sheltered from the currents of the Bosphorus, provided anchorage for the imperial fleet and attracted merchant shipping from across the Mediterranean world.
The Conquest and Its Aftermath
The siege of Constantinople lasted fifty-four days, from 6 April to 29 May 1453. The defending force numbered some 8,500 men, including a company of Genoese mercenaries under Giovanni Giustiniani-Longo, against a regular Ottoman army of no fewer than fifty thousand. The Conqueror battered the city walls with cannons larger than any yet seen in Europe — a victory for modern siege weaponry over medieval fortifications. When Giustiniani was wounded and fled to his ship, the defenders’ morale collapsed, and Ottoman forces entered through a breach opened by cannon fire. The Venetian bailo and other European witnesses recorded the final assault.
The three-day sack permitted by religious law was stopped by Mehmed on the first day. He entered the city in procession, prayed at Hagia Sophia, and converted the great church into the imperial mosque. The patriarchate was reestablished under Gennadios Scholarios, ensuring continuity for the Orthodox Christian population. The Conqueror then ordered the immediate construction of a palace — first on the site of the Forum Tauri (completed 1455), then, feeling insecure in the heart of the city, the new Topkapi Palace (Saray-i Cedid) on Seraglio Point overlooking the Bosphorus, begun in 1459.
Repopulation and Reconstruction
After the conquest, Istanbul’s population had been severely depleted. Mehmed II issued orders to the judges and governors of the realm to send to the capital one family in every ten from the empire’s cities. Merchants, men of means, and artisans were forcibly settled from Amasra (1459), Old and New Phocaea (1460), Trebizond (1461), Corinth and Argos in the Morea (1458, 1463), Karaman (1470s), Euboea (1473), and Caffa (1475). According to the contemporary observer Giovannantonio Angiolello, “within a short time, these new arrivals had constructed remarkable homes and churches.” Muslims, Christians, and Jews — including the Jews expelled from Spain after 1492 — were settled in designated quarters, creating the cosmopolitan fabric that would define Ottoman Istanbul.
The system of vakifs (pious foundations) created the great cultural and commercial complexes of the city. Hagia Sophia became the Great Mosque; Mehmed II ordered the construction of the Fatih Mosque complex (1463–1470), the first great imperial mosque of Ottoman Istanbul, with its associated medreses, hospital, library, and baths. The Covered Bazaar (Kapalıçarşı) grew around the original bedestan built by the Conqueror, becoming the commercial heart of the city.
Role in Trade and Administration
Istanbul became the seat of the Ottoman imperial government, the Porte (Bab-ı Ali), from which the grand vizier and the imperial council administered the empire. The Topkapi Palace housed the Inner and Outer Services — the palace school (Enderun) where pages were trained for high office, the treasury, and the imperial kitchens. The city’s customs duties contributed to the treasury bureaus: the Istanbul Mukataasi Kalemi managed the domanial leases of Salonika, Tirhala, and Bursa, the market dues of Istanbul and Edirne, and revenues from silk and gold-and-silver manufacture.
By the sixteenth century, Istanbul was the largest city in Europe outside Italy, a hub for the spice trade, silk from Bursa and Iran, slaves from the Black Sea region, and grain from Egypt and the Danube lands. The city’s docks at the Golden Horn and the arsenal at Kasimpasa made it the center of Ottoman naval power.
Key Connections
Istanbul was intimately connected with the earlier Ottoman capitals of Bursa (the first capital in Asia) and Edirne (the second capital in Europe), which served as models for its development. Its conquest forced the Genoese colony of Galata, across the Golden Horn, to accept Ottoman suzerainty. The Venetian bailo maintained a permanent diplomatic mission in the city, reflecting the tense relationship with Venice that oscillated between trade and war throughout the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries.

entities/jidda.title: Jidda (Jeddah / Cidde)
entities/jidda.content:
Jidda — Judda (جدة) / Cidde (جده)
Jidda (Ottoman Turkish: جده, Cidde; Arabic: جدة, Jidda or Judda) was the principal seaport of Mecca and the most important Red Sea entrepot for the Indian Ocean spice trade. Under Ottoman rule, Jidda became a vital link between the empire and the wider Islamic world, serving both as the landing point for pilgrims arriving by sea for the Hajj and as the primary harbor for goods arriving from India, East Africa, and Southeast Asia.
Geography and Strategic Position
Jidda lies on the west coast of the Arabian Peninsula, on the Red Sea, approximately 80 km west of Mecca. Its natural harbor, protected by coral reefs, made it the safest anchorage on the Hejazi coast and the natural port of entry for maritime traffic to the Holy Cities. The city sits at the juncture of the sea routes from the Bab al-Mandab (connecting to the Indian Ocean) and the Suez route from Egypt.
Under Ottoman Rule
The Hejaz came under Ottoman suzerainty in 1517 when the Sherif of Mecca, Barakat II, submitted to Selim I and sent the keys of Mecca and Medina to the Ottoman sultan. Inalcik records that in this same year, while Selim was still in Cairo, a Portuguese fleet entered the Red Sea to attack Jidda and Mecca. The Sherif of Mecca prepared to flee to the hills, and the people of the Hejaz begged the Ottoman admiral Selman Reis not to abandon them. Selman repulsed the Portuguese attack on Jidda, saving the city from sack.
Inalcik notes that Selman Reis again repulsed Portuguese attacks on Jidda in 1525, then advanced from Jidda to subjugate Yemen and Aden. The city was fortified with walls and artillery to protect against further Portuguese incursions, and a permanent Ottoman garrison was stationed there.
The Ottomans appointed a beylerbey of the Hejaz who resided mainly in Mecca but maintained administrative oversight of Jidda’s customs and port operations. The city’s governor (kaimakam) was responsible for security, the regulation of the pilgrimage traffic, and the collection of customs duties.
Role in Trade
Jidda was the central entrepot for the Red Sea trade under Ottoman rule. Inalcik reports that every year twenty ships, laden with spices, arrived at Jidda, the seaport of Mecca. Ottoman pilgrims returning from Mecca carried spices, dyes, and Indian cloth through Jidda on their way home to all parts of the empire. The city’s customs revenues were substantial and were earmarked in part for the upkeep of the Holy Cities and for the provision of food and water to pilgrims.
Goods transiting through Jidda included: pepper, cinnamon, cloves, and other spices from India and Indonesia; textiles from Gujarat and Bengal; coffee from Yemen; slaves from East Africa; and incense from the Horn of Africa. In return, Ottoman merchants brought gold, silver, copper, woolen cloth, and timber to the port.
Pilgrimage Function
Jidda was the primary sea gateway for pilgrims to Mecca. Pilgrims arrived from Egypt (via Suez), from India, from Southeast Asia, from Yemen, and from East Africa. The city was the first place where pilgrims touched Arabian soil, and it contained hostels, warehouses, and markets specifically serving the pilgrimage traffic. The Ottoman authorities maintained strict security along the Jidda-Mecca road and regulated the supply of water and provisions.
Key Connections

Mecca: Connected by the 80-km road inland; Jidda was Mecca’s window to the sea
Suez: Primary Egyptian counterpart; the Jidda-Suez route was the main Red Sea shipping lane
Aden: Southern counterpart at the mouth of the Red Sea; Aden’s political fate under the Ottomans was linked to Jidda’s security
India (Calicut, Cambay): Jidda was the terminus of the Indian Ocean spice ships
Medina: Second Holy City of Islam, also provisioned through Jidda’s port

entities/john-hunyadi.title: John Hunyadi (János Hunyadi)
entities/john-hunyadi.content:
John Hunyadi — János Hunyadi (John Hunyadi, c. 1406–1456)
John Hunyadi (c. 1406 – 11 August 1456), known in Hungarian as János Hunyadi and in Ottoman sources as “the Geta John” (Γέτης Ἰωάννης, “John the Getan”), was Regent of Hungary and the most formidable Christian military commander of the mid-15th century. He was the principal adversary of Sultan Murad II and the early reign of Mehmed II, famous for his campaigns against the Ottoman empire including the Long Campaign (1443–44), the Crusade of Varna (1444), the Second Battle of Kosovo (1448), and his greatest victory — the defence of Belgrade against Mehmed II in 1456.
Early Career and the Long Campaign
Hunyadi rose from relatively modest Transylvanian nobility to become Voivode of Transylvania and Captain-General of Hungary. His first major campaign against the Ottomans was the so-called Long Campaign of 1443–1444, conducted in alliance with King Vladislaus III of Poland and Hungary (Władysław III) and the Serbian despot George Branković. This winter campaign pushed deep into Ottoman territory as far as Sofia, forcing Murad II to negotiate a ten-year truce at Szeged in 1444.
The Crusade of Varna
The peace was short-lived. Urged by the Papal legate Cardinal Julian Cesarini, King Vladislaus and Hunyadi broke the truce and launched a new crusade in the autumn of 1444. Murad II, recalled from retirement, met the crusader army at Varna on 10 November 1444. The battle ended in disaster for the Christians: King Vladislaus was killed, and Hunyadi barely escaped. Mehmed II, in his pre-siege speech to his commanders at Constantinople in 1452, alluded to these events when he spoke of how the Hungarians had previously threatened the Ottomans:

“More recently the city stirred up against us the Geta John (John Hunyadi) with the Paeonians and Dacians, who, crossing the Danube with great force two or three times and invading our empire, caused much trouble for my father” — Mehmed II, speaking of Hunyadi’s campaigns under Murad II (Book I, §69).

The Second Battle of Kosovo (1448)
Hunyadi launched another major campaign in 1448, marching into Serbia with a Hungarian and Wallachian army. Murad II intercepted him on the plain of Kosovo Polje (the Field of Blackbirds), the same site where the Serbs had fallen in 1389. The battle lasted three days (17–19 October 1448). The Wallachian contingent defected, and Hunyadi was defeated. He was captured by the Serbian despot Branković, who held him for ransom before releasing him.
The Siege of Belgrade (1456)
Hunyadi’s finest hour came in 1456, when Mehmed II marched against the great fortress of Belgrade (Nándorfehérvár), the key defensive position on the Danube controlling the gateway to Hungary. Kritoboulos records that Mehmed gathered a great army, had very many powerful cannons made, and marched through Thrace, Macedonia, and the pass of Haimos (the Balkan Mountains) to reach the fortress.
Belgrade was formidable: “surrounded on two sides by two rivers, on the one side by the Danube, which flows past it to the north, and on the south side by the Sava” (Book II, §89), with high and steep banks, a double stone wall, and a deep water-filled moat. The landward side was defended by “a sufficient garrison of warlike Hungarians armed from head to foot” (Book II, §90).
Mehmed bombarded the walls with massive cannons and ordered his troops to fill the moat. Kritoboulos describes the critical moment:

“And John [John Hunyadi], the commander of the Hungarians and Wallachians, sitting across the Danube opposite the fortress, with forty thousand infantry, watched what was happening. And when he learned that the wall had been demolished, that the moat was already completely filled up, and saw that the conflict was stronger than he had thought, and that the Sultan was attacking with his entire army, fearing for the city, lest it be taken by assault and force of arms, he secretly crossed the river with his infantry. And entering the city quietly, he remained there, none of those outside knowing that he had crossed” (Book II, §95).

Hunyadi’s arrival revitalized the defence. When Ottoman soldiers breached the wall and poured into the city, Hunyadi counterattacked:

“Then John appears, suddenly rushing out from the city with his men, and with a great shout immediately threw them into confusion, and strongly dismaying them, halted their further advance” (Book II, §101).

The battle turned decisively. The Ottomans were driven back:

“In this battle the Sultan’s forces fared very badly indeed… And not being able to resist, they turned their backs, while the Hungarians, emboldened, immediately pressed after them and pursued and slaughtered them very fiercely, step by step” (Book II, §102).

The pursuit reached the Ottoman camp itself. Mehmed II personally led a counter-charge and drove the Hungarians back to the city walls. Kritoboulos mentions that “they say that the Sultan himself was also wounded in the thigh by a spear during the battle, but the wound was not large, though deep” (Book II, §106). The Janissary ağası Karatzász was killed by a cannonball.
Mehmed finally lifted the siege and withdrew, acknowledging his first major defeat as sultan.
Death
Hunyadi did not long survive his greatest victory. A plague broke out in the Hungarian camp after the siege, and Hunyadi died of the disease on 11 August 1456, less than a month after the battle. He was buried in the cathedral of Gyulafehérvár (Alba Iulia).
Legacy
John Hunyadi is remembered as one of the greatest military commanders of the age, the man who stopped the Ottoman advance into Hungary for a generation. The noon bell ordered by Pope Callixtus III to commemorate the victory at Belgrade is still rung today in Catholic churches. He was the father of Matthias Corvinus, who became one of Hungary’s greatest kings. In Ottoman memory, embodied in Kritoboulos’ text, he appears as “the Geta John” — a figure formidable enough to be invoked by Mehmed II himself in his speech to his commanders as an example of the threats that Constantinople had brought upon the Ottoman state.

entities/koci-bey.title: Koçi Bey (Qochi Bey / Gurijalı Koja Mustafa Bey)
entities/koci-bey.content:
Koçi Bey — Qochi Bey (Gurijalı Koja Mustafa Bey)
Koçi Bey (also spelled Qochi Bey, full name Gurijalı Koja Mustafa Bey) was a 17th-century Ottoman official and the author of one of the most influential works of Ottoman political literature, the Risale (c. 1630). His treatise is the most famous of the Ottoman nasihatname (book of counsel) tradition and served as a direct predecessor to the later reform works of Sarı Mehmed Pasha.
Life
Little is known of Koçi Bey’s biography. He was of Georgian origin (Gurijalı), and “Koja” (Koca) indicates seniority or stature. He served in the Ottoman palace establishment and gained the ear of Sultan Murad IV (r. 1623–1640), to whom he addressed his reform proposals.
The Risale (c. 1630)
Koçi Bey’s Risale (“Treatise”) concerns itself little with theoretical statecraft but offers numerous concrete, detailed suggestions for the reform of the empire. Rather than abstract political philosophy, it diagnoses specific institutional failures — the breakdown of the timar system, the corruption of the devshirme, the decline of military discipline — and prescribes remedies. The work has provided the materials for most subsequent discussions of Ottoman decline by both European and Turkish historians.
The Risale exists in multiple editions: the first printed in Istanbul in 1277 AH (1860), with later editions edited by Ahmed Vefik Pasha. It was translated into German by W.F.A. Behrnauer (Z.D.M.G., 1860), into Russian by V.D. Smirnov (1873), and into French by Pétis de la Croix (1725) under the title Canon de Sultan Suleyman II, Representé à Sultan Mourad IV.
Influence
Koçi Bey’s work stands in a direct line of the nasihatname tradition that includes the Kudatku Bilik (11th century), Nizam al-Mulk’s Siyasetname (11th century), the Qabusname, Lutfi Pasha’s Asafname (16th century), and Hasan Kafi Aqhisari’s Usul al-hikam fi nizam al-alam (16th century). Sarı Mehmed Pasha’s Kitab-i Güldeste (early 18th century) is a later contribution to this same genre. Koçi Bey is distinguished among these authors by the practical, diagnostic character of his analysis — he wrote not as a philosopher but as an experienced palace insider identifying the specific points at which the Ottoman apparatus had broken down.

entities/lisbon.title: Lisbon (Lizbon / Lisboa)
entities/lisbon.content:
Lisbon — لزبون (Lizbon) / Lisboa
Lisbon (Portuguese: Lisboa; Ottoman Turkish: لزبون, Lizbon; Arabic: لشبونة, Lishbuna) was the capital of the Kingdom of Portugal and the metropolitan center of the Portuguese maritime empire. From Lisbon, Vasco da Gama departed in 1497 to open the Cape Route to India, and to Lisbon returned the spice-laden fleets that bypassed the traditional Ottoman-controlled routes through the Red Sea and Persian Gulf. For the Ottoman Empire, Lisbon was the source of the Portuguese naval threat that the Ottomans confronted in the Indian Ocean, the Red Sea, and the Persian Gulf throughout the sixteenth century.
Geography and Strategic Position
Lisbon lies at approximately 38.7° N, 9.1° W, on the northern bank of the Tagus River (Rio Tejo) estuary, at the point where the river widens into a vast natural harbor — the Mar da Palha (Sea of Straw) — before emptying into the Atlantic Ocean approximately 15 km downstream. The Tagus estuary, one of the finest natural harbors in Europe, provided deep, sheltered anchorage for the largest fleets. The city is built on a series of hills on the northern shore, with the harbor on the south and west. Its position at the southwestern edge of Europe, projecting into the Atlantic, made Lisbon the natural departure point for Atlantic exploration and the terminus for ships returning from Asia around the Cape of Good Hope.
Before the Portuguese Age of Discovery, Lisbon was a marginal Mediterranean-Atlantic trading port. After da Gama’s voyage, it became the entrepot of the European spice trade and the seat of the Casa da India (India House), which administered the Portuguese monopoly on the Cape Route.
Lisbon and the Disruption of Ottoman Trade
The opening of the Cape Route by Vasco da Gama (1497-1499), who landed at Calicut in 1498, fundamentally altered the structure of Eurasian trade. Before da Gama, spices reached Europe through the Red Sea and Persian Gulf routes controlled by the Mamluks (and later the Ottomans), with Venetian merchants acting as the primary European purchasers in Alexandria, Beirut, and Aleppo. The Portuguese Cape Route bypassed these intermediaries entirely, delivering spices directly to Lisbon for distribution throughout Europe.
Inalcik assesses the impact: “the annual purchase of twelve thousand quintals [of spices by the Venetians in Alexandria] between 1560 and 1564 was the same amount as before Vasco de Gama’s discovery of the sea route to India. As a result the Lisbon market underwent periodic crises” (Inalcik, p. 5428-5432). This passage reveals that the Ottoman spice trade was far more resilient than traditional historiography has suggested. The Lisbon market, despite being the official monopoly supplier of spices to Europe, could not undercut the traditional Red Sea routes. In 1564, a Portuguese spy in Egypt reported that thirty thousand quintals of spices had arrived in Alexandria — a volume that directly challenged Lisbon’s monopoly.
The Portuguese in Lisbon reacted to this competition by attempting to enforce a naval blockade of the Red Sea and Persian Gulf. The Portuguese crown prohibited the sale of ships, naval stores, and weapons to Muslim powers, and the cartaz system was designed to intercept and tax any Indian Ocean shipping not licensed by Portuguese authorities.
Relations with the Ottoman Empire
Portugal and the Ottoman Empire were the two great rival powers of the sixteenth-century Indian Ocean. The confrontation unfolded across multiple theaters:
1509: The Portuguese destroyed a Mamluk fleet at Chaul. The Mamluk sultan turned to the Ottoman sultan for assistance, and the Ottomans “immediately sent material and craftsman to Suez to build ships” (Inalcik, p. 5405-5407).
1516-1517: The Ottoman conquest of Syria, Egypt, and the Hejaz brought the Ottomans into direct confrontation with Portuguese power in the Red Sea. Selim I ordered the construction of a fleet at Suez “to drive the Portuguese out of the Indian Ocean” (Inalcik, p. 5410-5412).
1517-1525: The Ottoman admiral Selman Reis repulsed Portuguese attacks on Jidda and advanced on Yemen and Aden (Inalcik, p. 5412-5413).
1538: A major Ottoman fleet of thirty ships sailed for Diu in northern India but failed to dislodge the Portuguese, partly because the local Gujarati sultan refused to cooperate (Inalcik, p. 5416-5420).
1552: The Ottoman expedition against Hormuz failed to oust the Portuguese from the Persian Gulf chokepoint (Inalcik, p. 1752-1754).
1578-1585: Taking advantage of the Portuguese defeat at Alcazar (Alcacer Quibir) in Morocco, where King Sebastian of Portugal was killed, an Ottoman fleet sailed from Suez and captured Portuguese factories on the East African coast. The Prince of Mombasa recognized Ottoman suzerainty in 1585 (Inalcik, p. 2011-2014).
1580: The Spanish Habsburgs annexed Portugal (the Iberian Union, 1580-1640), making the Portuguese Empire a target of the Anglo-Ottoman alliance. The Ottoman sultan “showed an interest in efforts to revive the kingdom of Portugal” (Inalcik, p. 1956-1957), potentially as an independent state freed from Spanish control.
1603: A Persian diplomatic mission to Lisbon, led by the shah’s envoy, “carried with it two hundred bales of silk and tried to prove that silk would be cheaper when shipped by sea” (Inalcik, p. 2051-2053). This was part of Shah Abbas I’s effort to bypass Ottoman transit routes by selling Persian silk directly to Europe via the Indian Ocean.
Lisbon as a Market
The Casa da India in Lisbon organized the annual spice fleet. Each year, a fleet of Portuguese ships — the Armadas da India — departed Lisbon in March or April, rounded the Cape of Good Hope during the southern winter, and arrived in Goa or Cochin in September. The return fleet, laden with pepper, cinnamon, cloves, nutmeg, and other Asian goods, departed India in December or January, catching the winter Monsoon across the Indian Ocean, rounded the Cape, and arrived in Lisbon between June and September. The Lisbon market then distributed spices throughout Europe, primarily through Antwerp (until the Dutch Revolt) and later through Amsterdam.
Despite Portuguese efforts, the Ottoman spice trade survived. Inalcik notes that “European merchants bought some of the spices at Damascus, exporting them through Beirut, while a large part of the cargo was forwarded to Bursa and Istanbul” (Inalcik, p. 5439-5442). The customs regulations of Bursa for 1545 show European merchants buying spices there, and by 1582 customs receipts on spices in Bursa had reached 7,250 gold ducats (Inalcik, p. 5442-5444).
Key Connections

Goa: Portuguese capital in India; the administrative center of the Estado da India subordinate to Lisbon
Seville/Cadiz: Spanish ports; Portugal’s Iberian rival before 1580 and partner in the Iberian Union
Antwerp: Primary distribution center for the Lisbon spice trade in the early sixteenth century
Suez: Ottoman naval base for Indian Ocean operations; the Lisbon-Suez axis defined the Ottoman-Portuguese naval confrontation
Aden: Ottoman-controlled Red Sea port; the Lisbon fleet’s spices competed with those routed through Aden and Jidda
London: English capital; after 1580, London merchants received Ottoman capitulations and competed with Lisbon for the Levant trade
Hormuz: Portuguese vassal in the Persian Gulf, whose cartaz system was administered in coordination with Lisbon
Cairo/Alexandria: Ottoman Egyptian ports; the resilience of the Red Sea spice route kept these markets competitive with Lisbon

entities/london.title: London (Londra / London)
entities/london.content:
London — لندره (Londra) / London
London (Ottoman Turkish: لندره, Londra; English: London) was the capital of the Kingdom of England and, after the union of the crowns in 1603, the center of English commercial and naval power. In the Ottoman context of the sixteenth century, London was significant as the seat of Queen Elizabeth I’s government, which forged an unprecedented Anglo-Ottoman diplomatic and commercial alliance against their shared Habsburg enemy, Spain. The capitulations granted to English merchants in 1580 marked a turning point in Ottoman-European relations and established the framework for the Levant Company’s dominance of Ottoman trade.
Geography and Strategic Position
London lies at approximately 51.5° N, 0.1° W, straddling the River Thames at the head of its estuary, approximately 80 km from the North Sea. The city’s position at the lowest bridging point of the Thames — where the river narrows and the first solid ground suitable for a bridge foundation appears — made it the natural hub of English trade and communications. The Thames estuary connects London to the ports of the Low Countries, France, the Baltic, and the Atlantic. From the Ottoman perspective, London was the most distant significant European capital, accessible by sea around Iberia and through the Strait of Gibraltar, or overland through France and the Habsburg territories.
Anglo-Ottoman Relations and Trade
The Anglo-Ottoman alliance was driven by a common enemy: Habsburg Spain. As Inalcik explains, “with Spain as a common enemy, the English and Ottoman governments clearly saw the advantage of a rapprochement” (Inalcik, p. 6226-6228). Queen Elizabeth I corresponded with Sultan Murad III in terms of shared Protestant-Islamic opposition to Catholic Spain, and the relationship was formalized through a series of capitulations (commercial treaties).
The key milestones of the Anglo-Ottoman relationship were:

1553: Suleyman I granted English merchants the right to trade freely in the Ottoman Empire, but they did not initially exploit this privilege (Inalcik, p. 6219-6221).
1560s: English merchants sought alternative routes to obtain spices directly, exploring a Moscow-Iran-Hormuz route. The Ottomans sent an embassy to the shah in 1562 to prevent this diversion (Inalcik, p. 6221-6224).
1580-1583: Sultan Murad III granted English merchants capitulations — first a preliminary grant in 1580, followed by a more extensive one in 1583 (Inalcik, p. 6229-6230).
11 September 1581: The Turkey Company was founded by royal charter. It later united with the Venice Company to become the Levant Company (Inalcik, p. 6230-6232).
The Ottoman government lowered the customs rate for English merchants to 3 percent, while the French and other foreigners paid 5 percent until 1673 (Inalcik, p. 6232-6235).

The English competed fiercely with France and Venice, the former monopolists of the Levant trade. English merchants sold fine-quality woollens at low prices, and imports of English tin and steel were vitally important for the Ottoman arms industry (Inalcik, p. 6242-6244). Venetian trade fell sharply, and French trade declined by half by 1630, while the Levant Company came to dominate Ottoman markets. English consuls were established in Istanbul, Izmir, Aleppo, and Alexandretta (Inalcik, p. 6245-6247).
London as a Center of Ottoman Interest
From the Ottoman perspective, London was important not only as the source of English trade but as a diplomatic counterweight to Habsburg power. The Ottoman sultans sent encouraging letters to the Dutch rebels and “attempted to establish friendly relations with the English by granting them capitulations” (Inalcik, p. 1954-1956). The destruction of the Spanish Armada in 1588 — in which English naval power, supported indirectly by Ottoman pressure on Spain’s Mediterranean flank, played a decisive role — had major consequences in the Mediterranean as Spanish naval power declined (Inalcik, p. 1957-1958).
English pirates, sometimes in cooperation with Algerian corsairs, began raiding Spanish and even Ottoman merchant shipping, with Queen Elizabeth “turning a deaf ear to all complaints” (Inalcik, p. 6240-6242). English attempts to break into the Indian Ocean after 1591 did not harm the Levant Company, since many in London considered the Levant trade more important (Inalcik, p. 6248-6249).
The English presence in the Mediterranean also brought new naval technology. Inalcik notes Sir Thomas Sherley’s 1607 observation that “one English warship could defeat ten Turkish galleys” (Inalcik, p. 1993-1994), reflecting the growing dominance of the Atlantic-style sailing ship over the Mediterranean galley. The arrival of English and Dutch ships in the Mediterranean towards the end of the sixteenth century transformed naval warfare in the region.
Key Connections

Istanbul: Ottoman capital; seat of the English ambassador and the Levant Company’s chief factor
Izmir (Smyrna): Major Levant Company trading post; English trade transformed Izmir from a small town into a major port
Aleppo: English consulate and trading center for the Persian Gulf route
Lisbon: Portuguese capital; England’s intermittent ally against Spain and rival in India
Cadiz/Seville: Spanish ports; England’s principal enemies in the Atlantic and Mediterranean
Moscow: Explored as alternative transit route to Iran and Hormuz for English merchants seeking spices
The Hague: Dutch capital; the Dutch traded under the English flag in the Levant until receiving their own capitulations in 1612

entities/mahmud-pasha.title: Mahmud Pasha
entities/mahmud-pasha.content:
Mahmud Pasha
Mahmud Pasha (محمود پاشا) was the foremost sadrazam (صدر اعظم, grand vizier) of Mehmed II’s reign, serving two terms (1456–1466 and 1472–1474). He was the most powerful Ottoman statesman between the conquest of Constantinople and his execution in 1474, and both Tursun Beg and Kritoboulos treat him as the central figure of Mehmed’s administration after the sultan himself.
Career
Mahmud Pasha rose to prominence in the years following the conquest of Constantinople. He was appointed sadrazam in 1456 and for the next decade commanded the most important military campaigns of Mehmed’s reign — the gaza (غزا, holy war) against the infidel. He led expeditions into Serbia, the Peloponnese, Anatolia, Bosnia, and Albania, and was responsible for the conquest of strategic kale (قلعه, fortresses) including Smederevo (Semendre), Amasra, and Lesbos (Mytilene). Tursun Beg’s account becomes notably more detailed during the period of Mahmud Pasha’s first grand vizierate, since Tursun personally served under him and participated in the campaigns.
Kritoboulos describes Mahmud Pasha as a commander of exceptional energy and strategic ability. During the Trebizond campaign of 1461, he led the advance force through the Taurus Mountains and survived an ambush attempt. He was instrumental in the conquest of the Peloponnese and in the relief of Mytilene against Venetian attack.
Dismissal and Execution
Mahmud Pasha was dismissed from the sadrazam in 1466. He was reappointed in 1472 but was executed in 1474 — katl (قتل, execution) — probably on charges related to his alleged sympathy for Şehzade Cem (شهزاده جم, Prince Cem), the sultan’s rebellious son.
Legacy
Mahmud Pasha left a significant architectural legacy: cami (جامع, mosques), köprü (كوپرى, bridges), and imaret (عمارت, public kitchens) that Kritoboulos mentions in the context of Mehmed’s building program.

entities/matthias-corvinus.title: Matthias Corvinus (Matthias Hunyadi, Mátyás Király)
entities/matthias-corvinus.content:
Matthias Corvinus — Matthias I of Hungary (Mátyás Király, ماتیاش کوروین)
Matthias Corvinus (1443–1490), born Matthias Hunyadi, was King of Hungary and Croatia from 1458 to 1490, the son of the great regent John Hunyadi. He was the most formidable Hungarian opponent of Mehmed II in the latter half of the 15th century. In Kritobulos’ text he appears as “the king of the Paeonians” (Παίονες, Kritobulos’ classical name for the Hungarians) and “the king of the Paeonians and Dacians.” His campaigns against the Ottomans in Bosnia, his recapture of Jajce, and his confrontation with Mehmed II in 1464 form a major episode of Book V.
Background and Rise to Power
Matthias was the younger son of John Hunyadi, the hero of the 1456 Siege of Belgrade. After his father’s death from plague later that same year, and the execution of his elder brother László by King Ladislaus V in 1457, Matthias himself was imprisoned. The death of Ladislaus V (1457) and the desperate need for a strong king to resist the Ottoman advance led the Hungarian estates to elect Matthias king in 1458. He was crowned in 1458 and spent the early years of his reign consolidating power against both internal rivals (the Habsburgs, who also claimed Hungary) and the expanding Ottoman empire of Mehmed II.
The Black Army
Matthias is famous for creating and commanding the Fekete Sereg (Black Army), a standing professional mercenary army funded by his innovative taxation system — the first such standing army in Europe since the fall of the Western Roman Empire. This force, composed primarily of Bohemian, German, and Hungarian mercenaries, was heavily equipped with firearms, artillery, and armored cavalry (the páncélos). It enabled Matthias to wage simultaneous wars against the Habsburgs (to secure his claim to Bohemia and Austria) and the Ottomans in the south. Kritobulos, writing from the Ottoman perspective, refers to the Hungarian defenders at Jajce as “brave, warlike men chosen from the very best” (Book V, §27).
The Conquest of Bosnia and the Fall of Jajce (1463)
After Mehmed II’s conquest of the Kingdom of Bosnia in 1463 — the execution of King Stephen Tomašević and the fall of the fortress of Bobovac — the Ottomans installed a garrison at Jajce, the Bosnian royal seat. Matthias responded swiftly. Kritobulos records in Book V (§15–17) that during the winter, “the king of the Paeonians and Dacians marched into Dalmatia and the province of the Bosnians, which the emperor had previously subdued, in order to seize it if possible and to get his hands on the fortresses within it, especially Jajce, driving out the emperor’s garrison from it.”
Kritobulos provides the strategic rationale from the Hungarian perspective: “For it seemed to him a terrible thing, and a great ruin and manifest dissolution of his own kingdom and realm, that the emperor should hold this province and the fortresses within it, since it lay within easy reach of his kingdom, neighboring Hungary, and was conveniently situated for raiding it” (Book V, §15).
Matthias’ campaign was remarkably successful. He “immediately caused the greater part of the fortresses to revolt from the emperor, partly drawing them to himself by fair words and agreement, partly by intimidation and threats, and a few he also subdued by arms.” Jajce itself fell “with ease, since those within had risen up, were killing each other, and voluntarily joined him” (Book V, §16). He placed strong garrisons in the recovered fortresses and returned to Hungary.
Mehmed II’s Counter-Campaign and the Second Siege of Jajce (1464)
Mehmed II, taking the loss of Jajce very ill, prepared a major expedition throughout the winter of 1463–1464. Kritobulos describes his response: “when he learned of this, taking the matter ill, immediately made preparations to march with a great army not only against the Bosnians and the revolted fortresses, but also to invade Hungary itself” (Book V, §18).
In the spring of 1464, Mehmed marched on Jajce with his full army, bringing cannons and siege equipment. The second siege of Jajce was one of the most ferocious of Mehmed’s reign. Kritobulos describes two mass assaults. In the first (Book V, §21–23), the Ottoman heavy infantry breached the wall with cannons and attempted to storm through. The fighting was fierce: “sometimes the heavy infantry prevailing gained the upper hand over the Hungarians and climbed up onto the wall; sometimes, being thrust back by the Hungarians, they were violently driven out; and not a few perished there, fighting even as they fell.” The Hungarians “gained great strength, and the heavy infantry, being hard pressed, suffered very greatly, many noble and brave men falling among them.” Mehmed was forced to withdraw and ordered a retreat.
In the second assault (Book V, §24–28), Mehmed himself led from the front ranks, offering vast rewards and promising the city for plunder. The struggle reached a pitch of savagery where, as Kritobulos writes, the soldiers “almost casting off human nature, they hacked and hewed each other mercilessly.” Yet again the Hungarians prevailed: “they were all brave, warlike men chosen from the very best.” Seeing his men falling in great numbers, Mehmed abandoned the assault and decided to blockade the city instead.
The Confrontation at the Crna River (1464)
While Mehmed remained before Jajce, Matthias marched out with a large army to relieve the city. Kritobulos records (Book V, §30) that “as soon as he learned that the emperor had invaded Bosnia and was besieging the city of Jajce, [he] gathered a great army and, having duly equipped himself with everything he had, set out against the emperor,” reasoning that he would either force Mehmed to lift the siege or split the Ottoman army.
Mehmed, however, chose not to engage in person. He dispatched Mahmud Pasha with a substantial force, including a portion of the imperial bodyguard, to meet the Hungarians. The two armies encamped facing each other across the river Erigon (the Crna River, the Crni Drim, modern Crna Reka in North Macedonia), with “a distance of at most about twenty-five stades, so that they could see each other” (Book V, §32).
Mahmud crossed the river and wished to engage, but Mehmed sent word forbidding a general battle, saying: “There is no need at all for it, when you now hold the crossing of the river in your hands; it is sufficient if you remain there and keep an eye on what the enemy is doing” (Book V, §33). Matthias, learning that Jajce would not be relieved by battle, secretly sent word to the garrison to hold out, then set fire to his camp by night and withdrew.
Mahmud, perceiving the retreat, pursued vigorously. Kritobulos describes the scene vividly (Book V, §35–37): “crossing the river hastily, he pressed after them with great speed. And overtaking them, he engaged the rearguard, which constituted no small part of the army and had been assigned to guard the baggage train, and routing it, he pursued it as far as the main army.” The rearguard’s flight threw the entire Hungarian army into confusion: “there ensued a great flight, everyone all at once fleeing in disorder, without any order or consideration.” Mahmud pressed after them, “slaying and killing them mercilessly,” and returned to Mehmed with all their “military equipment, wagons, weapons, horses, and the baggage carriers themselves.”
The Aftermath
Despite this defeat, Matthias’ overall position remained strong. Mehmed could not retake Jajce by assault and eventually abandoned the siege, contenting himself with subjugating the lesser fortresses. He had the captured Hungarians taken to Constantinople and executed. The fortress of Jajce remained in Hungarian hands until 1527, a lasting memorial to Matthias’ success.
Wars Against the Habsburgs
Matthias fought a prolonged war with Frederick III, Holy Roman Emperor, who also claimed the Hungarian crown. In 1485, Matthias captured Vienna itself, making it his capital for the remainder of his life. These western wars drained resources that might otherwise have been used against the Ottomans, a choice that has been both praised (for securing Hungary’s western flank) and criticized (for relieving pressure on the Ottomans).
Renaissance Patronage
Matthias was one of the greatest Renaissance patrons north of the Alps. His library, the Bibliotheca Corviniana, was the largest collection of manuscripts in Europe outside the Vatican, containing some 2,000 to 3,000 codices, many of them beautifully illuminated. He corresponded with leading Italian humanists, invited scholars to his court, and established the Academia Istropolitana in Pozsony (Bratislava). His court at Buda became a center of humanist learning, architecture, and art, second only to the Italian city-states.
Death and Legacy
Matthias died in Vienna on 6 April 1490, at the age of 47. His death marked the beginning of Hungary’s decline. Without his strong leadership, the Black Army disintegrated, the treasury was depleted, and internal factionalism returned. Less than four decades after his death, the Ottoman army under Suleiman the Magnificent broke through at the Battle of Mohács (1526), and Hungary was partitioned.
In Ottoman historiography as preserved by Kritobulos, Matthias Corvinus is presented as a formidable and worthy opponent — a king whose capture of Jajce provoked the greatest military response Mehmed could muster, whose garrisons at Jajce fought with a valor that Kritobulos himself acknowledges. The Crna River campaign, though it ended in a Hungarian rout, did not break Matthias’ power; he continued to hold Bosnia’s key fortresses and remained a threat to Ottoman ambitions in the region for the rest of his reign. He stands as the last great Christian king capable of meeting the Ottomans on equal terms in central Europe.

entities/mecca.title: Mecca (Mekke / Makka)
entities/mecca.content:
Mecca — Makka (مكة) / Mekke (مكه)
Mecca (Ottoman Turkish: مكه, Mekke; Arabic: مكة, Makka) is the holiest city of Islam, the birthplace of the Prophet Muhammad and the site of the Ka’ba, toward which all Muslims pray. The Ottoman acquisition of Mecca in 1517 marked a transformative moment in the empire’s history, shifting the Ottoman state from a frontier gazi principality into an Islamic caliphate with responsibilities for the protection and welfare of the Holy Cities.
Geography and Strategic Position
Mecca is situated in a narrow, arid valley in the Hejaz region of western Arabia, approximately 80 km east of the Red Sea coast. The city is surrounded by barren hills (the mountains of Abu Qubays, Qayqan, and al-Nur) and lies at an elevation of about 280 meters above sea level. Its location is not naturally strategic for trade — it has no port and limited agricultural resources — but its religious significance made it the most important city in the Arabian Peninsula.
Under Ottoman Rule
The Ottoman connection to Mecca began before the conquest. Inalcik notes that in 1516, the Sherif of Mecca, a descendant of the Prophet Muhammad, proposed to send a delegation to Selim I, alarmed by Portuguese attacks in the Red Sea and seeking protection from the Ottoman sultan as the great gazi ruler. The Mamluk sultan al-Ghawri prevented this delegation from departing, but the Hejaz was already inclined toward Ottoman rule.
After the Battle of Marj Dabiq (August 1516), Selim I received the title “Servant of Mecca and Medina” (the Mamluks had borne the title “Protector of Mecca and Medina”) in the great mosque of Aleppo. On 17 July 1517, after the conquest of Egypt, the Sherif of Mecca sent Selim the keys of the Holy Cities and announced his submission. Inalcik emphasizes that the addition of Mecca and Medina “marks the beginning of a new era” — the empire was no longer a frontier state but an Islamic caliphate.
The Ottomans did not govern Mecca directly through a provincial administration in the usual sense. Instead, the hereditary Sherifs of Mecca (descendants of the Prophet through Hassan ibn Ali) continued to rule the city and the Hejaz as semi-autonomous vassals, with the Ottoman sultan recognized as suzerain. The khutba (Friday sermon) was read in the sultan’s name, and coins were struck in his honor. The sultan appointed a beylerbey of the Hejaz who served alongside the Sherif, and Ottoman troops garrisoned Jidda and Mecca.
Suleiman the Magnificent boasted in his imperial inscription: “I am Suleyman, in whose name the hutbe is read in Mecca and Medina.”
Maintenance of the Holy Cities
The Ottoman sultans took their role as “Servant of the Two Holy Cities” seriously. They invested heavily in the maintenance and expansion of the Great Mosque (al-Masjid al-Haram), including repairs to the Ka’ba, the construction of new minarets, and the renovation of the water supply system. The surre (imperial gift) sent annually from Istanbul in the pilgrimage caravan provided money, food, and cloth for distribution to the residents of Mecca and Medina. The Ottoman state also subsidized the import of grain from Egypt to feed the population of the Holy Cities.
Role in Pilgrimage
Mecca was the destination of the annual Hajj pilgrimage, one of the five pillars of Islam. Pilgrim caravans assembled in Cairo, Damascus, and Baghdad and traversed the Arabian desert to reach the Holy City. The Ottoman authorities organized and protected these caravans, appointing a surre emini (treasurer) and emir al-hajj (commander of the pilgrimage) to lead the convoys. The pilgrimage was both a religious obligation and a major logistical operation involving tens of thousands of people and animals each year.
Role in Trade
Inalcik notes that Mecca’s role in trade was largely indirect: pilgrims returning from Mecca carried spices, dyes, and Indian cloth back to their homelands, and the city’s markets catered to the vast transient population of pilgrims. However, the main commercial traffic passed through Jidda, Mecca’s seaport, rather than through the city itself. The Sherifs of Mecca derived income from customs duties at Jidda and from taxes on pilgrims and merchants.
Key Connections

Medina: Second Holy City, 450 km north of Mecca; connected by the Hejaz road used by pilgrims
Jidda: Seaport of Mecca, 80 km west; the main entry point for maritime pilgrims and goods
Cairo: Egyptian pilgrimage caravan route; Mecca was provisioned with Egyptian grain
Damascus: Syrian pilgrimage caravan route; the annual mahmal procession from Damascus was a major event
Aden & Yemen: Southern Arabian connection; the Sherif maintained ties with the rulers of Yemen

entities/medina.title: Medina (Medine / al-Madina)
entities/medina.content:
Medina — al-Madīna (المدينة) / Medine (مدينه)
Medina (Ottoman Turkish: مدينه, Medine; Arabic: المدينة المنورة, al-Madīna al-Munawwara, “the Radiant City”) is the second holiest city of Islam, the site of the Prophet Muhammad’s mosque and his tomb. Together with Mecca, Medina formed the pair of Holy Cities (Haremeyn) whose protection became the defining responsibility of the Ottoman sultans after the conquest of 1517.
Geography and Strategic Position
Medina is situated in the Hejaz region of western Arabia, approximately 450 km north of Mecca, on an oasis plateau at an elevation of about 620 meters. Unlike Mecca’s barren valley, Medina enjoys relatively abundant water from wells and underground springs, supporting date palm groves and some agriculture. Its position on the north-south caravan route through the Hejaz made it a natural stopping point for pilgrims traveling overland from Syria and Egypt.
Under Ottoman Rule
Medina came under Ottoman suzerainty alongside Mecca in 1517, when the Sherif of Mecca submitted to Selim I and delivered the keys of both Holy Cities. Inalcik records that Selim I received the title “Servant of Mecca and Medina” after the Battle of Marj Dabiq in 1516, signaling that the Ottomans now saw themselves as the protectors of Islam’s two holiest sites. The threat was immediate and real: while Selim was still in Cairo in 1517, a Portuguese fleet entered the Red Sea threatening to capture Mecca and Medina, and the construction of the Suez fleet was ordered in response.
Like Mecca, Medina was not incorporated into the Ottoman provincial system in the usual manner. The city was administered jointly by the hereditary Sherifs of Mecca and by Ottoman officials. A na’ib (deputy governor) representing the Ottoman sultan resided in Medina, overseeing the security of the Prophet’s Mosque, the distribution of imperial subsidies, and the regulation of the pilgrimage.
Suleiman the Magnificent’s famous imperial inscription declared: “I am Suleyman, in whose name the hutbe is read in Mecca and Medina. In Baghdad I am the shah, in Byzantine realms the Caesar, and in Egypt the sultan.”
Maintenance of the Prophet’s Mosque
The primary Ottoman concern in Medina was the maintenance and embellishment of the Prophet’s Mosque (al-Masjid al-Nabawi), which contains the tomb of Muhammad. Ottoman sultans from Selim I through Suleiman and subsequent rulers invested heavily in the mosque’s architecture:

The mosque was expanded and its dome over the Prophet’s tomb was renovated
New minarets were added in the Ottoman style
The interior was decorated with Iznik tiles, calligraphic inscriptions, and carpets sent from Istanbul
The Rawda (the garden between the Prophet’s tomb and his pulpit) was maintained as a particularly sacred space

The surre (imperial gift) from Istanbul allocated specific funds for Medina, including grain shipments from Egypt, money for the mosque’s upkeep, and stipends for the mosque’s caretakers, scholars, and the descendants of the Prophet (ashraf) living in the city.
Role in Pilgrimage
Medina was an essential destination for pilgrims who, after completing the Hajj rites in Mecca, would travel north to visit the Prophet’s Mosque and tomb. The ziyara (visit) to Medina was a deeply cherished act of devotion. The Ottoman authorities maintained the road between Mecca and Medina, established way-stations with water supplies, and protected the pilgrim convoys from Bedouin raids.
The Damascus pilgrimage caravan often stopped at Medina on its way to Mecca, and the Syrian mahmal (ceremonial litter) would be displayed in the city.
Role in Trade
Medina’s economy was heavily dependent on the pilgrimage traffic and on imperial subsidies. The city had some date cultivation and local trade in Hejazi products, but it lacked the commercial importance of Mecca or Jidda. The Ottoman state subsidized the import of grain, oil, and other necessities from Egypt, and the majority of the city’s income came from religious endowments (awqaf) established by Ottoman sultans and elite patrons.
Society and Learning
Medina was a center of Islamic learning, attracting scholars and jurists from across the Muslim world. The city housed numerous madrasas (religious colleges) and libraries, many endowed by Ottoman patrons. The population included Hejazi Arabs, descendants of the Prophet, Ottoman Turks, and pilgrims who settled permanently in the city.
Key Connections

Mecca: 450 km south; the two Holy Cities were inseparably linked in Ottoman policy and piety
Damascus: Starting point of the Syrian pilgrimage caravan that passed through Medina
Cairo: Source of grain and subsidies for the Holy Cities; the Egyptian pilgrimage caravan also visited Medina
Jidda: Seaport through which goods and pilgrims arriving by sea reached both Mecca and Medina
Istanbul: Imperial capital; the sultan’s surre and patronage sustained Medina’s economy and religious institutions

entities/mehmed-ii.title: Mehmed II (Fatih Sultan Mehmed)
entities/mehmed-ii.content:
Mehmed II — Fatih Sultan Mehmed (The Conqueror)
Mehmed II (1432–1481), known as Fatih (فاتح, “the Conqueror”), was the seventh sultan of the Ottoman dynasty and the conqueror of Constantinople in 1453. Born on 30 March 1432 in Edirne, he was the son of Sultan Murad II and a slave concubine Hüma Hatun (خاتون). He reigned twice: first briefly in 1444–1446, then definitively from 1451 until his death in 1481. His forty-seven-year career transformed the Ottoman state from a Balkan principality into a world empire.
Accession and Early Reign
Mehmed first acceded to the throne (cülûs, جلوس) at age twelve in 1444, when his father Murad II abdicated. This first reign was brief — a yeniçeri (يڭیچرى, Janissary) revolt and the threat of a crusader army forced Murad to return. Mehmed stepped aside and governed as sancakbeyi (سنجاق بكى, provincial governor) of Manisa (Magnesia) for five years, studying philosophy and science under distinguished teachers. He mastered Arabic and Persian literature, studied the works of Aristotle and the Stoics (Kritoboulos records that he was “exceedingly learned, understanding the entire Arabic and Persian literature, and even the works of the Peripatetics and Stoics”), and trained in advanced mathematics.
When Murad II died in February 1451, Mehmed rode from Manisa to Edirne and took the throne for the second time at age nineteen. He renewed treaties with the Byzantine emperor Constantine XI, the Venetian Republic, and the Serbian despot, reviewed the military defter (دفتر, registers), increased the yeniçeri corps, and audited the treasury.
The Conquest of Constantinople
Mehmed’s preparatory step was the construction of Rumeli Hisarı (روم ایلی حصاری, the European Fortress), also called Boğazkesen (boğazkesen, “the Strait-Cutter”), on the European shore of the Bosporus in the summer of 1452, opposite the existing Anatolian fortress. The siege began in April 1453. The şahi (شاهی, imperial) bombards cast stone balls weighing hundreds of kilograms. In a famous feat, Mehmed had seventy to eighty ships transported overland on greased rollers past the Byzantine chain boom across the Golden Horn. After a fifty-three-day siege, the walls were breached on 29 May 1453 (Fetih, فتح, the Conquest). Mehmed entered the city, visited Hagia Sophia, and proclaimed it the new Ottoman capital.
Rebuilding the Capital
Mehmed brought settlers from all parts of the empire, restored the aqueducts, and commissioned the Bedesten (بذستان, covered market), cami (جامع, mosques), hamam (حمام, bathhouses), and the new Saray-ı Cedid (سراى جديد, New Palace — the foundation of Topkapı Palace). He appointed Gennadios (George Scholarios) as the first Ecumenical Patriarch under Ottoman rule.
Military Campaigns
In the Balkans he conquered Serbia (1459), the Morea (1460), Bosnia (1463), and Albania (1466–1467), and fought wars against Hungary (the Belgrade campaign of 1456) and Venice (1463–1479). In Anatolia he absorbed the beyliks (بیلكلر, principalities) of Karaman, Sinop/İsfendiyar (1461), and Trebizond (1461), and defeated the Aq Qoyunlu ruler Uzun Hasan at the Battle of Otlukbeli (1473). He campaigned against Vlad the Impaler (1462) and Stephen the Great of Moldavia (1476), and established Ottoman suzerainty over the Crimean Khanate through the conquest of Caffa (1475).
Character and Learning
Mehmed collected Greek manuscripts, commissioned the translation of Ptolemy’s Geography into Arabic by the philosopher George Amiroutzes, and held philosophical discussions with scholars of various traditions. Kritoboulos says he “surpassed not only the foreign emperors who lived before him but also those of his own nation” in virtue, valor, military talent, and fortune.
Death and Legacy
Mehmed died on 3 May 1481 at Hünkar Çayırı near Gebze while preparing a campaign to “deliver Egypt and Syria.” His death triggered a succession struggle between his sons Bayezid and Cem (şehzade, شهزاده, princes). He was buried in his cami complex in Constantinople.

entities/mombasa.title: Mombasa (Mombasa)
entities/mombasa.content:
Mombasa (ممباسا / Mombasa)
Mombasa (Arabic: ممباسا, Mombasa; Swahili: Mvita) is a historic port city on a coral island off the coast of East Africa, now in modern Kenya. Under the Portuguese Empire it was a fortified trade station controlling the Indian Ocean ivory, gold, and slave trades. In 1585, during the Ottoman-Portuguese struggle for control of the Indian Ocean, the ruler of Mombasa briefly recognized Ottoman suzerainty, marking the furthest reach of Ottoman imperial ambition in the southern hemisphere.
Geography and Strategic Position
Mombasa lies at approximately 4.0° S, 39.7° E, on Mombasa Island, which sits in a broad natural harbor formed by the Indian Ocean and Mombasa Creek (Kilindini Harbor). The island is connected to the mainland by creeks and channels, with the Old Port on the eastern side facing the Indian Ocean and the modern deep-water harbor at Kilindini on the west. Mombasa is the natural terminus for trade routes from the African interior (the Kenya highlands and the Kilimanjaro region) and sits at the northern end of the Swahili Coast, a series of port cities stretching south to Kilwa and Mozambique.
Under Ottoman Rule
The Ottoman presence in East Africa was a consequence of the empire’s struggle with Portugal for control of the Indian Ocean spice trade. Inalcik records the context: “The Ottomans attempted to take advantage of the disaster that had befallen the King of Portugal at the battle of Alcazar in 1578, and a small Ottoman fleet sailed from Suez, capturing one by one the Portuguese factories on the east African coast. In 1585 the Prince of Mombasa recognized Ottoman suzerainty” (Inalcik, p. 2011-2014).
This Ottoman success was short-lived. The Spanish-Portuguese Habsburgs, united under Philip II, “despatched a strong fleet, punished the local chiefs who had recognized the Ottomans, and faced by a superior Portuguese force and a local Negro attack the Ottoman admiral had to surrender. The African project had ended in failure” (Inalcik, p. 2015-2018). The Portuguese reestablished their control over Mombasa and built Fort Jesus (1593) to secure the harbor permanently.
This episode represents the southernmost extension of Ottoman military and diplomatic activity. It reflected the broader Ottoman-Indian Ocean strategy that had begun with Selim I’s order to construct a fleet at Suez in 1517 and continued with expeditions to Yemen, Aden, Diu, and the Swahili Coast throughout the 16th century.
Role in Trade
Mombasa was a major entrepot for the East African trade. The city exported ivory, gold, timber, mangrove poles, slaves, and ambergris, while importing cotton cloth, glass beads, metal goods, and ceramics from India, Arabia, and the Far East. The Monsoon winds dictated the rhythm of trade: ships from the Arabian Sea arrived between December and February, and departed for the return voyage between April and August. Mombasa’s merchants were predominantly Swahili, Arab, and Indian, with connections extending to Oman, Gujarat, and the Comoros.
Key Connections

Malindi: Rival Swahili port to the north; allied with Portugal against Mombasa
Kilwa: Southern Swahili port city; former gold trade emporium
Mozambique Island: Portuguese administrative center for East Africa
Suez: Ottoman naval base; departure point for Indian Ocean expeditions
Aden/Yemen: Northern anchor of the Swahili sea route
Ormuz (Hormuz): Persian Gulf choke point contested by Ottomans and Portuguese
Goa: Portuguese capital in India; source of counter-expeditions

entities/mustafa-ii.title: Mustafa II
entities/mustafa-ii.content:
Mustafa II
Mustafa II (r. 1695–1703) was the twenty-second Ottoman sultan, whose short reign was defined by a prolonged war with the Holy League and ended with his deposition in the Edirne Vaq’ası of 1703.
Reign
Mustafa II succeeded his uncle Ahmed II in 1695. He personally led campaigns against the Habsburgs — a break from the tradition of sultans remaining in the capital — but these efforts failed to reverse Ottoman military decline. The decisive defeat at the Battle of Zenta (1697) by Prince Eugene of Savoy forced the Empire to sue for peace.
The Treaty of Karlowitz (1699), signed under Mustafa II, was a watershed in Ottoman history: it marked the first major territorial cession by the Empire in Europe, surrendering Hungary, Transylvania, Croatia, and Slavonia to the Habsburgs, and the Morea to Venice. The treaty established the principle of territorial integrity and introduced permanent diplomatic relations between the Porte and European powers.
Deposition — The Edirne Vaq’ası
Mustafa II spent much of his reign residing in Edirne (Adrianople), which became the de facto capital. This distance from Istanbul, combined with the dominance of the şeyhülislam Feydullah Efendi and the government’s failure to pay the troops, led to the rebellion known as the Edirne Vaq’ası (“Adrianople Happening”) in July 1703.
The revolt began in Istanbul among soldiers who had not received their salaries. It quickly escalated into a full rebellion. The grand vizier, Rami Mehmed Pasha, led an army from Edirne to confront the rebels, but the army defected. After a council of state, it was decided that Mustafa II should join the army — the defterdar Sarı Mehmed Efendi was sent to Adrianople to retrieve him. When the rebellion succeeded, Mustafa II was deposed and his brother Ahmed III was placed on the throne.
Mustafa II died in exile in Constantinople in 1704.

entities/orhan-i.title: Orhan I (Orhan Gazi)
entities/orhan-i.content:
Orhan I — Orhan Gazi (c. 1281–1362)
Orhan I (c. 1281–1362), known as Orhan Gazi, was the second ruler of the Ottoman dynasty, succeeding his father Osman Gazi around 1326. His reign of approximately thirty-six years transformed a small frontier principality into an organized state with a standing army, a fixed capital, and the first bridgehead in Europe. Inalcik describes Orhan as “the true founder of Ottoman power.”
Succession and the Capture of Bursa
Orhan’s accession followed the death of Osman Gazi, which coincided with the Ottoman conquest of Bursa on 6 April 1326. The capture of Bursa, the first major Byzantine city to fall to the Ottomans, was the defining military achievement of Orhan’s early reign. Bursa became the Ottoman capital and remained so until the conquest of Edirne later in the century.
On Osman’s death, a meeting to choose his successor was held in the zaviye (hospice) of Hasan the Ahi, nephew of the influential sheikh Edebali. Orhan was chosen. He bore the title of sultan and, as a token of independence, struck the first Ottoman silver coins — the akçe — in Bursa in 1327. Orhan and his son Suleyman later created hundreds of vakifs (religious endowments) in newly conquered areas for the ahis and dervishes, confirming the order’s important role in the establishment of the Ottoman state and dynasty.
Organization of the Ottoman State
Under Orhan, the Ottoman principality adopted the administrative and cultural traditions of the Seljuk sultanate. Scholars and administrators from established Seljuk centers — Kastamonu, Karahisar, Denizli — brought the traditions of Islamic statecraft to Bursa and the other gazi capitals. Each principality became a small sultanate.
Orhan established the first Ottoman medrese (theological college) in Iznik in 1331, converting a former church building for the purpose and appointing the scholar Davud of Kayseri as its first professor. This marked the formal beginning of the Ottoman scholarly establishment, the ulema.
In 1340, Orhan created a commercial center in Bursa with the construction of a bazaar and a bedestan (an enclosed market for the sale of valuable goods). The Arab traveler Ibn Battuta visited Bursa around 1333 and described it as “a great city with fine bazaars and broad streets.” This bedestan remained the commercial heart of Bursa for centuries.
Creation of the First Standing Army
Orhan laid the foundations of the Ottoman standing army. Under his reign, the first regular infantry and cavalry forces — the yaya and müsellem — were organized. The yayas were Turkish farmers who received land grants (timars) in return for military service as infantrymen. The müsellems were a privileged class of cavalry who served in exchange for tax exemptions. These forces provided Orhan with a disciplined, directly-controlled military instrument distinct from the irregular gazi raiders who had formed the core of Osman’s forces. Lands distributed to the yayas and müsellems came under direct state control, a precursor to the full timar system that would later define Ottoman provincial administration.
Conquest of Iznik and Izmit
Orhan completed the Ottoman subjugation of the Marmara region. In 1331, the former Byzantine capital of Iznik (Nicaea) fell after a prolonged siege. Orhan immediately established a medrese there and made the city a center of Islamic learning. In 1333, he defeated the Byzantine emperor Andronicus III at the Battle of Pelekanon, securing Ottoman dominance in northwestern Anatolia. The conquest of Izmit (Nicomedia) followed in 1337, eliminating the last significant Byzantine stronghold in Asia Minor east of Constantinople. These victories gave the Ottomans control of the Gulf of Izmit and the approaches to the Bosporus, positioning them for the eventual crossing into Europe.
The Capture of Gallipoli and the First European Foothold (1354)
The most consequential event of Orhan’s reign was the Ottoman acquisition of a foothold in Europe — the Rumeli — in 1354. This was preceded by a chain of strategic developments:
In 1345, a struggle for the throne of the neighbouring principality of Karesi gave Orhan an opportunity to annex the state. The principality lay on the eastern shore of the Dardanelles, and its troops, now in Ottoman service, began to advocate for an expedition across the strait into Europe. Orhan’s son, Suleyman Pasha, was appointed commander of the western frontier and prepared the venture.
In 1346, Orhan made a critical alliance with John VI Cantacuzenus, a claimant to the Byzantine throne, and married John’s daughter, Theodora. This dynastic marriage gave the Ottomans a pretext to intervene in Byzantine civil wars and to cross into Thrace as allies. In 1352, Suleyman went to Adrianople to assist Cantacuzenus against Serbian and Bulgarian forces. He took possession of Tzympe on the eastern shore of the Gallipoli isthmus and, despite Cantacuzenus’s demands, refused to evacuate the fortress, reinforcing it with fresh troops from Anatolia.
On the night of 1-2 March 1354, a severe earthquake destroyed the walls of Gallipoli and other fortresses in the area. Suleyman’s forces immediately occupied these strongpoints, repaired the fortifications, and garrisoned them with troops from Anatolia, establishing the Ottomans permanently on European soil. The event caused panic in Constantinople and the western Christian world; plans for a crusade were drawn up, and the question of Church union was taken up again with renewed zeal.
Expansion in the Balkans
Following the Gallipoli occupation, Suleyman transported Muslims — particularly nomadic groups who could be easily resettled — from Anatolia to Europe. New Turkish villages were established, and the frontiers were organized into right, left, and center marches, each under the authority of a gazi lord. Ottoman raids expanded the occupied territory steadily.
However, Suleyman’s sudden and tragic death in 1357, combined with the capture of Orhan’s youngest son, Halil, by Phocaean pirates, forced Orhan to make peace with Byzantium. The Byzantines attempted to regain Thrace by diplomatic means, and Suleyman’s conquests were threatened. When Halil was finally released in 1359, the gazis realized that delay would mean defeat and the loss of the European bridgehead. Orhan’s other son, Murad, commander of the Gallipoli march, and his tutor Lala Sahin began a new offensive aimed at the eventual capture of Adrianople.
Economic and Diplomatic Activity
In 1352, Orhan granted commercial capitulations to the Genoese, establishing the pattern of economic privileges to European maritime republics that would become a staple of Ottoman diplomacy. These ties with Genoa facilitated Ottoman access to European markets and naval technology.
Death and Legacy
Orhan died in 1362 and was buried in Bursa. He was succeeded by his son, Murad I, who took the title Hudavendigar (Emperor) and would extend Ottoman rule deep into the Balkans. Orhan’s reign laid the foundations of the Ottoman state: a fixed capital at Bursa, a standing army, a rudimentary administrative and educational system, a silver coinage, and — most decisively — a permanent foothold in Europe. From the Gallipoli bridgehead established under his authority, the Ottoman Empire would expand across the Balkans and transform itself from a frontier principality into a world empire.

entities/osman-i.title: Osman I (Osmân Gâzî)
entities/osman-i.content:
Osman I — Osmân Gâzî (c. 1258–1326)
Osman I (Osmân Gâzî, عثمان غازى), the eponymous founder of the Ottoman dynasty and the Ottoman state, was a frontier gâzî leader of semi-nomadic Turcomans operating on the Byzantine-Seljuk frontier in northwestern Anatolia. From his base at Söğüt, he built one of several competing beyliks (principalities) that emerged from the collapse of Seljuk authority under Mongol suzerainty — and his alone proved durable enough to grow into a world empire.
Historical Problem of Ottoman Origins
Osman’s rise presents a central historical problem. One early twentieth-century theory (Gibbons, Iorga) maintained that the Ottoman state was effectively a revived Byzantine Empire in Islamic form, created when the Greek population of the Marmara basin converted to Islam. Inalcik joins Köprülü and Wittek in dismissing this as groundless speculation. The historiography has instead located Ottoman origins in the political, cultural and demographic transformations of thirteenth- and fourteenth-century Anatolia, with Köprülü emphasizing the Anatolian Seljuk institutional inheritance and Wittek stressing the role of the gazâ (holy war) ideology.
Anatolia under the Seljuks and Ilkhanids
The Mongol invasions from the 1220s set the stage. After the Mongol victory at the Battle of Kösedağ in 1243, the Seljuk sultanate of Anatolia became a vassal state of the Ilkhanids of Iran. The Mongol presence drove Turcoman nomadic tribes westward into the mountainous frontier region between Byzantium and the Seljuk realm. A major uprising against Mongol rule in 1277 was bloodily suppressed by the Mamlûk-allied rebels and then by the Mongols themselves, who thereafter kept permanent forces in Anatolia. The frontier became a refuge for fleeing troops, political figures, and destitute villagers — its population swelled, and its restless nomads pressed for gazâ against Byzantine territory.
Between 1260 and 1320, gâzî leaders organizing these warlike Turcomans founded independent principalities in western Anatolia on lands wrested from Byzantium. The Byzantine historian Pachymeres records that the Palaeologi, preoccupied with Balkan affairs after retaking Constantinople in 1261, neglected the Asiatic frontiers, opening the way for Turcoman inroads. By the 1290s these raids amounted to a general invasion.
Osman as a Ghazi Frontier Leader
Osman Gâzî emerges in the record as a chieftain of semi-nomadic Turcomans fighting under the command of the Emir of Kastamonu. Of all the gâzî beys, he held territory furthest north and closest to Byzantium and the Balkans, a position that would prove decisive for the future.
Frontier tradition required that a leader win a major victory over the Christians and receive the title of bey from the Seljuk sultan before claiming independence. According to Ottoman tradition — which Inalcik treats as a later legitimizing invention rather than literal history — Osman captured an important Byzantine fortress, after which the Seljuk sultan proclaimed him bey, sending him the traditional symbols of authority: a robe of honour (hil’at), a flag, a horse, and a drum. The same tradition holds that Osman proclaimed his independence after his victory at Baphaeon and had the hutbe (Friday sermon) read in his own name.
The Ahî Connection and Mystic Legitimation
