Dervish orders dominated the spiritual life of the frontier, and a mystic origin was sought for Osman’s political power. The earliest accounts portray Osman receiving sanctification from Şeyh Edebali, probably the head of an ahî fraternity (fütüvvet organization of artisans and warriors). Edebali girded Osman with a gâzî’s sword and prophesied that his descendants would rule the world. Osman reinforced this alliance by marrying Edebali’s daughter, securing the support of the most influential spiritual authority on the frontier.
On Osman’s death, the succession meeting was held in the zâviye (dervish hospice) of Edebali’s nephew, Hasan the Ahî. Orhan and his son Süleymân later endowed hundreds of vakıfs (pious foundations) for these ahî communities across newly conquered lands, confirming the ahîs’ foundational role in the establishment of the Ottoman state.
Victory at Baphaeon and the Rise of the Osmanlı
In about 1302 Osman laid siege to Iznik (Nicaea), the former Byzantine capital. The Byzantine emperor dispatched a mercenary army of two thousand men against him. Osman ambushed and defeated this force at Baphaeon (Koyunhisar) in the summer of 1302. The defeat of an imperial army spread his fame. Contemporary Byzantine and Ottoman sources describe how gâzîs from throughout Anatolia flocked to his standard, and his followers took his name — they became known as Osmanlı (عثمانلى, Ottomans). It was after the victory of 1302 that the Ottoman principality became truly established.
Fresh waves of settlers of various origins from central Anatolia were drawn west by the prospect of easy conquest and settlement.
The Early Beylik and Its Institutions
The gâzî principalities of western Anatolia soon adopted the traditions and institutions of the Seljuk sultanate. Cities such as Kastamonu, Karahisar and Denizli, established in the old Seljuk frontier districts, became centres of Seljuk civilization. On former Byzantine territory, the new gâzî capitals — Milas, Balat, Birgi, Izmir, Manisa, and later Bursa — received administrators and scholars who brought the traditions of Islamic statecraft. Each principality became a small sultanate in miniature.
Osman’s principality was at first the poorest and most exposed of these. But the frontier society was both tolerant and complex. Greek frontier troops (the Byzantine akritai) mingled with Muslim gâzîs; a famous example is Mihal Gâzî, a Greek frontier lord who accepted Islam and fought alongside Osman’s warriors. The ideal of gazâ — continuous Holy War for the expansion of the Dârülislâm (Abode of Islam) — was the driving ethos of this society, tempered by practical tolerance toward Christians, who were guaranteed their lives, property and religion in return for obedience and the poll tax (haraç).
Osman’s Relationship with the Seljuk Sultanate
Osman operated within the Seljuk frontier framework. To become an independent leader, he needed the Seljuk sultan’s recognition. Although the formal investiture story may be a later dynastic fabrication, it reflects the institutional reality: the beyliks of western Anatolia emerged from the Seljuk uj (frontier) organization, and their rulers claimed legitimacy through a Seljuk chain of authority. The Ottoman dynasty later traced itself to the Kayı tribe of Oğuz Turks, a genealogy that Yazıcıoğlu (writing under Murad II) used to connect the Ottomans to central Asian khanship traditions, fusing the Islamic concept of the gâzî sultan with the Oğuz concept of the khan.
The Capture of Bilecik and Yarhisar
Among Osman’s early conquests were the Byzantine fortresses of Bilecik and Yarhisar, which secured the Ottoman hold on the Söğüt region and provided a springboard for further operations toward Iznik and the Marmara coast. According to Ottoman tradition, Bilecik was taken by a stratagem during a wedding celebration, a story that exemplifies the blend of frontier cunning and gâzî warfare that marked Osman’s early campaigns. Yarhisar, overlooking the Sakarya River valley, was captured soon after, consolidating Osman’s control over the key approaches to Bithynia.
Establishment at Söğüt
Osman’s base was the frontier district of Söğüt in the mountainous region of northwestern Anatolia, originally granted to his father Ertuğrul as a Seljuk uç (march) territory. From this modest winter pasture, Osman expanded his authority over the surrounding countryside, using Söğüt as both a military staging-ground and the symbolic seat of his fledgling dynasty. The choice of location — at the very edge of the Byzantine world, where the Sakarya River marked the frontier — proved strategically brilliant, allowing Osman to raid Byzantine territory while remaining sheltered from Mongol interference.
Succession and Death
Osman died around 1326, reportedly on the eve of the Ottoman capture of Bursa, which would become the first Ottoman capital under his son Orhan. On his death, a council met in the zâviye of Şeyh Edebali’s nephew, Hasan the Ahî, to choose his successor. They selected his son Orhan, who proved to be the true organizational founder of Ottoman power: Orhan struck the first Ottoman coins (silver akçe in Bursa, 1327), established the first medrese in Iznik (1331), and created a trading centre in Bursa with a bazaar and bedestan by 1340. The traveller Ibn Battuta visited Bursa around 1333 and described it as “a great city with fine bazaars and broad streets.”
Legacy
Osman Gâzî’s achievement was not that he built an empire — that would take his successors more than a century — but that he established a durable beylik with a coherent ideology (gazâ), a broad social coalition (gâzîs, ahîs, Turcoman nomads, and Christian frontier lords), and a strategic position that could absorb the influx of warriors and settlers drawn by its early success. The Ottoman dynasty never forgot its founder: every sultan was girded with Osman’s sword at his accession, a ceremony that traced its authority back to Şeyh Edebali’s original investiture.

entities/sari-mehmed-pasha.title: Sarı Mehmed Pasha (Baqqal-oghlu Şarı Hacı Mehmed Pasha)
entities/sari-mehmed-pasha.content:
Sarı Mehmed Pasha — Baqqal-oghlu Şarı Hacı Mehmed Pasha (the Defterdar)
Sarı Mehmed Pasha (d. 1717), also known as Baqqal-oghlu Şarı Hacı Mehmed Pasha, was an Ottoman finance minister (defterdar) and author of the reformist treatise Kitab-i Güldeste (also titled Naşa’iḥ ül-vüzera vel-ümera, “Advice for Viziers and Commanders”). Born in Constantinople, the son of a grocer (baqqal), his epithet Şarı (“yellow”) referred to his hair or complexion, while Hacı attested that he had completed the pilgrimage to Mecca.
Early Career
Between the ages of twelve and fifteen, he entered government service as an apprentice in the office of the rūznāmje-i evvel (chief day-book keeper of the treasury) in 1671 (AH 1082). He became a member of the household of Qilij Ali Efendi and rose through the ranks to become head of that bureau. During these years he gained the friendship of Rami Mehmed Pasha, then reis efendi (chief secretary of the divan), who would later elevate him.
Defterdar under Mustafa II
On 6 May 1703 (18 Dhu’l-Hijje 1114), Sarı Mehmed was appointed chief defterdar by Grand Vizier Rami Mehmed Pasha, replacing Sarı Mustafa Efendi. Barely two months later, the Edirne Vaq’ası (“Adrianople Happening”) erupted in Istanbul — a revolt triggered by the government’s failure to pay the troops. The rebellion removed Sultan Mustafa II from the throne and brought Ahmed III to power. Sarı Mehmed Efendi (not yet a pasha) was at Adrianople with the court, participated in the councils of state, and was sent to escort the sultan to the army. When the grand vizier’s army defected to the rebels, he went into hiding to save his life.
Service under Ahmed III
After the revolution, the rebels’ own defterdar proved incompetent. The military chiefs made overtures to Sarı Mehmed, who emerged from hiding, accepted the position, and received from Ahmed III “the robe of continuance in office.” His first great challenge was raising 4,688 purses (approximately 1.5 million piastres) to pay the mutinous troops and the coronation donative (cülûsiye) — the largest sum ever disbursed on such an occasion. A thousand purses came from the sultan’s private treasury; the rest was raised through confiscated estates and advance sales of tax farms. He later raised an additional 2,600 purses for troops who had gathered at the capital upon Ahmed III’s return.
Later Career and Execution
Despite these services, Sarı Mehmed was removed after a short time to make way for Firari Hasan Pasha, a rebel leader. He returned to the familiar post of rūznāmje-i evvel. Over the following fifteen years he served as defterdar four more times, interspersed with terms as beylerbey of Salonika and other provincial posts. He was present at the disastrous Battle of Peterwardein (1716), where the Ottoman army under Grand Vizier Ali Pasha was destroyed by Prince Eugene of Savoy; he helped save the Sacred Standard and fled to Belgrade.
Sarı Mehmed openly aspired to the grand vizierate, a fact that alarmed the court. In December 1716 he was removed from office and sent as governor to Salonika, then accused of oppressing the province and of being responsible for the fall of Temesvar. His property was confiscated and he was imprisoned in the castle of Kavala. In February 1717 (Rebi’ ül-evvel 1129), the chief doorkeeper Yusuf Agha executed him at Kavala; his head was brought to Constantinople and exposed at the Imperial Palace gate. His body was buried in the courtyard of the Great Mosque of Kavala.
Literary Works
Sarı Mehmed Pasha composed two major works. The Zübdet ül-veqa’i’ is a detailed chronicle of events from 1671 to 1703, valued for its financial precision and relatively plain style. The Kitab-i Güldeste (or Naşa’iḥ ül-vüzera vel-ümera) is a book of counsel for rulers in the nasihatname tradition, written at the behest of Ahmed III. It offers a rare insider’s perspective on the defects of the Ottoman state and proposes remedies, following a tradition that includes the works of Koçi Bey, Lutfi Pasha, and Hasan Kafi Aqhisari.

entities/selim-i.title: Selim I (Yavuz Sultan Selim / Selim the Grim)
entities/selim-i.content:
Selim I — Yavuz Sultan Selim (Selim the Grim)
Selim I (1470–1520), known in Ottoman tradition as Yavuz (“the Grim” or “the Stern”), was the ninth sultan of the Ottoman dynasty, reigning from 1512 to 1520. The son of Bayezid II and the father of Suleiman the Magnificent, Selim transformed the Ottoman state in eight years from a frontier principality rooted in Anatolia and the Balkans into a world empire spanning the Middle East. His conquest of Syria, Egypt, and the Holy Cities of Mecca and Medina, together with his annexation of eastern Anatolia and his destruction of the Safavid army at Chaldiran, made the Ottoman Empire the dominant power of the Islamic world and brought the old heartland of the Abbasid caliphate under Ottoman rule. Halil Inalcik treats his reign as the decisive break between the “definitive establishment” of the Ottoman Empire (1453–1526) and its emergence as a world power — the period when the empire ceased to be a frontier gazi state and became, instead, an Islamic caliphate.
Early Life and Governorship
Selim was one of eight sons of Bayezid II, born to one of the sultan’s concubines. As Ottoman custom required, he was sent as a prince to a provincial governorship, serving in Trebizond on the eastern Black Sea coast. This posting placed him directly on the frontier with the emerging Safavid state of Shah Ismail, whose agents stirred up the Kizilbas (Red Head) heresy among the Turcoman tribes of Anatolia. From his position in Trebizond, Selim observed the Kizilbas threat firsthand and developed the implacable hostility toward Safavid Persia that would define his reign. He demanded strong action against Shah Ismail while his father, Bayezid II, pursued a cautious, conciliatory policy.
Rebellion against Bayezid II
In the last years of Bayezid II’s reign, the succession struggle among the Ottoman princes tore the empire apart. Bayezid’s mild administration had encouraged disaffected elements in Anatolia — Turcoman nomads, dispossessed sipahis, and heterodox dervish orders — to rebel against Ottoman authority. The Safavid agents of Shah Ismail fanned these fires, and in 1511 the Kizilbas rose in full revolt in the highlands of western Anatolia, marching on Bursa under a Safavid agent. It was clear that the aged and sick sultan could no longer control the situation.
Each of the princes sought to win the Janissaries to his cause with promises of increased salaries. Although the sultan and the grand vizier preferred Prince Ahmed, it was Selim who won the support of the Janissaries — men who admired his warlike character, which reminded them of Mehmed the Conqueror. In 1511 Selim took arms against his brothers, and the Janissaries sided decisively with him over Ahmed. On 24 April 1512 Selim forced his father to abdicate. Bayezid II died shortly afterward, possibly poisoned, on the road to his retirement in Dimetoka.
Elimination of Rivals
Selim I, one by one, eliminated his brothers who were rivals for the throne. He also executed his nephews and any other potential claimants, applying the Law of Fratricide codified by Mehmed the Conqueror with a thoroughness that earned him his grim reputation. Inalcik notes that these purges followed the established dynastic principle: to preserve the unity of the empire, the victorious prince removed all threats to his rule.
The Kizilbas Purges and the Campaign against Shah Ismail
Before confronting Shah Ismail directly, Selim conducted a sweeping purge of Safavid partisans within his own domains. He imprisoned and executed some forty thousand Kizilbas supporters in Anatolia who were suspected of allegiance to the Safavid shah. This was a religious as well as a political measure: Selim denounced Ismail as a Shiite heretic, presenting the coming war not merely as a struggle for territory but as a jihad against those who had corrupted Islam.
Shah Ismail, for his part, replied as Uzun Hasan had done before him — reminding Selim of the fate that had befallen Bayezid I at the hands of Timur, and threatening to invade Anatolia. The two armies met on the plain of Chaldiran (Caldiran), east of Lake Van, on 23 August 1514.
The Battle of Chaldiran
The battle was a decisive Ottoman victory. The Ottomans possessed overwhelming superiority in firearms — cannon and muskets — which Selim deployed to devastating effect against the Safavid cavalry. The Safavids, like the Mamluks, had been slow to adopt gunpowder weapons, and their traditional mounted archers could not stand against the Ottoman artillery and Janissary musket-fire. Shah Ismail himself was wounded and narrowly escaped capture, his camp and his harem falling into Ottoman hands.
Chaldiran was one of the most consequential battles of the early modern Middle East. It broke the Safavid threat to Ottoman Anatolia, established Ottoman military dominance in the region, and fixed the eastern border of the Ottoman Empire for centuries. The victory temporarily subdued the Kizilbas threat and allowed Selim to annex the mountainous region from Erzurum to Diyarbekir. In 1516–17, the local dynasties and tribal chieftains in this area recognized Ottoman suzerainty. Anatolia was now secure against invasion from the east, and the routes to Azerbaijan, the Caucasus, and Baghdad were open to the Ottomans.
The Conquest of the Dulkadir Principality
In the year following Chaldiran, Selim invaded the buffer principality of Dulkadir (Dulkadiroglu) in southeastern Anatolia, a vassal state of the Mamluk sultanate. This act made a conflict with the Mamluks inevitable. The Mamluk rulers, a military caste of Turkish and Circassian slaves who had ruled Egypt and Syria for two and a half centuries, had long contested Ottoman influence over the Anatolian frontier principalities. The rivalry had simmered since Mehmed the Conqueror’s annexation of Karaman in 1468, and Bayezid II had fought inconclusive campaigns against the Mamluks between 1485 and 1491. Selim’s annexation of Dulkadir broke the buffer and brought the two powers to war.
The Conquest of Syria and Egypt (1516–1517)
By 1516 the entire Arab world, alarmed by Portuguese attacks in the Red Sea and Indian Ocean, had begun to fix its hopes on the Ottoman sultan as the great gazi ruler who could defend Islam. In that year, the Sherif of Mecca — a descendant of the Prophet Muhammad — proposed to send a delegation to Selim. The Mamluk sultan, al-Ghawri, prevented it; but the Arab lands were increasingly prepared to accept Ottoman rule. When Selim marched against the Mamluks, he announced to the Arabs that he came to free them from the Mamluk yoke and to protect the Islamic world.
Selim marched first against Aleppo. The city’s governor and people deserted to the Ottomans, and on 24 August 1516 — exactly two years after Chaldiran — Selim destroyed al-Ghawri’s army at Marj Dabiq, north of Aleppo. The Mamluk sultan died on the battlefield. In the great mosque of Aleppo, in the presence of the Abbasid caliph al-Mutawakkil, Selim received the title “Servant of Mecca and Medina.” The Ottoman army overcame the remaining Mamluk resistance and took Damascus and Jerusalem.
In Egypt, Tuman Bay proclaimed himself sultan and refused to submit. Selim crossed the Sinai Desert with his army, announcing that he would grant amnesty to the people and peasantry of Egypt — that his campaign was directed only against the Mamluk military caste. On 22 January 1517, at the Battle of Reydaniyya outside Cairo, Selim defeated Tuman Bay. The Mamluk sultan attempted to continue resistance by guerrilla methods but was captured and executed. Cairo fell, and the Mamluk sultanate — which had ruled Egypt since 1250 — was extinguished.
The Holy Cities and the Transformation of the Caliphate
On 17 July 1517, the Sherif of Mecca sent Selim the keys of the Holy Cities — Mecca and Medina — and announced his submission. Syria, Egypt, and the Hejaz now recognized Ottoman sovereignty, and Selim also laid claim to parts of the Yemen. Appointing Hayra Bay, the former Mamluk governor of Aleppo, as governor of Egypt, Selim returned to Istanbul.
Inalcik treats the addition of the Arab lands — and especially of Mecca and Medina — as the beginning of a new era. Selim sent to his palace in Istanbul the holy relics of the Prophet, considered the symbols of the caliphate. However, Inalcik explicitly rejects the traditional story that the Abbasid caliph al-Mutawakkil formally surrendered the office of caliph to Selim. By classical Sunni doctrine, the caliph had to descend from the Quraysh, the Prophet’s own tribe, and the classical concept of a single caliph for the whole Islamic community had had no force since the thirteenth century. Every Muslim ruler called himself caliph.
Nevertheless, Selim’s status changed radically. By annexing the old heartland of the caliphate, he became more than simply a gazi sultan on the frontiers of the Islamic world: he became at the same time protector of Mecca and Medina and guardian of the pilgrimage routes — a responsibility that carried more real authority than the title of caliph. The empire was no longer a frontier state but an Islamic caliphate, and the Ottoman sultans now considered themselves protectors not only of the frontiers but of the entire Muslim world. The Sherif of Mecca and the Muslim rulers of Sumatra and India would soon address the Ottoman sultan as “Protector of Islam,” and the khans of Turkestan would appeal to Istanbul to block Russian expansion toward the Volga.
Administration and Legacy
One result of Selim’s conquests was the elevation of religious law (the seriat) to a position of prime importance in the administration of the state. The new consciousness of the Ottoman rulers as protectors of all Islam reinforced the influence of the ulema and the conservative religious establishment. Another, equally important result was economic: the Ottomans now controlled the world’s richest centers of the transit trade. Ottoman state income doubled. The reserve treasury in the palace overflowed. With these resources, Selim’s son and successor, Suleiman I (1520–66), was able to support his plans for worldwide conquests.
Selim died in 1520 at the age of fifty, struck down by cancer (the “sultan’s disease” — a carbuncle or skin infection that proved fatal). He had reigned only eight years, but his conquests doubled the size of the Ottoman Empire and transformed its character permanently. The Venetian ambassador reported that Selim had been a man of iron will, cruel and decisive, who inspired terror in his own court as much as in his enemies. Ottoman chroniclers, while acknowledging his harshness, celebrated him as Yavuz — the Stern, the Grim — a sultan who crushed enemies of the faith and raised the empire to heights his predecessors could not have imagined.
Inalcik’s Assessment
Inalcik places Selim at the hinge of Ottoman history. Bayezid II’s reign of consolidation created the economic and military conditions — particularly the modernization of the army and the adoption of firearms — that made Selim’s conquests possible. Selim, in turn, broke the old pattern of the frontier principality: he made the Ottoman state into the dominant power of the Islamic world, the protector of the Holy Cities, and the heir of the caliphal tradition. His son Suleiman would inherit an empire whose income had doubled, whose treasury overflowed, and whose strategic position — controlling the trade routes between Europe, Asia, and Africa — made it a true world power. “This period of development,” Inalcik writes of the years leading up to Selim’s reign, “created the conditions necessary for the great conquests of Selim I and Suleiman I.”

entities/skanderbeg.title: George Kastrioti Skanderbeg (İskender Bey)
entities/skanderbeg.content:
George Kastrioti Skanderbeg — Iskender Bey
George Kastrioti, known to the Ottomans as İskender Bey (اسكندر بك, “Lord Alexander”) and to Western Christendom as Skanderbeg, was the leader of the Albanian rebellion against Ottoman domination that lasted from 1443 until his death in 1468. His name — İskender Bey — was given to him by the Ottomans, and both Tursun Beg and Kritobulos refer to him as such. Kritobulos, writing in Greek, calls him Alexander, prince of the Illyrians.
Early Life and Service Under the Ottomans
Skanderbeg was born in 1405 into the noble Kastrioti family, rulers of the region of Krujë (Croia) in central Albania. As part of the Ottoman devşirme (دوشیرمه, “collecting”) system — the levy of Christian boys for military service — young George was taken to the court of Sultan Murad II. There he received a military education, converted to Islam, and was given the name İskender and the title Bey (بك, lord). He rose through the Ottoman military ranks, fighting in campaigns across Anatolia and the Balkans, and was granted a timar (تیمار, military fief). He became one of the most capable Ottoman commanders of his generation, trusted by the sultan and familiar with Ottoman military tactics from within.
The Rebellion
In 1443, during the Battle of Niš against the Hungarian forces of John Hunyadi, Skanderbeg seized his moment. He abandoned the Ottoman ranks along with a contingent of Albanian soldiers, returned to Krujë, and recaptured his family’s fortress by trickery — presenting a forged document from the sultan to gain entry. He renounced Islam, re-embraced Christianity, and raised the standard of rebellion. Within weeks, he had consolidated control over much of central and northern Albania.
Skanderbeg’s revolt was remarkably successful for over two decades. He defeated successive Ottoman armies sent against him, exploiting the mountainous terrain of Albania to negate the Ottomans’ numerical superiority. He formed the League of Lezhë in 1444, uniting the Albanian chieftains under his command.
Alliance with Venice and Naples
Skanderbeg’s rebellion posed a strategic threat to the Ottomans not merely because of its military success, but because of the alliances it forged with Western powers. The Republic of Venice, whose commercial interests in the Adriatic were threatened by Ottoman expansion, became a key patron. Skanderbeg also entered into a vassalage relationship with the Kingdom of Naples under Alfonso V of Aragon, receiving financial and military support. In 1451, he signed the Treaty of Gaeta with Alfonso, formally recognizing Neapolitan suzerainty in exchange for protection and aid. These alliances made Skanderbeg a persistent headache for Mehmed II, who could not concentrate his forces on any single front without the risk of an Albanian-Venetian-Hungarian coalition forming in his rear.
The Venetian alliance became particularly significant after the fall of Constantinople in 1453, as Venice grew increasingly alarmed at Ottoman naval ambitions in the Adriatic. The Albanians, in Tursun Beg’s account, “broke the treaty they had made with the Ottoman state and crossed to the opposing side” in 1458-59, joining the Venetian-Hungarian alliance.
The Albanian Campaigns of Mehmed II
First Campaign and the Initial Ottoman Response
According to Tursun Beg, Mehmed’s advance toward the Adriatic following his Bosnian campaign of 1458-59 alarmed the Venetians, who drew the Albanians into their alliance with Hungary. In the summer of 1459-60, the Albanians began their attacks against Ottoman positions. Mehmed initially sent Balaban Pasha against them. Though Balaban Pasha won several early engagements, he was decisively defeated in the summer of 1460-61, and Yakup Pasha, one of his commanders, was killed.
“The Albanians’ continuous breaking of the peace and their harassing of the Turks in the mountainous terrain angered the Sultan and increased his wrath. Following these events, Fatih Sultan Mehmed’s patience ran out. ‘Our ghaza is directed toward the Albanian land,’ he decreed.”
The army assembled at Manastır (Bitola). Every ghazi “girded on his sword.” The Albanians, seeing the scale of the force arrayed against them, “climbed into rugged mountains and caves and hid themselves” and “held the passes.” The Ottoman forces pressed into the country with determination: “Mountains and deep valleys appeared to them like level plains.” Many towns and villages were conquered, and the campaign was conducted with exceptional harshness — “seven thousand” prisoners were executed at each staging post.
The Second Campaign and Iskender’s Siege of Elbasan
Tursun Beg records a second campaign into Albania. He describes the land of Albania as “full of extremely rugged and harsh mountains” — so difficult that even Alexander the Great was said to have been unable to enter it. Yet Sultan Abu’l-Fath (Mehmed II) “entered the land of Albania, subdued the infidel, and even had a fortress built in its very heart.”
The reason for the second campaign, Tursun writes, was that “the Albanian community was created rebellious and obstinate by nature. These swine showed obedience and loyalty only at times when the Sultan’s sword was circling over their heads, but later, under the leadership of the traitor Iskander, they joined together and chose sedition.”
Iskander (Skanderbeg) gathered his forces and marched upon Elbasan Castle, the Ottoman stronghold. Though the castle was besieged, it held — the defenders and fortifications protected it, and a relief force from the sanjak bey soon routed the enemy. But the insult to Ottoman honor demanded a response: the Sultan had become enraged and resolved to set out on campaign into Albania once again.
When Iskander Bey learned that Mehmed II was marching into Albania with an army, he immediately lifted the siege of Elbasan. This did not turn the Sultan from his decision.
The army wintered in Plovdiv, a harsh winter made bearable by high morale. In the spring of 1467, the imperial army entered Albania again, this time via the Belgrade side. The fiercest battle took place at a narrow pass called Bezurshek, a valley “no holy warrior’s foot had ever stepped into before.” The infidels were determined to block the pass, but were routed after a day-long battle and a night assault against the Anatolian troops that failed.
Tursun Beg records that “the whole of the Albanian land was captured. Countless captured infidel soldiers were put in chains and brought before the Sultan. And this time no mercy was shown to the infidels; nearly all were subjected to the sword and slain. No substantial force capable of defying again was left.”
Mahmud Pasha was sent to raid the fortress of Iskenderiye (Scutari, modern Shkodër), which “was attached to the Franks” (Venetian-held). He burned the town and plundered the surroundings. Iskander Bey “fled, barely saving his head.”
The Construction of Elbasan Fortress
The fortress of Elbasan (called Ilbasan by Tursun Beg) was the cornerstone of Mehmed II’s strategy to subdue Albania permanently. Built on the Yund (Vlonë/Myzeqe) plain — described as “the heart of Albania” — it was intended as a permanent base for Ottoman operations in the region.
Kritobulos provides a detailed account of its construction, though he does not name it explicitly. During his campaign against the Illyrians (Albanians), Mehmed II recognized that assaulting the impregnable fortress of Croia directly would be futile. Instead, he resolved that “a stronghold should be raised against them.” Exploring the country, he “came upon the site and ruins of an ancient city” in a suitable part of the land — once “a very fine and very beautiful city” — and decided to rebuild it.
Starting at the beginning of summer, “with great force, zeal and expense, and with imperial encouragement and supervision — for he himself was present everywhere at the work, explaining everything and urging everyone on” — the fortress was completed before the end of summer: “a notable and admirable work.” He settled it with inhabitants from the surrounding provinces, stocked it with provisions, weapons, stone-throwing cannons, and mortars, and left a garrison of four hundred of his most warlike and strongest bodyguard soldiers.
Tursun Beg’s account adds that the fortress was completed in a short time, located in a place surrounded by rugged mountains and passes, “extremely strong against any attack or siege,” with pleasant air and fertile surroundings. It was named Ilbasan (Elbasan, meaning “the place” in Turkish), appointed with a dizdar (fortress commander) and guards, stocked with provisions and munitions, and attached to the sancak of Ohrid.
The strategy was clear: from Elbasan, Ottoman forces could “constantly sally forth and plunder, and not permit the Illyrians to go out of the fortress, or to come down from the mountains and cultivate the land in winter, or to pasture their cattle.” The plan was to keep the Albanians “always shut in and harassed” until they were compelled to submit.
The Siege of Croia (Krujë)
Croia (Akçahisar to the Turks, Krujë to the Albanians) was the heart of the Albanian resistance — Skanderbeg’s ancestral fortress and the symbol of his revolt. Both sources describe it as virtually impregnable.
Kritobulos calls it “an utterly impregnable, very strong fortress… the citadel and bulwark of the entire province.” Murad II, Mehmed’s father, had “attempted by every means — by arms, stone-throwing engines and long blockade — but still could not take it; so impregnable it was.” Tursun Beg similarly records that Murad II besieged Akça Hisar castle for six months “but, due to its fortitude, did not mount an assault and, postponing its conquest, withdrew.”
In 1466, during his first Albanian campaign, Mehmed II arrived before Croia. He examined its strength and “decided that it was not at all necessary to attack such a stronghold and labor in vain.” Instead of a direct assault, he built Elbasan as a counter-fortress, left a governor (Palapanos/Balaban Pasha) with a sufficient army to besiege Croia, and returned to Constantinople.
The siege of Croia continued into the following year. Skanderbeg, who had meanwhile obtained Hungarian alliance, gathered his men, “secretly set an ambush, fell upon the emperor’s governor left there, named Palapanos, who was keeping Croia under siege and assaulting it, without his having any forewarning; and terrifying him with the mass assault and routing him, he drove him off, slew many of his men, and killed him himself, though he fought valiantly” (Kritobulos, §91). This was Balaban Pasha — the same commander Tursun Beg records as having first been sent against the Albanians years earlier.
After this victory, Skanderbeg brought provisions and weapons into Croia, strengthened the garrison, and secured the surrounding province, leaving the Ottoman forces in Elbasan effectively besieged themselves.
Mehmed’s Second March Against Croia
When the news of Balaban Pasha’s death reached him, Mehmed II “blazed with anger and without any delay, as quickly as possible, gathering as large a cavalry and infantry army as possible and equipping it well” at the beginning of spring 1467. He overran Illyria, seized the revolted region, and “carried out great slaughter among the rebels.”
Skanderbeg withdrew “into the safe and steep mountains, into his usual hiding places in the wild forests, unable to endure even the sight of the army.” The emperor proclaimed that his soldiers could freely plunder everything and take captives, sending the greatest part of the army into the mountains under Mahmud (Mahmud Pasha). For fifteen days the soldiers searched the mountains thoroughly — “the mountains, rocks, clefts, depths, caves, passes, nooks and hiding places” — and carried off immense booty in captives, cattle, and goods.
Skanderbeg, learning that the army had occupied the mountains, “immediately took to flight, and no one could hear word of where he had fled.”
Mehmed II then pressed on toward Croia, pitched camp beneath it, “and surrounding it with his army and setting up his cannons, besieged it.” But again the fortress proved too strong. After a few days, “when he realized that it was impossible to take it by assault with arms and force, for the fortress was very strong and utterly impregnable, nor would he subdue it by fair words,” Mehmed deemed it best to leave sufficient troops for a prolonged siege and return to Constantinople. “It was in his power to bring the fortress into his hands by hunger and prolonged blockade.”
That summer, a devastating plague swept through Thrace and Macedonia, reaching Constantinople itself, killing over six hundred people daily. Mehmed, on his return, diverted his route to avoid the plague, heading into the region of Mount Haemus and Upper Moesia (Nicopolis and Vidin), where he spent the autumn. He arrived back in Constantinople at the beginning of winter, ending the seventeenth year of his reign (1467).
Aftermath and Death
Skanderbeg’s death on January 17, 1468, at the age of 62, effectively ended organized Albanian resistance. Deprived of his leadership, the Albanian League dissolved, and the fortresses of Albania fell to the Ottomans one by one. Krujë itself held out until 1478, when it finally surrendered to the forces of Mehmed II after a new siege — ten years after Skanderbeg’s death.
Tursun Beg records that Akça Hisar (Krujë) eventually fell through starvation: “the infidels’ destitution and hunger reached such a level that whatever dogs, cats, and vermin they found, they ate.” The castle guards, “already exhausted from hunger, were not strong enough to fight.” They surrendered, but Mehmed II suspected they would resume resistance once resupplied, and “some of the ringleaders were subjected to the sword-law.”
The entire province of Albania was conquered and “entrusted to the sanjak bey.” The villages and hamlets around Elbasan Castle became “an abode of peace.”
Legacy
Skanderbeg remains a national hero of Albania, celebrated for his twenty-five-year resistance against the Ottoman Empire. In Ottoman sources, he is a rebel and a traitor who broke his treaty oaths — Tursun Beg calls him “the traitor Iskander” who led a community “created rebellious and obstinate by nature,” while Kritobulos, though more restrained, simply names him Alexander, prince of the Illyrians, and records his skillful use of guerrilla tactics and his ability to exploit the mountainous terrain. Both sources agree on the essential facts: Skanderbeg was a formidable military commander who understood Ottoman warfare from within, who forged alliances with Venice and Hungary that prolonged his resistance, and whose fortress of Croia was so strong that even Mehmed II could not take it by assault.

entities/sofia.title: Sofia
entities/sofia.content:
Sofia — صوفيه (Sofya)
Coordinates: 42°41’50”N 23°19’20”E
Sofia (Ottoman Turkish Sofya, صوفيه) is the Balkan city that the Ottomans made the administrative capital of the province of Rumelia. Captured in the mid-1380s during the rapid Ottoman expansion into the central Balkans, Sofia was transformed from a Bulgarian royal capital into the nerve center of Ottoman power in Europe, where the Beylerbey of Rumelia held court and from which the Ottoman military machine was supplied and directed.
Geography and Strategic Position
Sofia lies in a fertile valley at the foot of the Vitosha mountain, commanding the intersection of two of the most important routes in the Balkan peninsula: the north-south route from the Danube to the Aegean (through the Nishava and Struma valleys) and the east-west route from Constantinople to the Adriatic (the Via Militaris/Orta Kol). The city sits at an elevation of about 550 meters, surrounded by mountains that made it a natural fortress controlling access to the central Balkans.
This position made Sofia the indispensable waystation on the Ottoman military road to Hungary. Every army marching from Edirne or Istanbul toward Belgrade and the Danube passed through Sofia, and the city’s markets and depots supplied the campaigns into Serbia, Bosnia, and Hungary.
Ottoman Conquest and Administration
Sofia was captured around 1385 during the campaigns of Murad I and the frontier lords (uc beys) that followed the Maritsa and Morava valleys northward. The city had been an important Bulgarian center under Tsar Ivan Shishman, but it offered only limited resistance to the disciplined Ottoman forces. After its conquest, it was designated the seat of the Beylerbey of Rumelia, the supreme military governor of the European provinces, a position second only to the grand vizier himself.
Throughout the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, Sofia served as the administrative capital of Rumelia, governing an area stretching from the Adriatic to the Black Sea and from the Morea to the Danube. The beylerbey’s palace and the associated government offices gave the city an official character distinct from the commercial centers of the empire. The city’s Great Mosque and bedestan were built in the early Ottoman period, and a network of medreses and caravanserais developed around the administrative core.
Role in Trade
Although Sofia’s primary function was administrative, it was also a significant commercial hub. Merchants from the towns of Sofia, Provadiya, Plovdiv, Edirne, Nicopolis, and Silistra traded through the city, particularly in the transit trade to the Black Sea ports of Caffa and Kilia. Sofia’s position on the road from Istanbul to Belgrade made it a natural stopping point for caravans, and its bazaars handled goods from throughout the Balkans — woolens from Bulgaria, cottons from Macedonia, and spices and silks from the east.
The city’s merchants were active in the Black Sea trade, carrying Ottoman manufactured goods and textiles northward and returning with furs, slaves, and grain from the Ukrainian steppes and the Crimean ports.
The City Under Ottoman Rule
Sofia under the Ottomans maintained a mixed population of Muslims, Orthodox Christians, and a significant Jewish community. The city’s skyline was dominated by the Buyuk Cami (Great Mosque) and the numerous minarets of smaller mosques and mescids. The hot mineral springs that had made Serdica (the Roman name of the city) famous continued to be used, and the Ottoman bathhouses (hammams) became a characteristic feature of the urban landscape.
The city’s Christian population — mostly Bulgarian and Greek — retained their churches and were organized under the millet system, paying the cizye (poll tax) in exchange for religious autonomy. The city was not forcibly Islamized; conversion was gradual and piecemeal over the centuries of Ottoman rule.
Key Connections
Sofia’s primary connection was with Istanbul and Edirne along the imperial military road, and with Belgrade and Nish to the north as the staging ground for campaigns into Hungary. The city’s role as the seat of the Rumelian beylerbey made it a critical link in the chain of command between the capital and the frontier. Plovdiv (Filibe) to the east and Skopje (Uskub) to the southwest were its regional counterparts in the Rumelian administrative network.

entities/sokollu-mehmed-pasha.title: Sokollu Mehmed Pasha
entities/sokollu-mehmed-pasha.content:
Sokollu Mehmed Pasha
Sokollu Mehmed Pasha (صوقلو محمد پاشا; c. 1505–1578) was a Bosnian-born Ottoman statesman who served as grand vizier (sadrazam, صدر اعظم) under three sultans — Süleyman I (1565–1566), Selim II (1566–1574), and Murad III (1574–1579). His fourteen-year uninterrupted tenure as grand vizier made him the longest-serving holder of the office in the classical period, and during the reigns of Selim II and Murad III he was effectively the ruler of the Ottoman Empire. Halil Inalcik treats Sokollu as the central figure of the post-Süleymanic era, the last great statesman of the classical Ottoman order before the accelerating decline of the late sixteenth century.
Origins and Devsirme
Sokollu was born into a Christian family in the village of Sokolovići in Bosnia, then part of the Ottoman vassal state of the Sanjak of Bosnia. He was taken as a child through the devşirme (دوشيرمه) — the periodic levy of Christian boys for service to the sultan. Inalcik notes that Bosnia was unique in the Ottoman Empire in that even families that had converted to Islam provided children for the devşirme, a practice not observed elsewhere — a context that explains how Bosnian recruits like Sokollu entered the palace system.
Like all devşirme boys, Sokollu was converted to Islam, circumcised, given a Muslim name, and subjected to rigorous training in the palace schools of Topkapı. The ablest of these recruits rose through the ranks of the içoğlans (pages) and emerged into the highest offices of state — Inalcik describes how the palace functioned as the empire’s principal training ground for statesmen. Sokollu’s rise exemplified this system at its most successful.
Career under Süleyman I
Sokollu held a series of senior posts before reaching the grand vizierate. He served as Kapudan Pasha (قپودان پاشا, Lord High Admiral) and later as Beylerbeyi of Rumelia, the supreme military governor of the European provinces. In 1564–65, during the final years of Süleyman’s reign, he was elevated to the grand vizierate.
Inalcik identifies Sokollu as one of only three truly great grand viziers of Süleyman’s reign, alongside Ibrahim Pasha (1523–1536) and Rüstem Pasha (1544–1553, 1555–1561). Inalcik writes that “the famous grand vizier Sokollu Mehmed (1564–79) took no decision on matters of state without first consulting his confidential secretary, Ferîdûn,” who served as nişancı (نيشانجى, chief of the chancery) from 1573 to 1581. This reliance on a professional bureaucrat reflected the growing importance of the scribal class in Ottoman administration.
Süleyman died on 6 September 1566 during the siege of Szigetvár, with Sokollu as his grand vizier. Inalcik notes that the withdrawal from Malta in 1565 and Süleyman’s final Hungarian campaign in 1566 marked “the beginning of a halt in the Ottoman advance into central Europe and the Mediterranean.” The transition to Selim II placed Sokollu in an unprecedented position of power.
Effective Ruler under Selim II and Murad III
Selim II (reigned 1566–1574) was the first Ottoman sultan who did not personally lead his armies on campaign, preferring to remain in the palace. This abdication of the sultan’s traditional military role devolved effective authority onto Sokollu. Inalcik notes the pattern: “When Selîm II came to the throne he embarrassed the old grand vizier, Sokollu, by acting on the advice of his old tutor” — a sign that even Sokollu’s vast authority could be undercut by the sultan’s personal circle. The same pattern continued under Murad III (1574–1595), where the sultan’s tutor Sa’deddin became “the main voice directing the state’s domestic and foreign policies” between 1579 and 1599.
Despite these constraints, Sokollu was the dominant figure in Ottoman government for the decade after Süleyman’s death. His period in office coincided with some of the most dramatic events of late sixteenth-century Ottoman history.
The Don-Volga Canal and the Astrakhan Expedition
Sokollu’s most ambitious strategic conception was the plan to link the Don and Volga rivers by canal, allowing the Ottoman fleet to sail from the Black Sea into the Caspian. The objective was to oust the Russians from Astrakhan, project Ottoman power into the Caspian basin, and encircle Safavid Iran. Inalcik describes the plan and its fate:

In 1569 the Ottoman attempt to dig the canal and besiege Astrakhan failed. The grand vizier, Sokollu Mehmed Pasha, had formulated the plan, and his rivals now proposed that the empire should concentrate its forces in the Mediterranean rather than continue the expensive and difficult war in the north.

The failure of the Astrakhan campaign was a turning point. Sokollu’s rivals — likely including Lala Mustafa Pasha and Sinan Pasha — successfully argued for redirecting Ottoman resources toward the Mediterranean, a shift that led to the conquest of Cyprus (1570–1571) but also to the catastrophic defeat at Lepanto.
Cyprus, Lepanto, and the Mediterranean
Under Sokollu’s direction, the Ottomans conquered Cyprus from Venice in 1570–71. Inalcik considers this “the last great Ottoman military success” — a feat that required cutting Venetian supply lines, transporting a vast army across the Mediterranean, and besieging heavily fortified positions. The operation provoked the formation of the Holy League (Spain, Venice, and the Papacy), which crushed the Ottoman fleet at Lepanto on 7 October 1571.
Inalcik records that the Ottomans lost 200 out of 230 ships at Lepanto, with total casualties of 59,000 men on both sides. The Holy League even considered a direct attack on Istanbul. Yet Sokollu’s administration responded with remarkable speed: “During the winter all the Ottoman dockyards had laboured ceaselessly to replace the ships destroyed at Lepanto.” Venice, intimidated by this display of Ottoman resilience, made peace on 7 March 1573, renouncing all claims to Cyprus and paying a large indemnity. The fleet under Uluç Ali Pasha recaptured Tunis in 1574.
Administrative Reforms and Governance
Inalcik situates Sokollu within the broader evolution of the grand vizierate. Under Süleyman, the grand vizier had evolved from primus inter pares into a figure far above the other viziers — a development Inalcik traces to Süleyman’s willingness to entrust immense power to a chosen deputy. Sokollu inherited and exercised this enhanced authority.
The grand vizier’s powers, however, were not unlimited. Inalcik notes that “Murâd III appointed one of his close associates, Üveys Pasha, to the post of defterdâr (treasurer), to counteract the excessive influence of the grand vizier Sokollu.” The defterdar had independent authority over the treasury, and Inalcik quotes the kanunname of Mehmed the Conqueror: “Unless the defterdâr orders it, not a single akçe will enter or leave the Treasury.” This structural check on the grand vizier’s financial authority was deliberately deployed against Sokollu in his final years.
Sokollu’s administrative method relied heavily on his confidential secretary Ferîdûn, whom Inalcik describes as a key figure in the Sokollu administration. The reliance on expert bureaucrats reflected the growing sophistication — and complexity — of the Ottoman state apparatus.
The Sokollu Period and the Onset of Decline
Inalcik dates the beginning of Ottoman institutional decline to approximately 1575, three years before Sokollu’s death. The passage is significant:

From 1575, however, the reâyâ — tax-paying subjects — began to infiltrate [the kapıkulu ranks] and share their privileges, and in this way entered court and state service. Thus the basis of the slave system was destroyed and contemporary observers believed that this was the cause of the decline in obedience and discipline.

Sokollu’s own tenure thus straddles the watershed between the classical order and the onset of decay. Inalcik argues that contemporary Ottoman writers, seeking the causes of decline, “maintained that formerly only the grand vizier had represented the absolute authority of the sultan, but that weak sultans had later delegated their authority to irresponsible persons; the state administration had thus lost its unity.” Sokollu was the last grand vizier of the classical type who could claim to embody the unified authority of the sultan — after him, the power of the grand vizierate fragmented under palace intrigue, harem influence, and the independent authority of the ulema, the defterdar, and the Janissary agha.
Assassination
Sokollu Mehmed Pasha was assassinated in 1578. The chronological table in Inalcik records tersely: “1578 — Assassination of the grand vizier, Sokollu Mehmed; war with Iran (spring).” The assassination occurred at a critical moment — immediately before the outbreak of a major war with Safavid Iran (1578–1590), which under the command of Lala Mustafa Pasha would see Ottoman conquests in the Caucasus at Çıldır (1578) and the annexation of Georgia, Shirvan, and Derbent.
The precise circumstances of the assassination — a deranged dervish named Hasan who gained access to the grand vizier during a meeting — are not detailed in Inalcik but form part of the standard Ottoman chronicle tradition. His death removed the last restraining influence on the factional rivalries that would destabilize the Ottoman government for the next half-century.
Legacy
Sokollu Mehmed Pasha represented the classical Ottoman statesman ideal: a devşirme recruit who rose through merit to wield near-absolute authority on behalf of a divinely appointed sultan. Inalcik’s treatment emphasizes that Sokollu was the last grand vizier to exercise the kind of unified, comprehensive authority that the classical Ottoman system envisioned for the office. After his assassination, the checks on the grand vizierate — the defterdar’s financial independence, the kapı ağas’s control of palace patronage, the ulema’s judicial authority, and the emerging influence of the valide sultan and the sultan’s tutor — fragmented the power of the office and accelerated the empire’s decline into the seventeenth-century crisis.
His architectural legacy includes the Sokollu Mehmed Pasha Mosque in Istanbul (designed by the great architect Sinan), the Sokollu Mehmed Pasha complex in Lüleburgaz, and the Bridge of Sokolović in Višegrad (now a UNESCO World Heritage site) — physical monuments to the Bosnian-born statesman who, for fourteen years, effectively ruled the Ottoman Empire.

entities/suez.title: Suez (al-Suways / Suveys)
entities/suez.content:
Suez — al-Suways (السويس) / Suveys (سويس)
Suez (Ottoman Turkish: سويس, Suveys; Arabic: السويس, al-Suways) was the principal Red Sea port of Ottoman Egypt and the eastern terminus of the trans-Egyptian trade corridor connecting the Indian Ocean to the Mediterranean. Inalcik treats Suez as the Ottoman Empire’s primary naval base for operations in the Red Sea and Indian Ocean, and the shipyard from which successive Ottoman fleets were launched against the Portuguese.
Geography and Strategic Position
Suez is located at the northern end of the Red Sea, at the head of the Gulf of Suez, approximately 130 km east of Cairo. The city sits at the natural transit point where goods arriving from India, Arabia, and East Africa were unloaded and carried overland to the Nile for shipment to Cairo and Alexandria. The surrounding terrain is desert, with the Red Sea hills to the east and the Egyptian desert to the west. The site had been known since antiquity (the ancient Arsinoe/Clysma) but gained new importance under the Mamluks and especially under the Ottomans.
Under Ottoman Rule
Suez was a secondary settlement under the Mamluks, but the Ottoman conquest of Egypt in 1517 transformed it into a strategic naval base. Inalcik records that while still in Cairo in 1517, Selim I immediately sent material and craftsmen to Suez to build ships and ordered the construction of a fleet to drive the Portuguese out of the Indian Ocean. The Portuguese had entered the Red Sea that same year, threatening to capture Mecca and Medina, and the defense of the Holy Cities made Suez’s naval function an urgent imperial priority.
The arsenal at Suez became the Ottoman Empire’s second major shipbuilding facility after the imperial arsenal in Istanbul. In 1538 the Ottomans launched a major expedition from Suez against the Portuguese at Diu in India, though the local Muslim ruler of Gujarat refused to cooperate and the expedition ultimately failed. Inalcik notes that by 1546 the Ottomans had made Basra their second base after Suez for equipping fleets against the Portuguese.
Smaller Ottoman squadrons sailed from Suez in 1578 and 1585, capturing Portuguese factories on the East African coast and securing recognition of Ottoman suzerainty from the Prince of Mombasa.
Role in Trade
Suez was the Red Sea terminus of the spice and Indian goods route. Spices arriving from India and Indonesia at Jidda were transshipped across the Red Sea to Suez, then transported by caravan to Cairo and onward to Alexandria. Every year, as Inalcik notes, twenty ships laden with spices arrived at Jidda, the seaport of Mecca, and the goods were then forwarded to Suez for distribution. Ottoman pilgrims returning from Mecca carried spices, dyes, and Indian cloth through Suez on their way home.
Suez also served as the Egyptian port for the annual pilgrimage caravan to Mecca. Pilgrims from Cairo would gather at Suez for the sea crossing to Jidda, and the city’s markets provisioned the pilgrim convoys.
Key Connections

Cairo: Connected by the 130-km desert road; the Cairo-Suez route was the vital link between the Red Sea and the Nile
Jidda: Primary Red Sea counterpart; the Suez-Jidda sea route was the main artery of the Indian Ocean trade
Alexandria: Together with Suez formed the trans-Egyptian corridor (Suez—Cairo—Alexandria)
Basra: Second Ottoman naval base in the Indian Ocean theater, supplementing Suez
Mecca & Medina: Suez was the Egyptian gateway for pilgrimage traffic to the Holy Cities

entities/suleiman-the-magnificent.title: Suleiman I (Kanuni Sultan Süleyman / Suleiman the Magnificent)
entities/suleiman-the-magnificent.content:
Suleiman I — Kanuni Sultan Süleyman (the Magnificent / the Legislator)
Suleiman I (1494–1566), known to his own people as Kanuni (قانونى, “the Legislator”) and to Europeans as “the Magnificent,” was the tenth and longest-reigning Ottoman sultan, governing from 1520 until his death in 1566. His rule marked the apogee of Ottoman power, territorial extent, and institutional maturity. The Harvard historian Albert Howe Lybyer, whose 1913 study forms the core of this account, describes Suleiman’s empire as one whose power “nothing surpassed the manifestations of power that swept forth from Constantinople” amid the vast energies of the sixteenth century. His reign is the central case study for understanding the Ottoman Ruling Institution at its most developed and effective stage.
Accession and Early Reign
Suleiman succeeded his father, Selim I (Yavuz, “the Grim”), in 1520. He inherited an empire that had already expanded dramatically under Selim’s conquests of Egypt, Syria, and the Holy Cities of Mecca and Medina. He inherited as well the caliphal authority that Selim had consolidated, though the title itself was not commonly used by Suleiman in public documents and seems to have been unknown to Western writers of the sixteenth century.
His reign opened with two great triumphs that had eluded even Mehmed the Conqueror: the fortresses of Belgrade fell in 1521 and Rhodes in 1522. These victories announced a ruler as formidable as his most celebrated predecessors. The internal machinery of government he found in excellent order — the Ruling Institution, having been gathered closely about the sultans and constantly amended by them, needed no comprehensive codification. More remote matters, however — the feudal system, the bearing of taxation and land tenure on the subject population, criminal law, and the market regulations of the cities — had fallen into considerable disorder.
The Sultan as Head of State
Suleiman’s authority rested, Lybyer writes, “actually and immediately upon the military might which he controlled.” Psychologically, it was supported by the ancient Turkish tradition of absolute obedience to the ruler who led and fed his people, and by the undying allegiance of wide populations to the Caesar of New Rome, whose seat and splendor Suleiman had succeeded to. Theoretically and constitutionally, his power was that of the ancient caliphs of Islam.
In his capacity as caliph, Suleiman was head of the Islamic state, defender, executor, and interpreter of the Sacred Law, and defender of the faith. He was under obligation to punish heretics and unsubmissive infidels, to protect true believers, and to extend the area of his divinely-appointed rule. To him, after Allah and the Prophet, was due the absolute obedience of all good Moslems within his dominions. All the great institutions of the empire are to be thought of, Lybyer emphasizes, not as built upward from a basis in the popular will, but as extended downward from the divinely-appointed sovereign at the top.
“Suleiman’s authority rested actually and immediately upon the military might which he controlled.”
Suleiman was a devout Moslem whose piety increased in old age. He took seriously his duty of enforcing the provisions of the Sacred Law, not hesitating at unpopular measures like the prohibition of wine-drinking, or at those demanding self-sacrifice, such as the disapproval of musical instruments and silver plate. He had a fiery active period of youth when the liberal policy of the Ruling Institution was stronger in his mind, and a quieter old age when Moslem influence became predominant — it is not unlikely that a consciousness of his position as caliph grew upon him with advancing years.
Legislation and the Kanun-Nameh
Suleiman earned the name Kanuni not by producing a single comprehensive code, but by issuing a large number of Kanuns (written decrees of the sultan) that addressed the accumulated confusions of a rapidly grown empire. The law that demanded obedience within the Ottoman Empire was fourfold: the Sheri (Sacred Law of Islam), the Kanuns (written decrees of the sultans), the Adet (established custom), and the Urf (sovereign will of the reigning sultan). The Sheri was above the sultan and unchangeable by him; the Kanuns and the Adet were subordinate to the Urf; the Urf, when expressed and written, became Kanun and annulled all contradictory Kanuns and Adet.
The Multeka ol-Ebhar
The Sacred Law itself needed renovation. Mehmed II had commissioned a code from Khosrew Pasha in 1470, but by Suleiman’s day the Ottoman Empire had acquired Damascus, Bagdad, and Cairo — three seats of the later caliphate — and held the protectorate of the Holy Cities. A new code better adapted to the more widely Moslem character the empire had assumed was demanded. Suleiman charged Sheik Ibrahim Halebi (of Aleppo) with the task; the result, prepared before 1549, was the Multeka ol-ebhar, the “Confluence of the Seas,” which remained the foundation of Ottoman law until the reforms of the nineteenth century.
His Principal Kanuns
Suleiman’s own legislation dealt especially with timars (fiefs), rayahs (subject Christians), ceremonies, and criminal and market regulations. The most complete body of Kanuns he published was the Kanun-nameh Misr, a constitution for Egypt issued in 1532, probably inspired by his grand vizier Ibrahim following his visit to Egypt in 1524. The collection of the great Mufti Ebu Su’ud, which is called the Kanun-nameh of Suleiman, contains chiefly his ordinances in regard to land tenure and taxes of the subject Christians, together with laws to regulate the feudal system, and a few relating to judges and legal processes.
Limitations
Lybyer judges Suleiman “great as a legislator only by comparison with his predecessors.” His legislation was doubly hindered: by the conservatism of his people and his religion, which made radical departures practically impossible; and by the weakness inherent in despotic legislation, where the distance of the lawgiver from the subjects affected made true adaptation and complete enforcement impossible. Most of his laws professed an attempt to restore a former better state of affairs. Suleiman “set nothing in final order; and the ground had to be gone over again within fifty years after his death, in the reign of Achmet I.” The suspiciously great wealth of high officials like Ibrahim and Rustem, and the fact that fiefs and finances were in worse disorder than ever after no great time had elapsed, gives evidence that his laws were not faithfully enforced.
Nevertheless, he used severe measures to put down extortion, and strove by his market and police regulations to maintain justice, fairness, and order. If he did not enact measures directly to increase the welfare of the common people, his attempts to regulate the tax and tenancy systems tended to lighten their condition.
The Grand Vizierate
Under Suleiman, the office of grand vizier reached the climax of a noteworthy development. Whereas this official had formerly been the senior member of the sultan’s board of advisers — primus inter pares — he now became a personage far above his fellow-viziers. His position came to differ from theirs not merely in degree but in kind, a difference typified by the fact that in reporting to Suleiman after the meetings of the Divan, none spoke but the grand vizier. This development resulted, Lybyer argues, from Suleiman’s willingness to entrust much power to a chosen instrument who would thus relieve him of the immense cares of empire.
There were ordinarily four viziers in Suleiman’s reign, that being a sacred number with both Turks and Moslems. Seven grand viziers served him over forty-six years: Mohammed Piri Pasha (whom he found in office at his accession, serving six years in all) and Mohammed Sokolli (whom he left in office at his death, serving fifteen years). Thus in forty-six years there were only nine grand viziers in total. Three deserve to be called great: Ibrahim for his splendor, breadth of mind, and continuance in favor (thirteen years); Rustem for his financial shrewdness (fifteen years in two periods); and Mohammed Sokolli for his statesmanship (fifteen years without a break). All except Mohammed Piri Pasha were Christian renegades who had risen as slaves to the highest honor of the empire.
Ibrahim first held his master’s confidence for many years; Suleiman violated the rules of advancement in the government service to promote him. Later, Rustem came to full power, supported by Suleiman’s wife Roxelana (Hürrem Sultan) and his favorite daughter Mihrmah; for him Suleiman broke the rule of giving no high place to relatives of the imperial family. In Suleiman’s last years he left well-nigh everything to Ali and to Mohammed Sokolli.
The Divan
Suleiman did not preside at the Divan, the imperial council, and he has been greatly blamed for discontinuing the custom. Lybyer notes that “it is not impossible to sympathize with him” — spending several hours in deliberation on four days of each week during a lifetime was a prospect from which any man would shrink. Nevertheless, it was a serious rift in both great institutions of the empire at the most dangerous place, and its effect was decidedly to hasten their disintegration.
The Divan met four times each week throughout the year in time of peace: Saturday, Sunday, Monday, and Tuesday. Sessions lasted seven or eight hours. The grand vizier sat Turkish fashion in the middle of a long sofa extending round three sides of the hall. On his right sat the other viziers, with the Nishanji beyond them; on his left the two Kaziaskers, and beyond them the Defterdars. The Beylerbeys of Anatolia and Greece, the Kapudan Pasha, the Agha of the Janissaries, and the chief interpreter also had places. During the session, a simple meal of bread, meat, rice, fruit, and water was served to all in attendance.
Suleiman kept the Divan under control by means of a grated window in the wall of the room where it met. Not knowing when he might be listening there, his councillors had always to speak as if he were present with them. After the day’s work, those officials who had the right of audience went to the hall of audience to meet the sultan. The grand vizier alone reported on the decisions of the day, which the sultan usually approved as made, sometimes mitigating a decision or himself dictating a reply to an ambassador.
“The Divan … was a training-school of judges, administrators, and statesmen.”
Lybyer judges the Divan “a splendid ceremony” that transacted a great amount of administrative and judicial business. It was by no means a legislative chamber, but rather a combination of a president’s cabinet and a supreme court, unique in its character. It was “a training-school of judges, administrators, and statesmen, since men ordinarily rose from place to place among its offices as they gained experience.”
Finance and Taxation
Suleiman’s revenues have been variously estimated. Junis Bey, chief interpreter of the Ottoman court, and Alvise Gritti, business partner of the grand vizier Ibrahim, gave a total regular revenue of about four million ducats for the years between 1530 and 1537: 1,300,000 ducats from kharaj (tribute) from Anatolia and Greece; 1,600,000 from Egypt; 700,000 from Syria; 150,000 from Mesopotamia; and 250,000 from farms, islands, and customs of Constantinople and Pera.
Later estimates indicate growth. By the close of his reign, after large territories in Europe and Asia had been incorporated and after Rustem had made new arrangements, the total amount was probably seven or eight million ducats. Lybyer calculates the bullion value of six million ducats as less than fourteen million dollars, with purchasing power perhaps five times that of his era — equivalent to less than seventy million dollars for so great an empire, a remarkably small sum by modern standards.
This revenue, however, by no means covered all public expenses. It excluded the revenues devoted to the Moslem Institution, those specifically assigned by feudal grant to local government officers, and the income gathered by permanent fief-holders for their own support — the last probably amounted to about twice as much again. Allowing for everything, the equivalent of perhaps two hundred million dollars supported all central and local government. “Had there been no extortion,” Lybyer writes, “the people of the empire would not have been burdened heavily. Even with it … they probably did not suffer greatly in Suleiman’s time.”
The treasury department numbered more than eight hundred personnel organized into twenty-five bureaus or kalems, as instituted by Mehmed the Conqueror. These included bureaus for book-keeping, accounts of tithes and taxes, cavalry control, the capitation tax (jizyeh) on adult male subject Christians, accounts for the Holy Cities of Mecca and Medina, tributes paid in kind, grain stores for the border fortresses, and the chancery of the treasury department.
Court and Palace
Suleiman was known as the Legislator and the Conqueror, but beyond both these titles as the Magnificent. Lybyer writes that he “shone as head of the government and the army, but still more as head of the court.” His enormous establishment and his fondness for display and ceremony developed a similar spirit in all his kullar. Each officer of position became inordinately ambitious to have a large household, many horses, much portable wealth, and superb equipment on state occasions and in time of war.
This splendor ultimately proved ruinous. Suleiman’s own example embarrassed his finances to such an extent that he was willing that Rustem should require payment for office from newly-appointed great officials. Most of his kullar, in order to keep up display, were led to undignified and extortionate procedures. The pressure upon the subject population tended to become worse and worse. Lands began to grow less productive and to pass out of cultivation.
Yet in Suleiman’s time this weakness hardly appeared. Even after his best two sons had come to cruel deaths, and twenty thousand of his troops had died in vain at Malta, he went forth to his last campaign with a train which surpassed in pomp and splendor all that he had led before.
The Janissaries
The Janissaries in Suleiman’s time numbered between twelve and fourteen thousand. They were organized into one hundred and sixty-five ortas (regiments). Many were distributed in garrisons; probably not more than half resided in the capital. The Agha of the Janissaries was not himself a former Janissary but had come through the colleges of pages. He served as a sort of minister of war for them, directing enrolment, pay distribution, promotions, location, supplies, and clothing.
Suleiman succeeded, on the whole, in keeping the Janissaries in hand. They never revolted against him, and they supported him against his son Bayezid. He was able to lead them farther east than could his father Selim. Yet the Janissaries’ power was never far from the surface of politics. Busbecq, the Habsburg ambassador, relates how even the grand vizier Rustem, and Suleiman himself, felt toward these men when they were all together and their blood was hot. On one occasion Rustem sent word to Busbecq asking him to remove every cause of offense “which might occasion a quarrel with those atrocious scoundrels,” asking whether Busbecq was not aware “that it was war time, when they were masters, so that not even Solyman himself had control over them, and was actually himself afraid of receiving violence at their hands?”
“The Janissaries in Suleiman’s time numbered between twelve and fourteen thousand.”
Despite these tensions, the regular cavalry (the Spahis of the Porte) appear never to have caused Suleiman any special trouble. The Janissaries were constantly drained of their ablest men by promotion to higher posts, and could be repressed by punishment — now and then an especially active promoter of trouble was executed.
Military Campaigns
Suleiman’s reign saw the Ottoman army at its most formidable. The Ruling Institution, organized as an army, was victorious in battle throughout his reign. Belgrade (1521) and Rhodes (1522) — fortresses that had defied Mehmed the Conqueror — fell before his troops. At Mohács in 1526, the Hungarian kingdom was shattered. The siege of Vienna in 1529 failed, but in the thirty-five succeeding years he made large conquests, strengthened his power, and saw his prestige grow steadily.
His campaigns extended into Persia and Mesopotamia, and he died during the siege of Szigetvár in 1566, crushing the Hungarian frontier fortress in his last campaign. Lybyer records that in his final year he went forth “with a train which surpassed in pomp and splendor all that he had led before.”
The weakness of the Ottoman army as an institution was its essential indivisibility: only one great war could be waged at a time, although there were great enemies in two directions. An overwhelming defeat of the principal army would have been irreparably disastrous. But the army was to suffice for a long period, and its worst foes were to be internal rivalries and departures from its constitutive principles.
Family and Influence
Suleiman’s personal relationships had profound political consequences. His favorite wife, Roxelana (Hürrem Sultan), and his daughter Mihrmah both exerted significant influence. Lybyer notes that Roxelana “undoubtedly played some part” in the deaths of Suleiman’s son Mustapha and later of Roxelana’s own son Bayezid — victims of inexorable circumstances surrounding the succession. “In so far as she contributed to the fatal outcome, she hastened the fall of the empire.” Mihrmah, if tradition is correct, urged her father on to the great expedition against Malta — the expedition that broke his mailed fist upon a rocky isle and whose failure gave confidence to the Christian fleet that won at Lepanto.
In his late years Suleiman came greatly under the influence of the Ulema, who had readier access to him than did any other outside force. The influence of Roxelana and Mihrmah foreshadowed the power exerted in later reigns by far inferior and far worse women.
The Ruling Institution and Legacy
Lybyer’s central argument is that Suleiman’s government was, at its core, a vast slave-family — the Ruling Institution — which recruited its members almost entirely as Christian children, converted them to Islam, educated and trained them with the greatest care, and placed them in every position of military, administrative, and governmental authority. The men who guided Suleiman’s empire to its height were “sons of peasants and herdsmen, of downtrodden and miserable subjects, of unlettered and half-civilized men and women.” It is not easy to decide, Lybyer writes, “which is more to be admired, the ability by which such young men rose, or the confidence with which they were chosen and expected to rise.”
“Suleiman laid hold of many problems which had arisen, and through the agency of his ablest servants strove to set his house in order.”
Suleiman laid hold of many problems that had arisen and, through the agency of his ablest servants, strove to set his house in order. That he did not succeed in accomplishing more permanent results was due, Lybyer judges, to the fact that the task was too great for any man — the institution was too artificial to endure indefinitely.
After Suleiman the Magnificent, the Legislator, was to come Selim the Sot. The increase under Suleiman of the relative power of the grand vizier was a dangerous and eventually a disastrous development: it became easy for later sultans to withdraw into the harem, while the grand vizierate was left at the mercy of harem intrigue. The splendor and luxury of Suleiman’s example, while magnificent, was in the end to be ruinous.
Nevertheless, in the time of Suleiman the Ottoman government “seems to have attained, during the sixteenth century, the highest degree of perfection of which its constitution was capable” (Robertson, quoted by Lybyer). The empire took by siege Belgrade and Rhodes; it failed at Vienna and Malta; but for generations afterward its worst foes were not foreign armies but internal rivalries and departures from the principles that had built it.

entities/tabriz.title: Tabriz (Tebriz)
entities/tabriz.content:
Tabriz (تبريز / Tebriz)
Tabriz (Ottoman Turkish: تبريز, Tebriz) is a historic city in northwestern Iran (Persia), situated in the fertile valley of the Aji Chay River between the Sahand and Eynali mountains. It served as the capital of the Safavid Empire under Shah Ismail I and was the most important commercial entrepot on the overland silk route between Asia and the Mediterranean. Its proximity to the Ottoman frontier made it a prize contested repeatedly between the Safavids and the Ottomans throughout the 16th century.
Geography and Strategic Position
Tabriz lies at approximately 38.1° N, 46.3° E, in the Azerbaijan region of northwestern Iran. The city commands the approaches to the Caucasus through the Aras (Araxes) valley to the north and the routes leading westward to Erzurum and the Anatolian plateau, southward to Baghdad and Mesopotamia, and eastward to Qazvin and the Caspian provinces. Inalcik notes that in Mongol times there was “an imperial highway linking Tabriz to Konya through Erzurum, Erzincan and Sivas,” with twenty-three magnificent caravanserais still standing on the road between Sivas and Konya (Inalcik, p. 5249-5251). Silk caravans from Iran came to Aleppo “from Tabriz through Van, Bitlis, Diyarbekir and Birecik” (Inalcik, p. 5347-5348).
Under Ottoman Rule
Tabriz was the capital of the Safavid Shah Ismail I, the founder of the Safavid state and the great rival of Ottoman Sultan Selim I. After Selim’s decisive victory at Chaldiran in 1514, the Ottomans briefly occupied Tabriz, though Selim chose not to garrison it permanently. The city’s fortunes shifted again when Suleyman the Magnificent launched his campaign of 1534-35: “In this campaign of 1534-5 the Ottoman sultan took Tabriz and Baghdad and annexed Azerbaijan and Iraq” (Inalcik, p. 1746). Suleyman wintered in Tabriz in 1534 and returned there in the spring of 1535 (Inalcik, p. 9513, 9516).
The Ottoman hold on Tabriz proved tenuous. When war renewed in 1548, Suleyman marched against Iran a second time, and the struggle continued intermittently for seven years (Inalcik, p. 1757-1759). By the Treaty of Amasya (1555), Tabriz remained in Safavid hands while Baghdad was left to the Ottomans.
The city was taken again by the Ottomans later in the century: “Osmân Pasha captures Tabriz (September)” in 1585 during the protracted Ottoman-Safavid war (Inalcik, p. 9633). However, Shah Abbas the Great reconquered Tabriz on 21 October 1603, a recovery that began the reversal of Ottoman gains in the east (Inalcik, p. 9679-9680).
Lybyer notes that the distance from Tabriz to Constantinople (Istanbul) posed a strategic problem: “the journey from Vienna to Constantinople was much easier than that from Tabriz to Constantinople,” meaning the Austrians could threaten the capital while the Ottoman army was in the east, whereas the Persians could not (Lybyer, p. 3211-3214).
Role in Trade
Tabriz was the most important silk market on the overland route from Persia. Raw silk from the Caspian provinces of Gilan and Shirvan was brought to Tabriz for sorting, pricing, and onward shipment to the Mediterranean. European merchants, particularly Venetians, maintained commercial agents in Tabriz to purchase silk and other luxury goods. Inalcik records that in the late 15th century “a horse for the journey from Tabriz to Bursa and back could be rented for about nine gold ducats” (Inalcik, p. 6602-6603), indicating the regularity and organization of the caravan trade.
The silk-producing areas of Gilan and Shirvan recognized Ottoman suzerainty after Suleyman’s 1534-35 campaign, giving the Ottomans temporary control over the sources of the finest Persian silk (Inalcik, p. 1747-1748).
Key Connections

Erzurum/Erzincan: Western route into Anatolia
Van/Bitlis/Diyarbekir: Southern route to Aleppo
Qazvin/Isfahan: Eastern route to the Safavid heartland
Khoy/Nakhchivan: Northern route to the Caucasus
Bursa/Istanbul: Long-distance silk route across Anatolia
Baghdad: Southeastern route through the Zagros

entities/thomas-palaiologos.title: Thomas Palaiologos (Thomas Palaiologos, Despot of the Morea)
entities/thomas-palaiologos.content:
Thomas Palaiologos — Thomas Palaiologos, Despot of the Morea (Θωμάς Παλαιολόγος, توماس پالایولوگوس)
