Thomas Palaiologos (1409 – 12 May 1465) was Despot of the Morea (the Peloponnese) from 1449 to 1460, ruling jointly with his elder brother Demetrios Palaiologos. Unlike his brother, Thomas chose resistance over submission. After the Ottoman conquest of the Peloponnese in 1460, he fled to Corcyra and then to Rome, where he spent the remainder of his life seeking Western aid against the Ottomans and surrendering the most sacred relic of the Byzantine world — the head of St. Andrew the Apostle — to the Pope. His life is chronicled in Book III of Kritobulos of Imbros.
Background
Thomas was the youngest son of Emperor Manuel II Palaiologos and Helena Dragaš. His brothers included Emperor John VIII Palaiologos, Constantine XI Palaiologos (the last Byzantine Emperor), Theodore II Palaiologos, and Demetrios Palaiologos. After the death of Theodore II in 1448 and Constantine XI’s elevation to the imperial throne, the Morea was divided between Demetrios (who ruled the western and northern parts including Sparta) and Thomas (who held the eastern territories centered at Mistra).
First Ottoman Invasion (1458)
Mehmed II’s first campaign into the Peloponnese in 1458 was triggered by the despots’ failure to pay tribute. Kritobulos records that the emperor sent envoys demanding payment of the accumulated arrears. When Thomas attempted to negotiate, sending envoys with four thousand five hundred gold pieces as a partial payment and “begging for a treaty and peace” (Book III, §13), Mehmed mockingly took the partial tribute but said “the treaty would be made when he was inside the Peloponnese, thus mocking and ridiculing them for their folly and stupidity” (Book III, §14).
During this campaign, Thomas and Demetrios gathered their armies at Amyclaeion (near Sparta) but, upon learning that Mehmed had advanced to Tegea, “immediately scattered and took to flight. One of them, Thomas, withdrew to Mantinea, where his wife and children were; the other, Demetrios, to barren Epidaurus, which is now called Monemvasia” (Book III, §23). Mehmed conquered much of the Peloponnese in this campaign, including Corinth and Patras, and imposed a heavy tribute and Ottoman garrisons.
Civil War with Demetrios
After Mehmed’s withdrawal, the two brothers fell into open civil war. Kritobulos writes that they “began open war against each other and prosecuted it vigorously, invading each other’s territory with great armies, overrunning and plundering it, setting fire to settlements, capturing fortresses and partly also demolishing them” (Book III, §110).
Thomas proved the more capable commander. “When the Albanians there went over to Despot Thomas, and the most eminent men of Despot Demetrios also daily deserted to Thomas and surrendered the fortresses to him, Thomas became the victor, and having become master of the greater part, he pursued his brother and shut him up with his wife and daughter in barren Epidaurus, which is now called Monemvasia” (Book III, §111).
This victory was short-lived. Demetrios, desperate, sent Asanes to Mehmed II, offering to surrender the Peloponnese in exchange for his own safety. Moreover, Mehmed had learned that Thomas — breaking his treaty with the sultan — “had also seized something from his territory, and was entering into a new treaty with the Italians and calling them to his aid” (Book III, §114). This sealed the Peloponnese’s fate.
The Ottoman Conquest of 1460
Mehmed marched into the Peloponnese with his full army in the spring of 1460. While Demetrios surrendered peacefully, Thomas attempted to resist. Kritobulos records (Book III, §137) that Thomas “as soon as he heard of the emperor’s coming into the Peloponnese, well equipped one of his fortresses and placed a garrison in it, while he himself, together with his wife, went to Mantinea, a small coastal town, and shut himself up there, awaiting the outcome of the war.”
Thomas still hoped that the Peloponnese — “a province very strongly fortified by nature and filled with strong cities and hard-to-capture fortresses” — would not surrender so easily. But as Mehmed’s forces swept through the peninsula, taking fortresses by assault or by surrender, his hope evaporated.
Flight to Corcyra
When Thomas saw that “everything had been captured, and that the cities and fortresses — some taken by assault and arms, others surrendering without any force — had submitted, and that the entire Peloponnese, with the exception of two or three Venetian towns, was almost entirely occupied, then, having completely despaired of his cause, he embarked with his wife, children, and some nobles on two ships and departed for Corcyra” (Book III, §138).
Corcyra (modern Corfu) was a Venetian possession, a natural refuge for a Byzantine prince fleeing the Ottoman advance. Thomas’ partisans in the fortresses and cities, abandoning their posts, also fled to the Venetian towns of Coron and Methoni (Book III, §139).
Surrender of the Head of St. Andrew
From Corcyra, Thomas made his way to Rome, arriving in 1461. He was received by Pope Pius II (Enea Silvio Piccolomini), who had long dreamed of launching a new crusade against the Ottomans. Thomas brought with him the most precious relic in his possession: the head of St. Andrew the Apostle, the patron saint of the Byzantine Empire.
The head of St. Andrew had been preserved at Patras in the Peloponnese. As the last Orthodox ruler of the region, Thomas had taken it into his custody. In Rome, on 11 April 1462, Thomas formally surrendered the relic to Pope Pius II in a solemn ceremony. The head was placed in St. Peter’s Basilica, where it became a major pilgrimage attraction. This act was deeply symbolic: by surrendering the head of the apostle who was the brother of St. Peter (the first Pope), Thomas was enacting a spiritual and political surrender of the Eastern Church to the Western — a gesture of submission that the Papacy had sought since the Great Schism.
Pius II, in his Commentaries, describes Thomas as a man who had sacrificed his principality for the faith and who now lived in poverty in Rome, sustained by a papal pension of 300 ducats per month.
Exile in Rome and Death
Thomas lived in Rome for the remainder of his life, maintaining the title of Despot of the Morea and working tirelessly to organize a crusade. In 1463, Pius II formally proclaimed a crusade against the Ottomans, but the death of the pope at Ancona in 1464 ended any realistic hope of Western military intervention.
Thomas died in Rome on 12 May 1465. He was buried in St. Peter’s Basilica, a rare honor for a Byzantine prince. His epitaph proclaimed him “Thomas Palaeologus, Despot of the Peloponnese, who after the loss of his kingdom fled to the Apostolic See.” His remains were later moved to the Vatican Grottoes.
Family and Legacy
Thomas was married to Catherine Zaccaria (also known as Caterina), daughter of Centurione II Zaccaria, the last Prince of Achaea. They had two sons and two daughters:

Andrew Palaiologos (1453–1502) — inherited the title of Despot and claimed the Byzantine imperial title, living in Rome and eventually selling his claims to Charles VIII of France and later to Ferdinand and Isabella of Spain
Manuel Palaiologos (1455–1512) — returned to Constantinople and converted to Islam, living as a pensioner of Mehmed II under the name “Mehmed Pasha”
Sophia (Zoe) Palaiologina (c. 1449–1503) — married Ivan III of Russia in 1472, bringing Byzantine court ceremonial and the double-headed eagle to Moscow; through her, the Palaiologan claim passed to the Russian tsars, who adopted the ideology of “Moscow, the Third Rome”
Helena Palaiologina (c. 1442–1473) — married Lazar Branković of Serbia

In Kritobulos’ account, Thomas appears as the more warlike of the two Morean despots — the brother who fought, who sought Western aid, and who would not accept Ottoman suzerainty. His flight to Rome and his surrender of St. Andrew’s head were not merely acts of political exile but profoundly symbolic gestures that shaped the way the fall of the Byzantine Empire was understood in the West. Through his daughter Sophia, Thomas’ bloodline influenced the history of Russia, making him in a sense the ancestor of the Russian imperial claim and the ideology of Moscow as the Third Rome.

entities/trebizond.title: Trebizond (Trabzon)
entities/trebizond.content:
Trebizond (طرابزون / Trabzon)
Trebizond (Ottoman Turkish: طرابزون, Trabzon; Greek: Trebizous) is a historic port city on the southeastern coast of the Black Sea, in northeastern Anatolia. It was the capital of the Empire of Trebizond (1204-1461), a Byzantine successor state ruled by the Komnenos dynasty, before its conquest by Sultan Mehmed II. Under Ottoman rule, Trebizond became an important provincial capital and a key node in the Black Sea trading network.
Geography and Strategic Position
Trebizond lies at approximately 41.0° N, 39.7° E, on a narrow coastal plain between the Pontic Mountains and the Black Sea. The city commanded the natural harbor at the terminus of the Zigana Pass, the principal route from the Black Sea coast across the Pontic Alps into the interior of eastern Anatolia and Persia. This position made it the traditional northern outlet for Persian and Central Asian trade: “Merchants from the Italian maritime states now met the caravans from the far east and Iran not only at Ayas in the south and Trebizond in the north” (Inalcik, p. 5246-5247). A branch of the Mongol-era imperial highway from Sivas to Constantinople “competed with the sea route to Constantinople from Trebizond” (Inalcik, p. 5252-5253).
Under Ottoman Rule
The Empire of Trebizond was the last major Byzantine Greek state to fall to the Ottomans. In 1454, two years after the conquest of Constantinople, Mehmed II sent the Ottoman fleet into the Black Sea, compelling the “Comnene kingdom of Trebizond” to pay tribute (Inalcik, p. 1232-1233). The final conquest came in 1461, when Mehmed II personally led a combined land and sea campaign to take the city. Unlike other deposed rulers whom he pensioned, Mehmed eliminated the Komnenoi entirely, considering them dangerous (Inalcik, p. 1246-1247).
Trebizond became the capital of an Ottoman sanjak (later eyalet) and was typically assigned as an appanage for imperial princes. During the late 15th and early 16th centuries, the future Sultan Selim I served as governor of Trebizond, using it as a base for frontier operations against the Safavids and the Georgian principalities. Fisher records that the fiery Selim “prepared an army to resist the Persians from Trebizond” (Fisher, p. 4229), and that Shah Ismail later protested “the warlike action of Selim on the frontier of Trebizond” (Fisher, p. 4294-4295).
Trebizond was one of the cities where Ottoman princes held their courts alongside Konya, Manisa, Amasya, Kastamuni, and Kaffa (Fisher, p. 4749-4751). During the Venetian-Ottoman wars, the treaty of 1503 expressly permitted Venetian merchants to travel to “Istanbul, Pera, Trebizond, Kaffa, and pass the Straits” (Fisher, p. 3817-3818).
Role in Trade
Trebizond was the principal Black Sea terminus of the Persian and Central Asian silk routes before the Ottoman conquest shifted the flows westward to Bursa. Inalcik notes that after Bayezid I extended Ottoman dominions eastwards to Erzincan, “Persian silk caravans no longer followed the route through Trebizond, preferring to go overland to Bursa” (Inalcik, p. 5279-5281). Nevertheless, Trebizond remained an active port throughout the Ottoman period.
The city shipped local products including wine, arak, hazel-nuts, and ships’ masts to Caffa and other Black Sea ports (Inalcik, p. 5589-5590). During the Persian wars, “great quantities of grain were shipped to Trebizond from the ports on the Danube, and iron balls from Kigi near Erzurum were sent from Trebizond across the Black Sea and up the Danube to the Hungarian battlefields” (Inalcik, p. 6011-6014), demonstrating the city’s military logistical importance.
Key Connections

Erzurum: Inland route through the Zigana Pass into eastern Anatolia
Caffa (Kefe): Maritime trade across the Black Sea to the Crimea
Sinop/Istanbul: Coastal shipping westward along the Black Sea
Tabriz: Overland route through Erzurum into Persia
Giresun/Batumi: Coastal towns along the eastern Black Sea littoral
The Pontic hinterland: Supplied metals, timber, and hazelnuts for export

entities/tunis.title: Tunis (Tunus)
entities/tunis.content:
Tunis (تونس / Tunus)
Tunis (Ottoman Turkish: تونس, Tunus) is the historic capital of Tunisia (Ifriqiya), located on the Mediterranean coast of North Africa near the site of ancient Carthage. It sits at the western end of a lagoon (Lake Tunis) connected to the Mediterranean by a narrow channel, with its outport at La Goulette (Halk al-Wadi). Under the Ottomans, Tunis became one of the three regencies of the Barbary Coast, governed by a pasha appointed from Istanbul but increasingly dominated by local Janissary officers (deys) and corsair admirals.
Geography and Strategic Position
Tunis lies at approximately 36.8° N, 10.2° E, at the head of the Gulf of Tunis. The city is strategically positioned at the narrowest point of the Mediterranean, roughly equidistant between the Strait of Gibraltar and the Levant. It commands the sea lanes of the central Mediterranean and serves as the natural outlet for the agricultural and mineral wealth of the interior Maghreb. The city was protected by fortifications including the Kasbah and the marine fortress at La Goulette (Halk al-Wadi), which guarded the approach to the harbor.
Under Ottoman Rule
The Ottoman presence in Tunis began in 1534 when the corsair-admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa conquered the city, expelling the Hafsid ruler who had been a Spanish client. Inalcik records “Barbarossa’s conquest of Tunis (August)” in 1533 (Inalcik, p. 9508-9510). However, Charles V of Spain counterattacked and “captured Tunis in 1535” (Inalcik, p. 9516; also p. 1666), installing a Hafsid vassal. Inalcik notes further that “Uluç Alî captures Tunis (January)” in 1570 (Inalcik, p. 9602), this time permanently incorporating the city into the Ottoman Empire.
Tunis was organized as a regency (beylik) under an Ottoman pasha, but real power quickly passed to local military corps. Inalcik notes that “Tripoli, Tunisia and Algeria preserved their original character as frontier provinces” (Inalcik, p. 4665-4666). In the later 16th and 17th centuries, the corsair admirals and the Janissary deys exercised effective control, while the sultan’s authority became largely nominal. After the defeat at Lepanto, “naval forces from Tripoli, Tunis and Algiers no longer formed a regular part of the sultan’s fleets, but became corsairs acting on their own initiative” (Inalcik, p. 1964-1966).
Already before the Ottoman conquest, Tunis had sought Ottoman aid against Spanish pressure. Fisher notes that “Tunis, attacked by Spain, sent an ambassador to obtain aid from Bayezid” in the 1490s (Fisher, p. 1656), and that the Ottoman admiral Kemal Reis was sent “with some ships to Tunis” in 1505-1506 (Fisher, p. 4663-4664). The Tunisian ambassador helped broker the Ottoman-Egyptian rapprochement of 1497 (Fisher, p. 1658-1664).
Role in Trade
Tunis was the major entrepot for trade between the Maghreb interior and Christian Europe. The city exported olive oil, wheat, wool, leather, coral, and dates, while importing European textiles, metals, and manufactured goods. Under Ottoman rule, corsair activity (privateering against Christian shipping) became a major economic sector, supplying slaves and captured goods that were traded in the markets of Tunis. The foreign relations of Tunis continued throughout the Ottoman period; the French consul at Tunis, along with those in Algiers and Morocco, was part of an extensive network of European diplomatic representation in the Barbary states (Fisher, p. 5239-5241).
Key Connections

Algiers: Western Barbary regency; fellow Ottoman vassal and corsair ally
Tripoli: Eastern Barbary regency
Istanbul: Ottoman capital; nominal suzerain
La Goulette (Halk al-Wadi): Outport and fortress protecting the harbor
Sicily/Malta: Christian naval powers in the central Mediterranean
Spain: Chief European adversary during the 16th century
Carthage/Kairouan: Ancient cities of the Tunisian interior

entities/uzun-hasan.title: Uzun Hasan
entities/uzun-hasan.content:
Uzun Hasan
Overview
Uzun Hasan (c. 1423 — 1478) was the ruler of the Aq Qoyunlu (White Sheep Turkoman) confederation. His reign marked the height of Aq Qoyunlu power in eastern Anatolia, Persia, and Mesopotamia. To the historians and traders of Europe and the Ottoman Empire, he was a figure of great consequence — a powerful sovereign who allied with the Empire of Trebizond through his marriage to Theodora Komnene, fought Mehmed II, and sought alliances with Venice and other European states against the rising Ottoman power.
Rise to Power
Uzun Hasan succeeded his father, Kara Yuluk Osman, as leader of the Aq Qoyunlu around 1453. The Aq Qoyunlu were a Turkoman tribal confederation that controlled much of eastern Anatolia and northwestern Iran. Under Uzun Hasan’s leadership, the confederation expanded its territory, defeating the rival Qara Qoyunlu (Black Sheep Turkoman) under Jahan Shah in 1468.
Relations with the Empire of Trebizond
Uzun Hasan’s marriage to Theodora Komnene, the daughter of Emperor John IV of Trebizond, established a crucial alliance between the Aq Qoyunlu and the last Greek Christian empire in Anatolia. This alliance was central to the political dynamics of the region in the mid-15th century, as Trebizond sought protection against the expansionist policies of Mehmed II after the Fall of Constantinople in 1453.
Through this marriage, Uzun Hasan became a key figure in European hopes for an anti-Ottoman coalition. The Venetians, in particular, cultivated relations with him, seeing in the powerful Turkoman ruler a potential ally who could attack the Ottoman Empire from the east while European forces engaged from the west.
Conflict with Mehmed II
The alliance between Uzun Hasan and Trebizond was tested when Mehmed II marched on Trebizond in 1461. Despite Uzun Hasan’s promises of assistance, the campaign ended with the surrender of Trebizond and the absorption of the empire into Ottoman territory. Kritoboulos of Imbros records that Uzun Hasan, upon hearing of the fall of Trebizond, sent envoys to Mehmed II, seeking peace and acknowledging Ottoman supremacy.
The conflict between Uzun Hasan and Mehmed II continued for over a decade. Uzun Hasan entered into correspondence with Venice, signing treaties and receiving ambassadors. The Venetian Senate sent military supplies and advisors, but the promised full-scale European intervention never materialized in time.
The decisive confrontation came at the Battle of Otlukbeli (1473), where the Ottoman army, now equipped with advanced firearms and artillery, decisively defeated the Aq Qoyunlu forces. The battle was a turning point — Uzun Hasan’s son Mirza Zeynelabidin was killed in the fighting, and another son, Mirza Ugurlu Mehmed, fled the field.
Later Years and Legacy
After Otlukbeli, Uzun Hasan’s power declined. He died in 1478, and the Aq Qoyunlu confederation gradually fragmented after his death, eventually falling to the Safavid dynasty under Shah Ismail I in the early 16th century.
Uzun Hasan is remembered in both Ottoman and European sources as a powerful and ambitious ruler who successfully built a large empire in the fluid political landscape of post-Timurid western Asia. His willingness to engage with Christian powers, his marriage alliance with Trebizond, and his ultimately unsuccessful resistance to Ottoman expansion made him a figure of lasting historical interest.
Key Events

1453 — Succeeds to leadership of Aq Qoyunlu
1458 — Marriage to Theodora Komnene of Trebizond
1461 — Trebizond falls to Mehmed II despite Uzun Hasan’s alliance
1468 — Defeats Qara Qoyunlu under Jahan Shah
1472 — Venetian alliance formalized
1473 — Battle of Otlukbeli
1478 — Death of Uzun Hasan

Assessment by Kritoboulos
Kritoboulos of Imbros, writing from the Ottoman perspective, describes Uzun Hasan as a powerful eastern ruler whose submission to Mehmed II was a matter of political necessity rather than genuine allegiance. In his account of the Trebizond campaign, Kritoboulos emphasizes Uzun Hasan’s initial promises to aid the Emperor of Trebizond, his later submission to Mehmed II, and the ultimately inconclusive nature of their early interactions.
Notes

Some accounts place Uzun Hasan’s birth around 1423, though precise dating is difficult

entities/venice.title: Venice (Venedik)
entities/venice.content:
Venice — ونديك (Venedik)
Coordinates: 45°26’15”N 12°18’57”E
Venice (Ottoman Venedik, ونديك) was the Ottoman Empire’s most formidable European rival and its most persistent commercial partner — the maritime republic whose galleys dominated the eastern Mediterranean and whose merchants penetrated every corner of the Levantine trade. The relationship between the Serenissima Repubblica and the Porte was one of perpetual tension: cycles of devastating naval wars punctuated by commercial treaties that recognized the mutual dependence of the two great Mediterranean powers.
Geography and Strategic Position
Venice occupies a group of 118 small islands in the marshy Venetian Lagoon at the northern end of the Adriatic Sea. This singular geography — a city built on water, protected by the lagoon from landward attack, and oriented entirely toward the sea — made it the supreme maritime power of the Mediterranean. From the Arsenal, the largest industrial complex in pre-modern Europe, Venice launched the fleets that controlled the Adriatic, the Ionian Sea, and the Aegean.
Venice’s empire (the Stato da Mar) consisted not of contiguous territory but of a chain of naval bases, islands, and coastal fortresses that stretched from the Adriatic to Cyprus and Crete. These included the key bases of Modon and Coron in the Morea, Negroponte (Euboea), Crete (Candia), Cyprus (from 1489), and the islands of the Cyclades and Ionian Sea. This network allowed Venice to control the trade routes of the eastern Mediterranean and to project naval power against the Ottomans.
Venetian-Ottoman Relations in the Fifteenth Century
The first major Ottoman-Venetian war (1463–1479) arose from competition in the Morea and the Aegean after Mehmed II’s conquest of Constantinople. The Ottomans seized Negroponte in 1470 — a blow from which the Venetian Aegean position never fully recovered — and raided the Friuli in northern Italy. The peace of 1479 left Venice in possession of Crete and some strongholds but forced the republic to pay an annual tribute and accept Ottoman supremacy in the Aegean.
During Bayezid II’s reign (1481–1512), relations with Venice were central to Ottoman foreign policy. The civil war between Bayezid and his brother Cem Sultan gave Venice diplomatic leverage, as Cem was held by European powers. The Ottoman fleet was rebuilt, and the Second Ottoman-Venetian War (1499–1503) saw the Ottomans take Lepanto (Naupaktos), Modon, Coron, and Navarino from Venice, establishing direct Ottoman control over the approaches to the Morea. The battle of Zonchio (1499) and the battle of Modon (1500) were among the first naval engagements in which the Ottomans deployed large cannon on ships.
Venetian diplomacy during this period was conducted through the bailo — the permanent Venetian ambassador in Istanbul, who served as both diplomat and intelligence officer. The Venetians maintained a sophisticated network of informants throughout the Ottoman Empire, and the reports of the baili (preserved in the Sanuto diaries and elsewhere) provide some of the most detailed contemporary accounts of Ottoman politics and society.
Economic Interdependence
Despite the wars, Venetian trade with the Ottoman Empire was the lifeblood of both economies. Venice imported raw silk from Bursa and Iran, cotton from Anatolia and Syria, wheat from Thrace and the Black Sea, spices from Egypt and the Levant, and slaves from the Black Sea region. In return, Venice exported finished woolen cloth, glass, soap, paper, and silver to Ottoman markets. The grain trade was especially important: Venetian ships carried wheat from Salonica and the Black Sea ports to feed the population of the lagoon.
The Venetian ducat was the standard currency of Mediterranean trade, and Ottoman tax registers frequently valued revenues in Venetian ducats. The customs duties collected on Venetian trade formed a significant part of both the Ottoman imperial treasury and the Venetian government’s revenues.
Key Connections
Venice’s primary Ottoman counterpart was Istanbul, where the bailo maintained a permanent embassy and where Venetian merchants occupied their own quarter. The Venetian-Ottoman struggle for control of the Morea and the Aegean islands — including Negroponte (Euboea), Modon, Coron, and Lepanto — defined the military geography of the fifteenth-century eastern Mediterranean. The republic’s rivalry with Genoa, its Italian maritime competitor, often intersected with Ottoman policy. The peace of 1503, which ended the second war, established a framework that lasted until the Ottoman conquest of Cyprus in 1571, and the two powers remained locked in a relationship that was part commercial partnership, part naval cold war.

entities/vlad-the-impaler.title: Vlad III Drăculea (Vlad the Impaler, Kazıklı Voyvoda)
entities/vlad-the-impaler.content:
Vlad III Drăculea — Vlad the Impaler (Kazıklı Voyvoda)
Vlad III, known as Vlad Drăculea (Vlad the Dragon’s Son) and universally called Vlad the Impaler (Kazıklı Voyvoda, قازیقلی وایووده, “the Staked Voivode”), was the voyvoda (وایووده, prince) of Wallachia whose conflict with Mehmed II in 1462 produced one of the defining military confrontations of the Conqueror’s reign. Both Tursun Beg and Kritobulos devote extended passages to his rebellion, the Ottoman campaign against him, and his spectacular — though ultimately doomed — defiance of the sultan.
Background and Rise to Power
Vlad was the son of Vlad II Dracul, a voyvoda who had been invested by the Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund. The young Vlad and his younger brother Radu were sent as hostages to the Ottoman court after their father accepted Ottoman suzerainty — a common practice for securing the loyalty of vassal princes. Kritobulos records that when John Hunyadi marched against their father and killed him, the two brothers fled to Sultan Murad II, who “received them, and, while they were still children, had them raised with great honor in the imperial palace, and at his death left them to his son the Emperor.” Mehmed II continued this arrangement, raising the brothers “beside himself with great humanity, honor, and imperial provision.” When the situation in Wallachia shifted, Mehmed intervened and installed Vlad “with a great army and at great expense, delivering to him the entire principality of the Getae, and binding him by agreement and oath.”
Character and Methods of Rule
Tursun Beg introduces Vlad with unmistakable hostility: “The Impaler Voivode was the governor of the Wallachian province, a blood-drinking monstrous infidel.” He compares Vlad to Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, the notoriously brutal Umayyad governor of Iraq, writing: “His tyranny and cruelty were at an extremely wicked level.” The scale of Vlad’s impaling was legendary even in its own time. Tursun Beg describes the voivode’s seat at Ağaçhisarı: “The area six miles across from it, he enclosed with a double-armed fence and, saying ‘I will make a garden,’ covered it with thick brush. Between these two fences, he filled it with impaled people — from Hungarian infidels, from the infidels of his own province, and from the infidels of the Moldavian province. Moreover, the location of his own castle is also a wooded and fenced place. On every branch of every tree there are countless corpses strung up.” So absolute was this law of terror that “whoever took down one of the hanged would immediately be put up in its place.”
Despite this savagery from the Ottoman perspective, Tursun Beg acknowledges that Vlad had been militarily effective: “Despite being so evil-doing, he had defeated the Hungarian folk, broken them severely, and also made other peers and equals bleed, achieving superiority.” This success bred pride, and Vlad began to consider rebellion.
The Breach with the Devlet (دولت, the Ottoman State)
The immediate catalyst for conflict was Vlad’s refusal to pay the annual haraç (خراج, tribute). While Mehmed II was on campaign against Trebizond in 1461, Vlad judged the opportunity ripe. Tursun Beg writes: “Upon returning from the Trabzon campaign, the accumulated tribute from the infidel for that year was demanded. His answer was: ‘This year I have been very busy with my surroundings and have paid considerable tribute to Hungary. Therefore I have no means to give the tribute to the Ottomans.’ So he neither came nor paid his tribute.”
But the defiance went far beyond words. Kritobulos records that Vlad “first, secretly crossing the Danube with no small force, he raided the Emperor’s neighboring province, namely the region of Nicopolis and Vidin, and after collecting vast booty and committing no small slaughter, recrossed and returned to his own country.” Mehmed, still seeking a diplomatic resolution, sent envoys to Vlad “to remind him of the treaty, to confer peacefully with him, and to inquire into the reasons for his defection.” The response was a direct challenge: Vlad “seized them and had them impaled, also threatening the Emperor with many curses.”
The Ottoman Campaign of 1462
Mehmed crossed the Danube with a vast army — Kritobulos records that he “overran nearly the entire country of the Getae, ravaging, devastating, overturning and scattering everything as he encountered it like a storm, seizing the fortresses, plundering the villages, and carrying off countless booty.” The gaziler (غازی لر, holy warriors) advanced under brutal conditions: Tursun records that “the heat was at such a level that one could roast kebab on the steel armor worn by the ghazis.”
Vlad had deployed strategic defenses, placing his beylerbeyi (بكلربكى, governor-general) with seven thousand select troops to protect his country from the direction of Moldavia. The lack of water drove the Ottoman army forward directly onto these positions. What followed was a decisive Ottoman victory: Mahmud Pasha arranged the regiments in perfect order, and when the Wallachians emerged from the forest they saw the formations and “the fire of terror fell into his heart.” The gaziler pursued, “slicing them like slicing cucumbers with the sword.” Of seven thousand Wallachians, only seven hundred remained. For approximately thirty days the Ottoman army surged through Wallachia, taking “much spoil, property, and many captives.”
The Night Attack
As the army withdrew to rest, Vlad — now cornered and desperate — launched his most famous tactical stroke: a night raid on the Ottoman camp. Tursun Beg describes him “like a moth circling around the camp.” Kritobulos provides the psychological portrait of a man with nothing left to lose: “Drakul, now utterly despairing of his cause and having no further hope of ruling, resolved upon death, that he might perish himself while doing some harm to his enemies.”
Tursun Beg’s account is the more dramatic: “One night, he fell upon the imperial army like a drop falling into the sea. First he struck the Anadolu askeri (Anatolian troops). He received a slap befitting his state and was scattered. Those who could flee fell upon the wrath of the Gate-people and the Janissaries. Here too they found no victory and fell, wounded and broken.” The decisive factor was confusion in the darkness: “But in the darkness of night, mistaking the place where the army crowd was for a path of escape, they marched there. This confusion became their destruction, for it brought them and dropped them among the Rumelian troops. In every tent, candles and lamps were burning. Here and there, heroic guard sentries waiting motionless shouted war cries. The infidels, stunned, became like water flowing in a rocky stream. They lost one another and were scattered.”
Kritobulos adds that Mehmed had anticipated the attack and ordered his soldiers to withdraw to trap Vlad. The wounded Vlad escaped with a few survivors.
Flight to Hungary, Imprisonment, and Death
Both sources agree on the aftermath. Tursun Beg records that Vlad’s forces were divided into three groups: “One group’s heads were separated from their bodies… one group fell captive… the third group were those who escaped from the field. They too went out with their commander and fled to Hungary.” Kritobulos writes that the Hungarians “seized him and cast him into prison.” Tursun Beg adds a note of grim irony: “He had previously done much harm to Hungary. He went of his own foot and entered his own trap.” Vlad was eventually released and briefly restored to the Wallachian throne in 1476 but was killed in battle later that year.
Radu the Fair Installed
With Vlad eliminated, Mehmed installed his brother Radu (called “Radu the Fair” or “Radu cel Frumos”) as the Ottoman vassal voyvoda of Wallachia. Tursun Beg writes: “His Majesty the Padishah gave the administration of this country to the previous Voivode Vlad’s brother, Radu Voivode, whose fortune was accepted and who spent many years of his life in the service of the state. Because of the excellence of his service, he was also granted a banner, a sword, and a belt” — the traditional Ottoman insignia of vassalage.
Legacy
Vlad the Impaler’s rebellion of 1462 was the most serious challenge to Ottoman authority in the Danubian principalities during Mehmed II’s reign. His use of terror as a weapon made him a figure of lasting infamy in Ottoman historiography. In Ottoman chronicles he is remembered as Kazıklı Voyvoda, the Staked Voivode, his name forever linked to the punishment he inflicted on others. Tursun Beg’s judgment is characteristically blunt: the Impaler Voivode was a warning of what befell those who broke faith with the sultan. Kritobulos, writing in a more classical mode, treats Vlad as a cautionary example of ingratitude and reckless ambition — a prince raised by imperial favor who repaid that favor with treachery, and who paid for it with his throne and his freedom.

entities/zaganos-pasha.title: Zağanos Pasha (Zagan Pasha)
entities/zaganos-pasha.content:
Zağanos Pasha — Zagan Pasha (ضاگانوس پاشا)
Zağanos Pasha (d. 1469), also known as Zagan Pasha, was one of Mehmed II’s most trusted commanders and viziers, playing a decisive role in the conquest of Constantinople in 1453. A devşirme (دوشیرمه, child levy) recruit of Albanian or Greek origin, he rose through the ranks under Murad II to become one of the most eminent men of the Ottoman court.
Early Career
Zağanos was recruited through the devşirme system and trained in the Janissary corps. He rose under Sultan Murad II, distinguishing himself in military service. He married a daughter of Murad II, cementing his place within the Ottoman dynastic household.
The Conquest of Constantinople
Zağanos was among Mehmed II’s closest military advisers during the 1453 siege of Constantinople. When the sultan walked around the landward side of the city to examine the walls and determine where they were weakest, Kritoboulos records that Mehmed took “Zaganos and Halil with him, the most eminent men of his entourage” (Book I, §116).
During the siege, Mehmed divided the assault on the city among his commanders. Zağanos was entrusted with the assault on Galata and the entire area of the Golden Horn:

“Dividing the entire city and wall on the land and sea sides into sections, he entrusts to Zaganos, together with some other troop commanders under him, the assault on Galata and the entire area in that direction, including the Golden Horn and the whole harbor” (Book I, §117).

Mehmed ordered him to bridge the Golden Horn strait — a crucial tactical innovation that allowed Ottoman troops to attack the city from multiple sides. On the night of the final assault, the sultan commanded:

“And you, Zaganos, crossing the bridge quickly, assault with great force the wall along the Golden Horn, taking with you also the ships in the harbor, so that they may support you in the work, and be a worthy man” (Book I, §207).

Zağanos advanced boldly over the bridge, bringing ladders and bridges to the wall, and attempted to have the heavy infantry climb by force, working in concert with the archers of the ships in the harbors (Book I, §223). After the city fell, the people of Galata submitted — “opening their gates, they received Zaganos with his army” (Book I, §253). Zağanos had been one of the voices advocating for the final assault against the caution of Halil Pasha.
Governor of Gallipoli and Fleet Commander
Following the conquest, Mehmed appointed Zağanos as governor of Callipolis (Gallipoli) and commander of the entire fleet. In this capacity he was dispatched to the Aegean islands:

“He sent Zaganos, the governor of Callipolis and commander of the entire fleet, with forty ships to the islands; and he, going there, moved the inhabitants of Thasos and Samothrace and settled them there” (Book V, §103).

This was part of Mehmed’s program to repopulate Constantinople after the conquest.
Dismissal After Belgrade
After the failed siege of Belgrade in 1456 — where the Ottomans were defeated by John Hunyadi’s Hungarian forces — Mehmed dismissed several of his senior commanders. Kritoboulos records:

“Then, a few days later, he also removes Zaganos from his office and rank, dismissing both himself and his daughter, whom he had only recently taken as wife, and sends him together with his daughter to Asia, giving them sufficient land for their sustenance” (Book I, §302).

In his place Mehmed installed Mahmud Pasha, his son-in-law through another daughter.
Later Career
Zağanos was later restored to favor. He served in the Peloponnese campaigns and continued to hold positions of authority. He died in 1469.
Family
Zağanos married first a daughter of Sultan Murad II, then a daughter of Mehmed II. The second marriage is noted by Kritoboulos as having occurred shortly before his dismissal — Mehmed sent away “both himself and his daughter, whom he had only recently taken as wife” (Book I, §302).
Legacy
Zağanos Pasha was one of the key military architects of the Constantinople conquest. His advocacy for the final assault, his command of the fleet, and his role in bridging the Golden Horn were critical to the Ottoman victory. The mosque complex bearing his name (Zağanos Pasha Camii) in Balıkesir and his tomb in Ankara remain as physical memorials.

events/conquest-of-bosnia.title: Conquest of Bosnia (1463)
events/conquest-of-bosnia.content:
Conquest of Bosnia (1463)
The Ottoman conquest of the Kingdom of Bosnia in 1463 was a decisive campaign by Sultan Mehmed II that destroyed the independent Bosnian kingdom, executed its last king, Stephen Tomašević, and brought nearly three hundred fortresses under Ottoman control. The campaign is narrated in detail by Kritobulos of Imbros in Book IV of his history of Mehmed II.
Background: The Bosnian Kingdom
The Kingdom of Bosnia, referred to by Kritobulos as the land of the “Paeonians dwelling within the Sava river, whom they also call Dalmatians and, in more recent times, Bosnians” (Section 95), was a substantial realm with extensive pastures, arable land, and natural defenses of vast forests, impassable mountains, and strong fortresses. The Bosnians maintained a firm alliance with the King of Hungary (the “Paeonians” in Kritobulos’ terminology), pledging mutual aid against any attacker.
Despite repeated Ottoman demands, the Bosnian kingdom refused to enter into any treaty with Mehmed II, pay annual tribute (as did their neighbors the Illyrians and Triballi/Serbs), or accept any form of vassalage. As Kritobulos writes, “they wished to remain independent, free, and completely autonomous” (Section 96). Mehmed had sent frequent summons for them to come to terms, but the Bosnians refused, trusting in their mountainous terrain and strong fortresses.
This refusal provoked repeated Ottoman punitive raids. Mehmed “frequently sent armies against them, and ravaged their country, overrunning it and carrying off countless men, women, children, and cattle as booty” (Section 97). Yet the Bosnians persisted in their defiance.
The Campaign of 1463
Angered by the Bosnian intransigence, Mehmed II prepared a major campaign in the winter of 1462-63. He assembled vast numbers of cavalry and infantry, along with all kinds of cannons and siege weapons. At the opening of spring 1463, he marched from Edirne (Adrianople) with his entire army.
The Ottoman advance was swift. Mehmed “quickly traversed his own realm” and upon reaching the Bosnian border, launched a lightning campaign, “destroying and devastating everything with fire and sword, capturing fortresses and placing garrisons in some, taking booty, and conquering everything that lay in his path, without anyone offering resistance” (Section 99). Within a few days, the greater part of the country was overrun.
The Siege and Capture of Jajce
King Stephen Tomašević had fled to the royal fortress of Jajce (,,Jajce” in the local tongue), which Kritobulos describes as “a very strong place.” Mehmed arrived before Jajce, surrounded it entirely, and offered the citizens terms of surrender. When they refused, he “palisaded the city on all sides, surrounded it with his camp, set up the cannons, and besieged it” (Section 101).
The Ottoman artillery quickly breached the walls. Kritobulos records that “within a few days he had brought down and demolished a large part of the wall with the cannons” (Section 101). Seeing the breach and fearing a full assault, the citizens secretly offered to surrender the city — without their king’s knowledge.
King Stephen Tomašević, perceiving the impending betrayal, fled the city by night in concealment. However, “the camp guards, however, recognizing him, pursued him vigorously, and overtaking him, took him prisoner and brought him to the Emperor; and the Emperor had him put to death immediately” (Section 103). With the king executed, the citizens surrendered Jajce.
Mehmed treated the population graciously, returning the city to them on condition of annual tribute, and entered the city to inspect it personally. He recognized Jajce’s strategic value: “it has a favorable situation, being neighboring to the country of the Paeonians (Hungarians), and being able to accommodate sufficient troops, it could be of great harm to them” (Section 105). He placed a strong garrison in Jajce under the command of one of his relatives.
The Submission of Three Hundred Fortresses
After securing Jajce, Mehmed turned to the remainder of the kingdom. Kritobulos reports that he “captured nearly three hundred fortresses. Of their leaders, he captured four” (Section 105). The entire Kingdom of Bosnia was thus subjugated in a single campaigning season.
Having settled affairs in Bosnia — placing garrisons and commanders in Jajce and the other fortresses, leaving a capable governor with sufficient troops — Mehmed returned to Edirne toward the end of autumn 1463, bringing vast booty. The year 6971 (1463 AD), the thirteenth year of his reign, came to an end.
The Hungarian Counterattack and Fall of Jajce
The Hungarian King Matthias Corvinus (referred to by Kritobulos as the “king of the Paeonians and Dacians”) viewed the Ottoman occupation of Bosnia as a direct threat to his own realm. During the winter following Mehmed’s departure, Matthias marched into Bosnia to seize back the province, especially Jajce. As Kritobulos writes, “it seemed to him a terrible thing, and a great ruin and manifest dissolution of his own kingdom and realm, that the emperor should hold this province” (Section 15, Book V).
Matthias succeeded in causing the greater part of the fortresses to revolt from the Ottomans, “partly drawing them to himself by fair words and agreement, partly by intimidation and threats” (Section 16, Book V). Jajce itself fell to the Hungarians when “those within had risen up, were killing each other, and voluntarily joined him” (Section 16). Matthias placed strong garrisons of armed and warlike men in Jajce and the recovered fortresses before returning to Hungary.
The Second Siege of Jajce (1464)
Mehmed II, upon learning of the Hungarian counterattack, took the matter ill and immediately prepared for a second campaign. During the winter he gathered as large an army as possible, along with cannons, mortars, iron, and copper. At the coming of spring 1464, he marched from Edirne back into Bosnia, determined first to retake Jajce.
Mehmed pitched camp before Jajce and first attempted negotiations, offering oaths in exchange for surrender. When the defenders refused, he laid waste to the surrounding area, dug a ditch around the fortress, set up his cannons, and began the siege. Within a few days his artillery “broke down and demolished the stone wall with the cannons” (Section 20, Book V).
First Assault and Repulse
Judging the breach sufficient, Mehmed prepared a full assault. He stationed himself at the breach with his household troops — “the best heavy infantry, archers, slingers, and especially musketeers” (Section 21, Book V). At the given signal, the soldiers rushed the breach with immense clamor. The Hungarian defenders met them with equal ferocity.
Kritobulos provides a vivid account of the struggle: “sometimes the heavy infantry prevailing gained the upper hand over the Hungarians and climbed up onto the wall; sometimes, being thrust back by the Hungarians, they were violently driven out; and not a few perished there, fighting even as they fell” (Section 22, Book V). The fighting continued for a considerable time, but the Hungarians gained the upper hand. Seeing his men suffering heavy losses, including many noble and brave men from his own bodyguard, Mehmed gave the signal to withdraw.
Second Assault and Repulse
After a few days of rest, Mehmed ordered a second assault. He himself went in the foremost ranks, proclaiming “very many and very great rewards, honors and considerable favors for those who should first scale the walls” (Section 24, Book V), and granted all the possessions of the townspeople to his soldiers for plunder.
The soldiers attacked with terrible shouts, using scaling ladders, rope ladders, spikes driven into the wall, and every possible means to get in. The emperor was present, watching each man’s assault. The Hungarians, however, “stood against them very valiantly, with great courage, fervor and speed” (Section 26, Book V). The struggle became so fierce that Kritobulos says the men “almost casting off human nature, they hacked and hewed each other mercilessly” (Section 26, Book V).
Many of the imperial bodyguard fell, fighting in the emperor’s presence. The Hungarians, fighting from above with ample missiles, prevailed everywhere. Seeing his men falling in great numbers and that the city could only be taken by prolonged blockade and hunger, Mehmed again withdrew his army.
Mahmud Pasha’s Defeat of King Matthias Corvinus
While Mehmed maintained the siege, news arrived that King Matthias Corvinus had gathered a great army and was marching to relieve Jajce. Matthias planned either to draw the emperor into open battle or to split the Ottoman forces. Mehmed decided to remain before Jajce and continue the siege, sending his grand vizier Mahmud Pasha (,,Mahmut Pasha”) with sufficient cavalry and infantry, including a considerable portion of his own bodyguard, to meet the Hungarian king.
Mahmud advanced swiftly and encamped near the Hungarian army, with the river Erigon (called ,,Brunosz” or Crna in the local tongue) separating the two forces. Mahmud wished to cross and engage, but Mehmed sent word forbidding it, saying: “There is no need at all for it, when you now hold the crossing of the river in your hands; it is sufficient if you remain there and keep an eye on what the enemy is doing” (Section 33, Book V).
When Matthias learned that Mehmed would not raise the siege and that Mahmud lay in ambush with a large army, he despaired of relieving Jajce. He secretly sent word to the city to hold out, claiming the emperor would soon withdraw. However, Matthias himself, setting fire to his camp by night, decamped and withdrew with his army.
Perceiving the retreat, Mahmud crossed the river hastily and pursued with great speed. Overtaking the Hungarian rearguard, he routed it and pursued it as far as the main army. The rearguard’s sudden flight threw the entire Hungarian camp into confusion, and “there ensued a great flight, everyone all at once fleeing in disorder, without any order or consideration” (Section 36, Book V). Mahmud pressed the pursuit, “slaying and killing them mercilessly,” capturing all their military equipment, wagons, weapons, and horses. “Very many of the enemy fell; barely two hundred were taken alive into captivity” (Section 37, Book V).
Aftermath
After Mahmud’s victory, Mehmed made every attempt on Jajce by every means, but could accomplish nothing. He abandoned the attempt to take it by assault, struck camp, and turned against the other fortresses of Bosnia. Within a few days he subdued them: some he placed strong garrisons in, others he demolished. The men were put to death; women and children were led away into slavery. He also overran no small part of Hungary itself, gathering immense booty. He returned to Constantinople toward the end of summer 1464 and disbanded the army.
Jajce remained in Hungarian hands for several more decades, but the core of the Bosnian kingdom was permanently annexed to the Ottoman Empire. The conquest eliminated the last independent Christian kingdom in the western Balkans and brought the Ottoman frontier directly adjacent to Hungary, setting the stage for centuries of Ottoman-Hungarian conflict.
References

Kritobulos of Imbros, The Life of Mehmed II, Book IV, Sections 95-105 (Bosnian campaign of 1463)
Kritobulos of Imbros, The Life of Mehmed II, Book V, Sections 15-38 (Hungarian counterattack and second siege of Jajce, 1464)

events/conquest-of-constantinople-1453.title: The Conquest of Constantinople (1453)
events/conquest-of-constantinople-1453.content:
The Conquest of Constantinople (1453)
The siege and fall of Constantinople in the spring of 1453 was the defining event of Mehmed II’s reign and one of the most consequential military operations of the fifteenth century. The conquest ended the Byzantine Empire, which had endured for over a thousand years since Constantine the Great’s foundation of the city in AD 330, and established the Ottoman state as the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean.
Strategic Background
By the time of Mehmed II’s accession in 1451, the Byzantine Empire had been reduced to little more than the city of Constantinople itself, a few Aegean islands, and the Peloponnese. The city was surrounded by Ottoman territory on all sides, but its massive Theodosian Walls — a triple line of fortifications stretching across the peninsula — had never been breached in over a thousand years. Mehmed’s predecessors had besieged the city several times without success. What changed in 1453 was the combination of Ottoman gunpowder technology (the great bombards), naval power, and Mehmed’s personal determination.
The Fortress on the Bosporus
Mehmed’s first strategic move was to build a fortress on the European shore of the Bosporus at its narrowest point, opposite an earlier Ottoman fortress on the Asian shore. The fortress — known as Boğazkesen (the Strait-Cutter) and later as Rumeli Hisarı — was designed to control passage between the Black Sea and the Sea of Marmara. Both Kritoboulos and Tursun Beg describe the construction in detail. Kritoboulos records that Mehmed personally marked out the foundations with stakes and drew the plan: a triangular fortress with walls seventeen cubits thick, massive corner bastions, and stone-throwing engines positioned so that their fire “skipped along the surface of the sea” to prevent any ship from passing. The fortress was completed in a single summer — an extraordinary feat of logistics and organization.
The Siege
The Ottoman army arrived before Constantinople in April 1453. The land forces encamped before the Theodosian Walls, stretching from the Marmara to the Golden Horn. The fleet blockaded the sea approaches. The great bombard, cast by the Hungarian engineer Urban, was positioned opposite the weakest section of the walls. Kritoboulos provides a detailed account of the bombard’s construction and its terrifying effect as it fired stone balls weighing hundreds of kilograms.
A critical moment in the siege was Mehmed’s decision to transport a large portion of his fleet overland into the Golden Horn, past the great chain boom that the defenders had stretched across the harbour mouth. Using greased rollers and a makeshift track, Ottoman crews dragged seventy to eighty ships over a hill and launched them into the inner harbour, outflanking the Byzantine naval defences.
The final assault came on 29 May 1453, after fifty-three days of siege. The Janissaries breached the walls near the Gate of St. Romanus. The Genoese commander Giovanni Giustiniani was wounded and evacuated, causing panic among the defenders. Emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos died in the chaos of the final fighting — his body was never positively identified. The city was sacked for three days according to Ottoman custom, though Mehmed ordered a halt to the looting after entering the city.
Aftermath
Mehmed entered Constantinople on the afternoon of 29 May and rode directly to Hagia Sophia, which was converted into a mosque. Both Tursun Beg and Kritoboulos record his actions in the conquered city. He ordered the looting to stop, granted freedom to those who had not resisted, and began the work of repopulation and reconstruction. The city, officially renamed Istanbul, became the new Ottoman capital.
The cultural and political consequences were immense. The fall of Constantinople sent shockwaves through Europe, contributed to the Renaissance by driving Greek scholars westward with their manuscripts, and disrupted the trade routes between Europe and Asia. For the Ottoman state, the conquest gave Mehmed the prestige and strategic capital to pursue an ambitious program of expansion that would continue for the rest of his reign.

events/conquest-of-peloponnese.title: The Conquest of the Peloponnese (1458-1460)
events/conquest-of-peloponnese.content:
The Conquest of the Peloponnese (1458-1460)
Prelude: Broken Tribute
After the capture of Constantinople in 1453, the Peloponnesian despots — the brothers Thomas and Demetrios Palaiologos — had called upon Sultan Mehmed II for aid against the Peloponnesian Illyrians (Albanians), who had revolted against them. In return, they promised an annual tribute of six thousand gold staters from the Peloponnese. The sultan sent a sufficient army; the despots subdued the Illyrians and made them tributaries.
Yet when Mehmed demanded the tribute in the years that followed, the despots repeatedly devised “vain pretexts and unbecoming reasons for delay” (Kritobulos, Book III, §2). They continued collecting tribute from the Illyrians but squandered it on their own expenses. Worse still, the despots quarreled among themselves and waged civil war against one another, throwing the Peloponnese into disorder.
Mehmed sent envoys both to demand payment and to assess the situation. He feared that the brothers’ dissension might draw the Italians — or some other Western power — into the Peloponnese, a province he himself intended to occupy. The Peloponnese, in his view, was “very strongly fortified by nature,” possessed strong cities and impregnable fortresses, and was “very conveniently situated for transporting the Thracians and Macedonians to Italy” (Kritobulos, Book III, §4). When the envoys returned empty-handed, reporting everything in confusion, Mehmed resolved to delay no longer.
The First Campaign (1458)
March to the Peloponnese
At the opening of spring of 1458 (the eighth year of Mehmed’s reign), the sultan set out from Adrianople with his entire army — cavalry and infantry — marching through lower Macedonia toward the Strymon River, taking with him “many weapons, all kinds of artillery, and as much copper and iron as possible” (Kritobulos, Book III, §8). He crossed into Thessaly and waited there, hoping the despots might yet yield and pay the tribute.
No envoys appeared. Mehmed marched through Phthiotis, crossed the Achaean mountains and the rivers Spercheios and Peneios, and arrived at the pass called Pyli (the Gate). He encamped in Phocis and Boeotia by the Asopus River, sending spies ahead to examine the passes of Cithaeron leading to the Isthmus.
Envoys from Despot Thomas arrived, bringing 4,500 gold pieces — a portion of the tribute — and begging for peace. Mehmed took the gold but declared the treaty would be made when he was inside the Peloponnese, “thus mocking and ridiculing them for their folly and stupidity” (Kritobulos, Book III, §14).
The Siege of Corinth
Crossing the Isthmus, Mehmed burst into Corinthia and pitched camp within four stadia of the city. He walked the perimeter with his chief officers, examining the defenses. The place was very strong: a high, isolated, steep site with only a single road leading up. The city lay on a slope, fortified with a triple wall. The citadel of Acrocorinth was “completely impregnable, being built on the summit of the height and fortified with very strong walls” (Kritobulos, Book III, §16).
He first offered terms; the Corinthians refused. He laid waste to their gardens, fields, and vineyards, then began the siege with artillery. The cannons could not reach the wall effectively — the steep terrain prevented bringing them close enough for forceful impact. Even if the wall fell, the ascent into the fort would be nearly impossible under fire from above.
Mehmed assaulted several times but was repulsed each time. Realizing the city could only be taken by hunger and lengthy blockade, he left a portion of the army under Mahmud Pasha to maintain the siege while he advanced into the interior.
The Submission of Tegea and the Flight of the Despots
Advancing into the Peloponnese, Mehmed subdued the fortresses around Corinth within a few days, “partly by arms, partly by force, partly by intimidation and fear, and partly also by persuasion” (Kritobulos, Book III, §21). He marched to Tegea (called Oinovamokhlion), besieged it, and accepted its surrender by agreement through Demetrios Asanes, the governor.
The despots, who had gathered their armies at Amyclaeion, scattered upon learning that Mehmed had reached Tegea. Thomas withdrew to Mantinea, where his wife and children were; Demetrios fled to barren Epidaurus (Monemvasia).
The Capture of Patras
Mehmed marched against Patras in Achaea over a steep and rocky road. The Patrans, hearing of his approach, abandoned the city in terror and fled with their wives and children — some to Venetian towns, others to Naupactus — leaving only the citadel under guard.
The sultan found the city deserted and gave the army permission to plunder it. He surrounded the citadel, positioned his artillery, and began the siege. The garrison, fearing bombardment, surrendered by agreement. Mehmed placed a garrison in the citadel and proclaimed that those Patrans who wished to return would receive freedom, tax exemptions, and restoration of their property. “For the city and the region pleased him greatly, being very fertile, lying in a very good location” at the mouth of the Gulf of Crissaeus (Kritobulos, Book III, §27).
The Desperate Assault on Corinth
Returning to Corinth, Mehmed found it still under siege after four months. The soldiery was grumbling; pack animals had died from the devastation of the region. Together with Mahmud and the other commanders, he resolved on a full assault.
The assault was ferocious. The heavy infantry forced their way through the gate of the demolished outer wall and seized it. But at the second wall they were struck from the front with long spears, maces, and javelins, and from above with enormous stones. Many fell. Mehmed gave the signal to withdraw, declaring that “it was not arms and soldiery but hunger that was the strongest enemy of this city” (Kritobulos, Book III, §36).
The plundering parties sent to Elis returned with immense booty: 35,000 head of livestock and over 40,000 men, women, and children. The livestock Mehmed distributed to the army; the captives he sent to Constantinople for resettlement.
The Treaty of Corinth
Asanes Matthew arrived from Sparta, sent by Despot Demetrios to negotiate. He climbed the impregnable side of Acrocorinth by night with seventy soldiers carrying grain, entered the city, and saw that the Corinthians could no longer hold out. He sent a herald to Mehmed.
The treaty was concluded: the Corinthians would retain their property; the region Mehmed had conquered — about a third of the Peloponnese — would remain his; the despots would keep the rest, paying an annual tribute of 3,000 gold pieces; and Mehmed would maintain peace and friendship with them, becoming their ally if attacked.
Mehmed took possession of Corinth, placing a garrison of 400 picked janissaries in both the citadel and the city, with a trusted commander. Other captured cities he either fortified or demolished. He left Amaris as governor and returned to Corinth at the beginning of autumn.
Athens and the Return
From Corinth, Mehmed crossed the Isthmus and went through Megara to Athens, which he greatly desired to see — “the Acropolis itself (the citadel) and those places where those men governed the state” (Kritobulos, Book III, §51). He ascended the Acropolis, admired and praised it, treated the inhabitants kindly with gifts, and stayed four days. He then marched through Boeotia and Plataea, inspecting places of Hellenic history, and examined the Euripus at Chalcis. He arrived at Adrianople by mid-autumn.
Interlude: The Repopulation of Constantinople
Arriving at Constantinople for the winter of 1458-1459, Mehmed devoted himself to repopulating the city. He settled Peloponnesians, transplanted inhabitants from Amastris and wealthy Armenians, and commanded his chief officers to build splendid dwellings, baths, inns, bazaars, and mosques. He selected the most beautiful site in the center of the city for a great mosque and began plans for an imperial palace on the promontory projecting into the sea. Mahmud Pasha constructed a vast and beautiful church (the future Fatih Mosque complex) with hospices, inns, and baths around it. Mehmed also ordered warships built throughout the empire, recognizing that “naval power and dominion over the sea were necessary for him, especially in distant campaigns” (Kritobulos, Book III, §76).
The Second Campaign (1460)
Civil War Among the Despots
That winter, the Peloponnesian despots fell out again. Their officials, insatiable and of evil character, quarreled and warred with one another, bringing the country into “complete disorder and confusion” (Kritobulos, Book III, §107). They approached each despot secretly with slanders, then openly deserted from one to the other, causing cities and fortresses to revolt. The despots waged open war against each other, invading each other’s territory, burning settlements, and capturing fortresses.
The Albanians went over to Thomas, and most eminent men of Demetrios’ camp also deserted daily, surrendering fortresses. Thomas became the victor, pursuing Demetrios and shutting him up in Monemvasia with his wife and daughter.
Despot Demetrios, desperate, sent Asanes to the emperor. According to some accounts, Demetrios offered to surrender the entire Peloponnese and betroth his daughter to Mehmed in exchange for another province. Mehmed, learning of the civil war and also that Thomas had broken his treaty, seized some territory, and was making alliances with the Italians, resolved to act immediately.
The March and the Surrender of Sparta
As spring opened, Mehmed marched from Adrianople and arrived at Corinth in 27 days. He waited three days for Demetrios; the despot sent Asanes with gifts instead. Mehmed arrested Asanes and then invaded — not the territory of Thomas, as expected, but the friendly territory of Demetrios, heading straight for Argos. This confirmed reports that the despots had voluntarily invited the sultan in.
Mehmed sent Mahmud Pasha by night to Sparta (Amyclaeion), who caught Demetrios there at dawn and shut him up. The emperor sent his scribe Thomas Katabolenos to make peaceful proposals. Demetrios, after some negotiation, came out and surrendered Sparta and its citadel. When Mehmed arrived, he rose from his seat before the despot, gave him his hand, seated him beside himself, and “conversing with him very peacefully and humanely, encouraged him with gentle and kind words to recover from his terror” (Kritobulos, Book III, §121). He rewarded Demetrios richly with money, robes, horses, and mules, and had his wife and daughter brought honorably from Epidaurus.
The Fall of Kasztrion
From Sparta, Mehmed marched against the fortress of Kasztrion, a very strong fortress at the foot of the great mountain near Sparta. The approach was rocky and difficult, with a single entrance defended by a triple wall, held by about 400 picked men.
When summoned, the defenders refused. Mehmed ordered an immediate assault. The defenders fought valiantly, shoving the heavy infantry down with lances and crushing them with enormous stones. Many fell. The next morning, Mehmed drew up the entire army, incited them with promises of rewards and plunder, and ordered a second assault. After a terrible struggle, the soldiers burst through the first and second walls and drove the defenders into the citadel. Crowded and without water, they surrendered.
Mehmed had all surviving men — about 300 — put to the sword, the women and children dragged into captivity, and the fortress demolished.
The Massacre at Gardikion
Next came Gardikion, an impregnable fortress on a steep rocky crag beside the pass to the mountain range called Ztigos, surrounded by deep chasms with only a single narrow path. An immense number of people had fled here as to a place of safety.
Mehmed first offered terms; they refused. He blockaded them. After a single day, tormented by hunger, thirst, and the summer heat, they surrendered unconditionally. Mehmed had all the men massacred and the women and children enslaved. He did this because they had chosen war when summoned peacefully, costing him many brave soldiers, and because they were mostly Illyrians against whom he bore anger for “their evil ways, their continual rebellions, their thefts and robberies” (Kritobulos, Book III, §134). He also wished to terrify the rest into submission.
The Total Submission of the Peloponnese
After these massacres, no one anywhere opposed him. Everyone voluntarily submitted — “about the greater part without any agreement, many not even awaiting his arrival” (Kritobulos, Book III, §135). Within a single summer, the entire province was in his hands: approximately 250 strong cities, fortresses, and watchtowers (Kritobulos, Book III, §140).
Mehmed treated those who submitted well, preserving their property and leaving them free from the army. Suitable fortresses he garrisoned and equipped; unsuitable ones he demolished. Some inhabitants he transplanted to Constantinople.
The Flight of Thomas
Despot Thomas, who had hoped the Peloponnese would not surrender so easily, shut himself up in Mantinea. When he saw that everything was captured and the entire Peloponnese was occupied — except for two or three Venetian towns — he despaired. He embarked with his wife, children, and some nobles on two ships and departed for Corcyra (Corfu).
Aftermath: Compensation for Demetrios
Mehmed marched out through the Isthmus, taking Demetrios with him along with his wife, daughter, and nobles. Arriving at Adrianople around mid-autumn, the sultan arranged for the despot’s future. He gave Demetrios the governorship and revenue of the Aegean islands Imbros and Lemnos, plus whatever people still remained in Thasos and Samothrace — a total annual revenue of 300,000 silver aspers. He also gave him the city of Ainos on the Thracian coast near the mouth of the Evros River, with all its tribute and revenues (another 300,000 aspers). Additionally, he allocated 10,000 aspers from the Adrianople mint for three years, so that Demetrios’ total annual revenue amounted to 700,000 silver aspers (Kritobulos, Book III, §144-146).
Thus the great and notable province of Pelops, which had been under Roman rule from ancient times, came entirely under Ottoman dominion.
Significance
The conquest of the Peloponnese eliminated the last significant Byzantine Greek state on the European mainland. It denied the Venetians and other Italian powers their hoped-for base of operations in southern Greece, secured the sea routes toward Italy, and provided Mehmed with a strategically vital province for his future ambitions. The speed of the conquest — accomplished within a single campaigning season each for the first and second expeditions — demonstrated the efficiency of the Ottoman military machine and the sultan’s ruthless strategic judgment.

events/conquest-of-trebizond.title: The Conquest of Trebizond (1461)
events/conquest-of-trebizond.content:
The Conquest of Trebizond (1461)
The Empire of Trebizond
The city of Trebizond (Trabzon) on the southeastern coast of the Black Sea was “from ancient times not only a very great and very beautiful city, but also a very old Hellenic city, which the Ionians and Athenians had founded” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §2). Situated on the eastern gulf of the Black Sea in Asia, it commanded a vast and richly producing region and was the common marketplace of Upper Asia — Armenia, Assyria, and all the surrounding territories.
In later times, Trebizond had become the imperial seat of one of the Comneni, an imperial lineage that had fled there from Byzantium. The Comnenian emperors ruled in succession over many peoples and cities, “in complete peace and without sedition, because the emperors were peace-loving and harmonious” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §5).
By the time of Mehmed II, however, the empire had fallen into factional strife and civil war. The ruling family raged against one another, the inhabitants fell out and attacked each other, and neighboring peoples rose up and frequently raided. As long as Constantinople held and the Romans were masters of the Straits, the Black Sea was closed to the sultan’s great fleet — but after the fall of Constantinople in 1453, everything changed.
Trebizond Becomes Tributary
After Mehmed captured Constantinople and became master of the Straits, the road to the Black Sea and the cities upon it lay open. Trebizond submitted and began to pay homage; its emperors “bending the knee before the Great Emperor, became tributaries” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §8). As long as they remained at peace, paid the tribute, and hatched no plots, Mehmed was at peace with them.
Under David Comnenus (r. 1459-1461), however, the situation deteriorated. The Trebizondines fell out among themselves and paid the tribute reluctantly. Worse, it became known that they were related by marriage to the neighboring princes — including Hasan the son of Tomuris (Uzun Hasan, the Aq Qoyunlu ruler of Tigranocerta/Amid, the Armenians and Medes) and even to the princes of the Iberians (Georgians) — and were plotting against their alliance with the sultan. Mehmed, “inflamed with wrath, launched a campaign against them, wishing to forestall them and deal with them before they could begin anything” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §9).
The Double Campaign: Land and Sea
During the winter of 1460-1461, Mehmed made extensive preparations. As soon as spring opened, he equipped the fleet for departure. Warships were fully armed — long galleys, fifty-oared vessels, and armored ships, about 300 in number, plus transport ships, cannon-carriers, and commercial vessels. They carried vast quantities of weapons: shields, helmets, spears, breastplates, lances, missile weapons, and implements for battering walls. The strongest and best-armed men were embarked as marines. Commanders of the fleet were Kasim Pasha, the governor of Gallipoli, and Yakub Pasha, “a man very experienced in naval affairs and the most excellent fleet commander” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §11).
The fleet sailed out through the Bosporus into the Black Sea with great speed and force, the crews rowing in rivalry, “shouting and exulting,” spreading terror wherever it appeared.
Meanwhile, Mehmed gathered the European cavalry and infantry, crossed into Asia, and transported horses, oxen, pack mules, camels, and every other preparation for war. Passing through Bithynia, he arrived at Prousa (Bursa), where he assembled the entire Asian army. After holding a memorial at his father’s tomb, he departed through Galatia into Paphlagonia.
The army, according to Kritobulos, amounted to 60,000 cavalry and at least 80,000 infantry, not counting pack animals and camp followers (Book IV, §15).
The Surrender of Sinope
Advancing through Cappadocia and crossing the river Halys, Mehmed arrived before Sinope, the most beautiful and richest city on the coast of the Black Sea. It was the common marketplace of the entire vicinity, abounding in every good thing — and its greatest treasure was copper, mined there in enormous quantities. The city had been governed for many years by Isma’il, a powerful man from a noble family, as a hereditary possession.
Mehmed pitched his camp before the city and found his fleet already anchored in the harbors — the fleet commanders had seized the harbors and surrounded the entire city and the small island with their ships.
Isma’il, seeing the vast host and that the camp by land and sea had surrounded him, “was astonished and began to consider what he should now do” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §19). After deliberation, he prepared vast and immensely valuable gifts and went out to meet the sultan.
Mehmed received him graciously and with due honor. After conferring at length, they came to an agreement: Mehmed took over Sinope and Isma’il’s entire realm, and in exchange gave Isma’il the governorship of Scopia (Skopje) in Europe — “the best and most fertile land, and in no way inferior to his own, either in revenue or in administration” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §21).
Kritobulos notes that Mehmed could adduce no fault against Isma’il. He took Sinope because its possession was valuable: it had safe harbors providing a suitable station for the fleet toward Trebizond, it lay in the middle of his empire, and he feared that Uzun Hasan might seize it first, since “Hasan was in every way striving after it and wishing to conquer it” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §23).
The Crossing of the Taurus Mountains
After settling affairs in Sinope, Mehmed commanded Kasim and Yakub to sail directly to Trebizond, occupy the harbors, and blockade the city. He himself advanced with the entire army into the interior toward Mount Taurus.
The Taurus is described as “the greatest mountain of Asia,” separating Lower Asia from Upper Asia, extending from Cape Mycale to the Black Sea near Sinope and uniting with the mountains of the Armenians and Medes. Only Alexander of Macedon (after Heracles and Dionysus) and the Roman Pompey had previously crossed it with an army — and in living memory, Tomuris (Timur), king of the Massagetae and Scythians.
Uzun Hasan, being related by marriage to the Emperor of Trebizond and wishing to help him (but also desiring to conquer Trebizond himself), gathered an army and went to the passes, intending to prevent Mehmed from crossing. Mehmed, learning of this, left Hasan to bide there while he himself cut a different, untrodden route — one that led directly to Tigranocerta, Hasan’s royal residence.
He sent Mahmud Pasha ahead with lightly armed infantry to seize the heights, passes, and hazardous places, then sent unarmed men to cut forest, level rocks, clear brush, and prepare a road. The Taurus, though bearing a single name, consisted of many difficult mountains: “sky-high, snow-covered peaks, very tall and steep crags, dizzying precipices, impassable rocky places, dense forests, and many difficult deep passes and terrible slopes” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §29). The route was so terrible that even lightly armed infantry could scarcely traverse it in eighteen days.
Mehmed organized his army carefully: infantry at the vanguard, baggage in the middle, cavalry with the rearguard. He himself stayed in the midst with a small cavalry force, drawing the vanguard into compact formation through narrow passes. He placed archers on both sides with strung bows, “the archers holding their strung and notched bows in their hands, and indeed crosswise toward the two sides, so that those on the left should shoot to the right and those on the right to the left” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §31).
The Assassination Attempt on Mahmud Pasha
During the crossing, a strange event occurred. A certain evil-minded man, “moved solely by his own madness and wicked soul,” sought the life of Mahmud Pasha. He shot an arrow that struck Mahmud on the forehead — but the wound was not mortal, as the assassin’s hands trembled from agitation. A great uproar arose; the soldiers seized the man and tore him to pieces. Mehmed, “seized by boundless sorrow, distress, and fear” for Mahmud, summoned his physician Yakub, who declared the wound not dangerous. The sultan richly rewarded the physician, and Mahmud soon recovered (Kritobulos, Book IV, §33-35).
Seventeen Days
Advancing incessantly for seventeen days, Mehmed forced his way through deep ravines, dizzying precipices, obstructed terrain, and untrodden wilderness “with great hardship and much exertion” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §36). He crossed the Taurus under arms and descended to the plain, pitching camp not far from Tigranocerta.
Uzun Hasan Sends His Mother
When Uzun Hasan learned that Mehmed had crossed the supposedly impassable Taurus with such a great army and was advancing directly under his royal residence, he was terrified — “as if struck by a thunderbolt” — and fell into terrible confusion. In his distress, he sent his aged mother to Mehmed with countless gifts, excusing himself for everything he had intended to do, asking forgiveness, and beseeching to be the sultan’s ally and friend (Kritobulos, Book IV, §37).
Mehmed received her in a friendly manner and with due honor, accepted Hasan as his friend and ally — but did not let her go immediately, taking her with him.
The Siege and Surrender of Trebizond
The Fleet’s Blockade
The fleet commanders, sailing before Trebizond, seized its harbors and landed. They joined battle with the Trebizondines who marched out before the city, routing them and driving them back with great force, shutting them up in the city. They became masters of the entire environs and the road leading to it, besieging the city by land and sea. They spent twenty-eight days in the siege, during which the citizens sometimes sallied forth — “and in these sallies were in no way inferior to the enemy; nevertheless, being few in number and heavily pressed by the multitude, they were driven back and again shut up in the city” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §40).
Mahmud Pasha’s Negotiations
Mahmud Pasha arrived with the land forces, preceding Mehmed by one day. He sent Thomas, the son of Katabolenos, as his envoy, offering the citizens and their emperor a surrender agreement.
