He told them it would be better to entrust themselves to the Great Emperor’s faith by agreement, under strong oaths and pledges — the emperor would be provided with much land and sufficient revenue, and the citizens might dwell in peace with their wives and children in their homeland. If they refused, they would “never again have the opportunity so much as to mention agreement and treaty,” once Mehmed took up the war with wrath and passion. Their fate would be “decided by arms and iron,” subjected to “slaughter, plunder, and slavery” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §41-43).
The citizens and their emperor inclined to the proposal, saying they would come to terms when the Great Emperor arrived.
The Surrender
The next day, Mehmed arrived and encamped before the city. He sent the same Thomas as herald, offering the same conditions. The Trebizondines prepared many beautiful gifts and selected the most eminent men among themselves, who went out, prostrated themselves before the sultan, and concluded a treaty. After giving and receiving oaths, they surrendered the city and themselves. They opened the gates and received Mahmud Pasha together with his army.
David Comnenus, the Emperor of Trebizond, came out from the city with his children and all his household to pay homage. Mehmed “received him graciously and humanely, and after shaking his hand and esteeming him fittingly, richly rewarded him, his children, and all who were with him” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §46).
The Inspection
Mehmed entered the city, walked around, and inspected its situation, strength, advantages, construction, and the great number of its inhabitants. He went up to the citadel and the imperial palace, examined and admired the strength of the fortifications, and “declared the city remarkable in every respect” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §47).
He then commanded that the Emperor of Trebizond and all those around him, as well as the most powerful and wealthiest men of the city — with their wives, children, and households — depart and board the galleys. He selected about 1,500 adolescent youths from the city and region, embarked them on the galleys, and sent the fleet away.
Aftermath
The Settlement
Mehmed appointed Kasim Pasha as governor of Trebizond, giving him 400 picked men from the imperial household troops as a garrison. After setting all affairs in order, he departed by the same route.
When he reached Uzun Hasan’s country, he dismissed the mother of Hasan with great gifts and honor, sending envoys with her to renew the treaty. Hasan reciprocated, sending envoys with gifts, thanking the sultan for the honor shown to his mother, and confirming the friendship and alliance.
Mehmed crossed the Taurus without any trouble, traversed the interior in a total of 28 days, arrived at Prousa, disbanded the army, and reached Constantinople toward the end of autumn.
Provision for David Comnenus
Upon arriving in Constantinople, Mehmed’s first care was to provide for the Emperor of Trebizond. He gave David and those with him sufficient land for their sustenance in the region of the Strymon River, from which he could collect an annual revenue of 300,000 silver coins (Kritobulos, Book IV, §53).
(Note: David Comnenus was later executed in 1463 on charges of plotting with Uzun Hasan, an event that falls outside the scope of Kritobulos’ account.)
The Philosopher George Amiroutzes
In the retinue of the Emperor of Trebizond was a certain George Amiroutzes, described as “a most excellent philosopher, who was entirely versed in all that pertains to natural science and metaphysics, arithmetic and geometry, and the theory of proportions” as well as rhetoric and poetics (Kritobulos, Book IV, §54). Mehmed, learning about him, summoned the man. After meeting and conversation, he admired him greatly, granted him a place beside himself, and deemed him worthy of frequent conversation — “presenting the opinions of the ancients and the difficult questions of philosophy, and discussing and solving them together with him; for the Emperor is very strong in philosophy” (Kritobulos, Book IV, §55).
Significance
The conquest of Trebizond eliminated the last surviving Byzantine Greek successor state. The Comnenian dynasty, which had ruled the Pontic Empire since the early thirteenth century, came to an end. The fall of Trebizond completed Ottoman mastery of the southern Black Sea coast and removed a potential ally for Uzun Hasan, with whom Mehmed would later clash at the Battle of Otlukbeli (1473). The sultan’s spectacular crossing of the Taurus — comparable in his mind, and in Kritobulos’, to the feats of Alexander the Great and Pompey — demonstrated his strategic boldness and the formidable reach of the Ottoman army.

events/edirne-vaqasi-1703.title: The Edirne Vaq'ası (1703)
events/edirne-vaqasi-1703.content:
The Edirne Vaq’ası (1703)
Prelude: The Resentful Capital
By the opening of the eighteenth century, the Ottoman court had been residing in Edirne (Adrianople) for over a decade. Sultan Mustafa II (r. 1695-1703) had abandoned Constantinople soon after his accession, preferring the hunting grounds and open plains of Thrace to the factions and pressures of the imperial capital. With the sultan went the grand vizier, the chief defterdar, and most of the high government — leaving Constantinople not merely neglected but injured. The city’s merchants, artisans, and janissary garrisons saw their incomes dwindle as the court’s spending shifted to Edirne.
The driving force behind the sultan in these years was the Şeyhülislam Feydullah Efendi, the chief mufti, who had been Mustafa’s tutor and enjoyed his absolute confidence. Feydullah placed his sons in the highest judicial offices — a breach of the established norms of the ulema career path that generated deep resentment among the religious establishment. The Treaty of Karlowitz (1699), which ended the long war with the Holy League and ceded Hungary, Transylvania, and the Morea to the Habsburgs and Venice, had already damaged Mustafa’s prestige. The combination of military defeat, absentee rule, and Feydullah’s nepotism created a reservoir of discontent at the capital.
The Outbreak
The immediate cause of the rebellion was fiscal. In the spring of 1703, salaries had fallen into arrears for the Janissary corps stationed in Constantinople — a recurring problem that had become endemic as the treasury struggled with the costs of the long war. On the 3rd of Rebi I, 1115 AH (July 18, 1703), the Jebeji Corps (the armourers) began a protest over unpaid wages. Within hours, the janissaries joined them, and the revolt spread to other military units and to the civilian population. Shops closed, the city’s markets emptied, and armed crowds gathered in the Hippodrome.
The rebels demanded the dismissal of Feydullah Efendi, the punishment of corrupt officials, and — most urgently — the return of the court to Constantinople. When news of the uprising reached Edirne, a great council of ministers was convened. Present was Sarı Mehmed Efendi (the future Sarı Mehmed Pasha), who had been appointed chief defterdar only two months earlier, on May 6, 1703, after the removal of his predecessor Şarı Mustafa Efendi. The council debated how to respond: negotiate, march against the rebels, or flee.
The March on Edirne
The rebels in Constantinople, distrusting the sultan’s promises, organized themselves into an alternative government. They appointed their own military commanders and began a march toward Edirne, carrying the green standard of the Prophet as their banner. Grand Vizier Rami Mehmed Pasha — one of the few men of the pen (ehl-i kalem) to rise to the grand vizierate — led the loyal army out to meet them.
Sarı Mehmed was sent back to Edirne to fetch Sultan Mustafa and bring him to the army, a mission he accomplished both successfully and quickly. When the two forces confronted each other, however, the loyal army melted away. The troops went over to the rebel side in a body. Mustafa II, seeing himself abandoned, abdicated on August 22, 1703 (9 Rebi II, 1115 AH). His brother was proclaimed sultan as Ahmed III.
Aftermath and the Treasury Crisis
Sarı Mehmed went into hiding when the new regime took power, not knowing whether he would be regarded as an enemy of the revolution. The rebel leaders had already appointed their own defterdar — Muhsin-zade Abdullah Efendi — who financed the initial stage of the rebellion largely with the confiscated wealth of Feydullah Efendi and his family. Feydullah himself was captured and killed by the mob; his body was dragged through the streets.
But the new defterdar quickly proved incompetent. The troops who had made the revolution demanded both their arrears and the customary accession donative (cülusiyye). Abdullah Efendi “displayed weakness and incompetence” before the vast sums required. The military chiefs, dissatisfied, made overtures to Sarı Mehmed in his hiding place. After receiving assurances that neither his life nor property would be endangered, he accepted the position, received from Ahmed III “the robe of continuance in office,” and took up his interrupted work as defterdar.
The task was staggering. The troops refused to disband until fully paid, and they adopted “a most threatening attitude.” The total required was 3,688 purses (1,537,666 piastres) — more than had ever been demanded on a similar occasion. Sarı Mehmed raised a thousand purses from the sultan’s private treasure, seized the confiscated estates of the previous administration, and sold tax-farms and government positions in advance. An additional thousand purses had to be sent as cülusiyye to the border garrisons, forwarded as drafts on government properties and revenues in the relevant districts. The total of 4,688 purses — the figure Sarı Mehmed proudly quoted in his Naṣā’iḥ ül-Vüzera — was raised within days, astonishing everyone who knew how empty the treasury had been.
Significance
The Edirne Vaq’ası was more than a palace revolt. It demonstrated that the capital city — neglected and resentful — could impose its will on the sultan, and that the janissaries, whatever their military failings against external enemies, remained the decisive political force at the heart of the empire. The rebellion forced the return of the court to Constantinople, where it remained until the end of the empire. It also brought to the fore Sarı Mehmed Pasha, whose financial acumen saved the new regime and whose subsequent treatise became one of the most important documents of the Ottoman nasihatname tradition.
The fall of Mustafa II and the accession of Ahmed III marked a generational shift in Ottoman politics. The age of the Köprülü grand viziers — who had dominated the late seventeenth century — gave way to a period of more complex factional struggles at court, and the empire’s fiscal and military problems, unresolved by the revolution, continued to deepen.

events/ottoman-venetian-war-1463-1479.title: Ottoman-Venetian War (1463-1479)
events/ottoman-venetian-war-1463-1479.content:
Ottoman-Venetian War (1463-1479)
The first major Ottoman-Venetian War under Mehmed II (1463-1479) was a prolonged conflict fought primarily in the Peloponnese (Morea), the Aegean Sea, and the coasts of Greece and Albania. The war is narrated in detail by Kritobulos of Imbros, whose account covers Books IV and V of his history of Mehmed II through the year 1467, though the war itself continued until the Treaty of Constantinople in 1479.
Causes and Pretext
The immediate cause of the war was the conduct of Omar Bey (,,Omar”), the Ottoman governor of the Peloponnese and the rest of Hellas. According to Kritobulos (Section 106), Omar, “moved by very slight pretexts” and angered that the Venetians showed him neither friendship nor favor, “suddenly fell upon the people of Naupactus (Lepanto), overran Naupactus and its environs, and plundered very many men, women, children, and cattle.” The attack was so sudden, occurring during a period of peace and treaty, that it nearly captured the city of Naupactus itself.
Omar also harassed the Venetian Peloponnesian cities of Coron, Methone (Modon), and others, “always raising vain pretexts and excuses” (Section 107). These actions weighed heavily upon Venice, but Kritobulos notes that the Venetians “had, in any case, long had a grudge for war against the Emperor from the time he conquered the Peloponnese. For they had always previously counted on that becoming theirs” (Section 107). The Venetian loss of the Peloponnese to Mehmed in 1458-1460 was the deeper cause; Omar’s raids merely provided the pretext.
The Venetian Expedition to the Peloponnese
Without any declaration of war, Venice equipped a great army and a fleet of seventy galleys and large transport ships. They embarked heavy infantry, armored soldiers, foreign mercenaries, stone-throwing cannons, bombards, iron, timber, quicklime, carpenters, masons, and all necessary siege equipment. As their plenipotentiary commander, they chose “a man among them of great authority, renowned for bravery and military talent both by land and by sea” (Section 108).
The fleet sailed from Venice into the Adriatic and Ionian Seas, past Corcyra (Corfu) and Leucas (Lefkada), between Ithaca and Cephallenia, arriving at Elis in the Peloponnese. From there they crossed the Crissaean Gulf and landed at Corinth at the Isthmus.
The Fortification of the Isthmus of Corinth
The Venetians disembarked and pitched camp at the Isthmus of Corinth. Their first objective was to fortify this narrow neck of land connecting the Peloponnese to mainland Greece, which would give them control of access to the entire peninsula. They landed all necessary materials and tools and, “with many hands, great force, and even greater diligence and enthusiasm” (Section 110), built a wall across the Isthmus from one sea to the other.
As the wall rose, some fortresses and towns in the Peloponnese defected from the Ottomans to the Venetians. The rest of the Peloponnese was “in a state of agitation, awaiting the future so that it too might defect from the Emperor” (Section 111).
Meanwhile, Omar Bey concentrated his forces in Corinth, guarding it and awaiting reinforcements from Mehmed, having already sent word of the Venetian landing. The Venetians completed their wall in great haste, leaving only three or four stades unfinished, though the wall was not raised very high.
Mahmud Pasha’s Defeat of the Venetians at the Isthmus
Mehmed II, upon learning of the Venetian invasion and the fortification of the Isthmus, “did not take the matter lightly” (Section 113). He immediately summoned his grand vizier, Mahmud Pasha (,,Mahmut Pasha”), and gave him sufficient troops of heavy infantry and archers, assigning to him the most warlike and best-armed soldiers from his own household. Mahmud was dispatched to the Peloponnese while Mehmed himself remained to finish settling affairs in Bosnia (see: Conquest of Bosnia (1463)).
Arriving in the Peloponnese, Mahmud encamped at the foot of Mount Cithaeron beyond the Isthmus. He secretly contacted Omar Bey, and they agreed on a coordinated attack at a given signal on an appointed day.
The Battle
At the appointed signal, Mahmud and Omar simultaneously sallied forth and fell upon the Venetian camp from within and without, “amidst the great shouting, clamor and rejoicing of the soldiers” (Section 3, Book V). A fierce hand-to-hand struggle ensued. The Venetians resisted for a short time, then “turning their backs, in disorderly confusion, fled as best they could” (Section 4, Book V).
The Ottoman heavy infantry pursued them mercilessly to the sea, slaying and capturing. The Venetians scrambled onto their galleys and withdrew just out of missile range, picking up survivors swimming in the sea. Many, pressed by the enemy, threw away their weapons; some jumped into the sea with their weapons and drowned from the weight of their arms.
A vast number of Venetians fell in the battle, “both citizens and foreigners; the commander-in-chief himself also fell, who was a worthy man” (Section 6, Book V). Barely three hundred were taken alive. The Ottoman soldiers plundered the entire Venetian camp, finding money, furniture, vessels, tools, and many weapons stripped from the dead.
The Fall of Argos and Resettlement of the Argives
After the victory at the Isthmus, Mahmud marched into the interior of the Peloponnese with Omar, subduing rebellious fortresses and Peloponnesians — “partly by force of arms and partly by persuasion” (Section 7, Book V). Within a few days, he brought the fortresses and the entire province under control.
Mahmud then arrived before Argos, a Venetian-held city. The Argives, seeing the vast Ottoman encampment, recognizing that their walls were weak and easily stormed, receiving no aid from anywhere, and fearing destruction if taken by force, surrendered themselves and the city to Mahmud without a fight, after receiving pledges.
Mahmud transported all the Argives — with their wives, children, and all possessions — unharmed to Constantinople (Byzantium). The city of Argos itself was destroyed. In Constantinople, the Emperor settled the Argives in the vicinity of the Monastery of Peribleptos (the Wondrous Virgin Mary), giving them houses, vineyards, and fields. The captured Venetians and rebellious Peloponnesians, however, were all put to death.
Having arranged everything in the Peloponnese, placed garrisons from the bravest imperial household troops, and delivered the entire province to Omar, Mahmud returned to Constantinople around mid-winter, bringing the prisoners.
The Venetian Campaign Against Lesbos and Mytilene
In the summer following Mahmud’s campaign, the Venetians targeted the island of Lesbos, specifically its capital Mytilene, with seventy galleys and three thousand heavy infantry. They brought stone-throwing cannons, mortars, scaling ladders, and all kinds of siege equipment.
The Venetian fleet sailed into the harbor of Mytilene and pitched camp before the city, summoning the defenders to surrender. The city refused, as it held a garrison of four hundred picked armored infantry from the imperial household troops. The Venetians laid waste to part of the land and began a siege, surrounding the city by land and sea.
The Ottoman artillery inside the city managed to repair damage to the walls overnight, palisading the broken sections with beams bound by chains to break the force of the stone cannonballs. Two of the smaller strongholds on Lesbos did surrender to the Venetians, raising their hopes of taking Mytilene itself.
The Ottoman Fleet’s Response
Mehmed II, upon learning of the Venetian attack on Mytilene, acted “faster than thought.” He equipped 110 galleys, loading them with heavy infantry, weapons, and missile devices. He delivered the fleet to Mahmud Pasha with orders to sail as swiftly as possible and engage the enemy.
Mahmud sailed from Constantinople through the Hellespont and put in at Gallipoli. Learning that four Venetian scout galleys were anchored in the harbor of Tenedos, he set out that very night and arrived at Tenedos at sunrise. He captured two of the galleys before they could escape; the other two escaped with great difficulty and reached Mytilene, reporting that the Ottoman fleet was approaching.
The Venetian commanders at Mytilene, hearing this news, “as though a thunderbolt had struck among them” (Section 45, Book V), abandoned everything — cannons, weapons, and all equipment — in utter confusion. They embarked upon their ships, taking even the inhabitants of the fortresses that had surrendered to them, and sailed away. Mahmud arrived at Mytilene approximately eight hours after their departure. He pursued them to the open sea but could barely discern their ships making for Lemnos. Returning to Mytilene, he spent four days setting things in order, leaving a sufficient garrison and supplies, and returned to Constantinople toward the end of autumn 1464.
Omar’s Victory at Patras (1466)
In the early autumn of 1466, the Venetians launched a campaign against Patras in Achaea with forty ships and two thousand heavy infantry. Landing before the city, they besieged it, surrounding it with their camp and setting up stone-throwing cannons. Some Peloponnesian allies who had recently revolted from the Ottomans joined them.
Omar Bey, the governor of the Peloponnese, though he did not have a large army, harassed the Venetians from outside, setting traps and laying ambushes. He posted spies on Mount Bus (Ox) to observe everything that happened in the Venetian camp and report back to him.
Seizing a favorable opportunity, Omar fell upon the Venetians unexpectedly. Terrifying them with a mass assault, he routed them and pursued them to the sea, “mercilessly slaying and capturing them all the way to the ships, and hurled those who were running about in disorder headlong into the sea” (Section 84, Book V). About six hundred Venetians fell; barely a hundred were captured alive. Many drowned in the sea, jumping in with their weapons to swim to their ships and sinking from the weight of their arms.
Omar plundered the dead and the entire Venetian camp, gathering vast wealth, weapons, tools, and equipment. He then went to Constantinople to the Emperor, who received him gladly, richly rewarded him, and had the captives put to death.
The Venetian Embassy and Failed Peace Negotiations
In the winter of that same year (1466-67), a Venetian embassy arrived at the Ottoman court, “with the desire that they should settle their differences and make peace with each other on equal terms, so that each side should hold what it held” (Section 90, Book V). Mehmed II rejected this proposal outright. His answer to the ambassadors was: “Go away, so that you may deliberate more wisely, if you wish to obtain peace and alliance from me.”
Mehmed’s demands were far more severe: he required the Venetians to return the islands they had occupied — Imbros and Lemnos — and to pay him annual tribute. These terms were unacceptable to Venice, and the war continued.
The Later Years of the War (1467-1479)
Kritobulos’ narrative concludes with the year 1467. The war, however, continued for another twelve years. The conflict spread to other theaters:

In Albania, the Ottomans under Mehmed II waged a prolonged campaign against the Albanian leader Skanderbeg (who died in 1468) and the Venetian-held fortress of Scutari (Shkodra).
The Ottomans captured the Venetian strongholds of Negroponte (Euboea) in 1470 after a major naval campaign.
Raids and counter-raids continued across the Aegean, Dalmatia, and the Peloponnese.
The Ottoman fleet, rebuilt and strengthened, challenged Venetian naval supremacy in the eastern Mediterranean.

The Treaty of Constantinople (1479)
The war finally ended with the Treaty of Constantinople in 1479, after sixteen years of conflict. The terms heavily favored the Ottomans:

Venice ceded to the Ottoman Empire the island of Lemnos and the fortress of Scutari in Albania.
Venice recognized Ottoman conquests in the Peloponnese and the Aegean.
Venice was required to pay an annual tribute of 10,000 ducats to the Porte for the right to trade in Ottoman territories.
A Venetian bailo (resident ambassador) was permitted to reside in Constantinople.

The treaty marked the beginning of Ottoman naval dominance in the eastern Mediterranean and established the framework for Ottoman-Venetian relations that would persist, punctuated by further wars, until the eighteenth century.
References

Kritobulos of Imbros, The Life of Mehmed II, Book IV, Sections 106-114 (Causes, Venetian expedition, fortification of Isthmus, Mahmud’s dispatch)
Kritobulos of Imbros, The Life of Mehmed II, Book V, Sections 1-46 (Mahmud’s victory at Isthmus, fall of Argos, Lesbos campaign, Patras campaign)
Kritobulos of Imbros, The Life of Mehmed II, Book V, Section 90 (Venetian embassy and failed peace negotiations)

events/ottoman-venetian-war-1499-1503.title: Ottoman-Venetian War of 1499-1503
events/ottoman-venetian-war-1499-1503.content:
Ottoman-Venetian War of 1499-1503
The Ottoman-Venetian War of 1499-1503 was a decisive conflict between the Ottoman Empire under Sultan Bayezid II and the Republic of Venice, fought primarily in the Aegean Sea, the Ionian Sea, the Morea (Peloponnese), and the Dalmatian coast. The war marked the emergence of the Ottoman Empire as a major naval power and permanently altered the balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean. By its conclusion, the Ottomans had captured the key Venetian strongholds of Lepanto (Naupaktos), Modon, and Coron, reducing Venetian presence in Greece to only a few coastal enclaves.
Background and Prelude to War
Relations between the Ottoman Empire and Venice had been governed by the peace treaty of 1481, signed after the death of Mehmed II. For nearly two decades, an uneasy truce had held, punctuated by frontier raids and mutual suspicion. Two factors drove the breakdown of peace in the late 1490s. First, Bayezid II, who had spent his reign consolidating Ottoman territory and fending off challenges from his brother Jem, came under pressure from the ghazi tradition that demanded renewed warfare against Christian powers. Second, Venice’s continued possession of strategic ports along the Greek coast — Modon, Coron, Lepanto, Napoli di Romania (Nafplio), and Monemvasia — was an obstacle to Ottoman dominance over Greece and the trade routes of the eastern Mediterranean.
By 1499, Bayezid had decided on war. He initiated a massive naval buildup, personally visiting the shipyards of Istanbul to oversee construction. Throughout the winter and spring of 1499, Ottoman shipyards at Gallipoli, Ismid, Salonica, Negroponte, and Valona worked at full capacity. The scale of the effort was unprecedented for the Ottomans — they were preparing to challenge Venice, the preeminent naval power of the age, on its own element.
The Naval Build-up
The Ottoman fleet that sailed in June 1499 for Corfu and the Ionian Sea numbered roughly 250 vessels, including seventy-eight regular galleys, twenty-five long galleys, and two enormous new ships of eighteen hundred tons each — the largest vessels in the world at that time. Fifteen thousand oarsmen were recruited, all Muslims, because Bayezid feared Christian oarsmen might prove faithless in battle. Ottoman spies in Venice reported the fleet’s movements, and the Venetians, caught off guard by the speed and scale of the Ottoman preparations, scrambled to assemble their own fleet under the command of Antonio Grimani.
Bayezid led the land campaign in person. From Edirne, he marched with an army of sixty to seventy thousand men through Plovdiv, Samakov, Uskub, and Monastir toward Lepanto. The beylerbey of Rumelia, Mustafa Pasha, was ordered to cooperate with the fleet in attacking the Morea and the Gulf of Corinth. A second army assembled at Ipsala in Rumelia, while Anatolian troops gathered near Bursa before crossing the Straits.
The Campaign of 1499
The Ottoman fleet, commanded by Kapudan Daud Pasha, sailed past Negroponte and Napoli di Romania but was becalmed for nearly a month off the island of Sapienza, near Modon. The delay vexed Bayezid, who took counsel with his vizirs. Ibrahim Pasha, the grand vizir, advised pressing on with the army to Lepanto, while the fleet was urged forward.
The Venetian fleet assembled at Corfu under Antonio Grimani, a wealthy politician who had no naval experience. Though outnumbered — roughly 100 ships against the Ottoman 241 — the Venetians had more large galleys and galleons, and their crews were professional seamen. The French, allied to Venice under Louis XII, promised to send ships but delayed.
On August 12, 1499, the two fleets met off Navarino. The engagement proved disastrous for Venice. Grimani and his brilliant but abrasive subordinate Andrea Loredano were at odds. When Loredano attacked the great Ottoman ship commanded by the captain Borrak, the three ships became locked together, and Borrak, seeing his position hopeless, fired his own vessel. All three ships exploded in flames. The terrifying spectacle broke Venetian morale, and they withdrew to Zante, leaving the Turks in control of the west coast of the Morea.
With the fleet defeated, the Ottoman army pressed the siege of Lepanto. The Venetian commander, Giovanni Moro, lacked artillery and, seeing no hope of relief, surrendered the city on August 28, 1499. The fall of Lepanto shocked Venice. Grimani was recalled in chains, tried, and exiled.
Bayezid ordered the construction of castles on both sides of the entrance to the Gulf of Corinth, sealing Venetian access to the region. He then returned to Edirne for the winter, leaving the fleet in the Gulf of Corinth.
The Dalmatian Diversion
To divide Venetian forces, Bayezid ordered Iskender Pasha to lead raids deep into Dalmatia and Friuli. Turkish forces ravaged the country around the Isonzo River, sent cavalry across the Tagliamento, and threatened Venetian possessions along the Dalmatian coast at Zara, Trau, Sibenico, Spalato, Budua, Antivari, Dulzigno, and Durazzo. The Venetians grew so alarmed that they feared an attack on Venice itself, but Iskender Pasha withdrew his forces with the approach of winter.
Preliminary Peace Soundings
Even before Lepanto fell, the Venetian Senate had considered sending envoys to Bayezid. After the loss, the agitation for peace grew. Alvise Manenti was dispatched to the Porte in early 1500 with Venetian demands: the return of Lepanto, the liberation of prisoners, the restoration of confiscated merchants’ goods, and the reaffirmation of the peace of 1481. The Ottoman vizirs — Mesih, Hersekoglu Ahmed, Yakub, and Mustafa Pashas — dismissed these demands as outrageous, given the outcome of the campaign. Bayezid offered his own terms: the frontier should be the sea itself, meaning Napoli di Romania, Monemvasia, Coron, and Modon must be ceded to Turkey, along with an annual tribute of ten thousand ducats. Venice refused, and the war continued.
The Campaign of 1500
Bayezid had anticipated Venice’s refusal. Throughout the winter and spring, he poured resources into building an even larger navy. Carpenters, coopers, and caulkers were dispatched with more than two hundred thousand ducats to Lepanto, Valona, and Prevesa. Ships were modeled along Venetian lines for galleys and French lines for galleons, and some newly built vessels were intentionally sunk to prevent their green timber from warping. By summer, thirty-four new galleys had been completed at Lepanto, twenty-six at Prevesa, and thirty at Valona, bringing the Ottoman fleet again to about 250 ships.
French Mediation and Venetian Diplomatic Efforts
Louis XII of France, who had taken Milan in April 1500, sent two ambassadors to Bayezid to mediate. The French envoys were blunt to the point of rudeness — they refused gifts, insisted on a direct audience, and declared that France and all her allies (the Pope, Spain, Portugal, England, Scotland, Hungary, Russia) would attack the Porte if peace were not made. Bayezid responded by naming his terms in a letter to Louis: Modon, Coron, and Napoli di Romania in exchange for peace. He granted a sixty-day waiting period for Venice to accept.
Venice, meanwhile, searched frantically for allies. Hungarian support was the most promising: Venice raised its subsidy from seventy thousand to one hundred thousand ducats annually to persuade King Vladislav to break the truce with Turkey. The Pope, Alexander VI, issued a jubilee bull for a crusade but sent no material aid. The Italian states — Florence, Naples, Ferrara, Milan, and Mantua — actually sent envoys to the Porte encouraging Bayezid to continue the war against Venice. Bayezid wisely kept their friendship, signing a formal treaty with Florence and recognizing its consul in Istanbul.
The Fall of Modon and Coron
By June 1500, the Ottoman army and fleet were ready. The main camp was established at Leondari in the Morea, and the fleet blockaded Modon. Bayezid led the land forces in person, bombarding the city throughout July. The siege dragged on, and the Ottomans grew discouraged. Then, on August 9, five Venetian ships ran the blockade and entered the port. The inhabitants and garrison, overjoyed and eager to unload the ships, nearly deserted the walls. Perceiving this, the Turks stormed the walls and captured the city on August 10. Modon — Venice’s most important harbor on the route to the Levant — was lost forever.
Ali Pasha, sanjakbey of the Morea, took captured Venetians to Coron and demanded its surrender. The Greek inhabitants overthrew the Venetian rulers, and Coron capitulated on August 15. Navarino followed soon after. The Ottoman army marched on Napoli di Romania, but the Venetian commander held firm, and Bayezid, receiving news of Hungarian mobilization, returned to Edirne. By the end of the 1500 campaign, only Napoli di Romania and Monemvasia remained in Venetian hands in the Morea.
The Hungarian Front
Bayezid’s haste to return north was justified. Hungary, Poland, Russia, and Wallachia were preparing to strike. In August 1500, two Hungarian envoys declared war at the Porte. Turkish forces were defeated at Semendra and Widin, and officers were captured. Though winter halted serious fighting, Bayezid prepared bridges and forces for a spring campaign, while the Tartars were unleashed against Poland and Russia.
Venetian Counter-strike: Cefalonia
In the autumn of 1500, the Venetian fleet, reinforced by a Spanish contingent, took the offensive. After capturing the fortress of Cefalonia on December 24, 1500, after a two-month siege, the Venetians felt their position improved. They also retook Navarino on December 3. Bayezid, concerned by the Hungarian threat and by simmering rebellion in Anatolia, offered peace, but Venice — buoyed by her recent success and the prospect of more allies — refused.
The Campaigns of 1501-1502
The war entered a stalemate phase. The main Ottoman fleet remained largely in port, threatened by the combined French, Spanish, and Portuguese fleets. Fighting became desultory: raids and counter-raids along the Dalmatian coast, in Albania, and in the Greek islands.
In June 1501, Kemal Reis, Bayezid’s most intrepid sea captain, led a raiding squadron from Modon to the Balearic Islands, Pianosa, and Sardinia, returning with more than a thousand slaves. In October, a combined French-Venetian fleet attacked Mytilene on Lesbos, ravaging the island, but failed to take the fortress. The French fleet then withdrew permanently, its commander Ravenstein having been bribed by the Turks.
In Albania, Firuz Bey held the inhabitants firmly and captured Durazzo in July 1501. Venetian possessions along the Dalmatian coast — Zara, Trau, Sibenico, Spalato — suffered Turkish raids as in previous summers. The fighting with Hungary remained inconclusive, as King Vladislav was reluctant to spend his subsidy and Bayezid’s attention was increasingly drawn eastward by the rise of Shah Ismail of Persia.
The Peace of 1503
By 1502, both powers were exhausted and ready for peace. Venice was weary of war, her treasury and arsenal nearly empty, her credit severely impaired. Doge Augustino Barbarigo died in September 1501, and his successor, Leonardo Loredano, saw no legitimate reason to continue the conflict. Bayezid, for his part, faced the growing menace of Shah Ismail’s Persia, rebellion in Anatolia, and the threat of a combined Christian league.
The first serious move toward peace came from the Porte. In January 1502, Venetian merchants imprisoned in Istanbul were freed. Andrea Gritti, the leading Venetian merchant in Turkey, was a warm friend of Hersekoglu Ahmed Pasha, the grand vizir. Before leaving for Venice, Gritti was commissioned to urge his government to make peace.
On September 13, 1502, Venice resolved to send Zaccaria di Freschi to Istanbul to negotiate. His commission required him to secure the acceptance of a Venetian bailo (ambassador), the restitution of merchants’ goods, the maintenance of existing frontiers, and — most difficult — a treaty that included Rhodes, Hungary, and other Christian nations.
Di Freschi reached Istanbul on December 7, 1502. Bayezid wished to negotiate separately with Venice and Hungary, and Hersekoglu was delegated to arrange the peace. The Turkish peace proposal, drawn on December 14, consisted of thirty-one articles. The key demand was the return of Santa Maura (Lefkada), which the Venetians had captured in 1502, along with restoration of Ottoman boundaries at Cattaro. In exchange, Venice would retain what she still held at the time, Venetian merchants could trade freely in the empire, and a Venetian bailo would be permitted to reside in Istanbul for one year.
Venice debated the terms through the winter. On January 23, 1503, the Senate instructed di Freschi to acquiesce on Santa Maura and to promise fifteen thousand ducats for damages. The formal treaty was drawn up on March 31, 1503.
Andrea Gritti was then dispatched to Istanbul to negotiate modifications. He arrived on July 9 and proved the ideal diplomat: he had lived in Turkey for years, knew the language, and was personally close to Hersekoglu. He secured the extension of the bailo’s residence from one to three years, obtained restoration of merchants’ goods, and raised the ducat’s official valuation from fifty to fifty-four aspers. The final treaty was formally signed and celebrated on August 10, 1503. Santa Maura was returned to Turkey on August 31.
Separately, Bayezid made peace with Hungary in March 1503, isolating Venice from her most threatening ally.
Aftermath and Significance
The Treaty of 1503 permanently altered the balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean. Venice retained Napoli di Romania and Monemvasia, as well as Zante and Cefalonia, but lost Lepanto, Modon, Coron, Navarino, and Durazzo — the ports that had been her lifeline to the Levantine trade. Turkish control of Greece and Albania was nearly complete.
For the Ottoman Empire, the war marked its emergence as a major naval power. Before 1499, Ottoman sea captains had been timid before Venetian galleys; by 1503, they had proven their superiority in armaments, courage, and seamanship. The victory gave the Ottomans dominance over the Greek Archipelago, and most Aegean islands became tributaries of the Porte.
The economic consequences were profound. With the loss of Modon and the enormous expense of the war, Venice was unable to mount a successful bid to recover the spice trade of the Orient — a trade that was simultaneously being diverted around Africa by Portuguese navigators. The weakening of Venetian sea power and the Ottoman acquisition of eastern Mediterranean ports paved the way for Selim I’s conquest of Syria and Egypt in 1516-1517, and for Suleiman the Magnificent’s successful siege of Rhodes in 1522.
Fisher, summarizing the significance, called the victory over Venice “one of the most significant victories in the annals of Ottoman history,” as it “sealed the fate of the eastern third of the Mediterranean for the following three hundred years.”

events/sahkulu-rebellion-1511.title: The Şahkulu Rebellion (1511)
events/sahkulu-rebellion-1511.content:
The Şahkulu Rebellion (1511)
The Şahkulu rebellion of 1511 was a major Safavid-inspired uprising of the Kızılbaş Turkoman tribes in Anatolia, led by a charismatic rebel known as Şahkulu (Shah Kuli, lit. “Servant of the Shah”). The rebellion posed one of the most serious internal threats to the Ottoman state during the reign of Bayezid II, paralyzing the capital and exposing the deep sectarian and political fractures within the empire on the eve of Selim I’s accession.
Origins
The rebellion grew out of longstanding persecution of the Kızılbaş — heretical Shia-leaning tribes whose religious allegiance inclined toward Shah Ismail of the Safavid dynasty in Persia. During years of Ottoman inquisition against these communities, Şahkulu had evaded capture. When Prince Korkud moved his princely seat from Attalya (Antalya) to Manisa, Şahkulu and his followers robbed him of many of his effects, an act that provided the initial material means for the revolt.
The uprising began in earnest in the regions of Attalya and Tekke, drawing on a base of Kızılbaş tribesmen whose grievances were both religious and economic. Many of the Karamanian beys and local leaders also joined the rebellion, though for political reasons — resentment of Ottoman central authority — at least as much as for faith.
The Campaign
Assault on Konya
By late February 1511, Şahkulu and his followers had massed sufficiently to attempt the capture of Konya, the former Karamanid capital and a major Anatolian city. The attack was repulsed by Princes Orhan and Mehmed, grandsons of Bayezid II, who successfully defended the city. Foiled at Konya, Şahkulu circled westward and northward.
Defeat of Karagöz Pasha
At Afyon Karahisar, the rebel army encountered Karagöz Pasha, the beylerbey of Anatolia, who had marched out to suppress the uprising. The Ottomans suffered a serious defeat. Karagöz narrowly escaped capture and fled southeast to his headquarters at Kütahya. The rebels pursued.
Capture of Kütahya
Kütahya, the seat of the Anatolian beylerbey, was considered impregnable. It held a substantial garrison of Janissaries and azabs (irregular infantry), along with the provincial treasury for paying and provisioning Ottoman troops. Şahkulu and his Kızılbaş warriors assaulted the city in May 1511, before the relief armies of Princes Korkud (marching from Manisa) and Ahmed (marching from Amasya) could arrive. The city fell. Karagöz Pasha and several sanjakbeys were captured.
For several days the rebels pillaged the city, acquiring vast booty and slaughtering troops and inhabitants. Karagöz Pasha was impaled — a deliberate humiliation and a message to the Porte.
Advance on Bursa
Following the capture of Kütahya, Şahkulu’s forces pressed northward, reaching the Sakarya River with Bursa — the old Ottoman capital and a city of immense symbolic and economic importance — as their objective. The news of their advance almost paralyzed Istanbul. The Porte, now fully alarmed, mobilized the crack troops of the empire.
Imperial Response
The grand vizier, Ali Pasha, was ordered to cross into Asia with a picked force of approximately 4,000 Janissaries and sipahis (cavalry) to confront Şahkulu. Princes Ahmed, Korkud, and Mehmed were called upon to support the grand vizier. Korkud, however, did not appear, whether from fright or from a false rumor that Bayezid II had died.
The Turning Point
As Ali Pasha’s formidable army approached, Şahkulu began a strategic retreat. Between the Sakarya River and Ankara, the rebels halted and occupied a defensible mountain position to defy the pursuing Ottomans. Ali Pasha moved to surround the rebels, but Şahkulu escaped during the night by a classic ruse: leaving his campfires burning while his army slipped away under cover of darkness.
The Janissaries were furious with Ali Pasha for losing the opportunity, blaming his decision to delay the attack until Prince Ahmed’s arrival. Joined by Ahmed at Ankara, Ali Pasha crossed the Kızıl Irmak (Halys River) and pursued toward Kayseri, capturing only stragglers.
The Battle of Sarmusaklı
Impatient with the slow pursuit, Ali Pasha left the infantry to advance under Ahmed’s command and took the Rumelian cavalry ahead. He caught up with the rebels at Sarmusaklı, near Kayseri, where Şahkulu’s forces were encamped in the hills. Despite his men being weary from a long ride under the hot sun, Ali Pasha attacked.
The battle was bloody and chaotic. Şahkulu was killed in the fighting. Ali Pasha himself fell in the same engagement, and the Ottoman horsemen, leaderless, withdrew from the field. The rebellion thus ended not with a decisive Ottoman victory but with both commanders dead.
Aftermath
Prince Ahmed arrived several days later to find the rebels scattered and the uprising subsided. He remained in the region for some weeks, clearing away remaining heretics. The Ottoman soldiers, however, turned to pillaging the Anatolian countryside, robbing inhabitants and taking many as slaves under the pretext that they were of the Safavid sect. The extent of this vengeance was significant but not as extensive as the massacres Selim I would order against the Kızılbaş a few years later.
Many of the surviving Kızılbaş fled to Persia seeking the protection of Shah Ismail. On their way, they robbed a caravan laden with silk for Bursa — an act that may have prompted Ismail to execute some of the fugitives. More probably, Ismail wished to dissociate himself from the rebellion and to avoid Ottoman suspicion of Safavid complicity. During the rebellion, Ismail had sent an ambassador to the Porte bearing the head of Shahabey Khan in a silver casket — a gesture of goodwill. The envoy was closely guarded throughout his stay to prevent him from learning of the succession crisis brewing among Bayezid’s sons.
Significance
The Şahkulu rebellion exposed multiple vulnerabilities in the Ottoman state: the appeal of Safavid religious propaganda among the Anatolian Turkoman population, the slow and unreliable response of the princes when called upon to defend the realm, the rivalry among Bayezid’s sons (Korkud, Ahmed, and Selim) that hampered coordinated military action, and the Janissary corps’ influence over military decision-making. The rebellion also foreshadowed the much larger confrontation between the Ottoman and Safavid empires that would dominate the early years of Selim I’s reign — the campaign that culminated in the Battle of Chaldiran in 1514. Prince Korkud’s indecisiveness during the crisis further damaged his standing and contributed to his eventual eclipse by the more decisive Selim.

events/treaty-of-karlowitz-1699.title: Treaty of Karlowitz (1699)
events/treaty-of-karlowitz-1699.content:
Treaty of Karlowitz (1699)
The Great Turkish War
The Treaty of Karlowitz, signed on January 26, 1699, brought an end to sixteen years of war between the Ottoman Empire and the Holy League — an alliance of the Habsburg Monarchy, the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, the Republic of Venice, and, after 1686, the Russian Tsardom. The war had begun with Ottoman confidence at its height: the grand vizier Kara Mustafa Pasha led an army of over 100,000 men to the gates of Vienna in 1683, determined to capture the Habsburg capital and break the power of Christian Europe once and for all.
The siege failed. The arrival of the Polish king John III Sobieski and his winged hussars on September 12, 1683 shattered the Ottoman army and set in motion a chain of defeats that would strip the empire of its European conquests. Habsburg armies under Charles of Lorraine and later Prince Eugene of Savoy pushed down the Danube, capturing Buda (1686), Belgrade (1688), and the great fortress of Temesvar. Venice, under Francesco Morosini, seized the Morea (the Peloponnese) in a series of campaigns between 1684 and 1687, including the bombardment and destruction of the Parthenon in Athens. Poland recovered Podolia. Russia — entering the war late — captured Azov from the Ottomans in 1696.
By 1697, the empire was exhausted. The treasury was empty, the timar system had collapsed, and the janissaries were mutinous. The crushing defeat of the Ottoman army at the Battle of Zenta on September 11, 1697 — where Prince Eugene annihilated the forces of Sultan Mustafa II while they were crossing the Tisza River — convinced even the most stubborn voices at the Porte that peace was necessary.
The Negotiations
Peace negotiations opened at Karlowitz (present-day Sremski Karlovci in Serbia) in November 1698. The Ottoman delegation was led by Rami Mehmed Pasha, the reis efendi (chief secretary of state), accompanied by the dragoman (chief interpreter) Alexander Mavrocordatos. The Habsburgs were represented by Count Luigi Ferdinando Marsigli, and the Venetians and Poles by their respective plenipotentiaries. The talks were mediated by English and Dutch ambassadors, who feared that the complete disintegration of the Ottoman Empire would dangerously strengthen Habsburg power.
The negotiations were conducted in a specially constructed circular pavilion with four separate entrances — one for each delegation — to avoid disputes over precedence. The Ottomans, unaccustomed to negotiating peace from a position of weakness, found the experience humiliating. Rami Mehmed Pasha, a man of the pen rather than the sword, showed considerable diplomatic skill in salvaging what he could from the wreckage of Ottoman ambitions.
The Terms
The treaty marked the first major territorial loss by the Ottoman Empire to its European neighbors and signaled the end of Ottoman expansion in Europe. Under its terms:
The Habsburg Monarchy received Hungary (except the Banat of Temesvar), Transylvania, Croatia, and Slavonia — nearly all of the territory that the Ottomans had conquered since the reign of Suleiman the Magnificent. The Republic of Venice retained the Morea, the island of Aegina, and the fortresses of Castelnuovo and Risano in Dalmatia — though Venice would lose the Morea again in the Ottoman reconquest of 1715. Poland-Lithuania recovered Podolia and the fortress of Kamieniec Podolski, which had fallen to the Ottomans in 1672. Russia negotiated a separate truce — the Treaty of Constantinople (1700) — which confirmed its capture of Azov and gave the Tsardom its first warm-water port on the Black Sea.
The Ottomans retained the Banat of Temesvar (their last territorial foothold north of the Danube) and the Morea remained under Venetian control only temporarily. The empire was no longer an expansionist power in Europe. Its frontier was now definitively drawn: the Danube and Sava rivers became the boundary with the Habsburgs, a line that would remain largely stable for the next century and a half.
Impact on the Ottoman State
Karlowitz is often taken as a watershed in Ottoman history — the moment when decline ceased to be a diagnosis and became an undeniable fact. The treaty forced the Ottoman political class to confront the empire’s military inferiority for the first time in a comprehensive way. The nasihatname tradition, always backward-looking in its prescriptions, gained new urgency. The loss of Hungary — the richest and most strategically important of the sultan’s European provinces — was a wound from which the classical Ottoman system never recovered.
Fiscally, the consequences were devastating. The war had cost far more than the empire could afford. The treasury had resorted to every expedient: debasement of the akçe, confiscation of officials’ estates, advance sale of tax-farms, and the introduction of the malikâne (life-term tax farm) system in 1695. The demobilization of the wartime army — much of it already a dysfunctional mixture of janissaries, provincial levies, and mercenaries — created a manpower crisis that fed into the unrest that exploded in the Edirne Vaq’ası just four years after the peace.
Militarily, Karlowitz triggered a period of reform. Sultan Mustafa II and his grand vizier, Grand Admiral Hüseyin Köprülü (who negotiated the Karlowitz terms), attempted to modernize the army along Western lines. A new engineering corps was created, French military advisers were consulted, and efforts were made to revive the timar system. None of these reforms succeeded in restoring the empire’s offensive capability, but they demonstrated that the shock of Karlowitz had at least broken the intellectual resistance to foreign models of warfare.
In Historical Memory
In the Ottoman historical tradition, Karlowitz became a symbol of national humiliation and a reference point for every subsequent discussion of reform. Sarı Mehmed Pasha, writing his Kitab-i Güldeste about 1715, does not dwell on Karlowitz directly — his frame of reference is still the ideal age of Suleiman the Magnificent — but the treaty’s shadow falls across every page of his book. The empty treasury, the neglected soldiers, the decayed fiefs, the emboldened enemies: these were the material conditions that Karlowitz made painfully visible.
The treaty also reshaped the politics of the Balkans. The Habsburg acquisition of Hungary and Transylvania brought Catholic rule to lands that had been under Ottoman administration for 150 years, setting the stage for the Rákóczi rebellion and the subsequent Habsburg consolidation. The Venetian occupation of the Morea introduced a brief episode of Catholic-Latin rule that ended with the Ottoman reconquest of 1715, only fifteen years after Karlowitz. The frontier on the Sava and Danube would hold, more or less, until the Balkan Wars of 1912-13 — a final territorial settlement for the Ottoman presence in Europe that had begun five centuries earlier on the plains of Thrace.

index.title: Ottoman History Wiki — Index
index.content:
Ottoman History Wiki — Index
Total pages: 67
Sources

kritobulos-history-of-mehmed — Kritobulos of Imbros’ contemporary Greek history of Mehmed II (1460s), covering the conquest of Constantinople and seventeen years of campaigns
tursun-beg-tarih-i-ebul-feth — Tursun Beg’s Ottoman chronicle of Mehmed the Conqueror (c. 1442–1488), covering his reign and the early Bayezid II period
george-sphrantzes-fall-of-byzantium — George Sphrantzes’ Byzantine eyewitness chronicle of the fall of Constantinople (1453)
fisher-foreign-relations-1481-1512 — Sydney Nettleton Fisher’s study of Ottoman diplomacy under Bayezid II (1481–1512)
lybyer-government-suleiman — Albert Howe Lybyer’s classic study of Ottoman institutions at their peak under Suleiman (1520–1566)
inalcik-ottoman-classical-age — Halil Inalcik’s magisterial survey of the Ottoman state, society, and economy from 1300 to 1600
ottoman-statecraft-book-of-counsel — Sarı Mehmed Pasha’s book of counsel for vezirs and governors (Naṣā’iḥ ül-vüzera), a late 17th-century mirror for princes on the decline of the Ottoman administration
stephens-incidents-of-travel — John Lloyd Stephens’ 1844 travelogue of Ottoman Egypt, the Sinai, and the Holy Land under Mehmed Ali Pasha’s rule

Foreign Relations and Diplomacy

foreign-relations-under-bayezid — Comprehensive treatment of Bayezid II’s diplomacy: the Cem crisis, Venice, Hungary, the Mamluks, Safavid Persia, Poland-Lithuania, the Crimean alliance, naval policy, tribute, and the diplomatic machinery

Internal Politics and Foreign Policy

internal-politics-and-foreign-policy — How the Janissaries, the ulema, vizieral factions, succession struggles, marriage alliances, and intelligence networks shaped Ottoman foreign relations

Key Figures

mehmed-ii — The Conqueror himself: biography, campaigns, character, and legacy
bayezid-ii — The Conqueror’s son, who consolidated the empire after Mehmed’s death and managed the Cem Sultan crisis
ahmed-iii — The Tulip Age sultan (r. 1703–1730), patron of reform literature, whose reign saw cultural efflorescence and the first Ottoman printing press
mustafa-ii — Sultan (r. 1695–1703) who personally led campaigns against the Habsburgs and was deposed in the Edirne Vaq’ası after the disaster at Zenta
suleiman-the-magnificent — Suleiman I, the tenth Ottoman sultan, legislator, and peak of Ottoman power
selim-i — Selim the Grim, conqueror of Egypt and Syria, who transformed the state into an Islamic caliphate
sokollu-mehmed-pasha — The Bosnian grand vizier who ran the empire under Selim II, builder of the Višegrad bridge
barbarossa-hayreddin-pasha — The great Ottoman admiral, victor of Preveza, who dominated the Mediterranean for Suleiman
osman-i — The founder of the Ottoman dynasty, ghazi frontier leader who established the beylik at Söğüt
orhan-i — The second Ottoman ruler, who captured Bursa, created the first standing army, and crossed into Europe at Gallipoli
mahmud-pasha — Grand Vizier of Mehmed II, commander of the major campaigns of 1456–1466
gedik-ahmed-pasha — Janissary general who rose from the ranks, served Mehmed II and Bayezid, and was executed in 1482
cem-sultan — Mehmed II’s younger son, whose imprisonment by the Hospitallers became a major diplomatic crisis
zaganos-pasha — Zağanos Pasha, Mehmed II’s trusted vizier and fleet commander, key figure in the conquest of Constantinople and the early campaigns
constantine-xi — Constantine XI Palaiologos, the last Byzantine Emperor, who fell defending Constantinople in 1453
john-hunyadi — John Hunyadi, Hungarian regent and military commander, victor at the Siege of Belgrade (1456)
matthias-corvinus — Matthias Corvinus, King of Hungary, opponent of Mehmed II in Bosnia, builder of the Black Army
david-comnenus — David Comnenus, the last Emperor of Trebizond, deposed and executed by Mehmed II in 1463
vlad-the-impaler — Vlad III Dracula, voivode of Wallachia, whose rebellion against Mehmed in 1462 led to the night attack and Ottoman invasion
skanderbeg — George Kastrioti Skanderbeg, leader of the Albanian revolt against Ottoman rule in the 1460s
uzun-hasan — Uzun Hasan, ruler of the Aq Qoyunlu confederation, defeated by Mehmed at the Battle of Otlukbeli (1473)
demetrios-palaiologos — Demetrios Palaiologos, Despot of the Morea, whose invitation to Mehmed II led to the Ottoman conquest of the Peloponnese
thomas-palaiologos — Thomas Palaiologos, Despot of the Morea, rival of Demetrios, who fled to Rome after the Ottoman conquest
sari-mehmed-pasha — Sarı Mehmed Pasha, seven-time chief defterdar under Ahmed III, author of the reformist Kitab-i Güldeste (Naṣā’iḥ ül-vüzera)
koci-bey — Koçi Bey, 17th-century palace official and author of the most influential Ottoman reform treatise, the Risale (c. 1630)

Mehmed II’s Campaigns (1453–1473)

conquest-of-constantinople-1453 — The siege and fall of Constantinople, the defining event of Mehmed’s reign
conquest-of-peloponnese — The complete Ottoman subjugation of the Peloponnese (Morea) in 1458–1460, ending the Despotate of the Morea
conquest-of-trebizond — The 1461 campaign that destroyed the Empire of Trebizond, including the surrender of Sinope and the crossing of the Taurus
conquest-of-bosnia — The 1463 conquest of the Kingdom of Bosnia, the sieges of Jajce, and the Hungarian counter-campaign
ottoman-venetian-war-1463-1479 — The first major Ottoman-Venetian war under Mehmed II, covering the battles at the Isthmus, Argos, and Patras

Later Wars (16th Century)

ottoman-venetian-war-1499-1503 — Bayezid II’s naval war with Venice over control of the Morea and the Aegean
sahkulu-rebellion-1511 — The Safavid-inspired Şahkulu uprising that nearly toppled Bayezid II

Later Events (17th–18th Century)

treaty-of-karlowitz-1699 — The watershed treaty ending the Great Turkish War, marking the first major Ottoman territorial cessions in Europe (Hungary, Transylvania, Morea)
edirne-vaqasi-1703 — The “Adrianople Happening”: the revolt that deposed Mustafa II, brought Ahmed III to power, and forced the return of the court to Constantinople

Institutions and Culture

the-janissaries-under-mehmed — The Janissary corps during Mehmed II’s reign: expansion, discipline, role in the conquest
mehmeds-building-program — Mehmed’s architectural and urban rebuilding of Constantinople after the conquest: mosques, palaces, fortresses, and infrastructure
ruling-institution-slave-family — The kapıkulu system: the sultan’s slave-administration recruited through the devşirme
devsirme-and-palace-school — The child levy system and the Enderun palace school that trained the sultan’s slave-administration
military-organization — The Janissaries, Sipahis of the Porte, feudal cavalry, auxiliary troops, and the timar system under Suleiman
court-and-government — The imperial household, the grand vizierate, the divan, taxation, and provincial administration at the empire’s peak
moslem-institution-ulema — The ulema, şeyhülislam, kazaskers, kadis, medreses, vakıf system, and the parallel religious-legal hierarchy
law-kanun-and-seriat — The dual system of Islamic divine law (şeriat) and sultanic secular law (kanun), with Suleiman’s Kanun-nameh
nasihatname-tradition — The Ottoman book-of-counsel genre: Lütfi Pasha, Koçi Bey, Katib Chelebi, Sarı Mehmed Pasha, and the reformist literature on decline
timar-decline — The collapse of the classical timar system: corruption of alay beys, sepet timarı, flight from military service, and the shift to tax-farming
fiscal-administration — The defterdar, the twenty-five bureaus, iltizam tax-farming, the malikâne system, debasement, and the fiscal crises of the 17th–18th centuries
sufi-orders — The major Sufi orders (Bektashi, Mevlevi, Halveti, Kadiri, Nakşbendi), the tekke and zaviye system, and their role in Ottoman society
culture-and-learning — The medrese system, ulema hierarchy, Ottoman historiography, poetry, and intellectual life

Economy, Trade, and Society

trade-venice-genoa-europe — Ottoman trade with Venice, Genoa, and Europe: capitulations, the bailo, merchant colonies, and the shift to French and English dominance
silk-route-under-ottoman — Bursa as silk centre, the Tabriz-Bursa route, Venetian and Florentine merchants, and the decline of the overland silk trade
spice-routes-under-ottoman — The Egypt-Syria spice axis, Portuguese diversion, Ottoman attempts to redirect trade through the Red Sea
guilds-and-esnaf — The esnaf system, guild hierarchy, ihtisab and the muhtesib, narh price controls, and the Ahi brotherhood
markets-and-agriculture — The çarşı and bedesten system, land tenure, the çift-hane peasant farm, agricultural taxation, and the impact of the price revolution
city-structure — The mahalle, bedesten, külliye, vakıf system, and the physical and social organization of Ottoman cities
roads-and-monetary-system — The road network, menzil post-station system, the akçe, debasement, and the impact of American silver
maritime-infrastructure — The Imperial Arsenal, galley fleet, Kapudan Pasha, Barbary corsairs, and the Black Sea as an Ottoman lake

Comparative Studies

mughal-ottoman-compared — Lybyer’s systematic comparison of the Mughal and Ottoman empires: institutions, wealth, religion, army, court, and government

Secondary Sources

History of Mehmed the Conqueror — Kritovoulos, trans. Charles T. Riggs (Princeton, 1954)

primary-sources/fisher-foreign-relations.title: Sydney Nettleton Fisher — The Foreign Relations of Turkey, 1481–1512
primary-sources/fisher-foreign-relations.content:
Sydney Nettleton Fisher — The Foreign Relations of Turkey, 1481–1512
Sydney Nettleton Fisher’s The Foreign Relations of Turkey, 1481–1512 (University of Illinois Press, 1948) is a scholarly study of Ottoman diplomacy during the reign of Bayezid II, covering the period from Mehmed II’s death through the early years of the 16th century. The work challenges the traditional view of Bayezid as a “slothful sultan,” arguing instead that he recognized the need to consolidate the empire his father had shaped after decades of continuous expansion.
Contents
Preface and Introduction
Fisher opens by noting how Bayezid II has been overshadowed by both his father Mehmed the Conqueror and his son Selim the Grim. He questions the conventional historical verdict that Bayezid’s reign was “feeble and inglorious,” pointing instead to the strategic consolidation of Ottoman power in the Balkans, Anatolia, and the Black Sea that occurred under his rule.
Relations with Venice
The ongoing Ottoman-Venetian conflict that had begun under Mehmed II, including the peace settlement and the shifting balance of power in the Aegean and the Adriatic.
Relations with Hungary and the Habsburgs
Ottoman-Hungarian border conflicts, the diplomatic and military struggle over the Danubian principalities, and the early emergence of Habsburg-Ottoman rivalry.
Relations with the Mamluk Sultanate
The growing tension between the Ottomans and the Mamluks over control of Anatolia and the holy cities of Islam, culminating in the conflicts that would lead to Selim I’s conquest of Egypt.
Relations with Safavid Persia
The rise of Shah Ismail and the Safavid order, the spread of Shia propaganda in Anatolia, and the early stages of the Ottoman-Safavid rivalry that would define the 16th century.
The Succession of Cem Sultan
The diplomatic crisis surrounding Prince Cem’s claim to the Ottoman throne, his flight to Rhodes, his imprisonment by the Hospitallers in Europe, and the international complications this created with France, the Papacy, and Naples.
Relations with Poland-Lithuania and Russia
Ottoman expansion into the Black Sea region, the conquest of Kili and Akkirman, and diplomatic contacts with the Grand Duchy of Moscow.
Relations with the Barbary States and the Western Mediterranean
The growth of Ottoman naval power and the emergence of the Barbary corsairs as an extension of Ottoman influence in the western Mediterranean.

primary-sources/george-sphrantzes-fall-o.title: George Sphrantzes — The Fall of the Byzantine Empire
primary-sources/george-sphrantzes-fall-o.content:
George Sphrantzes — The Fall of the Byzantine Empire
George Sphrantzes (Phrantzes, 1401–1477) was a Byzantine courtier, diplomat, and historian who served under the last Palaiologan emperors. His chronicle, which ends with his death in 1477, is one of the few Byzantine eyewitness accounts of the fall of Constantinople. The English translation by Marios Philippides (University of Massachusetts Press, 1980) includes both the genuine shorter chronicle (Chronicon Minus) and the expanded later version (Chronicon Maius), together with a substantial introduction covering the manuscript tradition, the historical context, and the problem of authorship.
Contents
Translator’s Introduction
Philippides’ introduction explains that the text now known as Sphrantzes’ chronicle is actually two separate works. The Chronicon Minus is the authentic work by Sphrantzes himself — a brief personal memoir covering events from 1401 to 1477. The Chronicon Maius is a much longer version that was expanded in the 1570s by Makarios Melissenos (also called Melissourgos), a Greek cleric and adventurer who fled to Naples after the failed Morean revolt following the Battle of Lepanto (1571). Melissenos is known to have forged documents, including a counterfeit imperial decree with a fake seal of Emperor Andronikos II. His expanded version of Sphrantzes incorporates material from other sources — notably the Latin account of Bishop Leonard of Chios, who was present at the siege — and adds digressions on the errors of Islam, the origin of earthquakes, and a forged letter from Cardinal Bessarion.
The introduction also covers the historical situation of the Byzantine Empire in the 15th century: the population knew themselves as “Romans” (Romaioi), the term “Byzantine” being a 17th-century French coinage. The final decades of the empire were marked by Ottoman pressure, civil conflict among the Palaiologoi, and desperate diplomatic overtures to the West culminating in the abortive Union of Churches at the Council of Florence (1439).
Book I — Origins and Early History (to 1425)
Covers the origins of the Palaiologan dynasty with an excursus into early Ottoman history. Events from 1401 to 1412 are treated in detail, including the Ottoman Interregnum and the early career of Manuel II Palaiologos. The book concludes with the death of Manuel II.
Book II — The Reign of John VIII Palaiologos (1425–1448)
Events during the reign of John VIII, including the Council of Ferrara-Florence and the Union of the Churches, the Ottoman siege of Constantinople in 1422, and the diplomatic manoeuvring between Byzantium and the West. Notably includes a digression on John VIII’s efforts to convert a Jew.
Book III — The Reign of Constantine XI and the Fall of Constantinople (1448–1453)
The centerpiece of the chronicle: the reign of the last Byzantine emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos. This book includes a detailed description of the 1453 siege and sack of Constantinople — the Byzantine perspective on the Ottoman bombardment, the naval blockade and the chain across the Golden Horn, the final assault on 29 May 1453, the death of Constantine XI, and the sack of the city. The Chronicon Maius version of this section draws heavily on Bishop Leonard of Chios’ Latin account, sometimes paraphrasing or directly translating it. The Chronicon Minus treats the siege only briefly — Sphrantzes himself was present but was captured during the sack, so his personal account records his own experience of the fall rather than a comprehensive military narrative.
Book IV — The Fall of the Morea (1453–1460)
Continues the story after Constantinople’s fall. The Morea (Peloponnese), still under the rule of Thomas and Demetrios Palaiologos, descends into civil conflict. The Ottoman conquest of the peninsula is completed in 1460. Many details not present in the Minus were added by Melissenos, especially regarding Monemvasia. Includes a letter supposedly written by Cardinal Bessarion, a digression on the errors of Islam, and a section on earthquakes.
Personal Narrative — Capture, Slavery, and Ransom
Throughout the chronicle, Sphrantzes records his own life: his service as a court official, his diplomatic missions, his presence during the final days of the empire. He was captured in the sack of Constantinople in May 1453, enslaved, and later ransomed in September 1453. After his release he travelled to the Morea and continued to serve until the Ottoman conquest was complete.

primary-sources/inalcik-ottoman-classica.title: Halil Inalcik — The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age 1300–1600
primary-sources/inalcik-ottoman-classica.content:
Halil Inalcik — The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age 1300–1600
Halil Inalcik’s magisterial survey (Weidenfeld & Nicolson, 1973; Phoenix Press, 2001) is the standard English-language overview of the Ottoman classical period. It covers the full arc from the origins of the Ottoman beylik around 1300 through the peak under Suleiman to the beginning of transformation around 1600, in two integrated halves: a chronological narrative and a thematic analysis of state, economy, and culture.
Part One: An Outline of Ottoman History, 1300–1600
Introduction: The Periods of Ottoman History — Inalcik’s periodization of Ottoman history: the frontier principality (1300–1453), the classical age (1453–1600), and the transformation that followed.
Chapter I: The Origins of the Ottoman State — The situation of Anatolia under the Seljuks and the Mongol Ilkhanids, the rise of Osman and the early beylik, the gaza tradition, and the debate over Ottoman origins.
Chapter II: From Frontier Principality to Empire, 1354–1402 — The conquest of Gallipoli, expansion into the Balkans under Murad I and Bayezid I, the Battle of Kosovo (1389), the Battle of Nicopolis (1396), and the defeat by Timur at Ankara (1402).
Chapter III: The Interregnum and Recovery — The civil war among Bayezid I’s sons, the reunification under Mehmed I, and the consolidation under Murad II.
Chapter IV: The Definitive Establishment of the Ottoman Empire, 1453–1526 — The reign of Mehmed II: the conquest of Constantinople, the rebuilding of the capital, the codification of laws, the campaigns in the Balkans and Anatolia, and the consolidation of the empire. The reign of Bayezid II and the Cem crisis. The reign of Selim I: the conquests of Syria, Egypt, and the Holy Cities. The Battle of Chaldiran (1514).
Chapter V: The Ottoman State as a World Power, 1526–96 — The reign of Suleiman: the conquest of Belgrade, Rhodes, Mohács, the siege of Vienna, the annexation of Iraq, the domination of the Mediterranean. The reigns of Selim II and Murad III.
Chapter VI: The Decline of the Ottoman Empire — Inalcik’s analysis of the decline thesis: the transformation of the military (the breakdown of the timar system, the rise of the Janissaries as a political force), the price revolution, the Celali rebellions, and the institutional changes of the late 16th century.
Part Two: The State
Chapter VII: The Rise of the Ottoman Dynasty — The origins and early development of the house of Osman, the problem of succession, the role of the dynasty in the state.
Chapter VIII: The Manner of Accession to the Throne — The evolution of succession practices from the early period through Mehmed II’s law of fratricide, the role of the Janissaries in accession, and the cülûs ceremony.
Chapter IX: The Ottoman Concept of State and the Class System — The Ottoman theory of state (devlet), the division of society into the Ruling Class (askerî) and the subjects (reaya), the legal and social distinctions between the two.
Chapter X: Law: Sultanic Law (Kanun) and Religious Law (Şeriat) — The dual legal system, the development of kanun from Mehmed II to Suleiman, the role of the şeyhülislam, the Kanun-nameh of Mehmed II and Suleiman, and the relationship between secular and sacred law.
Chapter XI: The Palace — The organization of the imperial palace, the inner service (enderun) and outer service (birun), the palace school, the harem, and the role of the palace in government.
Chapter XII: The Central Administration — The grand vizierate, the imperial council (divan), the financial administration (defterdarlık), the chancery (nişancı), and the judicial hierarchy (kazasker).
Chapter XIII: The Provincial Administration and the Timar System — The beylerbey and sancakbey system, the timar as the basic unit of provincial military and fiscal organization, the zeamet and has grants, and the relationship between land tenure and military service.
Part Three: Economic and Social Life
Chapter XIV: The Ottoman Empire and International Trade — Ottoman trade policy, the capitulations, trade routes (the Silk Road, the spice route, the Black Sea), relations with Venice, Genoa, and the Mediterranean powers, and the impact of Portuguese exploration.
Chapter XV: The Ottoman Cities and Road Network, Urban Population, Guilds and Merchants — The structure of Ottoman cities, the road system linking Europe, Asia, and Africa, urban demography, the guild organization (esnaf), the merchant class, and the market regulations (ihtisab).
Part Four: Religion and Culture
Chapter XVI: Learning, the Medrese and the Ulema — The medrese system, the hierarchy of teaching licenses, the curriculum, the career of the ulema, and the relationship between religious learning and state service.
Chapter XVII: Ottoman Scholarship — The major Ottoman scholars and historians, the development of Ottoman historiography, and the intellectual production of the classical age.
Chapter XVIII: The Triumph of Fanaticism — The rise of religious orthodoxy and the Kadizadeli movement, the suppression of Sufi orders and popular religion, and the shift in the intellectual climate of the 17th century.
Chapter XIX: Popular Culture and the Tarikats — Mystic Orders — The role of the dervish orders (tarikats), Bektaşism, Mevlevism, and popular religious practice in Ottoman society.

primary-sources/kritobulos-history-of-me.title: Kritobulos of Imbros — History of Mehmed the Conqueror
primary-sources/kritobulos-history-of-me.content:
Kritobulos of Imbros — History of Mehmed the Conqueror
Kritobulos of Imbros was a Greek scholar, administrator, and historical writer who served under Mehmed II after the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople. His history, composed in classical Greek in the 1460s, covers the first seventeen years of Mehmed’s reign (1451–1467). It is a contemporary account written from within the Ottoman system, by a Greek subject who held office under the sultan. The text was discovered in the Serail library in Constantinople in the 1860s by a series of European scholars — Tischendorf, Miller, and Newton — and first published as a Greek edition by Karl Müller. The 1875 Budapest edition by Szabó Károly includes the Greek text, a Hungarian translation, and additional contemporary writers. Kritobulos opens his work with a philosophical justification: he writes to record Mehmed’s deeds in the Hellenic tongue so that they might be known to “every civilized nation, and even to those who dwell beyond the Pillars of Hercules and on the British Isles.”
Contents
The Dedicatory Letter (To Sultan Mehmed II)
Kritobulos opens with a formal Renaissance-era dedication in nine sections. He addresses Mehmed as “the greatest emperor, king of kings, fortunate, victorious, triumphant, invincible Mehmed, by God’s will lord of earth and sea.” He argues that it would be unjust for the deeds of ancient commanders — Alexander, the Persian kings, the Roman generals — to be preserved in Greek historical writing while Mehmed’s far greater achievements are allowed to “sink into the abyss of oblivion.” He singles out Mehmed as the ruler who “hast united deeds with words and philosophy with rulership.” He defends his choice of Greek over Arabic and Persian, arguing that Greek holds “the greatest honor in the world” and that his work will reach not only the Hellenes but all civilized nations through translation. The letter outlines the scope of the work: the war against the Romans and the capture of Constantinople, the campaigns in Ainos, the Peloponnese, Sinope, Trebizond, the rebellion of Drakuly (Vlad the Impaler) and the Getae, the conquest of Mytilene and Lesbos, the Bosnian and Paeonian campaigns, the Illyrian campaigns, the five-year war against the Venetians, and the building projects within the city — all within a seventeen-year period.
The Preface
Kritobulos explains his historiographical method. He states his intent to write “everything exactly as it happened, point by point, adapting my narrative to the facts, never separating the deeds from the circumstances, maintaining the order of persons and events.” He anticipates criticism from fellow Greeks who might accuse him of publicizing his own nation’s misfortunes. He defends himself with a long philosophical reflection on the cyclical nature of empire: “For who does not know that ever since men have lived, rule and power have not remained with the same people in general, nor have they been confined to one nation or people, but rather, as if wandering and migrating from people to people and from place to place, they have traveled everywhere?” He cites the example of the Jewish historian Josephus, who praised Roman virtue while condemning wicked individuals within his own nation. The preface establishes the work’s intellectual framework: this is a Thucydidean-style history written by a Greek who accepts Ottoman rule as a historical inevitability.
Book 1 — The Accession and the Conquest of Constantinople (1451–1453)
The death of Murad II in 1451 in the sixty-ninth year of the Byzantine era (AD 1451). Mehmed’s accession at age twenty, coming over from Asia. The extraordinary celestial portents at his birth and accession — earthquakes, lightning, thunder, and nocturnal lights — which the seers and soothsayers interpreted as heralding a ruler who “would have the greatest power in the world.” Mehmed’s character as described by Kritoboulos: a highly learned ruler who understood Arabic and Persian literature, the works of the Peripatetics and Stoics, and advanced mathematics, keeping before his mind “the Alexanders, the Pompeys, and the emperors, kings and generals of that time.”
Mehmed’s first acts as sultan: making a treaty with Emperor Constantine XI, reviewing the entire empire, establishing law in the provinces, replacing governors, inspecting military registers, increasing the Janissary corps, auditing the treasury, and recovering wasted public revenues. The strategic plan to build a fortress on the European shore of the Bosporus to control the strait and lay the groundwork for the siege of Constantinople.
The construction of the fortress (Rumeli Hisarı) in the spring of 1452. The detailed description of the site: the narrowness of the strait, the swift currents, the whirlpools — a place which “the ancient Hellenes also observed, calling this place ‘Straits.‘” Mehmed himself marks out the foundations with stakes, draws the plan of the fortress with its triangular shape, seventeen-cubit-thick walls, and massive bastions.
