The fortress is completed before the summer ends, using huge stones clamped with iron and lead, and equipped with stone-throwing engines that “skipped along the surface of the sea” to prevent any ship from passing without permission.
The embassy of Emperor Constantine attempting to prevent the construction and Mehmed’s reply: “I believe, O Roman men, that I am neither harming you nor acting against the treaties and agreements with this matter, but rather securing my own property.” The accidental skirmish that triggers open war. Mehmed’s speech to his commanders urging war against Constantinople, recounting the history of Ottoman expansion from the Taurus mountains to the Danube, the defeats suffered under Bayezid at the hands of the West and under Murad against Timur, and the constant subversion from the city.
The spring 1453 siege. The land army, the fleet of galleys, the great bombard cannons casting huge stone balls. The extraordinary transport of Ottoman ships overland past the Byzantine chain across the Golden Horn — seventy to eighty ships dragged across a makeshift greased track built over several miles. The final assault on 29 May 1453, the breaching of the walls, the death of Justiniani (the Genoese commander) and of Emperor Constantine XI, the sack of the city and the looting of churches. Mehmed’s entry into the city. Kritoboulos compares the fall to Troy, Babylon, Carthage, Rome, and Jerusalem. Mehmed’s orders to stop the looting and his plans for resettlement.
Book 2 — The Aegean, Serbia, and the Belgrade Campaign (1455–1457)
The repopulation and rebuilding of Constantinople: Mehmed brings settlers from all parts of the empire, grants them houses and property, restores the aqueducts, builds the Grand Bazaar, establishes markets and baths. The appointment of Gennadios (George Scholarios) as the first Ecumenical Patriarch under Ottoman rule, with full ecclesiastical authority.
The fleet expedition against Rhodes and Naxos: a catastrophic storm destroys twenty-five Ottoman ships, forcing the fleet to return. Kritoboulos describes the storm in vivid detail — the wind, the towering waves, the wreckage scattered along the coast.
The affair of Ainos (Enez): the usurpation by Dorieusz after the death of the legitimate lord, the widow’s appeal to Mehmed, the Ottoman occupation. Kritoboulos himself is appointed governor of Imbros.
The war against the Triballians (Serbs). The geography of Serbia and the history of Murad II’s prior conquests. The rebellion of Lazar Branković. Mehmed’s campaign, the siege of Novo Brdo (the famous Serbian silver-mining town), and the treaty with Lazar.
The Belgrade campaign of 1456. Mehmed’s attempt to capture the great Hungarian fortress of Belgrade, defended by John Hunyadi. The fierce Ottoman assault, Hunyadi’s counterattack, the Ottoman retreat. Kritoboulos records the death of Hunyadi shortly after the campaign. Lazar’s imprisonment and ransom, his death, and the final Ottoman conquest of Serbia.
City improvements in Constantinople: bridges, paved roads, the bazaar complex, aqueducts, public baths. Mehmed’s vision for restoring the city to its former glory.
Book 3 — The Peloponnese Campaigns (1458–1460)
Mehmed’s first campaign into the Peloponnese (Morea) in 1458. The advance through the Isthmus of Corinth, the siege and capture of Corinth (the key fortress controlling access to the peninsula), the submission of Patras and Tegea. The despots Thomas and Demetrios Palaiologos, who ruled the Morea under Ottoman suzerainty, descend into civil war.
The naval expedition against Lesbos (Mytilene). The recovery of Imbros and Lemnos from the Venetians and papal forces. Kritoboulos provides a detailed account of the Ottoman fleet operations in the Aegean.
Mehmed’s second Peloponnesian campaign in 1460. The complete conquest of the Morea: Demetrios Palaiologos surrenders to Mehmed, Thomas Palaiologos flees to Corcyra (Corfu) and then to Italy. Kritoboulos records the capture of approximately 250 fortresses and strongholds across the peninsula. The destruction of the fortifications of Kasztrion and Gardikion.
Extended descriptions of Mehmed’s building program in Constantinople: the construction of the new mosque (the Fatih Mosque complex), the imperial palace (the Old Palace and Topkapı), the shipyards at the Golden Horn, and the foundations established by Grand Vizier Mahmud Pasha.
Book 4 — Sinope, Trebizond, Wallachia, and Bosnia (1460–1463)
The conquest of Sinope in 1461. The surrender of İsmail Bey of the İsfendiyarids, who surrenders his capital in exchange for the governorship of Scopia (Skopje). Kritoboulos notes Mehmed’s strategic reasoning: Sinope was one of the key Black Sea ports, and its acquisition secured the Anatolian coast.
The crossing of the Taurus Mountains. An ambush attempt on Mahmud Pasha by local forces is thwarted. The appearance of Hasan of Tigranocerta, who submits via his mother’s intervention. The approach to Trebizond.
The siege and surrender of Trebizond (Trabzon), the capital of the Empire of Trebizond under Emperor David Komnenos. David Komnenos is deported with his family. The philosopher George Amiroutzes, a prominent Trebizondine scholar, enters Mehmed’s circle. Fifteen hundred youths are conscripted into the Janissary corps. Kasim Pasha is appointed governor of the new province.
The rebellion of Vlad III Dracula (Drakuly) in Wallachia. Vlad’s refusal to pay tribute, his infamous impaling of Ottoman prisoners, and the Ottoman campaign against him. Vlad’s night attack on the Ottoman camp. His defeat and flight to Hungary. His brother Radu the Fair installed as the Ottoman vassal voivode of Wallachia.
The conquest of Lesbos (Mytilene) in 1462. The betrayal of Nicorezzo Gattilusio, the Genoese lord of the island. His execution. The island’s population is divided: some remain under Ottoman rule, some are sent to Constantinople for resettlement, and some are enslaved.
The fortification of the Dardanelles: Mehmed’s naval buildup and the construction of the twin fortresses (Kilitbahir and Çimenlik) to control the strait.
The first Bosnian campaign. The siege of Jajce, the royal capital of the Kingdom of Bosnia. The capture of approximately three hundred fortresses and strongholds. The submission and execution of King Stephen Tomašević.
Book 5 — The Venetian War and the Albanian Campaigns (1464–1467)
The outbreak of the Ottoman-Venetian war. The naval battle at the Isthmus of Corinth. The fall of Argos to the Ottomans. The Venetian siege of Mytilene (Lesbos), which the Ottomans had captured two years earlier. The Venetian campaign against Patras. Naval engagements at Tenedos.
The second siege of Jajce in Bosnia against the Hungarian king (referred to as the “Paeonians” in Kritoboulos’ classical terminology). Mahmud Pasha’s pursuit and rout of the Hungarian king’s army.
The first Albanian campaign. The revolt of Skanderbeg (George Kastrioti İskender Bey). The seizure of the mountain passes, the devastation of the Albanian countryside. Mehmed’s construction of the fortress of Elbasan (Ilbasan) in central Albania as a permanent Ottoman base — a rectangular fortress with strong walls, designed to dominate the region and supply future campaigns.
The second Albanian campaign. The siege of Croia (Krujë), Skanderbeg’s capital. Mehmed’s efforts to capture the strategically vital fortress, which held out through repeated assaults. Kritoboulos provides a description of the mountainous Albanian terrain and the difficulties of campaigning there.
The construction of Mehmed’s new palace in Constantinople. Kritoboulos describes the splendour of the palace architecture, the gardens, the courtyards, and the audience halls.
The translation of Ptolemy’s Geography into Arabic by the philosopher George (Amiroutzes), commissioned by Mehmed — evidence of the sultan’s personal interest in geography and cartography.
The great plague of 1467 in Thrace, Macedonia, and Constantinople. Kritoboulos provides a detailed clinical description of the plague’s symptoms, its spread through the population, and its devastating mortality.
The appearance of a comet in 1468, which Kritoboulos records as an atmospheric portent.

primary-sources/lybyer-government-suleim.title: Albert Howe Lybyer — The Government of the Ottoman Empire in the Time of Suleiman the Magnificent
primary-sources/lybyer-government-suleim.content:
Albert Howe Lybyer — The Government of the Ottoman Empire in the Time of Suleiman the Magnificent
Albert Howe Lybyer’s classic study (Harvard Historical Studies, Vol. XVIII, 1913) is a foundational English-language work on Ottoman institutional history. Lybyer’s central thesis distinguishes two parallel structures: the Ruling Institution (kapıkulu), the sultan’s personal slave-administration recruited through the devşirme, and the Moslem Institution, the religious and legal hierarchy of the ulema. The work is built on primary sources including Ottoman chronicles, European traveller accounts (Benedetto Ramberti’s 1534 description of the Ottoman court), the kanun-nameh of Suleiman, and the writings of Mufti Ebu Su’ud. The text is from Project Gutenberg, fully clean and readable (13,800 lines, 819 KB).
Chapter I — The Character of the Ottoman State in General
Lybyer opens by defining the Ottoman state as a despotism limited by Islamic law (şeriat), the sultan’s own kanun (secular law), and the practical constraints of governing a multi-ethnic empire. He surveys the territorial basis of the empire under Suleiman, the peoples subject to it, and the two great institutions of government: the Ruling Institution and the Moslem Institution. He cites contemporary European descriptions of the Ottoman system.
Chapter II — The Ruling Institution: As a Slave-Family
The core of Lybyer’s analysis. The Ruling Institution was the sultan’s personal slave-administration — the kapıkulu. Lybyer describes the methods of recruiting the “tribute boys” (devşirme), their slave status, their training, and their absolute dependence on the sultan. He covers the harem, the eunuchs, the royal family, and other Ottoman slave-families. He argues that Ottoman slavery was fundamentally different from classical or American slavery: the slaves of the sultan held the highest offices in the empire.
Chapter III — The Ruling Institution: As Missionary Enterprise and Educational System
The ideological dimension: the requirement of conversion to Islam for devşirme boys, the sincerity of conversion, and the missionary motive behind the system. The educational scheme covers the colleges of pages in the palace, the harem as an educational space, the acemi oğlan (apprentice Janissaries) system, and the principle of advancement based on merit. Lybyer includes details on punishments and discipline within the palace school.
Chapter IV — The Ruling Institution: As an Army
The military organization of the empire. The Janissary corps (yeniçeri): organization, recruitment, pay, and role. The sipahis of the Porte (the household cavalry). The feudal sipahis (provincial timar-holders) and their officers. Other troop types: azabs, akıncıs, topçus (artillerymen), cebecis (armorers). Discipline, ardor, and the supreme command. Lybyer notes the indivisibility of the army as an instrument of the sultan’s will.
Chapter V — The Ruling Institution: As a Nobility and a Court
The privileges of the kullar (slaves of the sultan) and the deliberate policy of making nobility non-hereditary — the sultan’s slaves could not pass their status or offices to their children. The organization of the sultan’s court: the inside service (the enderun, palace pages), the outside service (birun, the outer palace), the ceremonies of the court, and the influence of court life on Ottoman governance.
Chapter VI — The Ruling Institution: As Government
The administrative structure. The sultan as head of state and legislator: the distinction between şeriat (divine law) and kanun (sultanic law). The legislation of Suleiman, including the Kanun-nameh (law code). The viziers and the grand vizierate, the defterdars (treasurers), taxation in the Ottoman Empire, Suleiman’s income (detailed figures), the nişancı (chancellor who affixed the imperial tuğra), and the imperial council (divan).
Chapter VII — The Moslem Institution of the Ottoman Empire
The religious and legal hierarchy. Financial support of the Moslem Institution through vakıf (pious foundations). The educational system (medreses). The clergy, seids (descendants of the Prophet), and dervish orders. The jurists and the müftü (chief jurisconsult, the şeyhülislam). The judicial system and the role of the kadı (judge). Lybyer presents the Moslem Institution as a parallel structure to the Ruling Institution, with its own hierarchy, training, and career paths.
Chapter VIII — Comparison of the Two Great Institutions
Lybyer’s synthesizing chapter: likenesses between the Ruling and Moslem Institutions, their differences, their interactions, and their relative power. He concludes that under Suleiman the Ruling Institution was dominant, but the Moslem Institution provided the legitimacy and legal framework that made the empire governable.
Appendices
Appendix I — The Second Book of the Affairs of the Turks (1534), supposedly by Benedetto Ramberti, translated from Italian. A first-hand European account of the Ottoman court and administration.
Appendix II — Pamphlet of Junis Bey and Alvise Gritti (1537), in the original Italian. A diplomatic document from the Franco-Ottoman alliance period.
Appendix III — Incomplete Table of Contents of the Kanun-Nameh of Suleiman the Magnificent as arranged by Mufti Ebu Su’ud, translated from Turkish. The actual law code sections covering criminal law, taxation, land tenure, and military organization.
Appendix IV — The Government of the Mogul Empire in India. A comparative study of Ottoman and Mughal institutions: personnel, government, army, court, and the role of Islam.
Appendix V — Bibliographical Notes. Covers the origins of Ottoman governmental ideas and works on the Ottoman government in the sixteenth century.

primary-sources/ottoman-statecraft-book-.title: Sarı Mehmed Pasha — Ottoman Statecraft: The Book of Counsel for Vezirs and Governors
primary-sources/ottoman-statecraft-book-.content:
Sarı Mehmed Pasha — Ottoman Statecraft: The Book of Counsel for Vezirs and Governors
Sarı Mehmed Pasha (d. 1717), known as the Defterdar (Chief Treasurer), was an Ottoman finance minister and state servant who served under Sultans Mehmed IV, Suleiman II, Ahmed II, Mustafa II, and Ahmed III. His Naṣā’iḥ ül-vüzera ve’l-ümera (Book of Counsel for Vezirs and Governors) is one of the most important surviving examples of Ottoman nasihatname (advice literature) from the post-classical period. The work was edited, translated, and published with a facing Turkish text by Walter Livingston Wright, Jr. of Princeton University in 1935 (reprinted by Greenwood Press, 1971). The book reflects on the administrative and fiscal decay of the Ottoman state in the late 17th and early 18th centuries, offering a diagnosis of the empire’s ills from the perspective of a veteran bureaucrat who witnessed the crises of the post-Köprülü period.
Contents
Preface
Wright opens by situating the work in the tradition of Ibn Khaldunian decline theory and Benjamin Davis Shreve’s foundation at Princeton for the study of the decay of nations. He notes that the decline of the Ottoman Empire has never received thorough scholarly attention, and the present work aims to fill that gap by publishing and translating a key Ottoman administrative text for the first time in a Western language.
Introduction — I. Authorship and Manuscripts
1. The Problem of Authorship. The anonymous Naṣā’iḥ ül-vüzera identifies its author as the defterdar in office at the accession of Ahmed III in A.H. 1115 (1703). Wright cross-references the Fındıqlılı Mehmed Agha continuation of Hajji Khalifa’s Fedhleke and the Ta’rikh-i Rashid to identify the author as Baqqāl-oghlu Şarı Ḥājji Meḥmed Efendi — later known as Sarı Mehmed Pasha — who served as Chief Defterdar during the crisis of the Edirne Vaqʻası (the 1703 rebellion that deposed Mustafa II).
2. Biographical Sketch. Sarı Mehmed Pasha’s career is traced from his early service as a treasury secretary through his appointment as defterdar, his role in the 1703 revolution, his dismissal, reinstatement as nişancı (chancellor), and eventual execution in 1717. His career illustrates the turbulence of Ottoman high administration in the period.
3. The Manuscripts. Wright describes the surviving manuscript copies of the work, including the Istanbul and Vienna manuscripts, their collation, and the textual problems involved in establishing the Turkish text.
Introduction — II. The Book
1. Predecessors of Mehmed Pasha. A survey of the Ottoman nasihatname tradition: earlier mirrors-for-princes such as Kınalızade’s Ahlak-ı Alai, Lütfi Pasha’s Asafname, and Koçi Bey’s Risale. Sarı Mehmed Pasha’s work is situated within this genre as a late-17th-century response to perceived imperial decline.
2. The Ottoman System. Wright provides an overview of the classical Ottoman administrative structure: the sultan’s authority, the Ruling Institution (kapıkulu slaves), the Muslim Institution (the ulema), the timar system, the Janissaries, and the fiscal apparatus. This section establishes the normative baseline against which the author’s complaints about corruption and decay are measured.
3. The Sultan. Ottoman theories of sovereignty: the sultan as shadow of God on earth, the limits of his authority imposed by Islamic law (şeriat) and custom (kanun), and the ideal of the just ruler.
4. The Grand Vezir. The grand vezir’s role as absolute deputy of the sultan, his ceremonial position, and the practical limits on his power imposed by palace intrigue, Janissary influence, and the ulema.
5. The Standing Army. The Janissary corps, the Topçu (artillery), and the other kapıkulu military corps: their organization, pay, and the erosion of discipline in the 17th century.
6. The Feudal Army. The timar-holding sipahis, the provincial cavalry, the decline of the timar system through tax-farming (iltizam) and its replacement by the malikâne system.
7. The Treasury. Ottoman fiscal administration: the treasury (hazine), the defterdar’s office, sources of revenue, the impact of debasement, the fiscal crises of the late 17th century.
8. Evils of Administration. Wright’s synthesis of the specific abuses that the author identifies: bribery, sale of offices, extortion of the peasantry, Janissary interference in government, neglect of the timar system, and the breakdown of the classical balance between the Ruling and Muslim Institutions.
9. Conclusions. The historiographical value of the work as a witness to Ottoman administrative thought and the conditions of the empire in the post-Karlowitz period (after the Treaty of Karlowitz, 1699).
Translation — The Text of the Naṣā’iḥ ül-vüzera ve’l-ümera
I. Prologue. The author’s invocation, his statement of purpose, and the occasion for the work: the sultan’s request for counsel on the condition of the empire.
II. First Chapter: On the Behavior and Habits of the Grand Vezir. The qualities required of a grand vezir — justice, piety, knowledge of finance, military experience — and the dangers of appointing unqualified men through bribery or favoritism.
III. Second Chapter: On Official Positions and the Harmfulness of Bribes. A detailed condemnation of the sale of offices (rüşvet) and its corrosive effect on the administration. The author traces specific administrative failures to the purchase of positions by unqualified candidates.
IV. Third Chapter: On the Secretary of the Treasury and the Members of the Divan. The proper conduct of the defterdar and the divan members, the importance of accurate record-keeping, and the need for accountability in fiscal matters.
V. Fourth Chapter: On the Condition of the Bektashi Corps. The Janissaries’ connection to the Bektashi Sufi order, the corruption and indiscipline that had overtaken the corps, and the need for reform.
VI. Fifth Chapter: On the Condition of the Rayas and the Harmfulness of Tyranny. The plight of the peasantry under oppressive tax-collection, the breakdown of the timar system, and the author’s argument that the prosperity of the state depends on the welfare of the tax-paying subjects.
VII. Sixth Chapter: On the Condition of the Enemy and the Ever-Victorious Frontier. The military situation on the empire’s frontiers after the setbacks of the Habsburg war, the qualities required of frontier commanders, and the defense of the borders.
VIII. Seventh Chapter: On Avarice and Liberality. A moral-ethical chapter on the virtues and vices of statesmen: greed, generosity, pride, humility, envy, hypocrisy, and their effects on administration.
IX. Eighth Chapter: On Faithful Friendship and the Harmfulness of Calumny. The importance of trustworthy counselors and the destructive role of slander and backbiting in the palace and the divan.
X. Ninth Chapter: On the State of the Zi’amet and Timar. The author’s most detailed administrative analysis: how the timar and zi’amet fiefs had been misappropriated, the loss of military manpower, and the need to restore the system to its classical form.
XI. Appendix I. Additional notes and observations on fiscal reform.
XII. Appendix II. Further recommendations on military and administrative matters.
Turkish Text
The facing Ottoman Turkish text of the Naṣā’iḥ ül-vüzera ve’l-ümera, transcribed and edited from the manuscript sources.
Index
A full index to the translation and introduction, arranged at the rear of the volume.

primary-sources/stephens-incidents-of-tr.title: John Lloyd Stephens — Incidents of Travel in Egypt, Arabia Petraea, and the Holy Land
primary-sources/stephens-incidents-of-tr.content:
John Lloyd Stephens — Incidents of Travel in Egypt, Arabia Petraea, and the Holy Land
John Lloyd Stephens (1805–1852) was an American lawyer, diplomat, traveler, and the pioneer of Maya archaeology. His Incidents of Travel in Egypt, Arabia Petraea, and the Holy Land (Harper & Brothers, 1844) recounts his journey through Ottoman Egypt, the Sinai Peninsula, and Ottoman Syria (including Palestine) in 1835–1836. The work is one of the most popular 19th-century American travelogues of the Middle East and provides extensive first-hand observation of Ottoman provincial administration, the hajj route, Bedouin society, the condition of Christian and Jewish communities, and the monuments of Pharaonic and Biblical antiquity under the rule of Mehmed Ali Pasha. The text is drawn from the Carnegie Library copy at the Pennsylvania State College.
Contents of the First Volume
Preface
Stephens explains his motives for travel: a desire to see the lands of the Bible and the monuments of ancient civilization, coupled with the advantages of his diplomatic appointment as United States special agent to the Sublime Porte.
Chapter I — Alexandria
Arrival at Alexandria. Description of Pompey’s Pillar, the catacombs, the Warwick Vase, and the Pasha’s canal. First impressions of Ottoman Egypt under the governorship of Mehmed Ali Pasha. The boats of the Nile.
Chapter II — From Alexandria to Cairo
The journey upriver. Stephens contrasts his experiences with the conventional travelers’ tales he had read. An unintended bath in the Nile. The iron rule of Mehmed Ali Pasha. Entrance into Cairo and a chat with a pasha.
Chapter III — Cairo: The Slave Market and the Pyramids
The slave-market at Cairo — a detailed description of the human trade under Ottoman rule. The tomb of the Pasha. The Pyramid of Cheops, oppressive attentions of the Bedouin Arabs, and the Sphinx.
Chapter IV — Journey up the Nile
Travel up the Nile. An Arab burial observed. Pilgrims en route to Mecca. Trials of patience with local officials. A hurricane on the Nile. Experience of a Turkish bath.
Chapter V — Sport and Adventure on the Nile
Sporting on the Nile. A recluse encountered. An Egyptian village scene. Siout (Asyut). A wolf-race among the tombs. Adventure with a provincial governor.
Chapter VI — Slavery and Crocodiles
Slavery in Egypt — Stephens’ observations on the institution as practiced under Ottoman rule. How to catch a crocodile. An elaborate practical joke. Imaginary perils of travel. Arabs not so bad as they might be.
Chapter VII — Dendera and Thebes
The Temple of Dendera. Practice versus theory in Egyptian archaeology. Regulating the sun. The French at Thebes. The Curse of Pharaoh. An Egyptian tournament. Preparations for dinner. An English traveling lady.
Chapter VIII — The Cataracts and Nubia
The Rock of the Chain. Ravages of the plague. Deserted quarries. A youthful navigator. Ancient inscriptions. An awkward discovery at a temple.
Chapter IX — Ascent of the Cataracts
Navigation past the cataracts. A nautical patriarch. Political improvement under Mehmed Ali. A Nubian damsel’s wardrobe. A test of friendship. East and West contrasted. Moonlight on the Nile.
Chapter X — Thebes: Temples and Tombs
Thebes and its great ruins. The Obelisk of Luxor (now in Paris). An avenue of sphinxes. Karnak. The Mummy-pits. The Tombs of the Kings. The Memnonium.
Chapter XI — The Arabs and the Pasha
The relationship between the Bedouin Arabs and Mehmed Ali Pasha’s government. March into the desert. Arab Christians encountered. A cold reception. Arab punctuality. A night in a convent. An Arab Christian priest. Speculative theology.
Chapter XII — Memphis and the Pyramids
A traveling artist and antiquary. An Egyptian sugar-house. Grecian architecture. A melancholy greeting. Tyranny of the Pasha — further observations on Mehmed Ali’s rule. Amateurs of physic. Memphis. Adventure with a wild boar. Perils of a pyramid. The catacombs of birds. Amor patriae.
Chapter XIII — Into the Desert
A good word for the Arabs. A prophecy fulfilled. Ruins of a lost city. A sheik of the Bedouins. Interviews and negotiations. A Hadj, or Pilgrimage to Mecca. Mohammedan heaven for wives. A French sheik. The bastinado. Departure for the desert.
Chapter XIV — Suez and the Red Sea
The caravan. Arab political economy. A projected railroad. The sirocco. Suez. A traveled Englishman. The Red Sea. Embarkation of pilgrims for Mecca. A misadventure. Scriptural localities. The bitter fountain (Marah).
Chapter XV — Sinai
The aspect of the mountains. Arab graves. The Pasha and the Bedouins. The value of water in the desert. Perplexing inscriptions. Habits of the Arabs. Ethics of the desert. Breach of the marriage vow. Arrival at the Convent of St. Catharine. An excess of welcome. Greece and America compared. Amor patriae.
Chapter XVI — Ascent of Mount Sinai
Ascent of Sinai. A miracle described. The Grotto of Elias. A monkish legend. The pinnacle of Sinai. Anchorites. Mohammed and his camel. An argument with monks. Legend of St. Catharine. The Rock of the Tables. The stone struck by Moses. Description of the Convent of St. Catharine. Habits and character of its inmates.
Chapter XVII — The Convent of St. Catharine
Diet of the monks. Advantages of abstinence. Scruples overcome. A mysterious brother. The convent burial-place. Strange charnel-house.
Contents of the Second Volume (Excerpted)
The second volume continues the journey through Ottoman Syria and Palestine covering Jerusalem, Bethlehem, the Dead Sea, the River Jordan, Nazareth, the Sea of Galilee, Damascus, and Baalbek, with detailed observations of Ottoman administration in the Levant, the condition of Christian and Jewish communities under Ottoman rule, the competing European consular jurisdictions, and the pilgrimage routes in the Holy Land.

primary-sources/tursun-beg-tarih-i-ebul-.title: Tursun Beg — Tarih-i Ebul Feth
primary-sources/tursun-beg-tarih-i-ebul-.content:
Tursun Beg — Tarih-i Ebul Feth (History of the Father of Conquest)
Tursun Beg (Turnisa Bey) wrote the Tarih-i Ebul Feth (History of the Father of Conquest) between approximately 1497 and 1500, during the reign of Bayezid II. The work covers the Ottoman state from Mehmed II’s first accession to the throne in 1442 through to the 1480s, encompassing a forty-seven-year span. Tursun Beg was a timar-holder and state official who rose to the position of chief finance minister (defterdar). He was present at the conquest of Istanbul in 1453 and accompanied the sultan into Hagia Sophia. He later undertook the registration of houses and shops in the newly conquered city under the delegation of Sultan Mehmed himself. The modern Turkish edition was prepared for publication by Ahmet Tezbaşar, based on the Ottoman Historical Society Journal edition, with three surviving manuscript copies in the Topkapı Palace Revan Library and the Ayasofya Library.
A notable feature of the work is its uneven coverage — events during the period when Tursun Beg personally accompanied campaigns are narrated in great detail, while later events after Mahmud Pasha’s dismissal from the grand vizierate are summarized or omitted entirely. The important affair of Sultan Cem is not mentioned at all. The book contains extensive Arabic and Persian verse passages which the author included for artistic reinforcement of his prose narrative.
Contents
Editor’s Foreword (A Few Words)
Ahmet Tezbaşar’s preface describes the scope of the work, the manuscript tradition, and the editorial choices made in preparing the modern Turkish edition. The three surviving manuscripts are all written in naskh script. The editor explains the simplification process: the original 15th-century Ottoman language is heavily dominated by Arabic and Persian vocabulary, so a freer approach was taken, with certain passages unrelated to historical events being omitted. A glossary was added for historical and military terms. The editor also notes the difficulty of reading place names, country names, and personal names pertaining to Anatolia and Rumelia.
About the Author
Tursun Beg’s actual name was Turnisa Bey, corrupted by common usage to Tursun. Despite holding high state office as defterdar, there is almost no record of him in history or biography books — his name appears only once in Sehi Bey’s tezkire. What is known comes from autobiographical remarks scattered through his own work. He was a timar-holder from a young age, likely the son of a military commander or zeamet-holder. He was proficient in Arabic and Persian, had studied the sciences and arts, and was personally known to Sultan Mehmed. After the conquest he gave up his timar to serve under his uncle Hacı Ali Bey (the governor of Bursa), and was entrusted by the sultan with registering the population and properties of the newly conquered Istanbul. Later in his career he rose to the position of defterdar under Mahmud Pasha’s patronage.
Traditional Opening
The history begins with the classical Ottoman historiographical framework: praise of God (hamd), praise of the Prophet (na’t), and praise of Sultan Bayezid II. A philosophical discourse on the necessity of sultanate draws on Nasir al-Din Tusi’s Akhlaq-i Nasiri and the four cardinal virtues. Stories are included about Alexander the Great and Ali ibn Abi Talib, along with sayings of Mahmud Pasha. This section traces the Ottoman sultanate through Murad II, Mehmed II’s first accession and the subsequent transfer back to Murad II, leading to Mehmed’s second and definitive accession.
The Second Accession and the Karaman Campaign
Mehmed’s reaccession to the throne after the death of Murad II. The Janissary discipline incident, where the new sultan asserts his authority over the soldiery. The Karaman campaign against the Karamanid beylik, one of Mehmed’s early military actions in Anatolia.
The Building of Boğazkesen (Rumeli Hisarı)
The strategic reasoning behind constructing the fortress on the European shore of the Bosphorus — the narrowest point of the strait. Tursun Beg provides a detailed description of the fortress and its construction process, the division of labour among the pashas for building sections of the walls, and the resulting confrontation with the Byzantine emperor Constantine XI.
The Conquest of Constantinople (1453)
The longest and most detailed section of the work. The accidental skirmish that triggered open hostilities between the Ottomans and Byzantines. The massive siege operations: the land army under Mehmed’s personal command, the naval forces, the great bombard cannons. The extraordinary feat of transporting Ottoman ships overland past the Byzantine chain boom across the Golden Horn. The final assault on 29 May 1453, the breaching of the walls, the death of Emperor Constantine XI (referred to as the Tekfur), and the sack of the city. Mehmed’s entry into the city and his visit to Hagia Sophia.
The Rebuilding of Istanbul
The immediate aftermath of the conquest: Mehmed’s program of repopulating the devastated city, the restoration of water systems, the construction of mosques (including the first imperial mosque complex), the building of the new palace (Saray-ı Cedid), the Eyüb Sultan complex, and the Kilitbahir fortress on the Dardanelles.
The Conquest of Aynoz and Taşoz
The capture of Aynoz (Ainos/Enez) on the Thracian coast and the island of Taşoz (Thasos), expanding Ottoman control over the northern Aegean.
The Belgrade Campaign (1456)
The reasons for the campaign against the Hungarian fortress of Belgrade. The siege operations, the intervention of John Hunyadi, the fierce battle, and the Ottoman withdrawal. Tursun Beg notes the death of Hunyadi shortly after the campaign.
The Circumcision Festival
A detailed account of the grand circumcision festival (Sünnet Düğünü) held in Edirne for the princes Bayezid and Mustafa — an important state ceremony demonstrating Ottoman wealth, power, and dynastic continuity.
The First Morea Campaign (1458)
Mehmed’s first campaign into the Peloponnese (Mora). The conquest of Corinth (Kurisus) and Patras (Ballubedreh), bringing the peninsula under Ottoman suzerainty.
Mahmud Pasha’s Laz Expedition and the Conquest of Semendre and Amasra
Grand Vizier Mahmud Pasha’s military expedition to the Laz country in northeastern Anatolia. The sieges of Resova, Mul, Smederevo (Semendre), Güğercinlik, and Mačva. The conquest of Amasra on the Black Sea coast.
The Conquest of the İsfendiyar Province and Sinop (1461)
The submission of İsmail Bey of the İsfendiyarid dynasty. The surrender of Sinop, one of the key Black Sea ports, and the incorporation of the İsfendiyar province into the Ottoman domain. İsmail Bey was granted the governorship of Scopia (Skopje) in exchange.
The Conquest of Koyluhisar and Trebizond (1461)
The campaign that culminated in the surrender of Trebizond (Trabzon), capital of the Empire of Trebizond under Emperor David Komnenos. The conquest of Koyluhisar fortress along the way. The pacification of Uzun Hasan’s Aq Qoyunlu, who had been allied with the Komnenoi.
The Matter of the Impaler Voivode (Vlad Dracula, 1462)
The campaign against Vlad III Dracula (Kazıklı Voyvoda), the voivode of Wallachia. Vlad’s brutal methods of impaling Ottoman prisoners. The Ottoman expedition, Vlad’s famous night attack on the Ottoman camp, his defeat and flight into Hungary. Radu the Fair (Vlad’s brother) installed as the Ottoman vassal voivode of Wallachia.
The Conquest of Midilli (Lesbos/Mytilene, 1462)
The naval campaign against the Genoese-held island of Lesbos. The siege of Mytilene, the betrayal of Nicorezzo Gattilusio, and the incorporation of the island into the Ottoman realm.
The Conquest of Bosnia (1463)
The campaign against the Kingdom of Bosnia. The capture of King Stephen Tomašević. The siege of Jajce (Yayçe) and the conquest of associated fortresses. Cave warfare tactics employed during the Bosnian campaign.
The Death of Karamanoglu İbrahim Bey and the Karaman Succession
The death of İbrahim Bey of the Karamanids and the subsequent struggle between his sons İshak Bey and Pir Ahmed Bey. Pir Ahmed Bey sought Ottoman support, leading to further Ottoman intervention in Karaman.
The Infidel Alliance and the Second Bosnia Campaign
The Venetian-Hungarian-Albanian coalition against the Ottomans. The defence of the Morea. The second Bosnia campaign and the recapture of Jajce. Mahmud Pasha’s relief of Midilli against Venetian attack.
The Siege of İzvornik (Zvornik)
Winter campaigning and the Hungarian siege of Zvornik on the Drina River. The use of Martolos messengers for communication through enemy territory. The Hungarian withdrawal.
The Albanian Campaigns and the Construction of Elbasan (1466–1467)
The first raid into Albania (Arnavutluk), the revolt of Albanian forces under Skanderbeg (İskender Bey), and the construction of Elbasan (İlbasan) Castle as a permanent Ottoman stronghold in the region. The second campaign into Albania, featuring mountain warfare and the attempt to capture Croia (Krujë).
The Sultan’s Karaman Campaign and the Expulsion of Pir Ahmed
Mehmed’s personal campaign into Karaman against Pir Ahmed, who had rebelled against Ottoman authority. Pir Ahmed’s expulsion from Karaman and the full incorporation of the beylik into the Ottoman state.
The Conquest of Ağriboz (Negroponte, 1470)
The conquest of the Venetian-held island of Negroponte (Eğriboz/Chalkis), a major Venetian fortress in the Aegean. The siege operations, the naval blockade, and the fall of the island to the Ottomans.
The Conquest of Alâiye (Alanya)
The incorporation of the coastal fortress and port of Alâiye (Alanya) on the southern Anatolian coast into the Ottoman domain.
The Battle of Otlukbeli and the Defeat of Uzun Hasan (1473)
The decisive Ottoman victory over Uzun Hasan’s Aq Qoyunlu at the Battle of Otlukbeli. The campaign narrative covers the mobilization of the Ottoman army, the march through eastern Anatolia, the battle itself with its use of artillery and Janissary infantry against the Aq Qoyunlu cavalry, and the pursuit of the defeated forces. This victory secured Ottoman dominance over eastern Anatolia.
The Conquest of Kefe (Caffa, 1475)
The naval expedition against the Genoese colony of Caffa (Kefe/Feodosia) in the Crimea. The surrender of the city and the establishment of Ottoman suzerainty over the Crimean Khanate.
The Campaign Against Kara Boğdan (Moldavia, 1476)
The campaign against Stephen the Great of Moldavia (Kara Boğdan). The Battle of Valea Albă (Războieni), where Stephen inflicted losses on the Ottomans, followed by the Ottoman counter-campaign and Stephen’s eventual submission as a vassal.
The Conquest of İskenderiye and Akça Hisar
Further military operations against Venetian and Hungarian holdings: the conquest of İskenderiye (Scutari/Shkodër) and Akça Hisar, consolidating Ottoman control in Albania and Bosnia.
Gedik Ahmed Pasha’s Campaign and the Rhodes Expedition
Gedik Ahmed Pasha’s naval campaign against Polya (Puglia/southern Italy), raiding the Italian coast. Mesih Pasha’s unsuccessful expedition against the Hospitaller-held island of Rhodes.
The Sultan’s Plan to Deliver Egypt and Syria
Mehmed’s intention to campaign against the Mamluk Sultanate in Egypt and Syria, a project cut short by his death. This section indicates the scope of Mehmed’s ambitions in his final years.
The Death of Sultan Mehmed Han (1481)
A detailed account of the sultan’s final illness and death. Tursun Beg notes the year as 1481 (or possibly 1479 in some recensions). The reaction of the state officials and the army, the succession crisis, and the beginning of the interregnum.
The Days of Interregnum (Fetret Günleri)
The struggle for succession between the princes Bayezid and Cem following Mehmed’s death. The Janissary uprising, the murder of Grand Vizier Mehmed Pasha (the Conqueror’s last vizier), and the factional conflicts at court.
Sultan Bayezid Han’s Accession to the Ottoman Throne
Bayezid II’s consolidation of power after defeating his brother Cem’s challenge. The re-establishment of central authority and the new sultan’s early policies.
The Conquest of Kili and Akkirman (1484)
Under Bayezid II, the conquest of the Black Sea fortresses of Kili (Chilia) and Akkirman (Cetatea Albă/Bilhorod-Dnistrovskyi), securing Ottoman control over the mouths of the Danube and Dniester rivers.
Karagöz Bey’s Expedition and Ali Pasha’s Campaign
The later military expeditions under Bayezid II’s reign: Karagöz Bey’s campaign into the Arab lands and Ali Pasha’s march against Karaboğdan (Moldavia), continuing Ottoman expansion into the early 1490s.
Glossary (Lugatçe)
A comprehensive Ottoman-era glossary appended by the modern editor, covering administrative, military, and technical terminology used throughout the work — from A through Z, with Ottoman terms and their Turkish and English equivalents.
